title: The Smell of Autumn Arriving
text_zh: 我一直觉得秋天是有气味的。不是桂花的香，虽然那也是秋天的标志。我说的是一种更微妙的东西——空气变干的时候，混合着落叶、远处烧什么东西的烟，还有一种说不清楚的凉意。每年大概九月中旬，我会在某个早晨突然闻到这种气味，然后心里想：秋天到了。这个判断跟温度无关，跟日历也无关，完全是鼻子告诉我的。我问过几个朋友，他们都觉得我在说一种不存在的东西。也许每个人感知季节的方式不同。我的秋天从鼻子开始，也许你的秋天从眼睛开始。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yìzhí juéde qiūtiān shì yǒu qìwèi de. Bú shì guìhuā de xiāng, suīrán nà yě shì qiūtiān de biāozhì. Wǒ shuō de shì yì zhǒng gèng wēimiào de dōngxi——kōngqì biàn gān de shíhou, hùnhé zhe luòyè, yuǎnchù shāo shénme dōngxi de yān, hái yǒu yì zhǒng shuō bù qīngchu de liángyì. Měi nián dàgài jiǔ yuè zhōngxún, wǒ huì zài mǒu gè zǎochen tūrán wén dào zhè zhǒng qìwèi, ránhòu xīnli xiǎng: qiūtiān dào le. Zhège pànduàn gēn wēndù wúguān, gēn rìlì yě wúguān, wánquán shì bízi gàosu wǒ de. Wǒ wènguò jǐ gè péngyou, tāmen dōu juéde wǒ zài shuō yì zhǒng bù cúnzài de dōngxi. Yěxǔ měi gè rén gǎnzhī jìjié de fāngshì bùtóng. Wǒ de qiūtiān cóng bízi kāishǐ, yěxǔ nǐ de qiūtiān cóng yǎnjing kāishǐ.
text_en: I've always felt that autumn has a smell. Not osmanthus fragrance, though that's also a marker of autumn. I mean something more subtle—when the air turns dry, mixed with fallen leaves, smoke from something burning in the distance, and an indescribable coolness. Every year around mid-September, one morning I'll suddenly catch this scent, and think: autumn has arrived. This judgment has nothing to do with temperature, nothing to do with the calendar—it's purely what my nose tells me. I asked a few friends and they all thought I was describing something that doesn't exist. Perhaps everyone perceives the seasons differently. My autumn starts with the nose; maybe yours starts with the eyes.

title: The Last Bench in the Park
text_zh: 公园的角落有一张长椅，漆已经剥落了大半，木头露出灰白色的纹理。每天下午三点左右，一个穿深色外套的老人会来坐一会儿。他不看手机，不跟人聊天，只是坐着，偶尔抬头看看树梢。有时候他带一个保温杯，但很少喝。我在对面的长椅上注意到他，纯粹是因为他是唯一一个坐下来什么都不做的人。公园里其他人都在锻炼、遛狗、刷短视频。他的静止反而成了一种引人注目的存在。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán de jiǎoluò yǒu yì zhāng chángyǐ, qī yǐjīng bōluò le dà bàn, mùtou lùchū huībái sè de wénlǐ. Měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn zuǒyòu, yí gè chuān shēn sè wàitào de lǎorén huì lái zuò yíhuìr. Tā bú kàn shǒujī, bù gēn rén liáotiān, zhǐ shì zuò zhe, ǒu'ěr táitóu kànkan shùshāo. Yǒu shíhou tā dài yí gè bǎowēn bēi, dàn hěn shǎo hē. Wǒ zài duìmiàn de chángyǐ shàng zhùyì dào tā, chúncuì shì yīnwèi tā shì wéiyī yí gè zuò xiàlái shénme dōu bú zuò de rén. Gōngyuán lǐ qítā rén dōu zài duànliàn, liú gǒu, shuā duǎn shìpín. Tā de jìngzhǐ fǎn'ér chéng le yì zhǒng yǐn rén zhùmù de cúnzài.
text_en: In the corner of the park there's a bench, its paint mostly peeled away, the wood showing pale gray grain. Every day around three in the afternoon, an old man in a dark jacket comes and sits for a while. He doesn't look at his phone, doesn't chat with anyone — he just sits, occasionally glancing up at the treetops. Sometimes he brings a thermos, but rarely drinks from it. I noticed him from the bench across the way, purely because he's the only person who sits down and does nothing. Everyone else in the park is exercising, walking dogs, scrolling through short videos. His stillness has become a kind of conspicuous presence.

title: The Sound of the Elevator
text_zh: 住了三年以后，我才意识到我们楼的电梯有一种独特的声音。不是机械的嗡嗡声——每部电梯都有那个。我说的是它到达每一层时发出的那一声轻响，像是金属在叹气。搬来的第一年我完全没注意到。第二年，有一天深夜等电梯的时候突然听见了。从那以后就再也无法忽略。现在每次听到那个声音，我就知道有人到家了，或者有人正在离开。整栋楼的生活节奏都藏在这个声音里。
text_pinyin: Zhù le sān nián yǐhòu, wǒ cái yìshí dào wǒmen lóu de diàntī yǒu yì zhǒng dútè de shēngyīn. Bú shì jīxiè de wēngwēng shēng — měi bù diàntī dōu yǒu nàge. Wǒ shuō de shì tā dàodá měi yì céng shí fāchū de nà yì shēng qīng xiǎng, xiàng shì jīnshǔ zài tànqì. Bān lái de dì yī nián wǒ wánquán méi zhùyì dào. Dì èr nián, yǒu yì tiān shēnyè děng diàntī de shíhou tūrán tīngjiàn le. Cóng nà yǐhòu jiù zài yě wúfǎ hūlüè. Xiànzài měi cì tīng dào nàge shēngyīn, wǒ jiù zhīdào yǒu rén dào jiā le, huòzhě yǒu rén zhèngzài líkāi. Zhěng dòng lóu de shēnghuó jiézòu dōu cáng zài zhège shēngyīn lǐ.
text_en: After living here three years, I finally realized our building's elevator has a distinctive sound. Not the mechanical hum — every elevator has that. I mean the soft sound it makes arriving at each floor, like metal sighing. My first year here I didn't notice it at all. In the second year, one late night while waiting for the elevator, I suddenly heard it. After that I could never unhear it. Now every time I hear that sound, I know someone has come home, or someone is leaving. The whole building's rhythm of life is hidden in that sound.

title: The Closing Ritual
text_zh: 我家楼下的水果店每天晚上九点半关门。老板不是直接拉下卷帘门，而是有一套完整的流程。先把门口的水果一箱一箱搬进去，每搬一箱都会低头看一眼，好像在跟它们确认今天的表现。然后用一块湿布擦秤台，再把塑料袋整理好。最后站在门口抽一根烟，看看街上还有没有人经过。这整个过程大概需要二十分钟。我有时候从窗户往下看，觉得这不像是在关店，更像是一个人在结束自己的一天——缓慢、认真、带着一种不急不慢的尊严。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóuxià de shuǐguǒ diàn měi tiān wǎnshang jiǔ diǎn bàn guān mén. Lǎobǎn bú shì zhíjiē lā xià juǎnlián mén, ér shì yǒu yí tào wánzhěng de liúchéng. Xiān bǎ ménkǒu de shuǐguǒ yì xiāng yì xiāng bān jìnqù, měi bān yì xiāng dōu huì dītóu kàn yì yǎn, hǎoxiàng zài gēn tāmen quèrèn jīntiān de biǎoxiàn. Ránhòu yòng yí kuài shī bù cā chèng tái, zài bǎ sùliào dài zhěnglǐ hǎo. Zuìhòu zhàn zài ménkǒu chōu yì gēn yān, kànkan jiē shàng hái yǒu méiyǒu rén jīngguò. Zhè zhěng gè guòchéng dàgài xūyào èrshí fēnzhōng. Wǒ yǒu shíhou cóng chuānghù wǎng xià kàn, juéde zhè bú xiàng shì zài guān diàn, gèng xiàng shì yí gè rén zài jiéshù zìjǐ de yì tiān — huǎnmàn, rènzhēn, dài zhe yì zhǒng bù jí bú màn de zūnyán.
text_en: The fruit shop downstairs from my apartment closes at 9:30 every night. The owner doesn't just pull down the rolling shutter — he has a complete routine. First he carries the fruit crates in from the doorway one by one, glancing down at each one as if confirming how it performed today. Then he wipes the scale with a damp cloth and tidies up the plastic bags. Finally he stands in the doorway smoking a cigarette, checking if anyone's still passing by on the street. The whole process takes about twenty minutes. Sometimes I watch from my window and feel it's not like closing a shop — it's more like a person finishing their day: slow, deliberate, with an unhurried dignity.

title: The Regulars at the Noodle Shop
text_zh: 我常去的那家面馆很小，只有六张桌子。去的次数多了以后，我开始认出一些常客。早上七点半左右会来一个穿制服的中年男人，每次都点牛肉面加一个鸡蛋，吃得很快，从不看手机。中午有一对老夫妻，两个人共享一碗面和一盘凉菜，吃得很慢，偶尔说几句话。下午三点会来一个年轻女生，戴着耳机，点最便宜的素面，在那儿坐很久，好像在写什么东西。我不知道他们的名字，但我知道他们的习惯。这种了解算不算一种认识？我们在同一个空间里各自生活，彼此之间只有一种无声的默契。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de nà jiā miànguǎn hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu liù zhāng zhuōzi. Qù de cìshù duō le yǐhòu, wǒ kāishǐ rèn chū yìxiē chángkè. Zǎoshang qī diǎn bàn zuǒyòu huì lái yí gè chuān zhìfú de zhōngnián nánrén, měi cì dōu diǎn niúròu miàn jiā yí gè jīdàn, chī de hěn kuài, cóng bú kàn shǒujī. Zhōngwǔ yǒu yí duì lǎo fūqī, liǎng gè rén gòngxiǎng yì wǎn miàn hé yì pán liángcài, chī de hěn màn, ǒu'ěr shuō jǐ jù huà. Xiàwǔ sān diǎn huì lái yí gè niánqīng nǚshēng, dài zhe ěrjī, diǎn zuì piányi de sùmiàn, zài nàr zuò hěn jiǔ, hǎoxiàng zài xiě shénme dōngxi. Wǒ bù zhīdào tāmen de míngzi, dàn wǒ zhīdào tāmen de xíguàn. Zhè zhǒng liǎojiě suàn bú suàn yì zhǒng rènshi? Wǒmen zài tóng yí gè kōngjiān lǐ gèzì shēnghuó, bǐcǐ zhī jiān zhǐ yǒu yì zhǒng wúshēng de mòqì.
text_en: The noodle shop I frequent is small — only six tables. After going enough times, I started recognizing the regulars. Around 7:30 AM, a middle-aged man in uniform comes in, always orders beef noodles with an egg, eats quickly, never looks at his phone. At noon there's an elderly couple who share one bowl of noodles and a plate of cold dishes, eating slowly, exchanging a few words now and then. At 3 PM a young woman comes in wearing earbuds, orders the cheapest vegetarian noodles, sits for a long time, apparently writing something. I don't know their names, but I know their habits. Does this kind of understanding count as knowing someone? We each live our lives in the same space, connected only by a silent, unspoken accord.

title: The Map That Keeps Changing
text_zh: 我手机里有一张三年前截屏的地图，上面标着我当时常去的地方：一家独立书店、一个很好的咖啡馆、一条适合散步的小巷。现在打开实时地图再看，三个地方都不在了。书店变成了奶茶店，咖啡馆变成了连锁药房，小巷被围起来正在施工。城市就是这样——它不会通知你它在改变。你只是某一天按照记忆走过去，发现路还在，但目的地已经消失了。地图可以更新，但记忆里的那个版本会一直留着。有时候我会想，我真正怀念的不是那些地方本身，而是我在那些地方时的自己。
text_pinyin: Wǒ shǒujī lǐ yǒu yì zhāng sān nián qián jiépíng de dìtú, shàngmiàn biāo zhe wǒ dāngshí cháng qù de dìfāng: yì jiā dúlì shūdiàn, yí gè hěn hǎo de kāfēiguǎn, yì tiáo shìhé sànbù de xiǎo xiàng. Xiànzài dǎkāi shíshí dìtú zài kàn, sān gè dìfāng dōu bú zài le. Shūdiàn biàn chéng le nǎichá diàn, kāfēiguǎn biàn chéng le liánsuǒ yàofáng, xiǎo xiàng bèi wéi qǐlái zhèngzài shīgōng. Chéngshì jiù shì zhèyàng — tā bú huì tōngzhī nǐ tā zài gǎibiàn. Nǐ zhǐ shì mǒu yì tiān ànzhào jìyì zǒu guòqù, fāxiàn lù hái zài, dàn mùdìdì yǐjīng xiāoshī le. Dìtú kěyǐ gēngxīn, dàn jìyì lǐ de nàge bǎnběn huì yìzhí liú zhe. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng, wǒ zhēnzhèng huáiniàn de bú shì nàxiē dìfāng běnshēn, ér shì wǒ zài nàxiē dìfāng shí de zìjǐ.
text_en: On my phone there's a screenshot of a map from three years ago, marking places I used to frequent: an independent bookstore, a great café, an alley perfect for walking. Opening the live map now, all three are gone. The bookstore became a milk tea shop, the café became a chain pharmacy, the alley is fenced off for construction. That's how cities work — they don't notify you they're changing. You just walk somewhere one day following memory, and find the road is still there but the destination has vanished. Maps can be updated, but the version in your memory stays forever. Sometimes I think what I truly miss isn't those places themselves, but who I was when I was in them.

title: The Construction Sound Calendar
text_zh: 搬到这个小区以后，我发现可以通过施工的声音来判断时间和季节。早上七点，打桩机开始响，比闹钟准。中午十二点到一点，安静一个小时——工人们在吃饭。下午的声音会变，从打桩变成切割，最后变成收拾工具的零碎声。到了冬天，开工时间推迟半小时；夏天则提前。节假日完全安静，安静得让人有些不习惯。有一次春节放了十五天假，等施工声重新响起来的那个早晨，我竟然觉得有一种奇怪的安心感——好像城市的心跳恢复了。
text_pinyin: Bān dào zhège xiǎoqū yǐhòu, wǒ fāxiàn kěyǐ tōngguò shīgōng de shēngyīn lái pànduàn shíjiān hé jìjié. Zǎoshang qī diǎn, dǎ zhuāng jī kāishǐ xiǎng, bǐ nàozhōng zhǔn. Zhōngwǔ shí'èr diǎn dào yì diǎn, ānjìng yí gè xiǎoshí — gōngrén men zài chīfàn. Xiàwǔ de shēngyīn huì biàn, cóng dǎ zhuāng biàn chéng qiēgē, zuìhòu biàn chéng shōushi gōngjù de língsuì shēng. Dào le dōngtiān, kāigōng shíjiān tuīchí bàn xiǎoshí; xiàtiān zé tíqián. Jiéjiàrì wánquán ānjìng, ānjìng de ràng rén yǒuxiē bù xíguàn. Yǒu yí cì Chūnjié fàng le shíwǔ tiān jiǎ, děng shīgōng shēng chóngxīn xiǎng qǐlái de nàge zǎochen, wǒ jìngrán juéde yǒu yì zhǒng qíguài de ānxīn gǎn — hǎoxiàng chéngshì de xīntiào huīfù le.
text_en: After moving to this neighborhood, I discovered I could tell the time and season by construction sounds. At 7 AM, the pile driver starts — more reliable than an alarm clock. From noon to one, an hour of quiet — the workers are eating. The afternoon sounds shift, from pile driving to cutting, finally to the scattered sounds of packing up tools. In winter, the start time pushes back half an hour; in summer it moves earlier. Holidays are completely silent, so silent it feels strange. Once during a fifteen-day Spring Festival break, the morning the construction sounds resumed, I felt an oddly reassuring sensation — as if the city's heartbeat had returned.

questions.q_zh: 中午为什么安静一个小时？
questions.q_en: Why is it quiet for an hour at noon?
questions.explanation: The noon quiet coincides with lunchtime -- the workers are eating, so the drilling and hammering pause.

questions.q_zh: 春节以后施工声重新响起时，作者有什么感觉？
questions.q_en: How did the author feel when construction resumed after Spring Festival?

options.pinyin: yì zhǒng qíguài de ānxīn gǎn

questions.explanation: The passage captures the reaction: the morning the construction sounds resumed.

questions.q_zh: 作者通过叙述想引发读者什么思考？
questions.q_en: What reflection does the author want to provoke in the reader?

options.text: 对日常生活更深的理解
options.pinyin: duì rìcháng shēnghuó gèng shēn de lǐjiě

questions.explanation: The passage reframes an ordinary scene as meaningful, pushing the reader to look closer at routines.

title: The Hospital Cafeteria at Midnight
text_zh: 我陪家人住院的那一周，每天深夜都去医院食堂吃饭。白天的食堂很普通，跟任何单位食堂没什么区别。但凌晨的食堂完全是另一个世界。灯光调得很暗，只有角落几张桌子坐着人——有穿手术服出来的医生，有守了一夜的家属，偶尔还有几个穿着病号服的病人。没人说话，或者说话声音很低。每个人面前都有一碗面或者一份简单的炒饭。在那个时间、那个地方吃东西，食物的味道变得不一样了。不是更好吃或者更难吃，而是每一口都显得格外真实。那是人在疲惫和担忧之后，对自己最基本的照顾。
text_pinyin: Wǒ péi jiārén zhùyuàn de nà yì zhōu, měi tiān shēnyè dōu qù yīyuàn shítáng chīfàn. Báitiān de shítáng hěn pǔtōng, gēn rènhé dānwèi shítáng méi shénme qūbié. Dàn língchén de shítáng wánquán shì lìng yí gè shìjiè. Dēngguāng diào de hěn àn, zhǐ yǒu jiǎoluò jǐ zhāng zhuōzi zuò zhe rén — yǒu chuān shǒushù fú chūlái de yīshēng, yǒu shǒu le yí yè de jiāshǔ, ǒu'ěr hái yǒu jǐ gè chuān zhe bìnghào fú de bìngrén. Méi rén shuōhuà, huòzhě shuōhuà shēngyīn hěn dī. Měi gè rén miànqián dōu yǒu yì wǎn miàn huòzhě yí fèn jiǎndān de chǎofàn. Zài nàge shíjiān, nàge dìfāng chī dōngxi, shíwù de wèidào biàn de bù yíyàng le. Bú shì gèng hǎochī huòzhě gèng nánchī, ér shì měi yì kǒu dōu xiǎnde géwài zhēnshí. Nà shì rén zài píbèi hé dānyōu zhīhòu, duì zìjǐ zuì jīběn de zhàogù.
text_en: The week I stayed with a family member at the hospital, I went to the hospital cafeteria every night. During the day it's ordinary, no different from any institutional cafeteria. But at dawn it's a completely different world. The lights are dimmed, only a few tables in the corners are occupied — doctors still in surgical scrubs, family members who've been up all night, occasionally a few patients in hospital gowns. No one talks, or they speak very softly. Everyone has a bowl of noodles or a simple fried rice in front of them. Eating at that hour, in that place, food tastes different. Not better or worse, but every bite feels extraordinarily real. It's the most basic act of caring for yourself after exhaustion and worry.

questions.q_zh: 凌晨的食堂和白天有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is the cafeteria at dawn different from daytime?

options.pinyin: dēngguāng àn, rén shǎo, hěn ānjìng
options.text_en: Dim lights, fewer people, very quiet

questions.explanation: At dawn the lights are dimmed and almost no one is there -- a stark contrast to the busy daytime cafeteria.

questions.q_zh: 这段话给读者留下了什么思考空间？
questions.q_en: What space for thought does this passage leave the reader?

options.pinyin: guānyú gèrén jiàzhí hé yìyì de fǎnsī
options.text_en: reflection on personal value and meaning

options.pinyin: rúhé zhuàn gèng duō de qián

questions.explanation: The open-ended close raises questions about what matters, prompting personal reflection.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么每天深夜去食堂？
questions.q_en: Why does the author go to the cafeteria every night?

options.text_en: He's staying with a hospitalized family member

questions.explanation: The passage explains: I went to the hospital cafeteria every night.

title: The Light in Apartment 1402
text_zh: 对面楼十四层第二间公寓的灯总是在凌晨两点到三点之间亮着。我注意到这件事完全是偶然——失眠的时候站在阳台上发现的。后来我开始留意，连续观察了两个星期，发现规律非常稳定：两点左右亮，三点左右灭。窗帘是拉上的，只能看到一层暖黄色的光透出来。我开始猜测：是夜班回来的人？是照顾婴儿的父母？是写论文的研究生？还是跟我一样睡不着的人？这个问题没有答案，但想的过程本身让失眠变得没那么难受了。我们各自在自己的窗户后面，共享同一片夜色。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn lóu shísì céng dì èr jiān gōngyù de dēng zǒngshì zài língchén liǎng diǎn dào sān diǎn zhī jiān liàng zhe. Wǒ zhùyì dào zhè jiàn shì wánquán shì ǒurán — shīmián de shíhou zhàn zài yángtái shàng fāxiàn de. Hòulái wǒ kāishǐ liúyì, liánxù guānchá le liǎng gè xīngqī, fāxiàn guīlǜ fēicháng wěndìng: liǎng diǎn zuǒyòu liàng, sān diǎn zuǒyòu miè. Chuānglián shì lā shàng de, zhǐ néng kàndào yì céng nuǎn huáng sè de guāng tòu chūlái. Wǒ kāishǐ cāicè: shì yèbān huílái de rén? Shì zhàogù yīng'ér de fùmǔ? Shì xiě lùnwén de yánjiūshēng? Háishì gēn wǒ yíyàng shuì bù zháo de rén? Zhège wèntí méiyǒu dá'àn, dàn xiǎng de guòchéng běnshēn ràng shīmián biàn de méi nàme nánshòu le. Wǒmen gèzì zài zìjǐ de chuānghù hòumiàn, gòngxiǎng tóng yí piàn yèsè.
text_en: The light in the second apartment on the fourteenth floor of the building across from mine is always on between 2 and 3 AM. I noticed this entirely by accident — standing on my balcony during a bout of insomnia. Later I started paying attention, observing for two consecutive weeks, and found the pattern remarkably stable: on around two, off around three. The curtains are drawn, showing only a layer of warm yellow light filtering through. I began to speculate: someone coming home from a night shift? Parents caring for an infant? A grad student writing a thesis? Or someone who, like me, simply can't sleep? There's no answer to this question, but the process of wondering itself made insomnia less miserable. Each of us behind our own windows, sharing the same night sky.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎么发现这盏灯的？
questions.q_en: How did the author discover this light?

options.text: 失眠时站在阳台上偶然发现的
options.pinyin: shīmián shí zhàn zài yángtái shàng ǒurán fāxiàn de
options.text_en: By chance while standing on the balcony during insomnia

questions.explanation: The discovery was accidental: standing on the balcony during a bout of insomnia, the author noticed the light.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中最关键的转折是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most important turning point in this passage?
questions.explanation: The pivot comes when the narrator's perception shifts from casual to conscious.

questions.q_zh: 猜测1402住户的过程对作者有什么影响？

ràng tā qù qiāo mén wèn
options.text_en: Made him go knock and ask

questions.explanation: Speculating about the unknown resident gave the author something to think about, making sleepless nights less miserable.

title: The Language I Dream In
text_zh: 有人问我，你做梦的时候说什么语言？这个问题比听起来复杂得多。在国内的时候，梦里自然都是中文。出国以后的头几年，梦还是中文的。后来慢慢变了——有时候梦里的人说英文，有时候说一种根本不存在的语言，我听得懂但醒来以后完全想不起来。最奇怪的是，偶尔会梦到自己在说一种带口音的中文，那个口音不是我的，也不是我认识的任何人的。语言学家说，梦里的语言反映的是身份认同的深层状态。如果这是真的，那我的身份认同大概正处于一种混合和重组的过程中——还没有定型，也许永远不会。
text_pinyin: Yǒu rén wèn wǒ, nǐ zuòmèng de shíhou shuō shénme yǔyán? Zhège wèntí bǐ tīng qǐlái fùzá de duō. Zài guónèi de shíhou, mèng lǐ zìrán dōu shì Zhōngwén. Chūguó yǐhòu de tóu jǐ nián, mèng háishì Zhōngwén de. Hòulái mànmàn biàn le — yǒu shíhou mèng lǐ de rén shuō Yīngwén, yǒu shíhou shuō yì zhǒng gēnběn bù cúnzài de yǔyán, wǒ tīng de dǒng dàn xǐng lái yǐhòu wánquán xiǎng bù qǐlái. Zuì qíguài de shì, ǒu'ěr huì mèng dào zìjǐ zài shuō yì zhǒng dài kǒuyīn de Zhōngwén, nàge kǒuyīn bú shì wǒ de, yě bú shì wǒ rènshi de rènhé rén de. Yǔyán xuéjiā shuō, mèng lǐ de yǔyán fǎnyìng de shì shēnfèn rèntóng de shēncéng zhuàngtài. Rúguǒ zhè shì zhēn de, nà wǒ de shēnfèn rèntóng dàgài zhèng chǔyú yì zhǒng hùnhé hé chóngzǔ de guòchéng zhōng — hái méiyǒu dìngxíng, yěxǔ yǒngyuǎn bú huì.
text_en: Someone asked me what language I dream in. The question is far more complicated than it sounds. When I was still in China, dreams were naturally all in Chinese. The first few years after going abroad, dreams were still in Chinese. Then gradually things changed — sometimes people in my dreams speak English, sometimes a language that doesn't exist at all, one I understand but can't recall after waking. The strangest part is occasionally dreaming of myself speaking Chinese with an accent that isn't mine, and isn't anyone I know's either. Linguists say the language of dreams reflects the deep state of identity. If that's true, then my identity is probably in a process of mixing and recombining — not yet settled, and perhaps never will be.

questions.q_zh: 出国以后，作者梦里的语言怎么变了？
questions.q_en: How did the author's dream language change after going abroad?

options.text: 有时英文，有时不存在的语言
options.pinyin: yǒu shí Yīngwén, yǒu shí bù cúnzài de yǔyán
options.text_en: Sometimes English, sometimes a nonexistent language

questions.explanation: The shift is described: sometimes a language that doesn't exist at all.

questions.q_en: What's the strangest phenomenon in the author's dreams?

options.pinyin: tīng bù dǒng biérén shuō de huà

options.text: 说一种带陌生口音的中文
options.pinyin: shuō yì zhǒng dài mòshēng kǒuyīn de Zhōngwén
options.text_en: Speaking Chinese with an unfamiliar accent

questions.explanation: The strangest part: in dreams, the author speaks Chinese but with an unfamiliar accent -- neither native nor foreign.

questions.q_zh: 作者对自己的身份认同怎么看？
questions.q_en: How does the author view their own identity?

options.text_en: In a process of mixing and recombining

questions.explanation: The mixing of dream languages mirrors the author's sense that identity is fluid, not fixed.

title: The Meeting That Could Have Been an Email
title_zh: 一封邮件就能解决的会议
text_zh: 今天开了一个两小时的会。议题是讨论下次会议的时间。我没有在开玩笑。先是部门主管花了十五分钟解释为什么需要开这个会来讨论下次会议的时间——因为上次用邮件讨论没有达成一致。然后每个人依次发言，说自己哪天有空。有两个同事的时间完全对不上，于是大家开始讨论是不是可以分成两组开。讨论分组又花了四十分钟。最后决定：下次会议的时间，再发一封邮件确认。散会的时候，坐我旁边的同事小声说：「我觉得我的灵魂刚才离开了身体大约四十五分钟。」我看了一眼他的笔记本，上面画满了小鱼。
text_pinyin: Jīntiān kāi le yí gè liǎng xiǎoshí de huì. Yìtí shì tǎolùn xià cì huìyì de shíjiān. Wǒ méiyǒu zài kāi wánxiào. Xiān shì bùmén zhǔguǎn huā le shíwǔ fēnzhōng jiěshì wèishénme xūyào kāi zhège huì lái tǎolùn xià cì huìyì de shíjiān — yīnwèi shàng cì yòng yóujiàn tǎolùn méiyǒu dáchéng yízhì. Ránhòu měi gè rén yīcì fāyán, shuō zìjǐ nǎ tiān yǒu kòng. Yǒu liǎng gè tóngshì de shíjiān wánquán duì bú shàng, yúshì dàjiā kāishǐ tǎolùn shì bú shì kěyǐ fēn chéng liǎng zǔ kāi. Tǎolùn fēnzǔ yòu huā le sìshí fēnzhōng. Zuìhòu juédìng: xià cì huìyì de shíjiān, zài fā yì fēng yóujiàn quèrèn. Sànhuì de shíhou, zuò wǒ pángbiān de tóngshì xiǎoshēng shuō:「Wǒ juéde wǒ de línghún gāngcái líkāi le shēntǐ dàyuē sìshíwǔ fēnzhōng.」Wǒ kàn le yì yǎn tā de bǐjìběn, shàngmiàn huà mǎn le xiǎo yú.
text_en: Today we had a two-hour meeting. The agenda was to discuss the time of the next meeting. I am not joking. First the department head spent fifteen minutes explaining why we needed a meeting to discuss the time of the next meeting — because the last email discussion hadn't reached consensus. Then everyone took turns saying which days they were free. Two colleagues' schedules were completely incompatible, so the group started discussing whether to split into two sub-meetings. Discussing the split took another forty minutes. The final decision: the time of the next meeting would be confirmed by another email. As the meeting ended, my colleague sitting next to me whispered: 'I think my soul left my body for about forty-five minutes.' I glanced at his notebook — it was covered in little fish drawings.

questions.q_zh: 这次会议的议题是什么？
questions.q_en: What was this meeting's agenda?

options.pinyin: tǎolùn xià cì huìyì de shíjiān
options.text_en: Discussing the time of the next meeting

questions.explanation: Absurdist detail: the entire meeting's agenda was to discuss the time of the next meeting.

questions.q_en: What was the final decision?

options.text_en: Set the time for the next meeting

questions.q_zh: 同事的笔记本上画了什么？

questions.explanation: While everyone else pretended to engage, the colleague's notebook was covered in little fish doodles.

title: Grandmother's Dumplings
text_zh: 我奶奶包饺子从来不看食谱，也不量任何东西。面粉加多少水，全凭手感。馅儿的调味也是——一把盐、几滴酱油、一点姜末，她的「一把」「几滴」「一点」每次都不完全一样，但最后的味道却总是对的。我试过让她教我，她站在旁边说：「面粉不够，再加一点。」我问：多少算一点？她说：「你摸一下面团就知道了。」我摸了，完全不知道。这种知识没法用语言传递，只能通过几十年的重复来获得。现在奶奶手不太稳了，包出来的饺子有大有小，形状也不太规则。但味道还是一样的。
text_pinyin: Wǒ nǎinai bāo jiǎozi cónglái bú kàn shípǔ, yě bú liáng rènhé dōngxi. Miànfěn jiā duōshǎo shuǐ, quán píng shǒugǎn. Xiànr de tiáowèi yě shì — yì bǎ yán, jǐ dī jiàngyóu, yìdiǎn jiāng mò, tā de「yì bǎ」「jǐ dī」「yìdiǎn」měi cì dōu bù wánquán yíyàng, dàn zuìhòu de wèidào què zǒngshì duì de. Wǒ shì guò ràng tā jiāo wǒ, tā zhàn zài pángbiān shuō:「Miànfěn bú gòu, zài jiā yìdiǎn.」Wǒ wèn: duōshǎo suàn yìdiǎn? Tā shuō:「Nǐ mō yíxià miàntuán jiù zhīdào le.」Wǒ mō le, wánquán bù zhīdào. Zhè zhǒng zhīshi méi fǎ yòng yǔyán chuándì, zhǐ néng tōngguò jǐ shí nián de chóngfù lái huòdé. Xiànzài nǎinai shǒu bú tài wěn le, bāo chūlái de jiǎozi yǒu dà yǒu xiǎo, xíngzhuàng yě bú tài guīzé. Dàn wèidào háishì yíyàng de. Yěxǔ wèidào shì zuìhòu xiāoshī de dōngxi.
text_en: My grandmother never looks at a recipe or measures anything when making dumplings. How much water to add to the flour — all by feel. Seasoning the filling too: a handful of salt, a few drops of soy sauce, a bit of minced ginger. Her 'handful,' 'few drops,' and 'a bit' are never exactly the same, but the final taste is always right. I once asked her to teach me. She stood beside me and said: 'Not enough flour, add a little more.' I asked: how much is a little? She said: 'Feel the dough and you'll know.' I felt it. I had no idea. This kind of knowledge can't be transmitted through language — it can only be acquired through decades of repetition. Now grandmother's hands aren't so steady, and her dumplings come out different sizes, not quite regular in shape. But the taste is still the same.

questions.q_zh: 这段话对'时间'的处理方式说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the way this passage handles 'time' suggest?

options.text: 时间不只是客观的流逝
options.pinyin: shíjiān bù zhǐ shì kèguān de liúshì
options.text_en: time is more than objective passing

options.pinyin: xūyào gèng hǎo de guǎnlǐ shíjiān

questions.explanation: The slow, deliberate pacing treats time as experiential, not just measured.

questions.q_zh: 作者学包饺子的结果怎样？
questions.q_en: How did the author's dumpling-learning go?

options.text: 摸了面团但完全不知道
options.pinyin: mō le miàntuán dàn wánquán bù zhīdào
options.text_en: Felt the dough but had no idea

options.pinyin: zuò de bǐ nǎinai hái hǎo

questions.explanation: The passage describes: said: 'Feel the dough and you'll know.' I felt it.

questions.q_zh: 如果去掉这段话的最后一句，意义会怎样变化？

title: Why Escalators Have Brushes
title_zh: 自动扶梯为什么有刷子
text_zh: 你有没有注意过自动扶梯两侧底部那一排黑色的刷子？大多数人以为它们是用来清洁鞋子的。其实不是。那些刷子的真正作用是安全防护。自动扶梯的台阶和侧板之间有一条很窄的缝隙，如果鞋子、衣服或者包的带子被卷进去，后果可能很严重。刷子的存在是为了让你的脚本能地跟缝隙保持距离——当你感到刷子碰到鞋子或裤腿的时候，会不自觉地把脚往中间移。这是一种不需要文字说明的设计语言。好的公共设施设计就是这样：你甚至不知道它在保护你，但它确实在保护你。
text_pinyin: Nǐ yǒu méiyǒu zhùyì guò zìdòng fútī liǎng cè dǐbù nà yì pái hēisè de shuāzi? Dàduōshù rén yǐwéi tāmen shì yòng lái qīngjié xiézi de. Qíshí bú shì. Nàxiē shuāzi de zhēnzhèng zuòyòng shì ānquán fánghù. Zìdòng fútī de táijiē hé cèbǎn zhī jiān yǒu yì tiáo hěn zhǎi de fèngxì, rúguǒ xiézi, yīfu huòzhě bāo de dàizi bèi juǎn jìnqù, hòuguǒ kěnéng hěn yánzhòng. Shuāzi de cúnzài shì wèi le ràng nǐ de jiǎo běnnéng de gēn fèngxì bǎochí jùlí — dāng nǐ gǎndào shuāzi pèng dào xiézi huò kùtuǐ de shíhou, huì bù zìjué de bǎ jiǎo wǎng zhōngjiān yí. Zhè shì yì zhǒng bù xūyào wénzì shuōmíng de shèjì yǔyán. Hǎo de gōnggòng shèshī shèjì jiù shì zhèyàng: nǐ shènzhì bù zhīdào tā zài bǎohù nǐ, dàn tā quèshí zài bǎohù nǐ.
text_en: Have you ever noticed the row of black brushes at the bottom of escalator sides? Most people think they're for cleaning shoes. They're not. The real purpose of those brushes is safety. There's a narrow gap between the escalator steps and the side panel — if shoes, clothing, or bag straps get pulled in, the consequences can be serious. The brushes exist to make your feet instinctively keep their distance from the gap — when you feel the brush touching your shoe or pant leg, you unconsciously move your foot toward the center. It's a design language that needs no written instructions. Good public infrastructure design works exactly this way: you don't even know it's protecting you, but it is.

title: The Way People Hold Umbrellas
text_zh: 下雨天站在天桥上往下看，你会发现每个人撑伞的方式都不一样。有人把伞举得很高，走得很快，好像雨只是一个小小的不便。有人把伞压得很低，缩着肩膀，仿佛想把整个世界挡在外面。情侣共撑一把伞的时候，通常是其中一个人的肩膀在淋雨，但两个人都假装没有注意到。最有意思的是那些不打伞的人——有的在跑，有的在慢慢走，还有的干脆站在屋檐下抽烟等雨停。一场雨就像一面镜子，照出每个人面对同一件事时不同的态度。
text_pinyin: Xià yǔ tiān zhàn zài tiānqiáo shàng wǎng xià kàn, nǐ huì fāxiàn měi gè rén chēng sǎn de fāngshì dōu bù yíyàng. Yǒu rén bǎ sǎn jǔ de hěn gāo, zǒu de hěn kuài, hǎoxiàng yǔ zhǐ shì yí gè xiǎo xiǎo de búbiàn. Yǒu rén bǎ sǎn yā de hěn dī, suō zhe jiānbǎng, fǎngfú xiǎng bǎ zhěng gè shìjiè dǎng zài wàimiàn. Qínglǚ gòng chēng yì bǎ sǎn de shíhou, tōngcháng shì qízhōng yí gè rén de jiānbǎng zài lín yǔ, dàn liǎng gè rén dōu jiǎzhuāng méiyǒu zhùyì dào. Zuì yǒu yìsi de shì nàxiē bù dǎ sǎn de rén — yǒu de zài pǎo, yǒu de zài mànmàn zǒu, hái yǒu de gāncuì zhàn zài wūyán xià chōu yān děng yǔ tíng. Yì chǎng yǔ jiù xiàng yí miàn jìngzi, zhào chū měi gè rén miànduì tóng yí jiàn shì shí bùtóng de tàidù.
text_en: Standing on a pedestrian bridge on a rainy day looking down, you notice everyone holds their umbrella differently. Some hold it high and walk fast, as if rain is just a minor inconvenience. Some press it low, shoulders hunched, as if trying to block out the whole world. When couples share an umbrella, usually one person's shoulder is getting rained on, but both pretend not to notice. Most interesting are those without umbrellas — some run, some walk slowly, and some simply stand under eaves smoking, waiting for the rain to stop. A rain shower is like a mirror, reflecting each person's different attitude toward the same situation.

title: The Cleaning Lady's Radio
text_zh: 我们写字楼的保洁阿姨每天早上五点半就开始工作。她随身带一个小收音机，调到一个播老歌的频道。声音开得很小，只有经过她身边的时候才听得到。有一次我加班到很晚，凌晨在走廊碰到她，收音机里正在放一首八十年代的歌。整层楼空荡荡的，只有那首歌和拖把碰地板的声音。我站了一会儿听，她注意到了，抬头对我笑了一下，什么也没说。她的世界里，这栋楼是安静的、属于她的。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiězìlóu de bǎojié āyí měi tiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn bàn jiù kāishǐ gōngzuò. Tā suíshēn dài yí gè xiǎo shōuyīnjī, tiáo dào yí gè bō lǎo gē de píndào. Shēngyīn kāi de hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu jīngguò tā shēnbiān de shíhou cái tīng de dào. Yǒu yí cì wǒ jiābān dào hěn wǎn, língchén zài zǒuláng pèng dào tā, shōuyīnjī lǐ zhèngzài fàng yì shǒu bāshí niándài de gē. Zhěng céng lóu kōngdàngdàng de, zhǐ yǒu nà shǒu gē hé tuōbǎ pèng dìbǎn de shēngyīn. Wǒ zhàn le yíhuìr tīng, tā zhùyì dào le, táitóu duì wǒ xiào le yíxià, shénme yě méi shuō. Nà yí kè wǒ tūrán juéde, tā hé wǒ suīrán zài tóng yí dòng lóu gōngzuò, dàn wǒmen jīnglì de wánquán shì liǎng gè bùtóng de shìjiè. Tā de shìjiè lǐ, zhè dòng lóu shì ānjìng de, shǔyú tā de.
text_en: The cleaning lady in our office building starts work at 5:30 every morning. She carries a small radio tuned to an oldies station. The volume is kept low — you can only hear it when you pass close by. Once I worked overtime very late and ran into her in the hallway at dawn. The radio was playing an 80s song. The entire floor was empty, nothing but that song and the sound of a mop on the floor. I stood and listened for a moment. She noticed, looked up and smiled at me, said nothing.

title: The Overnight Parking Lot
text_zh: 我家附近有一个露天停车场，白天停满了车，看起来跟任何停车场都一样。但凌晨两三点的时候走过那里，你会发现停车场有一种奇怪的美感。路灯把每辆车的影子拉得很长，金属车身上反射着橘黄色的光。偶尔有一辆车的防盗警报被风触发，响几声就停了，像是说了一句梦话。有些车的前挡风玻璃上夹着罚单，有些车的后座堆满了东西——搬家还没搬完，或者是住在车里的人。每辆车都是一个暂停的故事。白天它们只是交通工具，但在深夜，它们变成了各自主人生活状态的缩影。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yí gè lùtiān tíngchēchǎng, báitiān tíng mǎn le chē, kàn qǐlái gēn rènhé tíngchēchǎng dōu yíyàng. Dàn língchén liǎng sān diǎn de shíhou zǒu guò nàlǐ, nǐ huì fāxiàn tíngchēchǎng yǒu yì zhǒng qíguài de měigǎn. Lùdēng bǎ měi liàng chē de yǐngzi lā de hěn cháng, jīnshǔ chēshēn shàng fǎnshè zhe júhuáng sè de guāng. Ǒu'ěr yǒu yí liàng chē de fángdào jǐngbào bèi fēng chùfā, xiǎng jǐ shēng jiù tíng le, xiàng shì shuō le yí jù mènghuà. Yǒuxiē chē de qián dǎngfēng bōli shàng jiā zhe fádān, yǒuxiē chē de hòuzuò duī mǎn le dōngxi — bānjiā hái méi bān wán, huòzhě shì zhù zài chē lǐ de rén. Měi liàng chē dōu shì yí gè zàntíng de gùshi. Báitiān tāmen zhǐ shì jiāotōng gōngjù, dàn zài shēnyè, tāmen biàn chéng le gèzì zhǔrén shēnghuó zhuàngtài de suōyǐng.
text_en: Near my home there's an open-air parking lot, packed with cars during the day, looking like any parking lot. But walk past at two or three in the morning and you'll find the lot has a strange beauty. Streetlights stretch each car's shadow long, orange light reflecting off metal bodies. Occasionally a car alarm gets triggered by wind, beeps a few times and stops — like talking in its sleep. Some cars have parking tickets wedged under their windshield wipers, some have back seats piled with belongings — a move not yet finished, or someone living in their car. Every car is a paused story. By day they're just vehicles, but at night they become miniature portraits of their owners' lives.

title: The Corner Store That Sells Everything
text_zh: 我家小区门口有一个小卖部，面积大概只有十平方米，但里面什么都有。矿泉水、香烟、创可贴、充电线、老花镜、指甲刀、信封、电池——你想不到的东西它都有。老板是一个五十多岁的女人，永远坐在收银台后面看手机。你问她有没有某个东西，她头都不抬就说「左边第三排」或者「最下面那层」，从来没有错过。有一次我问她为什么能记住所有商品的位置，她说：「这些东西跟我待的时间比跟我老公待的时间都长。」这家小卖部存在的意义不仅仅是卖东西——它是一个社区的急救箱。你需要什么，它都有，而且永远开着。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā xiǎoqū ménkǒu yǒu yí gè xiǎomàibù, miànjī dàgài zhǐ yǒu shí píngfāng mǐ, dàn lǐmiàn shénme dōu yǒu. Kuàngquán shuǐ, xiāngyān, chuàngkětiē, chōngdiàn xiàn, lǎohuā jìng, zhǐjia dāo, xìnfēng, diànchí — nǐ xiǎng bú dào de dōngxi tā dōu yǒu. Lǎobǎn shì yí gè wǔshí duō suì de nǚrén, yǒngyuǎn zuò zài shōuyín tái hòumiàn kàn shǒujī. Nǐ wèn tā yǒu méiyǒu mǒu gè dōngxi, tā tóu dōu bù tái jiù shuō「zuǒbiān dì sān pái」huòzhě「zuì xiàmiàn nà céng」, cónglái méiyǒu cuòguò. Yǒu yí cì wǒ wèn tā wèishénme néng jì zhù suǒyǒu shāngpǐn de wèizhì, tā shuō:「Zhèxiē dōngxi gēn wǒ dāi de shíjiān bǐ gēn wǒ lǎogōng dāi de shíjiān dōu cháng.」Zhè jiā xiǎomàibù cúnzài de yìyì bù jǐnjǐn shì mài dōngxi — tā shì yí gè shèqū de jíjiù xiāng. Nǐ xūyào shénme, tā dōu yǒu, érqiě yǒngyuǎn kāi zhe.
text_en: Outside my residential compound there's a corner store, maybe ten square meters, but it has everything. Bottled water, cigarettes, band-aids, charging cables, reading glasses, nail clippers, envelopes, batteries — things you'd never think of, it has them all. The owner is a woman in her fifties who perpetually sits behind the register looking at her phone. Ask her if she carries something and without looking up she'll say 'third row on the left' or 'bottom shelf' — never wrong. Once I asked her how she remembers where everything is. She said: 'These things have spent more time with me than my husband has.' This store's purpose isn't just selling things — it's the community's first-aid kit. Whatever you need, it has, and it's always open.

title: The Post Office Queue
text_zh: 邮局大概是观察一个城市最好的地方之一。我去寄一个包裹，排在我前面的有七八个人。第一个是一位老太太，她要寄一箱自己腌的咸菜给在外地工作的儿子，每一步手续都要问三遍。窗口的工作人员很有耐心，但后面的队伍越来越长。第三个人是一个快递员，手里拿着一叠单子，动作极快，两分钟就办完了。排在我前面的是一个年轻妈妈，一手抱着孩子一手填单子，孩子一直试图抓她的笔。等轮到我的时候，我已经在邮局待了四十分钟。这四十分钟里我什么效率也没有，但我看到了至少五种完全不同的生活。邮局的队伍就是一个横截面——每个人带着自己的生活走进来，办完事又带着走出去。
text_pinyin: Yóujú dàgài shì guānchá yí gè chéngshì zuì hǎo de dìfāng zhī yī. Wǒ qù jì yí gè bāoguǒ, pái zài wǒ qiánmiàn de yǒu qī bā gè rén. Dì yī gè shì yí wèi lǎotàitai, tā yào jì yì xiāng zìjǐ yān de xiáncài gěi zài wàidì gōngzuò de érzi, měi yí bù shǒuxù dōu yào wèn sān biàn. Chuāngkǒu de gōngzuò rényuán hěn yǒu nàixīn, dàn hòumiàn de duìwu yuè lái yuè cháng. Dì sān gè rén shì yí gè kuàidì yuán, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí dié dānzi, dòngzuò jí kuài, liǎng fēnzhōng jiù bàn wán le. Pái zài wǒ qiánmiàn de shì yí gè niánqīng māma, yì shǒu bào zhe háizi yì shǒu tián dānzi, háizi yìzhí shìtú zhuā tā de bǐ. Děng lún dào wǒ de shíhou, wǒ yǐjīng zài yóujú dāi le sìshí fēnzhōng. Zhè sìshí fēnzhōng lǐ wǒ shénme xiàolǜ yě méiyǒu, dàn wǒ kàn dào le zhìshǎo wǔ zhǒng wánquán bùtóng de shēnghuó. Yóujú de duìwu jiù shì yí gè héngjiémiàn — měi gè rén dài zhe zìjǐ de shēnghuó zǒu jìnlái, bàn wán shì yòu dài zhe zǒu chūqù.
text_en: The post office might be one of the best places to observe a city. I went to mail a package; seven or eight people were ahead of me. First was an elderly woman mailing a box of homemade pickled vegetables to her son working in another city, asking about each step of the process three times. The clerk was patient, but the line kept growing. Third was a courier with a stack of forms, blazingly fast, done in two minutes. Ahead of me was a young mother, holding a child with one arm and filling out a form with the other, the child constantly trying to grab her pen. By the time it was my turn, I'd been at the post office forty minutes. In those forty minutes I accomplished nothing productive, but I saw at least five completely different lives. A post office queue is a cross-section — each person walks in carrying their life, and walks back out carrying it when they're done.

questions.q_zh: 老太太要寄什么？
questions.q_en: What was the elderly woman mailing?
questions.explanation: She was mailing homemade pickled vegetables to her child, carefully repeating each postal instruction three times to make sure nothing would go wrong.

questions.q_zh: 快递员用了多长时间办完？
questions.q_en: How long did the courier take?
questions.explanation: The courier finished everything in two minutes, contrasting sharply with the old woman's slow, careful process.

questions.q_zh: 这些人之间最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What matters most between these people?
questions.explanation: The unspoken attentiveness between them signals deep mutual understanding.

title: The Missing Step
text_zh: 我在一栋旧楼里工作了五年。每天上下楼梯，从一楼到四楼，走了几千次。有一天我突然注意到：从二楼到三楼有十七级台阶，但从三楼到四楼只有十六级。我数了三遍确认没有数错。这意味着什么？要么四楼的层高比三楼低，要么台阶的高度不同。我开始注意别的楼梯，发现很多旧楼都有类似的不规则。建筑师朋友告诉我，老建筑的楼梯经常是工人现场浇筑的，误差是正常的。但这个解释并没有完全消除我的好奇。我想知道的是：在我之前的五年里，有没有别人注意到这件事？还是只有无聊到数台阶的人才会发现？
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài yí dòng jiù lóu lǐ gōngzuò le wǔ nián. Měi tiān shàng xià lóutī, cóng yì lóu dào sì lóu, zǒu le jǐ qiān cì. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ tūrán zhùyì dào: cóng èr lóu dào sān lóu yǒu shíqī jí táijiē, dàn cóng sān lóu dào sì lóu zhǐ yǒu shíliù jí. Wǒ shǔ le sān biàn quèrèn méiyǒu shǔ cuò. Zhè yìwèi zhe shénme? Yàome sì lóu de cénggāo bǐ sān lóu dī, yàome táijiē de gāodù bùtóng. Wǒ kāishǐ zhùyì bié de lóutī, fāxiàn hěn duō jiù lóu dōu yǒu lèisì de bù guīzé. Jiànzhùshī péngyou gàosu wǒ, lǎo jiànzhù de lóutī jīngcháng shì gōngrén xiànchǎng jiāozhù de, wùchā shì zhèngcháng de. Dàn zhège jiěshì bìng méiyǒu wánquán xiāochú wǒ de hàoqí. Wǒ xiǎng zhīdào de shì: zài wǒ zhīqián de wǔ nián lǐ, yǒu méiyǒu biérén zhùyì dào zhè jiàn shì? Háishì zhǐ yǒu wúliáo dào shǔ táijiē de rén cái huì fāxiàn?
text_en: I've worked in an old building for five years. Taking the stairs every day from the first to the fourth floor, thousands of times. One day I suddenly noticed: there are seventeen steps from the second to the third floor, but only sixteen from the third to the fourth. I counted three times to make sure. What does this mean? Either the fourth floor has a lower ceiling height, or the steps are different heights. I started paying attention to other staircases and found many old buildings have similar irregularities. An architect friend told me that in old buildings, stairs were often poured on-site by workers, and variation is normal. But this explanation didn't fully satisfy my curiosity. What I want to know is: in the five years before me, did anyone else notice this? Or is it something only someone bored enough to count stairs would discover?

questions.q_zh: 作者发现了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author discover?

options.text: 不同楼层之间的台阶数不同
options.pinyin: bùtóng lóucéng zhī jiān de táijiē shù bùtóng
options.text_en: Different floors have different numbers of steps
The elevator is faster than stairs

questions.explanation: Counting stairs out of boredom, the author noticed eighteen steps between most floors but only sixteen between the third and fourth — the step counts vary by floor.

questions.q_zh: 建筑师朋友怎么解释这个现象？
questions.q_en: How did the architect friend explain this?

options.text: 老建筑的楼梯是现场浇筑的，有误差
options.pinyin: lǎo jiànzhù de lóutī shì xiànchǎng jiāozhù de, yǒu wùchā
options.text_en: Old building stairs were poured on-site with variations

questions.explanation: The architect explained that old building stairs were poured by hand on-site, making slight variations normal.

questions.q_zh: 从这里可以看出他/她什么样的性格？
questions.q_en: What does this reveal about their personality?
questions.explanation: Counting steps floor by floor to find a discrepancy reveals someone focused on precision and goals.

title: The Train Station Bookshop
text_zh: 大多数火车站书店卖的都是同样的东西：成功学、网络小说、旅游指南和几本过期的杂志。但我在一个小城市的火车站发现了一家不一样的书店。面积很小，选书却很用心——有诗集、有本地作家的散文、有几本设计很漂亮的独立出版物。老板是一个戴眼镜的年轻人，我问他为什么在火车站开这种书店，他说：「等火车的人有一种特殊的心理状态——他们正处于两个地方之间，思维是打开的。这个时候给他们一本好书，比在任何地方都有效。」我买了一本诗集，在火车上读完了。他说得对。那种悬浮在两地之间的状态，确实让文字变得更容易进入内心。
text_pinyin: Dàduōshù huǒchē zhàn shūdiàn mài de dōu shì tóngyàng de dōngxi: chénggōng xué, wǎngluò xiǎoshuō, lǚyóu zhǐnán hé jǐ běn guòqī de zázhì. Dàn wǒ zài yí gè xiǎo chéngshì de huǒchē zhàn fāxiàn le yì jiā bù yíyàng de shūdiàn. Miànjī hěn xiǎo, xuǎn shū què hěn yòngxīn — yǒu shījí, yǒu běndì zuòjiā de sǎnwén, yǒu jǐ běn shèjì hěn piàoliang de dúlì chūbǎnwù. Lǎobǎn shì yí gè dài yǎnjìng de niánqīng rén, wǒ wèn tā wèishénme zài huǒchē zhàn kāi zhè zhǒng shūdiàn, tā shuō:「Děng huǒchē de rén yǒu yì zhǒng tèshū de xīnlǐ zhuàngtài — tāmen zhèng chǔyú liǎng gè dìfāng zhī jiān, sīwéi shì dǎkāi de. Zhège shíhou gěi tāmen yì běn hǎo shū, bǐ zài rènhé dìfāng dōu yǒuxiào.」Wǒ mǎi le yì běn shījí, zài huǒchē shàng dú wán le. Tā shuō de duì. Nà zhǒng xuánfú zài liǎng dì zhī jiān de zhuàngtài, quèshí ràng wénzì biàn de gèng róngyì jìnrù nèixīn.
text_en: Most train station bookshops sell the same things: self-help books, web novels, travel guides, and a few expired magazines. But at a small city's train station I found a different kind of bookshop. Small in size but thoughtfully curated — poetry collections, essays by local writers, a few beautifully designed independent publications. The owner was a young man in glasses. I asked him why he'd open this kind of bookshop in a train station. He said: 'People waiting for trains are in a special psychological state — they're between two places, their minds are open. Give them a good book at this moment, and it's more effective than anywhere else.' I bought a poetry collection and finished it on the train. He was right. That state of suspension between two places really does make words enter the heart more easily.

questions.q_zh: 这家书店跟普通火车站书店有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is this bookshop different from typical train station bookshops?

options.text: 选书用心，有诗集和独立出版物
options.pinyin: xuǎn shū yòngxīn, yǒu shījí hé dúlì chūbǎnwù
options.text_en: Thoughtfully curated with poetry and independent publications

questions.explanation: Unlike typical station shops selling bestsellers, this one carries poetry and independent publications -- a curated selection.

questions.q_zh: 老板为什么选择在火车站开书店？
questions.q_en: Why did the owner choose to open at a train station?

options.text: 等车的人思维是打开的，更容易接受好书
options.pinyin: děng chē de rén sīwéi shì dǎkāi de, gèng róngyì jiēshòu hǎo shū
options.text_en: Train-waiters have open minds and are more receptive to good books
No other bookshops at the station

questions.explanation: The passage explains: asked him why he'd open this kind of bookshop.

questions.q_zh: 作者买了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author buy?
questions.explanation: Among the curated selection, the author chose a poetry collection -- drawn to something off the beaten path.

title: The Wrong Group Chat
text_zh: 今天同事老张把一条语音消息发错了群。他本来要发到家庭群，结果发到了部门工作群。内容是他用极其温柔的声音对他三岁的女儿说：「宝贝乖乖，爸爸今天会早点回来，给你买那个粉色的小兔子，好不好呀？」整个办公室安静了大约三秒钟，然后爆发出笑声。老张的脸瞬间变成了番茄色。他平时在办公室是那种说话声音最大、表情最严肃的人。大家笑了一会儿以后，有人在群里回复：「老张，我也想要粉色的小兔子。」又有人说：「可以早点回来吗？」老张没有撤回消息，而是回了一句：「你们都太老了，不配。」从那天起，「粉色的小兔子」成了我们部门的内部暗号，意思是「今天想早点下班」。
text_pinyin: Jīntiān tóngshì Lǎo Zhāng bǎ yì tiáo yǔyīn xiāoxi fā cuò le qún. Tā běnlái yào fā dào jiātíng qún, jiéguǒ fā dào le bùmén gōngzuò qún. Nèiróng shì tā yòng jíqí wēnróu de shēngyīn duì tā sān suì de nǚ'ér shuō:「Bǎobèi guāiguāi, bàba jīntiān huì zǎodiǎn huílái, gěi nǐ mǎi nàge fěnsè de xiǎo tùzi, hǎo bù hǎo ya?」Zhěng gè bàngōngshì ānjìng le dàyuē sān miǎozhōng, ránhòu bàofā chū xiàoshēng. Lǎo Zhāng de liǎn shùnjiān biàn chéng le fānqié sè. Tā píngshí zài bàngōngshì shì nà zhǒng shuōhuà shēngyīn zuì dà, biǎoqíng zuì yánsù de rén. Dàjiā xiào le yíhuìr yǐhòu, yǒu rén zài qún lǐ huífù:「Lǎo Zhāng, wǒ yě xiǎng yào fěnsè de xiǎo tùzi.」Yòu yǒu rén shuō:「Kěyǐ zǎodiǎn huílái ma?」Lǎo Zhāng méiyǒu chèhuí xiāoxi, ér shì huí le yí jù:「Nǐmen dōu tài lǎo le, bú pèi.」Cóng nà tiān qǐ,「fěnsè de xiǎo tùzi」chéng le wǒmen bùmén de nèibù ànhào, yìsi shì「jīntiān xiǎng zǎodiǎn xiàbān」.
text_en: Today colleague Lao Zhang sent a voice message to the wrong group chat. He meant to send it to his family chat but sent it to the department work group instead. The content was him saying in an extremely gentle voice to his three-year-old daughter: 'Be good baby, daddy will come home early today, I'll buy you that pink bunny, okay?' The entire office went silent for about three seconds, then erupted in laughter. Lao Zhang's face instantly turned tomato-red. He's normally the loudest, most serious-looking person in the office. After for a while, someone replied in the group: 'Lao Zhang, I want a pink bunny too.' Someone else said: 'Can you come home early?' Lao Zhang didn't recall the message, but replied: 'You're all too old, you don't deserve one.' From that day on, 'pink bunny' became our department's code phrase meaning 'I want to leave work early today.'

questions.q_zh: 作者选择这个视角有什么效果？
questions.q_en: What effect does the author's choice of perspective have?
questions.explanation: The first-person, ground-level viewpoint draws the reader into the narrator's experience.

questions.q_zh: 这件事为什么特别好笑？
questions.q_en: Why was this particularly funny?

options.text: 老张平时是最严肃的人
options.pinyin: Lǎo Zhāng píngshí shì zuì yánsù de rén
options.text_en: Lao Zhang is normally the most serious person
The pink bunny was too expensive

questions.explanation: The humor comes from contrast: Lao Zhang is normally the most serious person in the office.

questions.q_zh: 「粉色的小兔子」后来变成了什么意思？
questions.q_en: What did 'pink bunny' come to mean?

options.text_en: Wanting to leave work early today

questions.explanation: After the mishap, 'pink bunny' became office code for wanting to leave early -- shared humor turned inside joke.

title: The Museum Guard
text_zh: 我注意到博物馆的保安有一种特殊的表情。不是无聊，不是警惕，而是一种介于两者之间的东西——一种长期处于等待状态的平静。他们每天站在同样的位置，面对着同样的画或者雕塑，一站就是几个小时。我很好奇：他们会不会因为看了太多遍而真正理解了那些艺术品？有一次我跟一个保安聊天，问他最喜欢哪幅画。他指了指角落里一幅不太起眼的风景画，说：「这幅。因为下午两点到三点之间，阳光会从窗户照进来，刚好落在画的右下角。那个时候看它，跟其他时候完全不同。」我走到那幅画前面看了看，确实没什么特别的。但我相信他说的是真的——他拥有一种我永远不会有的观看角度。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhùyì dào bówùguǎn de bǎo'ān yǒu yì zhǒng tèshū de biǎoqíng. Bú shì wúliáo, bú shì jǐngtì, ér shì yì zhǒng jiè yú liǎng zhě zhī jiān de dōngxi — yì zhǒng chángqī chǔyú děngdài zhuàngtài de píngjìng. Tāmen měi tiān zhàn zài tóngyàng de wèizhì, miànduì zhe tóngyàng de huà huòzhě diāosù, yí zhàn jiù shì jǐ gè xiǎoshí. Wǒ hěn hàoqí: tāmen huì bú huì yīnwèi kàn le tài duō biàn ér zhēnzhèng lǐjiě le nàxiē yìshùpǐn? Yǒu yí cì wǒ gēn yí gè bǎo'ān liáotiān, wèn tā zuì xǐhuan nǎ fú huà. Tā zhǐ le zhǐ jiǎoluò lǐ yì fú bú tài qǐyǎn de fēngjǐng huà, shuō:「Zhè fú. Yīnwèi xiàwǔ liǎng diǎn dào sān diǎn zhī jiān, yángguāng huì cóng chuānghù zhào jìnlái, gānghǎo luò zài huà de yòu xià jiǎo. Nàge shíhou kàn tā, gēn qítā shíhou wánquán bùtóng.」Wǒ zǒu dào nà fú huà qiánmiàn kàn le kàn, quèshí méi shénme tèbié de. Dàn wǒ xiāngxìn tā shuō de shì zhēn de — tā yōngyǒu yì zhǒng wǒ yǒngyuǎn bú huì yǒu de guānkàn jiǎodù.
text_en: I've noticed museum guards have a particular expression. Not boredom, not alertness, but something between the two — a calm that comes from long-term waiting. They stand in the same spot every day, facing the same painting or sculpture, for hours at a time. I'm curious: might they, from having looked so many times, genuinely understand the artwork? Once I chatted with a guard and asked which painting was his favorite. He pointed to an unremarkable landscape in the corner and said: 'This one. Because between 2 and 3 PM, sunlight comes through the window and falls right on the lower right corner of the painting. Then, it looks completely different from any other time.' I walked over and looked at the painting — nothing special, really. But I believe what he said was true — he possesses a perspective I'll never have.

questions.q_zh: 作者形容保安的表情是什么样的？
questions.q_en: How does the author describe the guard's expression?
questions.explanation: The guard's expression is described as calm from long-term waiting -- not boredom, but settled patience.

questions.q_zh: 保安为什么喜欢那幅画？
questions.q_en: Why does the guard like that painting?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi huà de nèiróng hěn měi

options.text: 因为下午的阳光让它看起来完全不同
options.pinyin: yīnwèi xiàwǔ de yángguāng ràng tā kàn qǐlái wánquán bùtóng
options.text_en: Because afternoon sunlight makes it look completely different

options.text: 因为没有别人注意到它
options.pinyin: yīnwèi méiyǒu biérén zhùyì dào tā
options.text_en: Because no one else notices it

questions.explanation: The guard explained that afternoon sunlight transforms the painting -- same canvas, completely different appearance.

questions.q_zh: 这段话对'时间'的处理方式说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the way this passage handles 'time' suggest?

options.text: 时间不只是客观的流逝
options.pinyin: shíjiān bù zhǐ shì kèguān de liúshì
options.text_en: time is more than objective passing

options.pinyin: xūyào gèng hǎo de guǎnlǐ shíjiān

questions.explanation: The slow, deliberate pacing treats time as experiential, not just measured.

title: The Old Man Who Feeds Pigeons
text_zh: 广场上有一个喂鸽子的老人。他每天上午十点准时出现，坐在同一条长椅上，从口袋里掏出一个塑料袋，里面装着碎面包。鸽子们好像认识他，他还没坐下就已经聚过来了。他一小把一小把地撒，动作很慢，像是在做一件需要耐心的精细工作。我路过了很多次，从来没见他跟任何人说话。有一次下雨，他撑着伞照样来了，鸽子也照样来了。我突然想到，也许对他来说，这不是在喂鸽子——这是他一天里最重要的约会。他和鸽子之间有一个安静的协议：你来，我就在。这种不需要语言的陪伴，也许是最不容易辜负的一种。
text_pinyin: Guǎngchǎng shàng yǒu yí gè wèi gēzi de lǎorén. Tā měi tiān shàngwǔ shí diǎn zhǔnshí chūxiàn, zuò zài tóng yì tiáo chángyǐ shàng, cóng kǒudài lǐ tāo chū yí gè sùliào dài, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe suì miànbāo. Gēzi men hǎoxiàng rènshi tā, tā hái méi zuò xià jiù yǐjīng jù guòlái le. Tā yì xiǎo bǎ yì xiǎo bǎ de sā, dòngzuò hěn màn, xiàng shì zài zuò yí jiàn xūyào nàixīn de jīngxì gōngzuò. Wǒ lùguò le hěn duō cì, cónglái méi jiàn tā gēn rènhé rén shuōhuà. Yǒu yí cì xià yǔ, tā chēng zhe sǎn zhàoyàng lái le, gēzi yě zhàoyàng lái le. Wǒ tūrán xiǎng dào, yěxǔ duì tā lái shuō, zhè bú shì zài wèi gēzi — zhè shì tā yì tiān lǐ zuì zhòngyào de yuēhuì. Tā hé gēzi zhī jiān yǒu yí gè ānjìng de xiéyì: nǐ lái, wǒ jiù zài. Zhè zhǒng bù xūyào yǔyán de péibàn, yěxǔ shì zuì bù róngyì gūfù de yì zhǒng.
text_en: There's an old man who feeds pigeons in the square. He appears at exactly ten every morning, sits on the same bench, takes a plastic bag from his pocket filled with bread crumbs. The pigeons seem to know him — they gather before he even sits down. He scatters the crumbs in small handfuls, very slowly, as if performing delicate work that requires patience. I've passed by many times and never seen him talk to anyone. Once it rained and he came anyway with an umbrella; the pigeons came anyway too. It struck me that maybe for him, this isn't feeding pigeons — it's the most important appointment of his day. Between him and the pigeons there's a quiet agreement: you come, I'll be here. This kind of companionship that needs no language may be the kind least likely to disappoint.

questions.q_zh: 这篇文章的结构安排说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the structural arrangement of this passage suggest?

options.text: 作者有意引导读者的情感
options.pinyin: zuòzhě yǒuyì yǐndǎo dúzhě de qínggǎn
options.text_en: the author deliberately guides the reader's emotions

questions.explanation: The sequencing builds from observation to reflection, steering the reader's emotional response.

questions.q_zh: 文中的象征意义是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the symbolic meaning in this text?

options.text: 外在事物反映内心世界
options.text_en: external things reflect the inner world

questions.explanation: The physical object becomes a lens for inner experience -- detail mirrors feeling.

title: The Afternoon Nap Factory
text_zh: 中午一点到两点之间，我们整层办公室会变成一个午睡工厂。有人趴在桌上，有人歪在椅子里，有人戴着颈枕靠在墙上。打呼噜的声音此起彼伏，形成一种奇怪的交响乐。最神奇的是，所有人都在一点四十五分左右醒来——不是因为闹钟，而是因为某种集体的生物钟。醒来以后有大约五分钟的过渡期：揉眼睛、喝水、看手机、缓慢地从梦里回到现实。在那五分钟里，每个人的脸上都有一种茫然的柔软，好像壳还没有重新长回来。那是这个办公室一天中最真实的五分钟。
text_pinyin: Zhōngwǔ yì diǎn dào liǎng diǎn zhī jiān, wǒmen zhěng céng bàngōngshì huì biàn chéng yí gè wǔshuì gōngchǎng. Yǒu rén pā zài zhuō shàng, yǒu rén wāi zài yǐzi lǐ, yǒu rén dài zhe jǐng zhěn kào zài qiáng shàng. Dǎ hūlu de shēngyīn cǐqǐbǐfú, xíngchéng yì zhǒng qíguài de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Zuì shénqí de shì, suǒyǒu rén dōu zài yì diǎn sìshíwǔ fēn zuǒyòu xǐng lái — bú shì yīnwèi nàozhōng, ér shì yīnwèi mǒu zhǒng jítǐ de shēngwù zhōng. Xǐng lái yǐhòu yǒu dàyuē wǔ fēnzhōng de guòdù qī: róu yǎnjing, hē shuǐ, kàn shǒujī, huǎnmàn de cóng mèng lǐ huí dào xiànshí. Zài nà wǔ fēnzhōng lǐ, měi gè rén de liǎn shàng dōu yǒu yì zhǒng mángrán de róuruǎn, hǎoxiàng ké hái méiyǒu chóngxīn zhǎng huílái. Nà shì zhège bàngōngshì yì tiān zhōng zuì zhēnshí de wǔ fēnzhōng.
text_en: Between one and two in the afternoon, our entire floor becomes a nap factory. Some people slump over their desks, some lean sideways in their chairs, some wear neck pillows against the wall. Snoring rises and falls in waves, forming a strange symphony. The most magical part is that everyone wakes up around 1:45 — not from alarms, but from some collective biological clock. After waking there's about a five-minute transition: rubbing eyes, drinking water, checking phones, slowly returning from dream to reality. During those five minutes, everyone's face has a dazed softness, as if their shell hasn't grown back yet. Those are the most genuine five minutes of the office day.

title: The Sound of Someone Cooking Downstairs
text_zh: 每天傍晚六点左右，楼下会传来炒菜的声音。先是油热的噼里啪啦声，然后是食材下锅的刺啦声，接着是锅铲翻动的节奏。如果仔细听，还能闻到油烟味从窗户飘上来。我不知道是哪一户在做饭，但我对他们的菜单比对自己邻居的名字更熟悉——从声音可以判断今天是炒菜还是炖汤，是简单的还是复杂的。周末的声音通常更丰富，持续时间更长，好像在做一顿认真的饭。平时则很快，十五分钟解决。有时候下班回来很累，什么都不想做的时候，听到楼下开始炒菜，就觉得这一天还是有着落的。不是我的饭，但那个声音本身就是一种安慰。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn zuǒyòu, lóu xià huì chuán lái chǎo cài de shēngyīn. Xiān shì yóu rè de pīlipālā shēng, ránhòu shì shícái xià guō de cìlā shēng, jiēzhe shì guōchǎn fāndòng de jiézòu. Rúguǒ zǐxì tīng, hái néng wén dào yóuyān wèi cóng chuānghù piāo shànglái. Wǒ bù zhīdào shì nǎ yí hù zài zuòfàn, dàn wǒ duì tāmen de càidān bǐ duì zìjǐ línjū de míngzi gèng shúxī — cóng shēngyīn kěyǐ pànduàn jīntiān shì chǎocài háishi dùn tāng, shì jiǎndān de háishi fùzá de. Zhōumò de shēngyīn tōngcháng gèng fēngfù, chíxù shíjiān gèng cháng, hǎoxiàng zài zuò yí dùn rènzhēn de fàn. Píngshí zé hěn kuài, shíwǔ fēnzhōng jiějué. Yǒu shíhou xiàbān huílái hěn lèi, shénme dōu bù xiǎng zuò de shíhou, tīng dào lóu xià kāishǐ chǎo cài, jiù juéde zhè yì tiān háishì yǒu zhe luò de. Bú shì wǒ de fàn, dàn nàge shēngyīn běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng ānwèi.
text_en: Every evening around six, the sound of stir-frying rises from downstairs. First the crackling of hot oil, then the sizzle of ingredients hitting the wok, then the rhythm of a spatula turning. If you listen carefully, you can even smell the cooking fumes drifting up through the window. I don't know which household is cooking, but I'm more familiar with their menu than with my neighbors' names — from the sounds I can tell whether today is stir-fry or stew, simple or elaborate. Weekend sounds are usually richer, lasting longer, as if someone is making a proper meal. Weekdays are quick, done in fifteen minutes. Sometimes when I come home from work exhausted, wanting to do nothing, hearing the cooking start downstairs makes me feel the day still has somewhere to land. It's not my dinner, but the sound itself is a kind of comfort.

questions.q_en: How does the author tell what's being cooked downstairs?
questions.explanation: The author reads the cooking method from sound alone: sizzle means stir-frying, steady bubbling means stewing.

options.text_en: Comforted, feeling the day still has somewhere to land

questions.explanation: Hearing someone cook below gives comfort -- the day still has a domestic anchor, a place to land.

title: The Convenience Store at 3 AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店是一个没有身份的空间。白天你走进去是顾客、员工、学生、上班族，但凌晨三点，你只是一个需要东西的人。灯光很亮，跟外面的黑暗形成一种奇怪的对比。货架上的商品整整齐齐地排列着，不管外面的世界多混乱，这里永远井然有序。收银台后面的店员通常在看手机或者打瞌睡，你们之间的交流缩减到最少：扫码、付款、一个点头。来便利店的人各有各的原因：失眠的、加班的、刚从酒吧出来的、找不到回家路的。每个人进来的时候带着各自的故事，出去的时候手里多了一瓶水或者一个饭团。便利店不问你为什么来，它只是在那里，亮着灯等你。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn shì yí gè méiyǒu shēnfèn de kōngjiān. Báitiān nǐ zǒu jìnqù shì gùkè, yuángōng, xuéshēng, shàngbānzú, dàn língchén sān diǎn, nǐ zhǐ shì yí gè xūyào dōngxi de rén. Dēngguāng hěn liàng, gēn wàimiàn de hēi'àn xíngchéng yì zhǒng qíguài de duìbǐ. Huòjià shàng de shāngpǐn zhěngzhěngqíqí de páiliè zhe, bùguǎn wàimiàn de shìjiè duō hùnluàn, zhèlǐ yǒngyuǎn jǐngrán yǒuxù. Shōuyín tái hòumiàn de diànyuán tōngcháng zài kàn shǒujī huòzhě dǎ kēshuì, nǐmen zhī jiān de jiāoliú suōjiǎn dào zuì shǎo: sǎo mǎ, fùkuǎn, yí gè diǎntóu. Lái biànlì diàn de rén gè yǒu gè de yuányīn: shīmián de, jiābān de, gāng cóng jiǔbā chūlái de, zhǎo bú dào huíjiā lù de. Měi gè rén jìnlái de shíhou dài zhe gèzì de gùshi, chūqù de shíhou shǒu lǐ duō le yì píng shuǐ huòzhě yí gè fàntuán. Biànlì diàn bú wèn nǐ wèishénme lái, tā zhǐ shì zài nàlǐ, liàng zhe dēng děng nǐ.
text_en: A convenience store at 3 AM is an identity-free space. During the day you walk in as a customer, employee, student, office worker — but at 3 AM, you're just a person who needs something. The lights are bright, forming a strange contrast with the darkness outside. Products line the shelves in perfect order; no matter how chaotic the world outside, this place is always orderly. The cashier behind the counter is usually on their phone or dozing; the exchange between you is reduced to a minimum: scan, pay, a nod. People come to the convenience store for different reasons: insomnia, overtime, just left a bar, can't find the way home. Each person enters carrying their own story and leaves with a bottle of water or a rice ball in hand. The convenience store doesn't ask why you're here. It just sits there, lights on, waiting for you.

title: The Person I Am on Vacation
text_zh: 每次出去旅行，大概到第三天，我会变成一个完全不同的人。头两天还带着工作的惯性——走路快、吃饭快、时不时看手机。但到了第三天，某个时刻，身体里好像有一个开关被关掉了。我开始走得很慢，开始注意路边的花，开始在咖啡馆坐很久而不觉得是浪费时间。那个时候我会想：这才是真的我，还是这只是一个暂时的版本？每次旅行结束回到日常生活，这个「度假版的我」会在大约一天半之内消失。邮件、会议、截止日期像水一样漫上来，把那个缓慢的人淹没。也许问题不是「哪个才是真的我」，而是「为什么我的日常生活不允许那个人存在」。
text_pinyin: Měi cì chūqù lǚxíng, dàgài dào dì sān tiān, wǒ huì biàn chéng yí gè wánquán bùtóng de rén. Tóu liǎng tiān hái dài zhe gōngzuò de guànxìng — zǒulù kuài, chīfàn kuài, shí bù shí kàn shǒujī. Dàn dào le dì sān tiān, mǒu gè shíkè, shēntǐ lǐ hǎoxiàng yǒu yí gè kāiguān bèi guāndiào le. Wǒ kāishǐ zǒu de hěn màn, kāishǐ zhùyì lù biān de huā, kāishǐ zài kāfēiguǎn zuò hěn jiǔ ér bù juéde shì làngfèi shíjiān. Nàge shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng: zhè cái shì zhēn de wǒ, háishì zhè zhǐ shì yí gè zànshí de bǎnběn? Měi cì lǚxíng jiéshù huí dào rìcháng shēnghuó, zhège「dùjià bǎn de wǒ」huì zài dàyuē yì tiān bàn zhī nèi xiāoshī. Yóujiàn, huìyì, jiézhǐ rìqī xiàng shuǐ yíyàng màn shànglái, bǎ nàge huǎnmàn de rén yānmò. Yěxǔ wèntí bú shì「nǎge cái shì zhēn de wǒ」, ér shì「wèishénme wǒ de rìcháng shēnghuó bù yǔnxǔ nàge rén cúnzài」.
text_en: Every time I travel, around the third day, I become a completely different person. The first two days I still carry work's momentum — walking fast, eating fast, checking my phone. But on the third day, at some moment, it's as if a switch inside me gets flipped off. I start walking slowly, noticing flowers by the road, sitting in cafés for a long time without feeling it's a waste. In those moments I wonder: is this the real me, or just a temporary version? Every time I return from a trip to daily life, this 'vacation me' disappears within about a day and a half. Emails, meetings, deadlines rise like water, drowning the slow person. Perhaps the question isn't 'which is the real me,' but 'why doesn't my daily life allow that person to exist.'

title: The Library's Quietest Floor
text_zh: 大学图书馆的每一层都有不同的声音水平。一楼最吵，因为有咖啡区和小组讨论室。二楼稍微安静一些，大部分人在看书。三楼基本不允许说话，只有翻页和打字的声音。但四楼是真正的安静层——连打字声都被认为太吵了。四楼的人都有一种默契：你可以翻书，但要轻；你可以喝水，但瓶子不能有声音；你的手机必须静音，如果震动被听到，你会收到来自四面八方的目光。我在四楼学习了整整一个学期，离开以后发现自己养成了一个习惯：在任何安静的空间里，我会自动把所有动作的音量降到最低。四楼教会了我一种对安静的尊重。
text_pinyin: Dàxué túshūguǎn de měi yí céng dōu yǒu bùtóng de shēngyīn shuǐpíng. Yī lóu zuì chǎo, yīnwèi yǒu kāfēi qū hé xiǎozǔ tǎolùn shì. Èr lóu shāowēi ānjìng yìxiē, dà bùfèn rén zài kàn shū. Sān lóu jīběn bù yǔnxǔ shuōhuà, zhǐ yǒu fānyè hé dǎzì de shēngyīn. Dàn sì lóu shì zhēnzhèng de ānjìng céng — lián dǎzì shēng dōu bèi rènwéi tài chǎo le. Sì lóu de rén dōu yǒu yì zhǒng mòqì: nǐ kěyǐ fān shū, dàn yào qīng; nǐ kěyǐ hē shuǐ, dàn píngzi bù néng yǒu shēngyīn; nǐ de shǒujī bìxū jìngyīn, rúguǒ zhèndòng bèi tīng dào, nǐ huì shōudào lái zì sì miàn bā fāng de mùguāng. Wǒ zài sì lóu xuéxí le zhěngzhěng yí gè xuéqī, líkāi yǐhòu fāxiàn zìjǐ yǎngchéng le yí gè xíguàn: zài rènhé ānjìng de kōngjiān lǐ, wǒ huì zìdòng bǎ suǒyǒu dòngzuò de yīnliàng jiàng dào zuì dī. Sì lóu jiāo huì le wǒ yì zhǒng duì ānjìng de zūnzhòng.
text_en: Each floor of the university library has a different sound level. The first floor is loudest, with a coffee area and group discussion rooms. The second floor is slightly quieter, most people reading. The third floor doesn't allow talking — just the sound of pages turning and typing. But the fourth floor is the true quiet floor — even typing is considered too loud. Fourth-floor people share an understanding: you can turn pages, but gently; you can drink water, but the bottle can't make noise; your phone must be silent, and if vibration is heard, you'll receive looks from all directions. I studied on the fourth floor for an entire semester. After leaving, I discovered I'd developed a habit: in any quiet space, I automatically lower the volume of every action to minimum. The fourth floor taught me a kind of respect for silence.

questions.q_en: How does the fourth floor differ from the third?

title: The Note Under the Windshield
text_zh: 今天早上发现车的挡风玻璃下面夹着一张纸条，上面用圆珠笔写着：「你的车灯昨晚亮了一整夜。我帮你关了。——三楼的邻居。」我完全不知道是谁。这栋楼有八十多户，我只认识两三家。但某个我不认识的人，在昨天深夜注意到我的车灯没关，然后下楼帮我关掉了——虽然帮别人关车灯需要知道怎么开车门，这意味着他先试了一下，发现门没锁。这就引出了第二个问题：我昨晚忘了锁车门。一个善意的行为暴露了另一个疏忽。我把纸条夹在了后视镜上，决定今天烤一盘饼干放在三楼的走廊上，附一张纸条：「谢谢。——五楼忘锁车门的人。」
text_pinyin: Jīntiān zǎoshang fāxiàn chē de dǎngfēng bōli xiàmiàn jiā zhe yì zhāng zhǐtiáo, shàngmiàn yòng yuánzhūbǐ xiě zhe:「Nǐ de chēdēng zuówǎn liàng le yì zhěng yè. Wǒ bāng nǐ guān le. — sān lóu de línjū.」Wǒ wánquán bù zhīdào shì shéi. Zhè dòng lóu yǒu bāshí duō hù, wǒ zhǐ rènshi liǎng sān jiā. Dàn mǒu gè wǒ bú rènshi de rén, zài zuótiān shēnyè zhùyì dào wǒ de chēdēng méi guān, ránhòu xià lóu bāng wǒ guān diào le — suīrán bāng biérén guān chēdēng xūyào zhīdào zěnme kāi chēmén, zhè yìwèi zhe tā xiān shì le yíxià, fāxiàn mén méi suǒ. Zhè jiù yǐn chū le dì èr gè wèntí: wǒ zuówǎn wàng le suǒ chēmén. Yí gè shànyì de xíngwéi bàolù le lìng yí gè shūhu. Wǒ bǎ zhǐtiáo jiā zài le hòushì jìng shàng, juédìng jīntiān kǎo yì pán bǐnggān fàng zài sān lóu de zǒuláng shàng, fù yì zhāng zhǐtiáo:「Xièxie. — wǔ lóu wàng suǒ chēmén de rén.」
text_en: This morning I found a note tucked under my car's windshield, written in ballpoint pen: 'Your headlights were on all night. I turned them off for you. — Your third-floor neighbor.' I have no idea who this is. This building has over eighty units; I only know two or three. But someone I don't know noticed my headlights were on late last night, then went downstairs and turned them off — though turning off someone's headlights requires opening the car door, which means they tried first and found it unlocked. This raises a second issue: I forgot to lock my car last night. One act of kindness exposed another negligence. I clipped the note to my rearview mirror and decided to bake a tray of cookies today, leave them in the third-floor hallway with a note: 'Thank you. — The person on the fifth floor who forgot to lock their car.'

title: The Locksmith's Corner
text_zh: 小区门口有一个配钥匙的摊位。摊主是一个五十多岁的男人，头发花白，戴着一副老式眼镜。他的摊位很小，只有一张折叠桌和一台磨钥匙的机器。周围挂满了各种各样的空白钥匙，像一串串小小的风铃。大多数时候他都没有客人，就坐在那里看报纸或者听收音机。偶尔有人来配钥匙，他接过旧钥匙仔细看一眼，不说话，选一把空白的，放进机器里。几分钟以后一把新钥匙就好了。他把两把钥匙一起交给客人，说「试一下」。我搬来这个小区三年了，他一直在那个角落。不管春夏秋冬，不管有没有人来，他就在那里。我有时候想，他守的不是一个摊位，而是一种正在消失的生活节奏——不着急，不竞争，只要有人需要就够了。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū ménkǒu yǒu yí gè pèi yàoshi de tānwèi. Tānzhǔ shì yí gè wǔshí duō suì de nánrén, tóufa huābái, dài zhe yí fù lǎoshì yǎnjìng. Tā de tānwèi hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu yì zhāng zhédié zhuō hé yì tái mó yàoshi de jīqì. Zhōuwéi guà mǎn le gè zhǒng gè yàng de kòngbái yàoshi, xiàng yí chuàn chuàn xiǎoxiǎo de fēnglíng. Dà duōshù shíhou tā dōu méiyǒu kèrén, jiù zuò zài nàlǐ kàn bàozhǐ huòzhě tīng shōuyīnjī. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén lái pèi yàoshi, tā jiē guò jiù yàoshi zǐxì kàn yì yǎn, bù shuōhuà, xuǎn yì bǎ kòngbái de, fàng jìn jīqì lǐ. Jǐ fēnzhōng yǐhòu yì bǎ xīn yàoshi jiù hǎo le. Tā bǎ liǎng bǎ yàoshi yìqǐ jiāo gěi kèrén, shuō「shì yíxià」. Wǒ bān lái zhège xiǎoqū sān nián le, tā yìzhí zài nàge jiǎoluò. Bùguǎn chūn xià qiū dōng, bùguǎn yǒu méiyǒu rén lái, tā jiù zài nàlǐ. Wǒ yǒu shíhou xiǎng, tā shǒu de bú shì yí gè tānwèi, ér shì yì zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshī de shēnghuó jiézòu — bù zháojí, bù jìngzhēng, zhǐyào yǒu rén xūyào jiù gòu le.
text_en: At the entrance of the residential compound there's a key-cutting stall. The owner is a man in his fifties with graying hair and old-fashioned glasses. His stall is small — just a folding table and a key-grinding machine. Blank keys of all kinds hang around it like strings of tiny wind chimes. Most of the time he has no customers and just sits reading the newspaper or listening to the radio. Occasionally someone comes to get a key copied. He takes the old key, examines it carefully without speaking, selects a blank one, and puts it in the machine. A few minutes later the new key is ready. He hands both keys to the customer and says 'try it.' I've lived in this compound for three years and he's always been in that corner. Rain or shine, customers or not, he's just there. Sometimes I think what he's guarding isn't a stall but a disappearing rhythm of life — unhurried, uncompetitive, enough as long as someone needs him.

title: Morning Tai Chi in the Park
text_zh: 每天早上六点，公园里就开始热闹了，但是一种很安静的热闹。最东边的角落属于太极拳的人。他们大多是退休的老人，穿着宽松的衣服，跟着一个白头发的师傅慢慢地移动。从远处看，他们像是在水底下行走——每一个动作都有阻力，但看起来毫不费力。有一个阿姨总是站在最后面，动作比别人慢半拍，但她脸上的表情最专注。我问过师傅为什么太极拳要那么慢。他说「慢才能感觉到自己的身体，快的话你只是在做动作，慢的话你才是在练功夫」。旁边广场舞的音乐声很大，但太极的角落好像有一个隐形的墙，把噪音挡在外面。那种安静不是没有声音，而是内心的安静。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn, gōngyuán lǐ jiù kāishǐ rènao le, dànshì yì zhǒng hěn ānjìng de rènao. Zuì dōngbiān de jiǎoluò shǔyú tàijíquán de rén. Tāmen dà duō shì tuìxiū de lǎorén, chuān zhe kuānsōng de yīfu, gēn zhe yí gè bái tóufa de shīfu mànmàn de yídòng. Cóng yuǎnchù kàn, tāmen xiàng shì zài shuǐ dǐxia xíngzǒu — měi yí gè dòngzuò dōu yǒu zǔlì, dàn kàn qǐlái háo bú fèilì. Yǒu yí gè āyí zǒng shì zhàn zài zuì hòumiàn, dòngzuò bǐ biérén màn bàn pāi, dàn tā liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng zuì zhuānzhù. Wǒ wèn guò shīfu wèi shénme tàijíquán yào nàme màn. Tā shuō「màn cái néng gǎnjué dào zìjǐ de shēntǐ, kuài de huà nǐ zhǐ shì zài zuò dòngzuò, màn de huà nǐ cái shì zài liàn gōngfu」. Pángbiān guǎngchǎng wǔ de yīnyuè shēng hěn dà, dàn tàijí de jiǎoluò hǎoxiàng yǒu yí gè yǐnxíng de qiáng, bǎ zàoyīn dǎng zài wàimiàn. Nà zhǒng ānjìng bú shì méiyǒu shēngyīn, ér shì nèixīn de ānjìng.
text_en: Every morning at six the park comes alive, but with a quiet kind of liveliness. The eastern corner belongs to the tai chi people. They're mostly retired elderly, wearing loose clothing, following a white-haired master in slow movements. From a distance they look like they're walking underwater — every movement meets resistance yet looks effortless. One auntie always stands at the very back, half a beat behind everyone else, but with the most focused expression on her face. I once asked the master why tai chi has to be so slow. He said 'only when you're slow can you feel your own body; when you're fast you're just going through motions, when you're slow you're actually practicing kung fu.' The square dance music blares nearby, but the tai chi corner seems to have an invisible wall keeping the noise out. That kind of quiet isn't the absence of sound — it's inner stillness.

title: The Convenience Store at 3 AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店是一个奇怪的世界。灯光特别亮，和外面的黑夜形成强烈的对比。店员是一个年轻的女孩，看起来不困，在整理货架上的饮料。我买了一杯热咖啡和一个三明治。她扫完码以后说了一句「这么晚还没睡啊」，语气不是关心也不是好奇，只是一种轻轻的确认——我们都是在这个不正常的时间里醒着的人。柜台旁边坐着一个穿制服的快递员，低着头吃盒饭。角落里有一个学生在看书，面前放着两罐空的咖啡。窗外偶尔有一辆出租车经过。这里没有人认识谁，也没有人需要假装什么。凌晨三点的便利店是城市里最诚实的地方——每个人都是因为各自的原因还没有回家，而这盏灯为所有人亮着。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlìdiàn shì yí gè qíguài de shìjiè. Dēngguāng tèbié liàng, hé wàimiàn de hēiyè xíngchéng qiángliè de duìbǐ. Diànyuán shì yí gè niánqīng de nǚhái, kàn qǐlái bú kùn, zài zhěnglǐ huòjià shàng de yǐnliào. Wǒ mǎi le yì bēi rè kāfēi hé yí gè sānmíngzhì. Tā sǎo wán mǎ yǐhòu shuō le yí jù「zhème wǎn hái méi shuì a」, yǔqì bú shì guānxīn yě bú shì hàoqí, zhǐ shì yì zhǒng qīngqīng de quèrèn — wǒmen dōu shì zài zhège bú zhèngcháng de shíjiān lǐ xǐng zhe de rén. Guìtái pángbiān zuò zhe yí gè chuān zhìfú de kuàidìyuán, dī zhe tóu chī héfàn. Jiǎoluò lǐ yǒu yí gè xuéshēng zài kàn shū, miànqián fàng zhe liǎng guàn kōng de kāfēi. Chuāng wài ǒu'ěr yǒu yí liàng chūzūchē jīngguò. Zhèlǐ méiyǒu rén rènshi shéi, yě méiyǒu rén xūyào jiǎzhuāng shénme. Língchén sān diǎn de biànlìdiàn shì chéngshì lǐ zuì chéngshí de dìfāng — měi gè rén dōu shì yīnwèi gèzì de yuányīn hái méiyǒu huí jiā, ér zhè zhǎn dēng wèi suǒyǒu rén liàng zhe.
text_en: The convenience store at 3 AM is a strange world. The lights are exceptionally bright, forming a stark contrast with the darkness outside. The clerk is a young woman who doesn't look sleepy, organizing drinks on the shelf. I bought a hot coffee and a sandwich. After scanning the code she said 'still not asleep this late, huh' — not with concern or curiosity, just a light acknowledgment — we're both people awake at this abnormal hour. Beside the counter sits a delivery worker in uniform, head down eating a boxed meal. In the corner a student reads, two empty coffee cans in front of them. Occasionally a taxi passes outside the window. Nobody here knows anyone, and nobody needs to pretend to be anything. The convenience store at 3 AM is the most honest place in the city — everyone is here because of their own reasons for not going home, and this light stays on for all of them.

title: The Library's Unwritten Rules
text_zh: 我常去的图书馆有很多没有人写下来的规矩。比如，靠窗的位置永远属于那几个考研的学生，他们早上八点就来了，书包和水杯标记了领地。比如，二楼东边是安静区，就算没有贴牌子，新来的人也会自然地降低音量。再比如，自习室的插座旁边的位置最受欢迎，大家会把充电器插上然后绕很远的线。还有一个有趣的现象：如果一个人去洗手间或者去买水，把书留在座位上就表示「我还会回来」，没有人会坐那个位置。但是如果超过一个小时，旁边的人就会开始犹豫：这个人到底还来不来？这些规矩没有任何人制定，但大家都默默地遵守。我觉得这就是公共空间最好的样子——不需要管理者，只需要默契。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de túshūguǎn yǒu hěn duō méiyǒu rén xiě xiàlái de guījǔ. Bǐrú, kào chuāng de wèizhi yǒngyuǎn shǔyú nà jǐ gè kǎoyán de xuéshēng, tāmen zǎoshang bā diǎn jiù lái le, shūbāo hé shuǐbēi biāojì le lǐngdì. Bǐrú, èr lóu dōngbiān shì ānjìng qū, jiùsuàn méiyǒu tiē páizi, xīn lái de rén yě huì zìrán de jiàngdī yīnliàng. Zài bǐrú, zìxíshì de chāzuò pángbiān de wèizhi zuì shòu huānyíng, dàjiā huì bǎ chōngdiànqì chā shàng ránhòu rào hěn yuǎn de xiàn. Hái yǒu yí gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: rúguǒ yí gè rén qù xǐshǒujiān huòzhě qù mǎi shuǐ, bǎ shū liú zài zuòwèi shàng jiù biǎoshì「wǒ hái huì huílái」, méiyǒu rén huì zuò nàge wèizhi. Dànshì rúguǒ chāoguò yí gè xiǎoshí, pángbiān de rén jiù huì kāishǐ yóuyù: zhège rén dàodǐ hái lái bu lái? Zhèxiē guījǔ méiyǒu rènhé rén zhìdìng, dàn dàjiā dōu mòmò de zūnshǒu. Wǒ juéde zhè jiù shì gōnggòng kōngjiān zuì hǎo de yàngzi — bù xūyào guǎnlǐzhě, zhǐ xūyào mòqì.
text_en: The library I often go to has many unwritten rules. For example, the window seats always belong to those few grad-school-exam students who arrive by eight in the morning, their backpacks and water bottles marking territory. The east side of the second floor is the quiet zone — even without a sign, newcomers naturally lower their voices. The seats near the outlets in the study room are the most popular; people plug in their chargers and run cables a long way. There's also an interesting phenomenon: if someone goes to the restroom or to buy water and leaves their books on the seat, it means 'I'll be back,' and nobody will take that seat. But if more than an hour passes, the people nearby start to wonder: is this person actually coming back? Nobody established these rules, but everyone silently follows them. I think this is what a public space looks like at its best — no managers needed, just mutual understanding.

title: The Hospital Elevator
text_zh: 医院的电梯和其他地方的电梯不一样。首先它特别大，要能装下轮椅和病床。其次按钮的高度比较低，坐在轮椅上的人也能够得到。最不一样的是里面的气氛。在商场的电梯里，人们会看手机、聊天、笑。在医院的电梯里，大多数人沉默。有的人手里拿着检查报告，有的人眼睛红红的，有的人推着轮椅上的家人。每一层楼停下来，进来或出去的人脸上都写着不同的故事。四楼是产科——偶尔有人抱着新生儿笑着出来，那是整个电梯里最温暖的时刻。六楼以上是住院部——按那些楼层的人往往表情比较严肃。我注意到一个细节：在医院的电梯里，人们会主动帮别人按楼层、扶门。
text_pinyin: Yīyuàn de diàntī hé qítā dìfāng de diàntī bù yíyàng. Shǒuxiān tā tèbié dà, yào néng zhuāng xià lúnyǐ hé bìngchuáng. Qícì ànniǔ de gāodù bǐjiào dī, zuò zài lúnyǐ shàng de rén yě néng gòu de dào. Zuì bù yíyàng de shì lǐmiàn de qìfēn. Zài shāngchǎng de diàntī lǐ, rénmen huì kàn shǒujī, liáotiān, xiào. Zài yīyuàn de diàntī lǐ, dà duōshù rén chénmò. Yǒu de rén shǒu lǐ ná zhe jiǎnchá bàogào, yǒu de rén yǎnjing hóng hóng de, yǒu de rén tuī zhe lúnyǐ shàng de jiārén. Měi yì céng lóu tíng xiàlái, jìn lái huò chūqù de rén liǎn shàng dōu xiě zhe bùtóng de gùshi. Sì lóu shì chǎnkē — ǒu'ěr yǒu rén bào zhe xīnshēng'ér xiào zhe chūlái, nà shì zhěnggè diàntī lǐ zuì wēnnuǎn de shíkè. Liù lóu yǐshàng shì zhùyuàn bù — àn nàxiē lóucéng de rén wǎngwǎng biǎoqíng bǐjiào yánsù. Wǒ zhùyì dào yí gè xìjié: zài yīyuàn de diàntī lǐ, rénmen huì zhǔdòng bāng biérén àn lóucéng, fú mén. Yěxǔ shì yīnwèi zài zhège dìfāng, měi gè rén dōu gèng róngyì lǐjiě shénme jiào cuìruò.
text_en: Hospital elevators are different from elevators elsewhere. First, they're especially large — they need to fit wheelchairs and hospital beds. Second, the buttons are lower so people in wheelchairs can reach them. What's most different is the atmosphere inside. In a shopping mall elevator, people check phones, chat, laugh. In a hospital elevator, most people are silent. Some hold examination reports, some have red eyes, some push family members in wheelchairs. At each floor, the people entering or exiting carry different stories on their faces. The fourth floor is maternity — occasionally someone comes out smiling with a newborn, and that's the warmest moment in the whole elevator. The sixth floor and above is the inpatient ward — people pressing those floors tend to look more serious. I noticed a detail: in hospital elevators, people proactively help others press floors and hold doors.

title: The Missing Umbrella
text_zh: 办公室里有一个很小的谜。茶水间的伞架上本来有六把公用的透明雨伞，但是每到下雨天就会少一两把，等到晴天又会多出来。有时候多出来的不是原来那把——颜色不对，或者伞柄的形状不一样。我仔细观察了两个星期，发现了规律：每次少掉的伞，总是在两三天以后由不同的人带回来。我问了其中一个同事，她有点不好意思地说「那天雨太大了，我就先拿了一把，第二天忘了还，后来我自己的伞找到了，就把自己的放回去了」。原来消失的伞并没有真的消失，它们只是在公司和每个人的家之间旅行。伞架变成了一个小小的信任系统——没有登记本，没有监控，只靠大家的自觉。偶尔有一把伞永远消失了，但也偶尔会多出一把新的。整个系统保持着一种微妙的平衡。
text_pinyin: Bàngōngshì lǐ yǒu yí gè hěn xiǎo de mí. Cháshuǐ jiān de sǎn jià shàng běnlái yǒu liù bǎ gōngyòng de tòumíng yǔsǎn, dànshì měi dào xià yǔ tiān jiù huì shǎo yì liǎng bǎ, děng dào qíng tiān yòu huì duō chūlái. Yǒu shíhou duō chūlái de bú shì yuánlái nà bǎ — yánsè bú duì, huòzhě sǎn bǐng de xíngzhuàng bù yíyàng. Wǒ zǐxì guānchá le liǎng gè xīngqī, fāxiàn le guīlǜ: měi cì shǎo diào de sǎn, zǒng shì zài liǎng sān tiān yǐhòu yóu bùtóng de rén dài huílái. Wǒ wèn le qízhōng yí gè tóngshì, tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de shuō「nà tiān yǔ tài dà le, wǒ jiù xiān ná le yì bǎ, dì èr tiān wàng le huán, hòulái wǒ zìjǐ de sǎn zhǎo dào le, jiù bǎ zìjǐ de fàng huíqù le」. Yuánlái xiāoshī de sǎn bìng méiyǒu zhēn de xiāoshī, tāmen zhǐ shì zài gōngsī hé měi gè rén de jiā zhī jiān lǚxíng. Sǎn jià biàn chéng le yí gè xiǎoxiǎo de xìnrèn xìtǒng — méiyǒu dēngjì běn, méiyǒu jiānkòng, zhǐ kào dàjiā de zìjué. Ǒu'ěr yǒu yì bǎ sǎn yǒngyuǎn xiāoshī le, dàn yě ǒu'ěr huì duō chū yì bǎ xīn de. Zhěnggè xìtǒng bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng wēimiào de pínghéng.
text_en: There's a tiny mystery in the office. The umbrella rack in the tea room originally had six shared transparent umbrellas, but on rainy days one or two would disappear, then reappear on sunny days. Sometimes the ones that came back weren't the originals — wrong color, or different handle shape. I observed carefully for two weeks and found the pattern: each missing umbrella was always brought back two or three days later by a different person. I asked one colleague, and she said a bit sheepishly, 'it was raining so hard that day so I grabbed one, forgot to return it the next day, then found my own umbrella and put mine back instead.' So the missing umbrellas hadn't really disappeared — they were just traveling between the office and everyone's homes. The umbrella rack had become a small trust system — no sign-out sheet, no surveillance, just everyone's self-discipline. Occasionally one umbrella vanished forever, but occasionally a new one appeared. The whole system maintained a delicate balance.

questions.q_en: What is the most likely source of this feeling?
questions.explanation: The found umbrella triggers not just recognition but a rush of associated memory from the past.

questions.q_en: Why did the colleague return a different umbrella?

options.text_en: Found her own umbrella and put hers back instead

options.pinyin: wàng le shì nǎ yì bǎ

questions.explanation: The colleague found her own umbrella elsewhere and quietly replaced the borrowed one with a different one.

options.text_en: not necessarily, it depends on personal experience
