title: Speaking Two Languages at Home
text_zh: 我和妈妈说话用方言，和爸爸说话用普通话。这件事情我从来没有觉得奇怪，直到有一天朋友来我家吃饭，惊讶地问「你怎么跟你妈说一种话，跟你爸说另一种？」我才意识到这确实有点特别。妈妈是南方人，从小跟我说她老家的方言。爸爸是北方人，一直说普通话。他们两个人之间用普通话交流。所以在我们家的饭桌上，经常能听到两种语言来回切换。我说方言的时候声音会自然变软，用词也比较随意，因为方言对我来说意味着亲近和放松。说普通话的时候我会稍微正式一点，就像换了一个频道。有时候我找不到一个方言词的普通话说法，就会愣住。两种语言在我身体里和平共存，各自负责不同的情感领域。我不是两个人，但我确实有两个表达自己的入口。
text_pinyin: Wǒ hé māma shuōhuà yòng fāngyán, hé bàba shuōhuà yòng pǔtōnghuà. Zhè jiàn shìqing wǒ cónglái méiyǒu juéde qíguài, zhídào yǒu yì tiān péngyou lái wǒ jiā chīfàn, jīngyà de wèn「nǐ zěnme gēn nǐ mā shuō yì zhǒng huà, gēn nǐ bà shuō lìng yì zhǒng?」wǒ cái yìshí dào zhè quèshí yǒudiǎn tèbié. Māma shì nánfāng rén, cóng xiǎo gēn wǒ shuō tā lǎojiā de fāngyán. Bàba shì běifāng rén, yìzhí shuō pǔtōnghuà. Tāmen liǎng gè rén zhī jiān yòng pǔtōnghuà jiāoliú. Suǒyǐ zài wǒmen jiā de fànzhuō shàng, jīngcháng néng tīng dào liǎng zhǒng yǔyán lái huí qiēhuàn. Wǒ shuō fāngyán de shíhou shēngyīn huì zìrán biàn ruǎn, yòng cí yě bǐjiào suíyì, yīnwèi fāngyán duì wǒ lái shuō yìwèi zhe qīnjìn hé fàngsōng. Shuō pǔtōnghuà de shíhou wǒ huì shāowēi zhèngshì yìdiǎn, jiù xiàng huàn le yí gè píndào. Yǒu shíhou wǒ zhǎo bú dào yí gè fāngyán cí de pǔtōnghuà shuōfǎ, jiù huì lèng zhù. Liǎng zhǒng yǔyán zài wǒ shēntǐ lǐ hépíng gòngcún, gèzì fùzé bùtóng de qínggǎn lǐngyù. Wǒ bú shì liǎng gè rén, dàn wǒ quèshí yǒu liǎng gè biǎodá zìjǐ de rùkǒu.
text_en: I speak dialect with my mom and Mandarin with my dad. I never found this strange until one day a friend came to dinner and asked in surprise, 'how come you speak one language with your mom and another with your dad?' Only then did I realize it was a bit unusual. Mom is from the south and spoke her hometown dialect with me since I was small. Dad is from the north and has always spoken Mandarin. The two of them communicate in Mandarin. So at our dinner table you can often hear two languages switching back and forth. When I speak dialect my voice naturally softens and my word choice becomes more casual, because dialect means intimacy and relaxation to me. When I speak Mandarin I'm slightly more formal, like switching to a different channel. Sometimes I can't find the Mandarin equivalent of a dialect word and freeze. The two languages coexist peacefully inside me, each responsible for different emotional territories. I'm not two people, but I do have two doorways for expressing myself.

questions.q_zh: 爸爸和妈妈之间用什么语言交流？
questions.q_en: What language do the parents use with each other?
questions.explanation: The parents use Mandarin with each other because they come from different dialect regions, making Mandarin their shared common language.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中的'重复'有什么深意？
questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the 'repetition' in this passage carry?
questions.explanation: The repeated routine signals comfort and constancy -- anchoring, not numbing.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样看待自己的双语状态？
questions.q_en: How does the author view their bilingual state?

options.text: 两种语言和平共存，各负责不同情感
options.pinyin: liǎng zhǒng yǔyán hépíng gòngcún, gè fùzé bùtóng qínggǎn
options.text_en: Two languages coexist peacefully, each handling different emotions

questions.explanation: The passage describes: the two languages coexist peacefully inside me.

title: The Group Chat Name
text_zh: 我们大学寝室四个人有一个微信群，这个群的名字已经改了不下三十次。一开始叫「407寝室」，很正常。后来有人改成「四个找不到对象的人」，大家都笑了。再后来变成了「学术讨论组」——其实从来没有讨论过学术。毕业以后改成了「社会的毒打接受中心」，因为大家都开始上班了。有人结婚以后改成了「三个单身一个叛徒」。后来第二个人也结婚了，就变成了「二比二，公平竞争」。最近一次改名是上个月，因为四个人终于约好了一起吃饭，群名临时改成了「本周六火锅，谁迟到谁买单」。吃完火锅以后没有人改回去。我发现，这个群名的变化其实记录了我们四个人的人生阶段。将来如果有人写我们的传记，只要看群名的历史就够了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen dàxué qǐnshì sì gè rén yǒu yí gè wēixìn qún, zhège qún de míngzi yǐjīng gǎi le bú xià sānshí cì. Yì kāishǐ jiào「sì líng qī qǐnshì」, hěn zhèngcháng. Hòulái yǒu rén gǎi chéng「sì gè zhǎo bú dào duìxiàng de rén」, dàjiā dōu xiào le. Zài hòulái biàn chéng le「xuéshù tǎolùn zǔ」— qíshí cónglái méiyǒu tǎolùn guò xuéshù. Bìyè yǐhòu gǎi chéng le「shèhuì de dúdǎ jiēshòu zhōngxīn」, yīnwèi dàjiā dōu kāishǐ shàngbān le. Yǒu rén jiéhūn yǐhòu gǎi chéng le「sān gè dānshēn yí gè pàntú」. Hòulái dì èr gè rén yě jiéhūn le, jiù biàn chéng le「èr bǐ èr, gōngpíng jìngzhēng」. Zuìjìn yí cì gǎi míng shì shàng gè yuè, yīnwèi sì gè rén zhōngyú yuē hǎo le yìqǐ chīfàn, qún míng línshí gǎi chéng le「běn zhōuliù huǒguō, shéi chídào shéi mǎidān」. Chī wán huǒguō yǐhòu méiyǒu rén gǎi huíqù. Wǒ fāxiàn, zhège qún míng de biànhuà qíshí jìlù le wǒmen sì gè rén de rénshēng jiēduàn. Jiānglái rúguǒ yǒu rén xiě wǒmen de zhuànjì, zhǐyào kàn qún míng de lìshǐ jiù gòu le.
text_en: The four of us from our college dorm have a WeChat group that's been renamed no fewer than thirty times. It started as '407 Dorm' — perfectly normal. Then someone changed it to 'Four People Who Can't Find Partners' and Later it became 'Academic Discussion Group' — though no academics were ever discussed. After graduation it became 'Society's Beating Reception Center' because everyone started working. When one person got married it became 'Three Singles and One Traitor.' When the second person married, it changed to 'Two vs Two, Fair Competition.' The most recent rename was last month when the four of us finally managed to schedule dinner together — the group was temporarily renamed 'Hot Pot This Saturday, Whoever's Late Pays.' After the hot pot, nobody changed it back. The group name changes actually documented our life stages. If someone wrote our biography in the future, they'd only need to look at the name history.

questions.q_zh: 群名改了大约多少次？
questions.q_en: Roughly how many times has the group name been changed?
questions.explanation: The group name has been changed no fewer than thirty times, reflecting how the four dormmates keep renaming the chat to match inside jokes and life stages.

questions.q_zh: 这段话用什么来代替直接的情感表达？
questions.q_en: What does this passage use instead of direct emotional expression?
questions.explanation: Emotion surfaces through described actions and settings rather than explicit statements of feeling.

questions.q_zh: 从这段话可以推断人物是什么样的人？
questions.q_en: What kind of person can we infer the character is?
questions.explanation: The slow, attentive approach to each task reveals patience and meticulousness.

title: Grandma's Pickled Vegetables
text_zh: 奶奶有一个泡菜坛子，比我的年龄还大。坛子是暗红色的，肚子圆圆的，口很小，坛沿有一圈水槽，盖上盖子以后水会把空气隔绝在外面。奶奶说这个坛子是她结婚的时候外婆送的，里面的泡菜水从来没有换过——只是不断地加盐、加水、加新的蔬菜。所以这坛泡菜的味道已经发酵了几十年。每次去奶奶家，她都会打开坛子，用干净的筷子捞出几根萝卜或者几片白菜。泡菜的味道酸酸咸咸的，比任何餐厅的都好吃。我问奶奶秘诀是什么。她想了想说「没有秘诀，就是时间」。后来我试着自己做泡菜，买了新坛子，按照奶奶教的步骤，一个月以后味道还是差很远。我才明白，有些味道不是技术的问题，而是需要几十年的耐心和一个从来不中断的过程。
text_pinyin: Nǎinai yǒu yí gè pàocài tánzi, bǐ wǒ de niánlíng hái dà. Tánzi shì ànhóng sè de, dùzi yuányuán de, kǒu hěn xiǎo, tán yán yǒu yì quān shuǐcáo, gài shàng gàizi yǐhòu shuǐ huì bǎ kōngqì géjué zài wàimiàn. Nǎinai shuō zhège tánzi shì tā jiéhūn de shíhou wàipó sòng de, lǐmiàn de pàocài shuǐ cónglái méiyǒu huàn guò — zhǐ shì búduàn de jiā yán, jiā shuǐ, jiā xīn de shūcài. Suǒyǐ zhè tán pàocài de wèidào yǐjīng fājiào le jǐ shí nián. Měi cì qù nǎinai jiā, tā dōu huì dǎkāi tánzi, yòng gānjìng de kuàizi lāo chū jǐ gēn luóbo huòzhě jǐ piàn báicài. Pàocài de wèidào suānsuān xiánxián de, bǐ rènhé cāntīng de dōu hǎochī. Wǒ wèn nǎinai mìjué shì shénme. Tā xiǎng le xiǎng shuō「méiyǒu mìjué, jiù shì shíjiān」. Hòulái wǒ shì zhe zìjǐ zuò pàocài, mǎi le xīn tánzi, ànzhào nǎinai jiāo de bùzhòu, yí gè yuè yǐhòu wèidào háishi chà hěn yuǎn. Wǒ cái míngbai, yǒuxiē wèidào bú shì jìshù de wèntí, ér shì xūyào jǐ shí nián de nàixīn hé yí gè cónglái bù zhōngduàn de guòchéng.
text_en: Grandma has a pickle jar that's older than I am. It's dark red with a round belly and small mouth, with a water groove around the rim — once the lid is on, the water seals air out. Grandma says her mother gave it to her when she got married, and the pickling brine inside has never been replaced — she just keeps adding salt, water, and fresh vegetables. So the flavor of these pickles has been fermenting for decades. Every time I visit grandma, she opens the jar and fishes out a few radishes or cabbage leaves with clean chopsticks. The pickles taste sour and salty, better than any restaurant's. I asked grandma what the secret was. She thought for a moment and said 'there's no secret, just time.' Later I tried making my own pickles, bought a new jar, followed grandma's steps exactly — a month later the taste was still far off. Then I understood: some flavors aren't a matter of technique but require decades of patience and a process that's never been interrupted.

questions.q_zh: 泡菜坛子是谁送给奶奶的？
questions.q_en: Who gave grandma the pickle jar?
questions.explanation: The passage identifies: grandma has a pickle jar that's older than I am.

questions.q_zh: 从这段话可以看出什么样的关系？
questions.q_en: What kind of relationship does this passage reveal?
questions.explanation: The natural, unguarded interactions show authentic closeness without performance.

questions.q_zh: 作者自己做的泡菜怎么样？
questions.q_en: How did the author's own pickles turn out?
questions.explanation: The author tried making pickles independently, but the taste was far off from grandma's version.

title: The Balcony Garden
text_zh: 对面楼的五楼有一个阳台花园。我不认识那个住户，但每天早上能看到一个穿围裙的女人出来浇花。她的阳台不大，但种满了各种植物——有的开花，有的结果，有的只是绿叶。花盆排列得很整齐，大的在后面，小的在前面，每一盆都能晒到太阳。我注意到她浇花的方式很特别：不是从左到右按顺序，而是先浇看起来最渴的那几盆，然后再浇其他的。她浇完花会站在那里看一会儿，有时候用手摸一摸叶子，好像在确认它们今天的状态。有一次台风过后，我看到她阳台上几盆花倒了，泥土撒了一地。第二天一早她就在那里收拾，把每一盆重新整理好。一个星期以后阳台又恢复了原来的样子。我觉得那个阳台不只是一个花园——它是一个人对生活的态度的缩影：认真、耐心、不轻易放弃。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn lóu de wǔ lóu yǒu yí gè yángtái huāyuán. Wǒ bú rènshi nàge zhùhù, dàn měi tiān zǎoshang néng kàn dào yí gè chuān wéiqún de nǚrén chūlái jiāo huā. Tā de yángtái bú dà, dàn zhǒng mǎn le gè zhǒng zhíwù — yǒu de kāi huā, yǒu de jiéguǒ, yǒu de zhǐ shì lǜ yè. Huāpén páiliè de hěn zhěngqí, dà de zài hòumiàn, xiǎo de zài qiánmiàn, měi yì pén dōu néng shài dào tàiyáng. Wǒ zhùyì dào tā jiāo huā de fāngshì hěn tèbié: bú shì cóng zuǒ dào yòu àn shùnxù, ér shì xiān jiāo kàn qǐlái zuì kě de nà jǐ pén, ránhòu zài jiāo qítā de. Tā jiāo wán huā huì zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn yíhuìr, yǒu shíhou yòng shǒu mō yì mō yèzi, hǎoxiàng zài quèrèn tāmen jīntiān de zhuàngtài. Yǒu yí cì táifēng guò hòu, wǒ kàn dào tā yángtái shàng jǐ pén huā dǎo le, nítǔ sā le yí dì. Dì èr tiān yì zǎo tā jiù zài nàlǐ shōushi, bǎ měi yì pén chóngxīn zhěnglǐ hǎo. Yí gè xīngqī yǐhòu yángtái yòu huīfù le yuánlái de yàngzi. Wǒ juéde nàge yángtái bù zhǐ shì yí gè huāyuán — tā shì yí gè rén duì shēnghuó de tàidù de suōyǐng: rènzhēn, nàixīn, bù qīngyì fàngqì.
text_en: On the fifth floor of the building across from mine there's a balcony garden. I don't know the resident, but every morning I can see a woman in an apron come out to water her plants. Her balcony isn't large but it's packed with all kinds of plants — some flowering, some bearing fruit, some just green leaves. The pots are arranged neatly, large ones in back, small ones in front, each getting sunlight. I noticed her watering method is special: not left to right in order, but first watering whichever pots look thirstiest, then the rest. After watering she stands and looks for a while, sometimes touching the leaves, as if checking on their condition today. Once after a typhoon I saw several pots knocked over on her balcony, soil scattered everywhere. The next morning she was already there cleaning up, reorganizing every pot. A week later the balcony looked the same as before. I think that balcony isn't just a garden — it's a miniature of one person's attitude toward life: careful, patient, not easily giving up.

questions.q_zh: 女人浇花的顺序是什么？
questions.q_en: In what order does the woman water her plants?

options.pinyin: xiān jiāo kàn qǐlái zuì kě de

questions.explanation: The woman starts with whichever plant looks thirstiest, then moves to the rest -- attentive, not mechanical.

questions.q_zh: 台风以后发生了什么？
questions.q_en: What happened after the typhoon?

options.pinyin: tā bú zài zhòng huā le

options.text: 几盆花倒了，她第二天收拾好了
options.pinyin: jǐ pén huā dǎo le, tā dì èr tiān shōushi hǎo le
options.text_en: Some pots fell over, she cleaned up the next day

questions.explanation: After the typhoon, some pots had fallen over; she cleaned up the mess the next day without complaint.

title: The Rain on the Window
text_zh: 下雨的时候我喜欢关掉灯，坐在窗边看雨滴在玻璃上滑下来。每一滴雨都有自己的路线——有的直直地往下走，有的左右摇摆，有的会停在中间好久，突然和另一滴合并，变成一滴大雨珠飞快地冲到底部。小时候我把这当成赛跑：选两滴雨，看哪一滴先到达窗户的下边缘。现在长大了还是会不自觉地这么做。窗外的世界在雨水的扭曲下变得模糊，路灯变成一团团黄色的光晕，行人变成移动的影子。雨声是一种很好的白噪音，不像音乐那样需要注意力，只是存在着，填满了房间里每一个安静的角落。我发现下雨天让人更容易思考——也许因为整个世界都慢了下来，连时间都好像变得不那么赶了。
text_pinyin: Xià yǔ de shíhou wǒ xǐhuan guān diào dēng, zuò zài chuāng biān kàn yǔdī zài bōli shàng huá xiàlái. Měi yì dī yǔ dōu yǒu zìjǐ de lùxiàn — yǒu de zhí zhí de wǎng xià zǒu, yǒu de zuǒyòu yáobǎi, yǒu de huì tíng zài zhōngjiān hǎo jiǔ, tūrán hé lìng yì dī hébìng, biàn chéng yì dī dà yǔzhū fēikuài de chōng dào dǐbù. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ bǎ zhè dàng chéng sàipǎo: xuǎn liǎng dī yǔ, kàn nǎ yì dī xiān dàodá chuānghù de xià biānyuán. Xiànzài zhǎng dà le háishi huì bù zìjué de zhème zuò. Chuāng wài de shìjiè zài yǔshuǐ de niǔqū xià biàn de móhu, lùdēng biàn chéng yì tuántuán huángsè de guāngyūn, xíngrén biàn chéng yídòng de yǐngzi. Yǔ shēng shì yì zhǒng hěn hǎo de bái zàoyīn, bú xiàng yīnyuè nàyàng xūyào zhùyìlì, zhǐ shì cúnzài zhe, tián mǎn le fángjiān lǐ měi yí gè ānjìng de jiǎoluò. Wǒ fāxiàn xià yǔ tiān ràng rén gèng róngyì sīkǎo — yěxǔ yīnwèi zhěnggè shìjiè dōu màn le xiàlái, lián shíjiān dōu hǎoxiàng biàn de bú nàme gǎn le.
text_en: When it rains I like to turn off the lights and sit by the window watching raindrops slide down the glass. Each raindrop has its own path — some go straight down, some sway left and right, some pause in the middle for a long time then suddenly merge with another drop, becoming a large bead that races to the bottom. As a child I treated this as a race: pick two drops and see which reaches the bottom edge first. Now grown up, I still do it without thinking. The world outside becomes blurry through the distortion of rain — streetlights become clusters of yellow halos, pedestrians become moving shadows. The sound of rain is wonderful white noise, not like music that demands attention, just existing, filling every quiet corner of the room. I've found that rainy days make it easier to think — perhaps because the whole world has slowed down, and even time seems less rushed.

questions.q_zh: 作者小时候把雨滴当成什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author treat raindrops as when young?
questions.explanation: As a child, the author picked two raindrops on the window and watched them slide down, treating it as a race to see which would reach the bottom first.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为雨声和音乐有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does the author think rain sounds differ from music?

options.text: 雨声不需要注意力，只是存在着
options.pinyin: yǔ shēng bù xūyào zhùyìlì, zhǐ shì cúnzài zhe
options.text_en: Rain doesn't demand attention, just exists

questions.explanation: The contrast is drawn explicitly: just existing.

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者觉得下雨天更容易思考？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think rainy days are better for thinking?

options.text: 因为整个世界都慢了下来
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhěnggè shìjiè dōu màn le xiàlái
options.text_en: Because the whole world has slowed down

questions.explanation: Rain slows everything down -- with the whole world quieter and less rushed, thinking comes more naturally.

title: The Overnight Train
text_zh: 我坐过一次从北京到成都的夜间火车，硬卧，下铺。晚上九点上车，第二天下午一点到。火车刚开动的时候，车厢里很热闹：有人泡方便面，有人打牌，小孩子在过道里跑来跑去。但是到了十点左右，列车员过来把大灯关了，只留下过道的小灯，整个车厢突然安静下来。我躺在下铺，能听到火车有节奏的声音：咔嗒咔嗒，咔嗒咔嗒。窗帘没有完全拉上，偶尔能看到外面经过的小站——站台上几乎没有人，只有一盏昏暗的灯。我不知道那是什么地方，大概是某个我永远不会去的小城市。对面上铺的人已经开始打呼噜了，声音很稳定，让人莫名其妙地安心。在火车上睡觉有一种独特的感觉：身体在移动，但意识在休息。你醒来的时候已经到了另一个城市，像是被时间偷偷搬运了一段距离。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zuò guò yí cì cóng Běijīng dào Chéngdū de yèjiān huǒchē, yìngwò, xiàpù. Wǎnshang jiǔ diǎn shàng chē, dì èr tiān xiàwǔ yì diǎn dào. Huǒchē gāng kāidòng de shíhou, chēxiāng lǐ hěn rènao: yǒu rén pào fāngbiàn miàn, yǒu rén dǎpái, xiǎo háizi zài guòdào lǐ pǎo lái pǎo qù. Dànshì dào le shí diǎn zuǒyòu, lièchēyuán guòlái bǎ dà dēng guān le, zhǐ liú xià guòdào de xiǎo dēng, zhěnggè chēxiāng tūrán ānjìng xiàlái. Wǒ tǎng zài xiàpù, néng tīng dào huǒchē yǒu jiézòu de shēngyīn: kādā kādā, kādā kādā. Chuānglián méiyǒu wánquán lā shàng, ǒu'ěr néng kàn dào wàimiàn jīngguò de xiǎo zhàn — zhàntái shàng jīhū méiyǒu rén, zhǐ yǒu yì zhǎn hūn'àn de dēng. Wǒ bù zhīdào nà shì shénme dìfāng, dàgài shì mǒu gè wǒ yǒngyuǎn bú huì qù de xiǎo chéngshì. Duìmiàn shàngpù de rén yǐjīng kāishǐ dǎ hūlu le, shēngyīn hěn wěndìng, ràng rén mòmíngqímiào de ānxīn. Zài huǒchē shàng shuìjiào yǒu yì zhǒng dútè de gǎnjué: shēntǐ zài yídòng, dàn yìshí zài xiūxi. Nǐ xǐng lái de shíhou yǐjīng dào le lìng yí gè chéngshì, xiàng shì bèi shíjiān tōutōu bānyùn le yí duàn jùlí.
text_en: I once took an overnight train from Beijing to Chengdu — hard sleeper, lower bunk. Boarded at nine PM, arrived the next afternoon at one. When the train first started the carriage was lively: people making instant noodles, playing cards, kids running up and down the aisle. But around ten the conductor came and turned off the main lights, leaving only the small aisle lights, and the whole carriage suddenly fell quiet. Lying on my lower bunk I could hear the train's rhythmic sound: clickety-clack, clickety-clack. The curtain wasn't fully drawn and I could occasionally see small stations passing outside — nearly empty platforms with just a dim light. I didn't know where they were, probably some small city I'd never visit. The person on the upper bunk across from me had started snoring steadily, which was inexplicably reassuring. Sleeping on a train has a unique feeling: your body is moving but your consciousness is resting. When you wake up you're in another city, as if time secretly transported you across a distance.

questions.q_zh: 这段话传达的情绪是克制的还是外露的？
questions.q_en: Is the emotion conveyed in this passage restrained or overt?
questions.explanation: The passage embeds feeling in concrete detail rather than stating emotions outright.

title: Why Old Neighborhoods Feel Warmer
text_zh: 我在一个新建的高档小区住了两年以后，搬到了一个二十年前盖的老小区。朋友们都很惊讶，问我为什么要「降级」。但是住进来以后我才知道什么叫做真正的邻居关系。在新小区，我住了两年不知道隔壁住着谁。电梯里碰到邻居，大家都低着头看手机，最多点一下头。在老小区完全不一样。搬进来第一天，隔壁的阿姨就端着一盘饺子过来说「先吃着，搬家忙，别自己做了」。楼下的大爷每天傍晚在院子里下棋，谁路过都可以看一局。快递员知道每一户人家什么时候在家。我觉得新小区的问题不是设计不好，而是设计得太好了——每一户都是独立的、隔音的、不需要和任何人发生关系的。老小区虽然墙薄、走廊窄、停车位不够，但正是这些「不方便」创造了人和人之间交流的机会。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài yí gè xīn jiàn de gāodǎng xiǎoqū zhù le liǎng nián yǐhòu, bān dào le yí gè èrshí nián qián gài de lǎo xiǎoqū. Péngyoumen dōu hěn jīngyà, wèn wǒ wèi shénme yào「jiàngjí」. Dànshì zhù jìnlái yǐhòu wǒ cái zhīdào shénme jiào zuò zhēnzhèng de línjū guānxi. Zài xīn xiǎoqū, wǒ zhù le liǎng nián bù zhīdào gébì zhù zhe shéi. Diàntī lǐ pèng dào línjū, dàjiā dōu dī zhe tóu kàn shǒujī, zuì duō diǎn yíxià tóu. Zài lǎo xiǎoqū wánquán bù yíyàng. Bān jìnlái dì yī tiān, gébì de āyí jiù duān zhe yì pán jiǎozi guòlái shuō「xiān chī zhe, bānjiā máng, bié zìjǐ zuò le」. Lóu xià de dàyé měi tiān bàngwǎn zài yuànzi lǐ xià qí, shéi lùguò dōu kěyǐ kàn yí jú. Kuàidìyuán zhīdào měi yí hù rénjiā shénme shíhou zài jiā. Wǒ juéde xīn xiǎoqū de wèntí bú shì shèjì bù hǎo, ér shì shèjì de tài hǎo le — měi yí hù dōu shì dúlì de, géyīn de, bù xūyào hé rènhé rén fāshēng guānxi de. Lǎo xiǎoqū suīrán qiáng báo, zǒuláng zhǎi, tíngchē wèi bú gòu, dàn zhèng shì zhèxiē「bù fāngbiàn」chuàngzào le rén hé rén zhī jiān jiāoliú de jīhuì.
text_en: After living two years in a newly built upscale compound, I moved to an old compound built twenty years ago. Friends were all surprised, asking why I wanted to 'downgrade.' But only after moving in did I learn what real neighbor relationships are. In the new compound, I lived two years without knowing who lived next door. Meeting neighbors in the elevator, everyone looked down at their phones, at most giving a nod. The old compound is completely different. On my first day moving in, the auntie next door came over with a plate of dumplings saying 'eat these first, you're busy moving, don't cook yourself.' The grandpa downstairs plays chess in the courtyard every evening and anyone passing by can watch a game. The delivery workers know when each household is home. I think the problem with new compounds isn't bad design but too-good design — every unit is independent, soundproof, requiring no connection with anyone. The old compound has thin walls, narrow corridors, and not enough parking, but it's precisely these 'inconveniences' that create opportunities for people to interact.

questions.q_zh: 搬进来第一天邻居做了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the neighbor do on the first day?

options.text_en: Brought over a plate of dumplings

questions.explanation: On the very first day, the neighbor brought over a plate of dumplings -- no introduction needed, just warmth.

title: The Fruit Stand's Seasons
text_zh: 小区门口的水果摊是我的日历。春天的时候，摊上开始出现草莓和樱桃，颜色鲜艳，价格也贵。老板会说「刚上市的，尝尝」。到了夏天，西瓜堆成了小山，整个摊位都散发着甜味。秋天是水果最丰富的季节——苹果、梨、柿子、石榴、橘子，摊位不够大了，地上也摆满了箱子。冬天的种类最少，主要是橘子和苹果，偶尔有进口的车厘子，贵得让人犹豫。老板很会做生意，他从来不催你买，总是先让你尝一口。他切水果的刀法很快，一个苹果三秒钟就变成八瓣。我在这个摊位买了五年的水果，看着老板的女儿从小学生变成了初中生。她放学以后会在摊位旁边写作业，老板偶尔低头看她一眼。水果在变，季节在转，但那个父女俩守着的小摊位一直在那里，像这条街上最稳定的存在。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū ménkǒu de shuǐguǒ tān shì wǒ de rìlì. Chūntiān de shíhou, tān shàng kāishǐ chūxiàn cǎoméi hé yīngtáo, yánsè xiānyàn, jiàgé yě guì. Lǎobǎn huì shuō「gāng shàngshì de, cháng cháng」. Dào le xiàtiān, xīguā duī chéng le xiǎoshān, zhěnggè tānwèi dōu sànfā zhe tiánwèi. Qiūtiān shì shuǐguǒ zuì fēngfù de jìjié — píngguǒ, lí, shìzi, shíliu, júzi, tānwèi bú gòu dà le, dìshàng yě bǎi mǎn le xiāngzi. Dōngtiān de zhǒnglèi zuì shǎo, zhǔyào shì júzi hé píngguǒ, ǒu'ěr yǒu jìnkǒu de chēlízi, guì de ràng rén yóuyù. Lǎobǎn hěn huì zuò shēngyi, tā cónglái bù cuī nǐ mǎi, zǒng shì xiān ràng nǐ cháng yì kǒu. Tā qiē shuǐguǒ de dāofǎ hěn kuài, yí gè píngguǒ sān miǎozhōng jiù biàn chéng bā bàn. Wǒ zài zhège tānwèi mǎi le wǔ nián de shuǐguǒ, kàn zhe lǎobǎn de nǚ'ér cóng xiǎoxuéshēng biàn chéng le chūzhōngshēng. Tā fàngxué yǐhòu huì zài tānwèi pángbiān xiě zuòyè, lǎobǎn ǒu'ěr dītóu kàn tā yì yǎn. Shuǐguǒ zài biàn, jìjié zài zhuǎn, dàn nàge fùnǚ liǎ shǒu zhe de xiǎo tānwèi yìzhí zài nàlǐ, xiàng zhè tiáo jiē shàng zuì wěndìng de cúnzài.
text_en: The fruit stand at the compound entrance is my calendar. In spring, strawberries and cherries appear — bright colors, high prices. The owner says 'just came to market, try some.' In summer, watermelons pile up like small hills and the whole stand smells sweet. Autumn is the richest season for fruit — apples, pears, persimmons, pomegranates, oranges — the stand isn't big enough and boxes fill the ground too. Winter has the fewest varieties, mainly oranges and apples, occasionally imported cherries so expensive they make you hesitate. The owner is good at business, he never rushes you to buy, always letting you taste first. His knife work cutting fruit is fast; an apple becomes eight slices in three seconds. I've bought fruit at this stand for five years, watching his daughter grow from an elementary student to a middle schooler. After school she does homework beside the stand, and the owner occasionally glances down at her. The fruit changes, the seasons turn, but the little stand this father and daughter tend has always been there — like the most stable presence on this street.

questions.q_en: Which detail best reflects the author's attitude?
questions.explanation: Noticing what others ignore -- that careful attention is itself the author's stance.

options.text_en: a new way of seeing things

questions.explanation: The character's contentment and reflective stance suggest staying in this mode.

title: The Sound of Chopping Vegetables
text_zh: 傍晚六点左右，如果你打开窗户，就能听到整栋楼传来切菜的声音。嗒嗒嗒，嗒嗒嗒——每家的节奏都不一样。楼下那家切得又快又均匀，像是一个厨艺很好的人。隔壁那家比较慢，中间还会停下来，也许在看手机上的菜谱。有一家的声音特别重，刀剁在砧板上像打鼓一样，那应该是在剁肉馅。这些声音混在一起，加上油锅的滋滋声和偶尔的抽油烟机轰鸣，组成了一首属于傍晚的交响乐。我妈妈切菜的声音我一听就能认出来——不快不慢，很有节奏感，中间没有犹豫。她说这是做了三十年饭练出来的。我自己切菜的声音就差多了，有时候快有时候慢，还会不小心停下来检查切得均不均匀。也许再过三十年，我的切菜声也会变成别人窗口飘进来的安心的背景音。
text_pinyin: Bàngwǎn liù diǎn zuǒyòu, rúguǒ nǐ dǎkāi chuānghu, jiù néng tīng dào zhěng dòng lóu chuán lái qiē cài de shēngyīn. Dādādā, dādādā — měi jiā de jiézòu dōu bù yíyàng. Lóu xià nà jiā qiē de yòu kuài yòu jūnyún, xiàng shì yí gè chúyì hěn hǎo de rén. Gébì nà jiā bǐjiào màn, zhōngjiān hái huì tíng xiàlái, yěxǔ zài kàn shǒujī shàng de càipǔ. Yǒu yì jiā de shēngyīn tèbié zhòng, dāo duò zài zhēnbǎn shàng xiàng dǎ gǔ yíyàng, nà yīnggāi shì zài duò ròuxiàn. Zhèxiē shēngyīn hùn zài yìqǐ, jiā shàng yóu guō de zīzī shēng hé ǒu'ěr de chōu yóuyān jī hōngmíng, zǔchéng le yì shǒu shǔyú bàngwǎn de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Wǒ māma qiē cài de shēngyīn wǒ yì tīng jiù néng rèn chūlái — bú kuài bú màn, hěn yǒu jiézòugǎn, zhōngjiān méiyǒu yóuyù. Tā shuō zhè shì zuò le sānshí nián fàn liàn chūlái de. Wǒ zìjǐ qiē cài de shēngyīn jiù chà duō le, yǒu shíhou kuài yǒu shíhou màn, hái huì bù xiǎoxīn tíng xiàlái jiǎnchá qiē de jūn bù jūnyún. Yěxǔ zài guò sānshí nián, wǒ de qiē cài shēng yě huì biàn chéng biérén chuāngkǒu piāo jìnlái de ānxīn de bèijǐng yīn.
text_en: Around six in the evening, if you open the window, you can hear the sound of chopping vegetables from the whole building. Tap-tap-tap, tap-tap-tap — every household has a different rhythm. The one downstairs chops fast and evenly, like someone with good cooking skills. The one next door is slower, pausing in the middle — maybe checking a recipe on their phone. One household chops very heavily, the knife hitting the cutting board like a drum — that's probably mincing meat. These sounds blend together with the sizzle of oil pans and the occasional roar of range hoods, composing an evening symphony. I can recognize my mother's chopping sound instantly, neither fast nor slow, very rhythmic, no hesitation in between. She says that comes from thirty years of cooking. My own chopping sounds are much worse — sometimes fast, sometimes slow, occasionally stopping to check if the cuts are even. Maybe in another thirty years, my chopping sound will become the reassuring background noise drifting into someone else's window.

title: The Post Office Counter
text_zh: 我去邮局寄一个包裹给外地的朋友。现在大家都用快递，邮局的人很少。只有两个柜台开着，一个办理汇款，一个办理包裹。帮我办理的是一个中年女人，动作不快但非常仔细。她让我在包裹单上写收件人的地址和电话，我写完以后她又检查了一遍，发现我的邮编少写了一位数，让我补上。然后她称了重量，告诉我价格，问我要不要保价。整个过程不到十分钟，但每一步都有固定的流程。我注意到柜台后面的墙上贴着很多通知，最显眼的一张写着「请核对好地址再寄出」。旁边还贴着一张已经发黄的宣传画，上面写着「人民邮政为人民」。在快递公司到处都是的今天，邮局就像一个慢下来的时空：排队的人不着急，工作人员不慌张，包裹会按照它该走的速度到达。
text_pinyin: Wǒ qù yóujú jì yí gè bāoguǒ gěi wàidì de péngyou. Xiànzài dàjiā dōu yòng kuàidì, yóujú de rén hěn shǎo. Zhǐ yǒu liǎng gè guìtái kāi zhe, yí gè bànlǐ huìkuǎn, yí gè bànlǐ bāoguǒ. Bāng wǒ bànlǐ de shì yí gè zhōngnián nǚrén, dòngzuò bú kuài dàn fēicháng zǐxì. Tā ràng wǒ zài bāoguǒ dān shàng xiě shōujiàn rén de dìzhǐ hé diànhuà, wǒ xiě wán yǐhòu tā yòu jiǎnchá le yí biàn, fāxiàn wǒ de yóubiān shǎo xiě le yí wèi shù, ràng wǒ bǔ shàng. Ránhòu tā chēng le zhòngliàng, gàosu wǒ jiàgé, wèn wǒ yào bu yào bǎojià. Zhěnggè guòchéng bú dào shí fēnzhōng, dàn měi yí bù dōu yǒu gùdìng de liúchéng. Wǒ zhùyì dào guìtái hòumiàn de qiáng shàng tiē zhe hěn duō tōngzhī, zuì xiǎnyǎn de yì zhāng xiě zhe「qǐng héduì hǎo dìzhǐ zài jì chū」. Pángbiān hái tiē zhe yì zhāng yǐjīng fā huáng de xuānchuán huà, shàngmiàn xiě zhe「rénmín yóuzhèng wèi rénmín」. Zài kuàidì gōngsī dàochù dōu shì de jīntiān, yóujú jiù xiàng yí gè màn xiàlái de shíkōng: páiduì de rén bù zháojí, gōngzuò rényuán bù huāngzhāng, bāoguǒ huì ànzhào tā gāi zǒu de sùdù dàodá. Yěxǔ bú shì suǒyǒu shìqing dōu xūyào nàme kuài.
text_en: I went to the post office to mail a package to a friend in another city. Now that everyone uses express delivery, the post office has few people. Only two counters were open — one for money transfers, one for packages. The woman helping me was middle-aged, not fast but very careful. She had me write the recipient's address and phone number on the parcel form. After I finished she checked it again, found I'd left out one digit of the postal code, and had me add it. Then she weighed it, told me the price, and asked if I wanted insurance. The whole process took less than ten minutes, but every step had a fixed procedure. I noticed many notices on the wall behind the counter. The most prominent one read 'please verify the address before mailing.' Next to it was a yellowed propaganda poster reading 'People's Post Office Serves the People.' In a world full of express delivery companies today, the post office is like a pocket of slowed-down space-time: the people in line aren't hurried, the staff aren't flustered, and packages arrive at the speed they're meant to.

title: The Cat on the Wall
text_zh: 我们小区有一只橘色的猫，没有人知道它是谁家的，也许它不属于任何人。它最喜欢的地方是小区围墙上面的一段——那里刚好有一棵树的树枝伸过来，形成了一小片阴凉。每天下午两三点钟它都会出现在那里，有时候趴着，有时候坐着，尾巴懒洋洋地垂下来。它从墙上往下看小区里发生的一切：老人散步、小孩玩耍、狗被主人牵着走过。它从来不下来参加这些活动，只是观察。有时候有人抬头看到它，会说「又在那儿了」，语气里有一种熟悉的温暖。下雨天它会消失，但第二天太阳出来以后又准时回到墙上。有一次我走到墙下面想摸它，它低下头看了我一眼，眼神不害怕也不亲热，只是一种平静的打量——好像在说「我知道你在这里住，你也知道我在这里住，这就够了」。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū yǒu yì zhī júsè de māo, méiyǒu rén zhīdào tā shì shéi jiā de, yěxǔ tā bù shǔyú rènhé rén. Tā zuì xǐhuan de dìfāng shì xiǎoqū wéiqiáng shàngmiàn de yí duàn — nàlǐ gānghǎo yǒu yì kē shù de shùzhī shēn guòlái, xíngchéng le yì xiǎo piàn yīnliáng. Měi tiān xiàwǔ liǎng sān diǎnzhōng tā dōu huì chūxiàn zài nàlǐ, yǒu shíhou pā zhe, yǒu shíhou zuò zhe, wěiba lǎnyāngyāng de chuí xiàlái. Tā cóng qiáng shàng wǎng xià kàn xiǎoqū lǐ fāshēng de yíqiè: lǎorén sànbù, xiǎohái wánshuǎ, gǒu bèi zhǔrén qiān zhe zǒu guò. Tā cónglái bù xiàlái cānjiā zhèxiē huódòng, zhǐ shì guānchá. Yǒu shíhou yǒu rén táitóu kàn dào tā, huì shuō「yòu zài nàr le」, yǔqì lǐ yǒu yì zhǒng shúxī de wēnnuǎn. Xià yǔ tiān tā huì xiāoshī, dàn dì èr tiān tàiyáng chūlái yǐhòu yòu zhǔnshí huí dào qiáng shàng. Yǒu yí cì wǒ zǒu dào qiáng xiàmiàn xiǎng mō tā, tā dīxià tóu kàn le wǒ yì yǎn, yǎnshén bú hàipà yě bù qīnrè, zhǐ shì yì zhǒng píngjìng de dǎliang — hǎoxiàng zài shuō「wǒ zhīdào nǐ zài zhèlǐ zhù, nǐ yě zhīdào wǒ zài zhèlǐ zhù, zhè jiù gòu le」.
text_en: Our compound has an orange cat. Nobody knows whose it is — maybe it belongs to no one. Its favorite spot is a section of the compound wall where a tree branch extends over, creating a small patch of shade. Every afternoon around two or three it appears there, sometimes lying down, sometimes sitting, tail dangling lazily. From the wall it watches everything happening below: old people strolling, children playing, dogs being walked past on leashes. It never comes down to join these activities, only observes. Sometimes someone looks up and sees it and says 'there it is again,' with a warm familiarity in their voice. On rainy days it disappears, but the next day when the sun comes out it returns to the wall right on time. Once I walked beneath the wall wanting to pet it. It looked down at me — not afraid, not affectionate, just a calm appraisal — as if to say 'I know you live here, you know I live here, that's enough.'

questions.q_zh: 猫最喜欢的地方有什么特别？
questions.q_en: What's special about the cat's favorite spot?

options.text_en: There's shade from a tree branch

questions.explanation: A single tree branch extends over the wall and casts a small patch of shade — that precise spot is where the cat always settles.

questions.explanation: The cat looks at the author with calm appraisal -- unhurried, measuring, neither welcoming nor hostile.

title: The Changing Coffee Order
text_zh: 我在公司楼下的咖啡店工作了半年，注意到一个有趣的规律。有一个穿西装的男人每天早上八点十五分准时来，总是点同一杯咖啡：美式，中杯，不加糖。但是最近三个星期他的订单突然变了——先是换成了拿铁，然后改成了热巧克力，最后变成了一杯温热的牛奶。我忍不住观察他的变化：三周前他来的时候表情很紧张，手机一直响个不停。两周前他换了一身休闲装，脸上的表情放松了一些。上周他来的时候没有带公文包，坐在窗边慢慢地喝，看了半小时窗外的风景。今天他又来了，点了一杯美式——和最初一模一样。但他的眼神不同了，比以前平静很多。我猜他经历了什么重要的事情。一个人的咖啡订单也许比他自己的话更诚实——当你需要清醒的时候喝美式，需要安慰的时候喝甜的，找到答案以后又回到了原点。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài gōngsī lóu xià de kāfēi diàn gōngzuò le bàn nián, zhùyì dào yí gè yǒuqù de guīlǜ. Yǒu yí gè chuān xīzhuāng de nánrén měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn shíwǔ fēn zhǔnshí lái, zǒng shì diǎn tóng yì bēi kāfēi: měishì, zhōng bēi, bù jiā táng. Dànshì zuìjìn sān gè xīngqī tā de dìngdān tūrán biàn le — xiān shì huàn chéng le ná tiě, ránhòu gǎi chéng le rè qiǎokèlì, zuìhòu biàn chéng le yì bēi wēnrè de niúnǎi. Wǒ rěn bu zhù guānchá tā de biànhuà: sān zhōu qián tā lái de shíhou biǎoqíng hěn jǐnzhāng, shǒujī yìzhí xiǎng gè bù tíng. Liǎng zhōu qián tā huàn le yì shēn xiūxián zhuāng, liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng fàngsōng le yìxiē. Shàng zhōu tā lái de shíhou méiyǒu dài gōngwén bāo, zuò zài chuāng biān mànmàn de hē, kàn le bàn xiǎoshí chuāng wài de fēngjǐng. Jīntiān tā yòu lái le, diǎn le yì bēi měishì — hé zuìchū yì mó yíyàng. Dàn tā de yǎnshén bùtóng le, bǐ yǐqián píngjìng hěn duō. Wǒ cāi tā jīnglì le shénme zhòngyào de shìqing. Yí gè rén de kāfēi dìngdān yěxǔ bǐ tā zìjǐ de huà gèng chéngshí — dāng nǐ xūyào qīngxǐng de shíhou hē měishì, xūyào ānwèi de shíhou hē tián de, zhǎo dào dá'àn yǐhòu yòu huí dào le yuándiǎn.
text_en: I've worked at the coffee shop below my company's building for half a year and noticed an interesting pattern. A man in a suit comes at exactly 8:15 every morning and always orders the same thing: Americano, medium, no sugar. But the past three weeks his order suddenly changed — first to a latte, then hot chocolate, finally a cup of warm milk. I couldn't help observing his changes: three weeks ago he looked tense, his phone ringing nonstop. Two weeks ago he came in casual clothes, his expression more relaxed. Last week he came without a briefcase, sat by the window and slowly drank, watching the scenery outside for half an hour. Today he came again and ordered an Americano — exactly the same as the beginning. But his eyes were different, much calmer than before. I suspect he went through something important. A person's coffee order might be more honest than their own words — when you need alertness you drink an Americano, when you need comfort you drink something sweet, and after finding your answer you return to where you started.

questions.q_en: How did the man's appearance change over three weeks?

title: The Bench Between Two Buildings
text_zh: 小区里有一张长椅放在两栋楼之间的空地上。不知道是谁放的，也不知道放了多少年。它是木头做的，漆已经掉了大半，但坐起来还很结实。早上，退休的老人坐在上面晒太阳，有的闭着眼睛，有的看着不远处的孩子在玩。中午，外卖骑手会在那里坐一会儿，吃完一个面包就骑车走了。下午，年轻的妈妈推着婴儿车经过会停下来歇一歇，给孩子喂一口水。傍晚，下班回来的人偶尔会坐在那里接完一个电话再上楼。到了晚上，长椅终于安静了下来，只有路灯照着它。这张长椅不属于任何人，所以它属于所有人。它什么也不做，只是提供一个可以坐下来的地方。但有时候，一个可以坐下来的地方就是一个人最需要的东西。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yì zhāng chángyǐ fàng zài liǎng dòng lóu zhī jiān de kòngdì shàng. Bù zhīdào shì shéi fàng de, yě bù zhīdào fàng le duōshao nián. Tā shì mùtou zuò de, qī yǐjīng diào le dà bàn, dàn zuò qǐlái hái hěn jiēshi. Zǎoshang, tuìxiū de lǎorén zuò zài shàngmiàn shài tàiyáng, yǒu de bì zhe yǎnjing, yǒu de kàn zhe bù yuǎn chù de háizi zài wán. Zhōngwǔ, wàimài qíshǒu huì zài nàlǐ zuò yíhuìr, chī wán yí gè miànbāo jiù qí chē zǒu le. Xiàwǔ, niánqīng de māma tuī zhe yīng'ér chē jīngguò huì tíng xiàlái xiē yì xiē, gěi háizi wèi yì kǒu shuǐ. Bàngwǎn, xiàbān huílái de rén ǒu'ěr huì zuò zài nàlǐ jiē wán yí gè diànhuà zài shàng lóu. Dào le wǎnshang, chángyǐ zhōngyú ānjìng le xiàlái, zhǐ yǒu lùdēng zhào zhe tā. Zhè zhāng chángyǐ bù shǔyú rènhé rén, suǒyǐ tā shǔyú suǒyǒu rén. Tā shénme yě bú zuò, zhǐ shì tígōng yí gè kěyǐ zuò xiàlái de dìfāng. Dàn yǒu shíhou, yí gè kěyǐ zuò xiàlái de dìfāng jiù shì yí gè rén zuì xūyào de dōngxi.
text_en: In our compound there's a bench placed on the open ground between two buildings. Nobody knows who put it there or how many years ago. It's made of wood, most of the paint has flaked off, but it's still sturdy to sit on. In the morning, retired elderly sit on it sunbathing, some with eyes closed, some watching children play nearby. At noon, food delivery riders sit there briefly, eat a bread roll and ride off. In the afternoon, young mothers pushing strollers stop to rest, giving their children a sip of water. In the evening, people coming home from work occasionally sit to finish a phone call before going upstairs. At night, the bench finally falls quiet, with only a streetlight shining on it. This bench belongs to no one, so it belongs to everyone. It does nothing, just provides a place to sit down. But sometimes a place to sit down is exactly what a person needs most.

questions.q_zh: 中午谁会用这张长椅？
questions.q_en: Who uses the bench at noon?
questions.explanation: At noon, food delivery riders claim the bench -- eating a quick bread roll before riding off again.

questions.q_zh: 为什么这段话值得仔细读第二遍？
questions.q_en: Why is this passage worth reading a second time?

options.text: 能发现更多隐含的意义
options.pinyin: néng fāxiàn gèng duō yǐnhán de yìyì
options.text_en: you can discover more hidden meanings

options.text: 内容太复杂，一遍读不懂
options.pinyin: nèiróng tài fùzá, yí biàn dú bù dǒng
options.text_en: it's too complex to understand in one reading

options.pinyin: bù zhíde dú dì èr biàn

questions.explanation: Layered details reward re-reading -- subtext emerges that a first pass may miss.

questions.q_zh: 文中哪个细节最能体现作者的态度？
questions.q_en: Which detail best reflects the author's attitude?
questions.explanation: Noticing what others ignore -- that careful attention is itself the author's stance.

title: The Office Plant Nobody Waters
text_zh: 办公室角落有一盆绿萝，不知道谁买来的，也不知道放了多久。神奇的是，它一直活着。我仔细观察了一个月，终于发现了秘密：不是没有人浇水，而是每个人都以为只有自己在浇。小王周一浇，因为他觉得「周末两天没人管它」。小李周三浇，因为那天她心情好。老张每天下班前都倒一点剩茶水进去。实习生小陈甚至买了植物营养液，每周偷偷滴几滴。结果这盆可怜的绿萝被浇了太多水，叶子开始发黄。我把这个发现告诉了大家，所有人都惊讶地说「原来你也在浇？」
text_pinyin: Bàngōngshì jiǎoluò yǒu yì pén lǜluó, bù zhīdào shéi mǎi lái de, yě bù zhīdào fàng le duō jiǔ. Shénqí de shì, tā yìzhí huó zhe. Wǒ zǐxì guānchá le yí gè yuè, zhōngyú fāxiàn le mìmì: bú shì méiyǒu rén jiāo shuǐ, ér shì měi gè rén dōu yǐwéi zhǐ yǒu zìjǐ zài jiāo. Xiǎo Wáng zhōuyī jiāo, yīnwèi tā juéde「zhōumò liǎng tiān méi rén guǎn tā」. Xiǎo Lǐ zhōusān jiāo, yīnwèi nà tiān tā xīnqíng hǎo. Lǎo Zhāng měi tiān xiàbān qián dōu dào yìdiǎn shèng cháishuǐ jìnqù. Shíxíshēng Xiǎo Chén shènzhì mǎi le zhíwù yíngyǎng yè, měi zhōu tōutōu dī jǐ dī. Jiéguǒ zhè pén kělián de lǜluó bèi jiāo le tài duō shuǐ, yèzi kāishǐ fā huáng. Wǒ bǎ zhège fāxiàn gàosu le dàjiā, suǒyǒu rén dōu jīngyà de shuō「yuánlái nǐ yě zài jiāo?」Hòulái wǒmen jiàn le yí gè xiǎo qún, guīdìng zhǐ yǒu zhōuyī hé zhōusì jiāo shuǐ, měi cì yóu yí gè rén fùzé. Xiànzài lǜluó zhǎng de bǐ yǐqián hǎo duō le. Yǒu shíhou tài duō de guānxīn hé méiyǒu guānxīn yíyàng wēixiǎn — guānjiàn bú shì fùchū duōshao, ér shì yǒu méiyǒu gōutōng.
text_en: In the office corner there's a pothos plant — nobody knows who bought it or how long it's been there. Miraculously, it's still alive. I observed carefully for a month and finally discovered the secret: it's not that nobody waters it, but that everyone thinks only they are watering it. Xiao Wang waters it Monday because 'nobody takes care of it over the weekend.' Xiao Li waters it Wednesday because she's in a good mood that day. Old Zhang pours in leftover tea before leaving work every day. Even the intern Xiao Chen bought plant nutrient solution and secretly adds drops each week. As a result the poor pothos was overwatered and its leaves started yellowing. When I told everyone about my discovery, they all said in surprise 'you water it too?'

questions.q_zh: 绿萝的叶子为什么发黄？
questions.q_en: Why did the pothos leaves turn yellow?

options.pinyin: bèi jiāo le tài duō shuǐ

questions.explanation: The passage explains: as a result the poor pothos was overwatered.

questions.q_zh: 他们后来怎么解决了这个问题？
questions.q_en: How did they solve the problem?

options.pinyin: huàn le yì pén jiǎ huā
options.text_en: Replaced it with a fake plant

options.text: 建了群，规定浇水的时间和人
options.pinyin: jiàn le qún, guīdìng jiāo shuǐ de shíjiān hé rén
options.text_en: Created a group chat with a watering schedule

questions.explanation: The passage describes: observed carefully for a month and finally.

questions.q_zh: 作者从这件事学到了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author learn from this?

options.text: 关键不是付出多少而是有没有沟通
options.pinyin: guānjiàn bú shì fùchū duōshao ér shì yǒu méiyǒu gōutōng
options.text_en: What matters isn't how much you give but whether you communicate

questions.explanation: When the author pointed out that everyone was overwatering the office plant, it revealed that the real issue wasn't effort but lack of coordination — communication matters more than individual care.

title: The Name I Never Use
text_zh: 我有一个英文名叫David，是上初中的时候英语老师给取的。她给全班每个人都取了英文名，男生不是David就是Tom，女生不是Lucy就是Amy。我用了三年以后就再也没有用过。上大学的时候有外教问我英文名是什么，我说了David，他点点头但看起来并没有觉得这个名字跟我有什么关系。后来去国外出差，外国同事叫我David的时候，我总是要停顿一秒才反应过来——因为那个名字在我心里不连接任何记忆或者情感。我的中文名是爸爸花了一个月才决定的，每个字都有特别的含义。它跟着我经历了所有重要的时刻——上学、毕业、工作、恋爱。一个名字不仅仅是一个符号，它是一个人身份的入口。当别人叫我中文名的时候，我知道他们叫的是真正的我。David只是一件我偶尔穿上的外套，脱下来以后我还是原来的自己。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yí gè yīngwén míng jiào David, shì shàng chūzhōng de shíhou yīngyǔ lǎoshī gěi qǔ de. Tā gěi quán bān měi gè rén dōu qǔ le yīngwén míng, nánshēng bú shì David jiù shì Tom, nǚshēng bú shì Lucy jiù shì Amy. Wǒ yòng le sān nián yǐhòu jiù zài yě méiyǒu yòng guò. Shàng dàxué de shíhou yǒu wàijiào wèn wǒ yīngwén míng shì shénme, wǒ shuō le David, tā diǎndiǎn tóu dàn kàn qǐlái bìng méiyǒu juéde zhège míngzi gēn wǒ yǒu shénme guānxi. Hòulái qù guówài chūchāi, wàiguó tóngshì jiào wǒ David de shíhou, wǒ zǒng shì yào tíngdùn yì miǎo cái fǎnyìng guòlái — yīnwèi nàge míngzi zài wǒ xīn lǐ bù liánjiē rènhé jìyì huòzhě qínggǎn. Wǒ de zhōngwén míng shì bàba huā le yí gè yuè cái juédìng de, měi gè zì dōu yǒu tèbié de hányì. Tā gēn zhe wǒ jīnglì le suǒyǒu zhòngyào de shíkè — shàngxué, bìyè, gōngzuò, liàn'ài. Yí gè míngzi bù jǐnjǐn shì yí gè fúhào, tā shì yí gè rén shēnfèn de rùkǒu. Dāng biérén jiào wǒ zhōngwén míng de shíhou, wǒ zhīdào tāmen jiào de shì zhēnzhèng de wǒ. David zhǐ shì yí jiàn wǒ ǒu'ěr chuān shàng de wàitào, tuō xiàlái yǐhòu wǒ háishi yuánlái de zìjǐ.
text_en: I have an English name, David, given to me by my English teacher in middle school. She gave everyone in the class English names — boys were either David or Tom, girls either Lucy or Amy. I used it for three years and never again after that. In college when a foreign teacher asked my English name, I said David; he nodded but didn't seem to feel the name had anything to do with me. Later on business trips abroad, when foreign colleagues called me David I always needed a one-second pause to react — because that name connects to no memories or emotions in my heart. My Chinese name took my father a month to decide, every character carrying special meaning. It's been with me through every important moment — school, graduation, work, love. A name isn't just a symbol; it's the entrance to a person's identity. When someone calls my Chinese name, I know they're calling the real me. David is just a coat I occasionally put on; after taking it off, I'm still my original self.

questions.q_zh: 英文名是谁取的？
questions.q_en: Who gave the English name?
questions.explanation: The passage identifies: have an English name.

questions.q_zh: 外国同事叫David的时候作者有什么反应？
questions.q_en: How does the author react when foreign colleagues call them David?

options.text: 要停顿一秒才反应过来
options.pinyin: yào tíngdùn yì miǎo cái fǎnyìng guòlái
options.text_en: Needs a one-second pause to react

questions.explanation: When foreign colleagues say 'David,' the author needs a one-second pause to realize they mean him.

questions.q_zh: 如果另一个人来到同样的场景，会有同样的感受吗？
questions.q_en: Would another person in the same setting have the same feelings?

options.text: 不一定，取决于个人经历
options.pinyin: bù yídìng, qǔjué yú gèrén jīnglì
options.text_en: not necessarily, it depends on personal experience

questions.explanation: Subjective responses depend on individual history -- same scene, different emotional resonance.

title: The Last Bus Home
text_zh: 晚上十点半的末班公交车和白天的完全不同。白天挤满了人，大家都赶时间，推来推去，很吵。末班车上只有几个人，每个人之间隔着好几个座位。车里的灯光有点暗，窗外的城市也开始安静下来。司机开得比白天慢，好像也不那么赶了。乘客们各自做着自己的事：有一个穿工装的年轻人靠在窗户上睡着了，手里还握着安全帽。有一个女孩戴着耳机在看手机，屏幕的光照在她的脸上。后排坐着一对老夫妻，老太太把头靠在老先生的肩膀上，两个人都闭着眼睛。我坐在中间，听着公交车晃动的声音和偶尔的刹车声。到站了，报站的声音在安静的车厢里格外清晰。我下车的时候回头看了一眼——车窗里那几个亮着的身影，很快就被夜色吞没了。末班车就像城市的一声叹息：一天就要结束了，大家都要回家了。
text_pinyin: Wǎnshang shí diǎn bàn de mòbān gōngjiāo chē hé báitiān de wánquán bùtóng. Báitiān jǐ mǎn le rén, dàjiā dōu gǎn shíjiān, tuī lái tuī qù, hěn chǎo. Mòbān chē shàng zhǐ yǒu jǐ gè rén, měi gè rén zhī jiān gé zhe hǎo jǐ gè zuòwèi. Chē lǐ de dēngguāng yǒudiǎn àn, chuāng wài de chéngshì yě kāishǐ ānjìng xiàlái. Sījī kāi de bǐ báitiān màn, hǎoxiàng yě bú nàme gǎn le. Chéngkèmen gèzì zuò zhe zìjǐ de shì: yǒu yí gè chuān gōngzhuāng de niánqīngrén kào zài chuānghu shàng shuì zháo le, shǒu lǐ hái wò zhe ānquán mào. Yǒu yí gè nǚhái dài zhe ěrjī zài kàn shǒujī, píngmù de guāng zhào zài tā de liǎn shàng. Hòu pái zuò zhe yí duì lǎo fūqī, lǎo tàitai bǎ tóu kào zài lǎo xiānsheng de jiānbǎng shàng, liǎng gè rén dōu bì zhe yǎnjing. Wǒ zuò zài zhōngjiān, tīng zhe gōngjiāo chē huàngdòng de shēngyīn hé ǒu'ěr de shāchē shēng. Dào zhàn le, bào zhàn de shēngyīn zài ānjìng de chēxiāng lǐ géwài qīngxī. Wǒ xià chē de shíhou huítóu kàn le yì yǎn — chē chuāng lǐ nà jǐ gè liàng zhe de shēnyǐng, hěn kuài jiù bèi yèsè tūnmò le. Mòbān chē jiù xiàng chéngshì de yì shēng tànxī: yì tiān jiù yào jiéshù le, dàjiā dōu yào huí jiā le.
text_en: The last bus at 10:30 PM is completely different from the daytime one. During the day it's packed, everyone rushing, pushing, noisy. The last bus has only a few people, several empty seats between each person. The interior lights are slightly dim, the city outside growing quiet too. The driver goes slower than in daytime, as if not in a rush anymore either. The passengers each do their own thing: a young man in work clothes has fallen asleep against the window, still holding his hard hat. A girl wearing earphones watches her phone, the screen lighting her face. An elderly couple sits in the back row, the wife resting her head on her husband's shoulder, both with eyes closed. I sit in the middle, listening to the bus's swaying sounds and occasional braking. A stop arrives, the announcement especially clear in the quiet bus. Getting off, I look back — those few lit silhouettes in the bus windows are quickly swallowed by the night. The last bus is like the city's sigh: the day is ending, everyone is going home.

questions.q_zh: 末班车和白天的公交车有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is the last bus different from daytime buses?
questions.explanation: Daytime buses are packed and noisy; the last bus has few riders and near-total quiet.

questions.q_zh: 根据这段话，人物接下来最可能做什么？
questions.q_en: Based on this passage, what would the character most likely do next?

options.pinyin: kāishǐ zuò wánquán bùtóng de shì

questions.explanation: The character's contentment and reflective stance suggest staying in this mode.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中的'重复'有什么深意？
questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the 'repetition' in this passage carry?
questions.explanation: The repeated routine signals comfort and constancy -- anchoring, not numbing.

title: Why We Keep Old Phone Numbers
title_zh: 为什么我们留着旧号码
text_zh: 我的手机通讯录里有三百多个联系人。我仔细看了一遍，发现至少有一半我已经不联系了：大学同学、以前的同事、某次旅行认识的人、帮我修过电脑的人。有些人我连名字都想不起来了，只剩下一个号码和一个模糊的备注。但我从来没有删除过任何一个。为什么呢？我想了想，也许是因为每一个号码都代表我人生里的一个瞬间——当我存下这个号码的时候，我们之间至少有一些联系，那个联系在那个时刻是真实的。删除一个号码就像是否认那个瞬间存在过。还有一种可能：我们留着旧号码是为了给自己一种安全感——万一有一天我们需要联系他们呢？虽然那一天大概永远不会来，但「可以联系」这件事本身就是一种安慰。通讯录就像一个人的社交化石层，每一层都记录了某个时期的生活。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de shǒujī tōngxùnlù lǐ yǒu sān bǎi duō gè liánxì rén. Wǒ zǐxì kàn le yí biàn, fāxiàn zhìshǎo yǒu yí bàn wǒ yǐjīng bù liánxì le: dàxué tóngxué, yǐqián de tóngshì, mǒu cì lǚxíng rènshi de rén, bāng wǒ xiū guò diànnǎo de rén. Yǒuxiē rén wǒ lián míngzi dōu xiǎng bù qǐlái le, zhǐ shèng xià yí gè hàomǎ hé yí gè móhu de bèizhù. Dàn wǒ cónglái méiyǒu shānchú guò rènhé yí gè. Wèi shénme ne? Wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng, yěxǔ shì yīnwèi měi yí gè hàomǎ dōu dàibiǎo wǒ rénshēng lǐ de yí gè shùnjiān — dāng wǒ cún xià zhège hàomǎ de shíhou, wǒmen zhī jiān zhìshǎo yǒu yìxiē liánxì, nàge liánxì zài nàge shíkè shì zhēnshí de. Shānchú yí gè hàomǎ jiù xiàng shì fǒurèn nàge shùnjiān cúnzài guò. Hái yǒu yì zhǒng kěnéng: wǒmen liú zhe jiù hàomǎ shì wèi le gěi zìjǐ yì zhǒng ānquángǎn — wànyī yǒu yì tiān wǒmen xūyào liánxì tāmen ne? Suīrán nà yì tiān dàgài yǒngyuǎn bú huì lái, dàn「kěyǐ liánxì」zhè jiàn shì běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng ānwèi. Tōngxùnlù jiù xiàng yí gè rén de shèjiāo huàshí céng, měi yì céng dōu jìlù le mǒu gè shíqī de shēnghuó.
text_en: My phone contacts list has over three hundred people. I looked through it carefully and found at least half I no longer contact: college classmates, former colleagues, someone from a trip, someone who fixed my computer once. Some I can't even remember by name — just a number and a vague note remain. But I've never deleted any of them. Why? I thought about it — maybe because each number represents a moment in my life. When I saved that number, there was at least some connection between us, and that connection was real in that moment. Deleting a number feels like denying that moment existed. There's another possibility: we keep old numbers for a sense of security — what if one day we need to reach them? Though that day will probably never come, that 'I could contact them' is itself a comfort. The contacts list is like a person's social fossil layer, each stratum recording a period of life.

questions.q_zh: 关系中的小细节说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What do the small details in the relationship suggest?

options.text_en: they don't really like each other

questions.explanation: The tiny, unspoken gestures between them signal the depth of long familiarity.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么不删除旧号码？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the author delete old numbers?

options.text: 每个号码代表人生的一个瞬间
options.pinyin: měi gè hàomǎ dàibiǎo rénshēng de yí gè shùnjiān
options.text_en: Each number represents a moment in life

questions.explanation: Each saved number marks a moment in the author's life -- deleting one would erase that trace.

questions.q_zh: 这些人之间最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What matters most between these people?
questions.explanation: The unspoken attentiveness between them signals deep mutual understanding.

title: The Breakfast Stall Couple
text_zh: 学校门口有一对卖煎饼的夫妻。每天早上五点半他们就到了，推着那辆改装过的三轮车，开始支起摊位。丈夫负责做煎饼——摊面糊、打鸡蛋、撒葱花、刷酱、加脆饼、折叠，整个过程不到两分钟，动作行云流水。妻子负责收钱和打包，顺便和客人聊几句。他们之间几乎不用说话就能配合：妻子收到扫码付款就轻轻敲一下台面，丈夫就知道开始做下一个了。忙的时候他们能同时应对十几个客人，一个人做、一个人卖、一个人收拾，两个人做三个人的工作。最让我感动的是有一次下大雨，客人比平时少了一半，妻子趁着空闲给丈夫递了一杯热水。他接过来喝了一口，什么也没说，继续摊下一个煎饼。那个动作里有一种无声的关心，比任何甜言蜜语都真实。
text_pinyin: Xuéxiào ménkǒu yǒu yí duì mài jiānbing de fūqī. Měi tiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn bàn tāmen jiù dào le, tuī zhe nà liàng gǎizhuāng guò de sānlún chē, kāishǐ zhī qǐ tānwèi. Zhàngfu fùzé zuò jiānbing — tān miànhú, dǎ jīdàn, sā cōnghuā, shuā jiàng, jiā cuìbǐng, zhédié, zhěnggè guòchéng bú dào liǎng fēnzhōng, dòngzuò xíngyúnliúshuǐ. Qīzi fùzé shōu qián hé dǎbāo, shùnbiàn hé kèrén liáo jǐ jù. Tāmen zhī jiān jīhū bú yòng shuōhuà jiù néng pèihé: qīzi shōu dào sǎo mǎ fùkuǎn jiù qīngqīng qiāo yíxià táimiàn, zhàngfu jiù zhīdào kāishǐ zuò xià yí gè le. Máng de shíhou tāmen néng tóngshí yìngduì shí jǐ gè kèrén, yí gè rén zuò, yí gè rén mài, yí gè rén shōushi, liǎng gè rén zuò sān gè rén de gōngzuò. Zuì ràng wǒ gǎndòng de shì yǒu yí cì xià dà yǔ, kèrén bǐ píngshí shǎo le yí bàn, qīzi chèn zhe kòngxián gěi zhàngfu dì le yì bēi rè shuǐ. Tā jiē guòlái hē le yì kǒu, shénme yě méi shuō, jìxù tān xià yí gè jiānbing. Nàge dòngzuò lǐ yǒu yì zhǒng wú shēng de guānxīn, bǐ rènhé tiányánmìyǔ dōu zhēnshí.
text_en: At the school entrance there's a couple selling jianbing. Every morning at 5:30 they arrive, pushing their converted three-wheeler, setting up the stall. The husband makes the jianbing — spreading batter, cracking an egg, scattering scallions, brushing sauce, adding a crispy cracker, folding — the whole process under two minutes, movements flowing like water. The wife handles money and packaging, chatting briefly with customers. They coordinate almost without speaking: when the wife receives a mobile payment she lightly taps the counter, and the husband knows to start the next one. When busy they handle over a dozen customers simultaneously — one making, one selling, one cleaning up — two people doing three people's work. What moved me most was during a heavy rain when customers were half the usual number. The wife took advantage of the lull to hand her husband a cup of hot water. He took it, drank a sip, said nothing, and continued making the next jianbing. In that gesture was a silent caring more real than any sweet words.

questions.q_zh: 丈夫做一个煎饼需要多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long does it take the husband to make one jianbing?
questions.explanation: The husband's speed is emphasized: one jianbing in under two minutes, each movement precise and fluid.

questions.q_zh: 妻子怎么告诉丈夫做下一个？
questions.q_en: How does the wife signal the husband to make the next one?
questions.explanation: The passage describes: and the husband knows to start the next one.

questions.q_zh: 下大雨的时候妻子做了什么让作者感动？
questions.q_en: What did the wife do during the heavy rain that moved the author?

options.pinyin: gěi zhàngfu dì le yì bēi rè shuǐ
options.text_en: Handed her husband a cup of hot water

questions.explanation: During a pause in the rain, the wife quietly handed her husband a cup of hot water — a small, caring gesture amid the chaos of running their street stall.

title: The Laundry on the Line
text_zh: 从我家阳台看出去，能看到对面好几栋楼的阳台。每一个阳台上都挂着晾晒的衣服，像是一面面彩色的旗帜。仔细看就能猜到每家的生活：有小孩子衣服的那家，一定有年幼的孩子，衣服小小的，颜色都很鲜艳。旁边那家挂着很多衬衫和西裤，看起来是一个上班族的家。再旁边那家的衣服都很大，颜色深，应该是一个单身的大个子男人。最上面一层的阳台有很多床单和被套在随风飘动——今天天气好，有人在晒被子。有一个阳台最有意思：上面同时挂着老年人的棉衣和婴儿的小袜子，中间是几件年轻女性的裙子。三代同堂的家庭，一根晾衣绳就能看出来。风吹过来的时候，所有的衣服一起摆动，大的小的、厚的薄的、鲜艳的朴素的，好像在一起跳一支慢慢的舞。这是城市里最朴实的风景——每个人的生活被晒在阳光下，没有任何隐藏。
text_pinyin: Cóng wǒ jiā yángtái kàn chūqù, néng kàn dào duìmiàn hǎo jǐ dòng lóu de yángtái. Měi yí gè yángtái shàng dōu guà zhe liàngshài de yīfu, xiàng shì yí miànmiàn cǎisè de qízhì. Zǐxì kàn jiù néng cāi dào měi jiā de shēnghuó: yǒu xiǎo háizi yīfu de nà jiā, yídìng yǒu niányòu de háizi, yīfu xiǎoxiǎo de, yánsè dōu hěn xiānyàn. Pángbiān nà jiā guà zhe hěn duō chènshān hé xīkù, kàn qǐlái shì yí gè shàngbānzú de jiā. Zài pángbiān nà jiā de yīfu dōu hěn dà, yánsè shēn, yīnggāi shì yí gè dānshēn de dà gèzi nánrén. Zuì shàngmiàn yì céng de yángtái yǒu hěn duō chuángdān hé bèitào zài suí fēng piāodòng — jīntiān tiānqì hǎo, yǒu rén zài shài bèizi. Yǒu yí gè yángtái zuì yǒu yìsi: shàngmiàn tóngshí guà zhe lǎoniánrén de miányī hé yīng'ér de xiǎo wàzi, zhōngjiān shì jǐ jiàn niánqīng nǚxìng de qúnzi. Sān dài tóngtáng de jiātíng, yì gēn liàngyī shéng jiù néng kàn chūlái. Fēng chuī guòlái de shíhou, suǒyǒu de yīfu yìqǐ bǎidòng, dà de xiǎo de, hòu de báo de, xiānyàn de pǔsù de, hǎoxiàng zài yìqǐ tiào yì zhī mànmàn de wǔ. Zhè shì chéngshì lǐ zuì pǔshí de fēngjǐng — měi gè rén de shēnghuó bèi shài zài yángguāng xià, méiyǒu rènhé yǐncáng.
text_en: Looking out from my balcony I can see the balconies of several buildings across the way. Each one has laundry hanging to dry, like colorful flags. Looking carefully you can guess each family's life: the one with children's clothes must have young kids — tiny garments in bright colors. The one next to it has many dress shirts and trousers, probably an office worker's home. The next one over has clothes that are all large and dark-colored — probably a single tall man. The top-floor balcony has bed sheets and duvet covers billowing in the wind — the weather's nice today, someone's sunning their quilts. The most interesting balcony has elderly cotton coats and baby socks hanging simultaneously, with young women's dresses in between. Three generations under one roof, you can tell from a single clothesline. When the wind blows, all the clothes sway together — large and small, thick and thin, bright and plain, as if dancing a slow dance together. This is the city's most unpretentious scenery — everyone's life hung out in the sunlight, nothing hidden.

questions.q_zh: 怎么猜出有小孩子的家庭？
questions.q_en: How can you tell which home has small children?

options.text: 有小小的、颜色鲜艳的衣服
options.pinyin: yǒu xiǎoxiǎo de, yánsè xiānyàn de yīfu
néng tīng dào xiǎohái de shēngyīn

questions.explanation: The author reads household composition from laundry: small, brightly colored clothes signal young children.

questions.q_zh: 最有意思的阳台有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What's special about the most interesting balcony?

options.text: 同时有老人、婴儿和年轻女性的衣服
options.pinyin: tóngshí yǒu lǎorén, yīng'ér hé niánqīng nǚxìng de yīfu
options.text_en: Clothes for elderly, baby, and young woman simultaneously

questions.explanation: That balcony displays old people's clothes, baby clothes, and young women's dresses all on one line — three generations living together, visible from the laundry alone.

questions.q_zh: 这个选择反映了什么？
questions.q_en: What does this choice reflect?
questions.explanation: The deliberate choice reveals underlying priorities and what the person finds worthwhile.

title: The Bookshop's Hidden Shelf
text_zh: 我家附近有一家独立书店，不大，但书的选择很好。我去了好几次以后才发现一个秘密：在最里面的角落有一个不起眼的书架，上面放的书没有分类标签，也没有价格标签。我问老板这些是什么书。他笑了笑说「这是交换书架——你可以带一本自己读完的书来，换走一本你想读的」。我仔细看了看，上面有各种各样的书：小说、散文、漫画、甚至还有一本旧的英语字典。有些书里面夹着小纸条，上面写着读者的感想。有一本小说的第一页写着「这本书陪我度过了一段很难的日子，希望它也能陪你」。另一本诗集里夹着一张书签，上面画了一朵小花。这个书架就像一个安静的对话空间——你不知道谁留下了这本书，但通过一本书，两个陌生人之间建立了一种看不见的联系。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yì jiā dúlì shūdiàn, bú dà, dàn shū de xuǎnzé hěn hǎo. Wǒ qù le hǎo jǐ cì yǐhòu cái fāxiàn yí gè mìmì: zài zuì lǐmiàn de jiǎoluò yǒu yí gè bù qǐyǎn de shūjià, shàngmiàn fàng de shū méiyǒu fēnlèi biāoqiān, yě méiyǒu jiàgé biāoqiān. Wǒ wèn lǎobǎn zhèxiē shì shénme shū. Tā xiào le xiào shuō「zhè shì jiāohuàn shūjià — nǐ kěyǐ dài yì běn zìjǐ dú wán de shū lái, huàn zǒu yì běn nǐ xiǎng dú de」. Wǒ zǐxì kàn le kàn, shàngmiàn yǒu gè zhǒng gè yàng de shū: xiǎoshuō, sǎnwén, mànhuà, shènzhì hái yǒu yì běn jiù de yīngyǔ zìdiǎn. Yǒuxiē shū lǐmiàn jiā zhe xiǎo zhǐtiáo, shàngmiàn xiě zhe dúzhě de gǎnxiǎng. Yǒu yì běn xiǎoshuō de dì yī yè xiě zhe「zhè běn shū péi wǒ dùguò le yí duàn hěn nán de rìzi, xīwàng tā yě néng péi nǐ」. Lìng yì běn shījí lǐ jiā zhe yì zhāng shūqiān, shàngmiàn huà le yì duǒ xiǎo huā. Zhège shūjià jiù xiàng yí gè ānjìng de duìhuà kōngjiān — nǐ bù zhīdào shéi liú xià le zhè běn shū, dàn tōngguò yì běn shū, liǎng gè mòshēngrén zhī jiān jiànlì le yì zhǒng kàn bú jiàn de liánxì.
text_en: Near my home there's an independent bookshop — small but with good selection. It was only after visiting several times that I discovered a secret: in the furthest corner is an unremarkable shelf with books that have no category labels or price tags. I asked the owner what these books were. He smiled and said 'this is the exchange shelf — you can bring a book you've finished and take one you want to read.' I looked carefully: there were all kinds of books — novels, essays, comics, even an old English dictionary. Some books had little notes tucked inside with readers' thoughts. On the first page of one novel someone had written 'this book got me through a really hard time, I hope it can accompany you too.' A poetry collection had a bookmark with a small flower drawn on it. This shelf is like a quiet conversation space — you don't know who left the book, but through it two strangers form an invisible connection.

questions.q_zh: 这个书架的书有什么特别？
questions.q_en: What's special about the books on this shelf?
questions.explanation: The books have no category labels or price tags, which the author only noticed after multiple visits — an unconventional approach that lets readers browse without commercial pressure.

questions.q_zh: 小说第一页写的是什么？
questions.q_en: What was written on the first page of the novel?

options.text: 这本书陪我度过了很难的日子
options.pinyin: zhè běn shū péi wǒ dùguò le hěn nán de rìzi
options.text_en: This book got me through hard times

options.pinyin: bú yào nòng zāng zhè běn shū
zhè běn shū shì jiǎ de

questions.explanation: A previous reader had handwritten on the first page: 'This book got me through hard times' — a personal inscription left behind for the next reader.

questions.q_zh: 这段话会让不同的人有不同的理解吗？
questions.q_en: Would different people interpret this passage differently?

options.text: 是的，因为涉及个人经历
options.pinyin: shì de, yīnwèi shèjí gèrén jīnglì
options.text_en: yes, because it involves personal experience

options.pinyin: zhǐ yǒu zhuānjiā cái néng lǐjiě

questions.explanation: Individual background shapes interpretation -- the same details carry different weight for each reader.

title: The Sound of the City at Dawn
text_zh: 有一次失眠，我在凌晨四点半的时候打开了窗户。那是我第一次听到城市从沉睡中醒来的过程。最先出现的声音是环卫工人的扫帚声——沙沙沙，节奏很稳定，从远到近，从近到远。然后是早班公交车发动的声音——低沉的轰鸣，在空旷的街道上格外清楚。接着是早起的鸟叫，叽叽喳喳的，好像在互相通报「天快亮了」。大约五点钟的时候，远处传来第一声铁门拉开的声音——某个早餐店开始营业了。五点半，零星的脚步声出现在人行道上。六点，汽车的发动机声逐渐多了起来。六点半，整个城市已经完全醒了——喇叭声、说话声、施工声混在一起，变成了那种我们每天都习以为常的背景噪音。但在那之前的两个小时里，城市是温柔的。它不是突然醒来的，而是一层一层慢慢地亮起来的。
text_pinyin: Yǒu yí cì shīmián, wǒ zài língchén sì diǎn bàn de shíhou dǎkāi le chuānghu. Nà shì wǒ dì yī cì tīng dào chéngshì cóng chénshuì zhōng xǐng lái de guòchéng. Zuì xiān chūxiàn de shēngyīn shì huánwèi gōngrén de sàozhou shēng — shāshāshā, jiézòu hěn wěndìng, cóng yuǎn dào jìn, cóng jìn dào yuǎn. Ránhòu shì zǎo bān gōngjiāo chē fādòng de shēngyīn — dīchén de hōngmíng, zài kōngkuàng de jiēdào shàng géwài qīngchu. Jiē zhe shì zǎo qǐ de niǎo jiào, jījī zhāzhā de, hǎoxiàng zài hùxiāng tōngbào「tiān kuài liàng le」. Dàyuē wǔ diǎnzhōng de shíhou, yuǎnchù chuán lái dì yī shēng tiě mén lā kāi de shēngyīn — mǒu gè zǎocān diàn kāishǐ yíngyè le. Wǔ diǎn bàn, língxīng de jiǎobù shēng chūxiàn zài rénxíngdào shàng. Liù diǎn, qìchē de fādòngjī shēng zhújiàn duō le qǐlái. Liù diǎn bàn, zhěnggè chéngshì yǐjīng wánquán xǐng le — lǎba shēng, shuōhuà shēng, shīgōng shēng hùn zài yìqǐ, biàn chéng le nà zhǒng wǒmen měi tiān dōu xíyǐwéicháng de bèijǐng zàoyīn. Dàn zài nà zhīqián de liǎng gè xiǎoshí lǐ, chéngshì shì wēnróu de. Tā bú shì tūrán xǐng lái de, ér shì yì céng yì céng mànmàn de liàng qǐlái de.
text_en: Once during insomnia I opened my window at 4:30 AM. It was my first time hearing the city wake from sleep. The first sound was the sanitation workers' brooms — swish swish swish, steady rhythm, far to near, near to far. Then the early bus starting up — a low rumble, especially clear on the empty streets. Next came the early birds chirping, as if announcing to each other 'dawn is coming.' Around five, from afar came the first sound of a metal gate sliding open — some breakfast shop starting business. At five-thirty, scattered footsteps appeared on sidewalks. By six, engine sounds gradually multiplied. By six-thirty the whole city was fully awake — horns, voices, and construction blending into the background noise we take for granted every day. But in those two hours before, the city was gentle. It didn't wake suddenly, but lit up layer by layer, slowly.

questions.q_zh: 最先出现的声音是什么？
questions.q_en: What was the first sound?
questions.explanation: The dawn sequence begins with sanitation workers' brooms sweeping from far to near -- the city's first voice.

questions.q_zh: 大约几点钟城市完全醒了？
questions.q_en: Around what time was the city fully awake?
questions.explanation: Stated in the passage: by six-thirty the whole city was fully awake.

questions.q_zh: 这段话如何处理'孤独'这个概念？
questions.q_en: How does this passage treat the concept of 'solitude'?

options.pinyin: bǎ tā dàng zuò yì zhǒng xiǎngshòu
options.text_en: treats it as something to enjoy

questions.explanation: Solitude here is framed as chosen and pleasurable, not as isolation or loneliness.

title: The Old Bicycle
text_zh: 楼下车棚里有一辆旧自行车，已经很久没有人骑了。轮胎瘪了，车把上落了一层灰，车篮里积了一些枯叶和雨水。它被夹在两辆电动车中间，几乎看不出来。我每次经过都注意到它，因为它让我想起小时候。那时候爸爸每天骑自行车送我上学，我坐在后座上，抓着爸爸的衣服，看着两旁的树快速地往后退。冬天的时候手很冷，爸爸会把一只手从车把上松开，反过来握住我的手暖一会儿，然后再握回车把。那辆自行车后来也变旧了，最后不知道扔在了哪里。现在看到这辆被遗忘的旧自行车，我想它的主人也许也有过类似的记忆——那些在车轮上度过的日子，那种简单的、被风吹过的快乐。每一辆旧自行车都曾经载着某个人去过某个地方，都曾经是某段生活的一部分。
text_pinyin: Lóu xià chē péng lǐ yǒu yí liàng jiù zìxíngchē, yǐjīng hěn jiǔ méiyǒu rén qí le. Lúntāi biě le, chē bǎ shàng luò le yì céng huī, chē lán lǐ jī le yìxiē kū yè hé yǔshuǐ. Tā bèi jiā zài liǎng liàng diàndòng chē zhōngjiān, jīhū kàn bù chūlái. Wǒ měi cì jīngguò dōu zhùyì dào tā, yīnwèi tā ràng wǒ xiǎngqǐ xiǎo shíhou. Nà shíhou bàba měi tiān qí zìxíngchē sòng wǒ shàngxué, wǒ zuò zài hòuzuò shàng, zhuā zhe bàba de yīfu, kàn zhe liǎng páng de shù kuài sù de wǎng hòu tuì. Dōngtiān de shíhou shǒu hěn lěng, bàba huì bǎ yì zhī shǒu cóng chē bǎ shàng sōngkāi, fǎn guòlái wò zhù wǒ de shǒu nuǎn yíhuìr, ránhòu zài wò huí chē bǎ. Nà liàng zìxíngchē hòulái yě biàn jiù le, zuìhòu bù zhīdào rēng zài le nǎlǐ. Xiànzài kàn dào zhè liàng bèi yíwàng de jiù zìxíngchē, wǒ xiǎng tā de zhǔrén yěxǔ yě yǒu guò lèisì de jìyì — nàxiē zài chēlún shàng dùguò de rìzi, nà zhǒng jiǎndān de, bèi fēng chuī guò de kuàilè. Měi yí liàng jiù zìxíngchē dōu céngjīng zài zhe mǒu gè rén qù guò mǒu gè dìfāng, dōu céngjīng shì mǒu duàn shēnghuó de yí bùfen.
text_en: In the bike shed downstairs there's an old bicycle that nobody has ridden for a long time. The tires are flat, dust has settled on the handlebars, dead leaves and rainwater have collected in the basket. Sandwiched between two electric scooters, it's barely noticeable. I notice it every time I pass because it reminds me of childhood. Back then dad rode a bicycle to take me to school every day. I sat on the back seat, gripping his clothes, watching the trees rush backward on both sides. In winter my hands would get cold, and dad would release one hand from the handlebars, reach back to warm my hand in his for a moment, then grip the handlebars again. That bicycle also grew old eventually and was discarded somewhere unknown. Now seeing this forgotten old bicycle, I think its owner perhaps has similar memories too — those days spent on wheels, that simple, wind-blown happiness. Every old bicycle once carried someone somewhere, was once part of someone's life.

questions.q_zh: 旧自行车现在是什么状态？
questions.q_en: What condition is the old bicycle in?

options.pinyin: lúntāi biě le, luò le huī

questions.explanation: The bicycle sits in the stairwell with flat tires and dust -- physical signs of long disuse.

questions.q_zh: 冬天爸爸怎么帮作者暖手？
questions.q_en: How did dad warm the author's hands in winter?

options.text: 松开车把握住作者的手
options.pinyin: sōngkāi chē bǎ wò zhù zuòzhě de shǒu
options.text_en: Released the handlebars to hold the author's hand

questions.explanation: The passage describes: then grip the handlebars again.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的核心主题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core theme of this passage?
questions.explanation: The passage's details all point toward people and their surroundings existing in quiet balance.

title: The Barbershop System
text_zh: 我去的那家理发店有一套自己的系统。进门以后先在平板电脑上签到，系统会自动分配理发师。等待区有免费的茶水和杂志。叫到你以后先洗头，洗头的人和剪头发的人是不同的人。洗完头坐到镜子前面，理发师会问你想要什么样的发型，但不管你怎么说，他最后都会说「好的，我知道了，交给我」。然后他开始剪，中间不太说话，偶尔问「这里的长度可以吗？」剪完以后他会用吹风机把碎头发吹掉，让你照一下后面的镜子。最后结账的时候，收银员会问要不要办会员卡——这一步是永远不会缺少的。整个过程大概四十分钟，每一步都很流畅。我已经在这里理了三年了，虽然我每次去都说「和上次一样」，但我的理发师总能记住上次是什么样的。也许这就是一个好系统的标志：让你感觉不到系统的存在。
text_pinyin: Wǒ qù de nà jiā lǐfà diàn yǒu yí tào zìjǐ de xìtǒng. Jìn mén yǐhòu xiān zài píngbǎn diànnǎo shàng qiāndào, xìtǒng huì zìdòng fēnpèi lǐfàshī. Děngdài qū yǒu miǎnfèi de cháshuǐ hé zázhì. Jiào dào nǐ yǐhòu xiān xǐ tóu, xǐ tóu de rén hé jiǎn tóufa de rén shì bùtóng de rén. Xǐ wán tóu zuò dào jìngzi qiánmiàn, lǐfàshī huì wèn nǐ xiǎng yào shénme yàng de fǎxíng, dàn bùguǎn nǐ zěnme shuō, tā zuìhòu dōu huì shuō「hǎo de, wǒ zhīdào le, jiāo gěi wǒ」. Ránhòu tā kāishǐ jiǎn, zhōngjiān bú tài shuōhuà, ǒu'ěr wèn「zhèlǐ de chángdù kěyǐ ma?」Jiǎn wán yǐhòu tā huì yòng chuīfēngjī bǎ suì tóufa chuī diào, ràng nǐ zhào yíxià hòumiàn de jìngzi. Zuìhòu jiézhàng de shíhou, shōuyínyuán huì wèn yào bu yào bàn huìyuán kǎ — zhè yí bù shì yǒngyuǎn bú huì quēshǎo de. Zhěnggè guòchéng dàgài sìshí fēnzhōng, měi yí bù dōu hěn liúchàng. Wǒ yǐjīng zài zhèlǐ lǐ le sān nián le, suīrán wǒ měi cì qù dōu shuō「hé shàng cì yíyàng」, dàn wǒ de lǐfàshī zǒng néng jìzhù shàng cì shì shénme yàng de. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì yí gè hǎo xìtǒng de biāozhì: ràng nǐ gǎnjué bú dào xìtǒng de cúnzài.
text_en: The barbershop I go to has its own system. Upon entering you check in on a tablet, and the system automatically assigns a barber. The waiting area has free tea and magazines. When called, you get your hair washed first — the washer and the cutter are different people. After washing you sit before the mirror and the barber asks what style you want, but no matter what you say, he always ends with 'okay, I got it, leave it to me.' Then he starts cutting, doesn't talk much, occasionally asks 'is this length okay?' After cutting he blows away hair clippings and has you check the back in a mirror. Finally at checkout, the cashier asks if you want a membership card — this step is never missing. The whole process takes about forty minutes, every step smooth. I've been going here three years. Though I always say 'same as last time,' my barber always remembers what last time was. Perhaps that's the mark of a good system: you don't feel it's there.

questions.q_zh: 进门以后第一步做什么？
questions.q_en: What's the first step after entering?
questions.explanation: The first thing customers do upon entering is check in on a tablet at the door, registering their arrival digitally before sitting down.

questions.q_zh: 文中的描写方式有什么效果？

options.text_en: makes the scene vivid and three-dimensional

questions.explanation: The precise sensory detail -- sound, texture, movement -- makes the scene feel physically present.

title: The Airport at Midnight
text_zh: 红眼航班出发前的机场有一种特别的气氛。大部分商店已经关了门，只有几家便利店还亮着灯。候机厅里的椅子上躺满了等待的人——有的用外套盖着脸，有的把行李箱当枕头，有的靠在一起互相取暖。广播里偶尔响起的航班信息打破了安静，但很快又安静下来。我找了一个角落坐下，旁边是一个独自旅行的老太太。她看起来八十多岁，穿得很整齐，手边放着一个小皮箱。我问她要去哪里。她说要去看在另一个城市工作的孙子。我又问她为什么坐这么晚的飞机。她笑了笑说「便宜」。然后她从包里拿出一个保温杯喝了一口水，又拿出一个小毛毯盖在腿上。看得出来她是一个很有经验的旅行者。凌晨两点登机的时候，她站起来，稳稳地拉着行李箱走向登机口。在午夜的机场里，每一个还醒着的人都有一个值得飞往的目的地。
text_pinyin: Hóngyǎn hángbān chūfā qián de jīchǎng yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de qìfēn. Dà bùfen shāngdiàn yǐjīng guān le mén, zhǐ yǒu jǐ jiā biànlìdiàn hái liàng zhe dēng. Hòujī tīng lǐ de yǐzi shàng tǎng mǎn le děngdài de rén — yǒu de yòng wàitào gài zhe liǎn, yǒu de bǎ xínglǐ xiāng dāng zhěntou, yǒu de kào zài yìqǐ hùxiāng qǔnuǎn. Guǎngbō lǐ ǒu'ěr xiǎng qǐ de hángbān xìnxī dǎpò le ānjìng, dàn hěn kuài yòu ānjìng xiàlái. Wǒ zhǎo le yí gè jiǎoluò zuò xià, pángbiān shì yí gè dúzì lǚxíng de lǎo tàitai. Tā kàn qǐlái bāshí duō suì, chuān de hěn zhěngqí, shǒu biān fàng zhe yí gè xiǎo píxiāng. Wǒ wèn tā yào qù nǎlǐ. Tā shuō yào qù kàn zài lìng yí gè chéngshì gōngzuò de sūnzi. Wǒ yòu wèn tā wèi shénme zuò zhème wǎn de fēijī. Tā xiào le xiào shuō「piányi」. Ránhòu tā cóng bāo lǐ ná chū yí gè bǎowēn bēi hē le yì kǒu shuǐ, yòu ná chū yí gè xiǎo máotǎn gài zài tuǐ shàng. Kàn de chūlái tā shì yí gè hěn yǒu jīngyàn de lǚxíngzhě. Língchén liǎng diǎn dēngjī de shíhou, tā zhàn qǐlái, wěnwěn de lā zhe xínglǐ xiāng zǒu xiàng dēngjī kǒu. Zài wǔyè de jīchǎng lǐ, měi yí gè hái xǐng zhe de rén dōu yǒu yí gè zhíde fēi wǎng de mùdìdì.
text_en: The airport before a red-eye flight has a special atmosphere. Most shops have closed, only a few convenience stores still lit. The waiting area chairs are full of reclining people — some with coats over their faces, some using suitcases as pillows, some leaning together for warmth. Occasional flight announcements break the quiet, but it quickly returns. I found a corner to sit in. Beside me was an elderly woman traveling alone. She looked over eighty, neatly dressed, a small leather suitcase at her side. I asked where she was going. She said to visit her grandson working in another city. I asked why she was taking such a late flight. She smiled and said 'it's cheaper.' Then she took out a thermos from her bag for a sip of water and a small blanket to put over her legs. You could tell she was an experienced traveler. At 2 AM boarding time she stood up, steadily pulling her suitcase toward the gate. In the midnight airport, everyone still awake has a destination worth flying to.

questions.q_zh: 老太太为什么坐红眼航班？
questions.q_en: Why does the elderly woman take the red-eye flight?
questions.explanation: The passage explains: the airport before a red-eye flight has a.

questions.q_zh: 老太太做了什么说明她有旅行经验？
questions.q_en: What showed the elderly woman was an experienced traveler?

options.pinyin: dài le bǎowēn bēi hé máotǎn

questions.explanation: She smiled and said the hard seat was cheaper, then pulled out a thermos and blanket from her bag — small comforts that showed she knew exactly how to travel long distances.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎么形容午夜机场里的人？
questions.q_en: How does the author describe the people in the midnight airport?

options.text: 每个人都有一个值得飞往的目的地
options.pinyin: měi gè rén dōu yǒu yí gè zhíde fēi wǎng de mùdìdì
options.text_en: Everyone has a destination worth flying to

options.pinyin: dōu zài bàoyuàn hángbān tài wǎn
options.text_en: All complaining about the late flight

questions.explanation: The author frames everyone in the midnight airport the same way: each has a destination worth flying to.

title: The Stray Dog That Knows Traffic Lights
text_zh: 学校附近有一只流浪狗，全身棕色的，不大不小，看起来已经在这个街区生活了很久。最让人惊讶的是它会看红绿灯。每次走到路口，它都会停下来等。绿灯亮了，它就和行人一起过马路，步伐不紧不慢，甚至会走在斑马线上。红灯的时候，就算路上没有车，它也不会闯过去，安静地坐在路边等着。附近的商店老板告诉我，这只狗在这里至少住了五年了。早上它在学校门口等学生给它面包吃，中午去旁边的饭店后门找剩菜，下午在公园里晒太阳。它没有主人，但整个街区都在照顾它。杂货店的阿姨给它准备了一个纸箱当窝，修车师傅给它取了一个名字叫「老黄」。虽然它是一只流浪狗，但它活得有尊严——它知道这个世界的规则，也知道怎么在规则里找到自己的位置。
text_pinyin: Xuéxiào fùjìn yǒu yì zhī liúlàng gǒu, quán shēn zōngsè de, bú dà bù xiǎo, kàn qǐlái yǐjīng zài zhège jiēqū shēnghuó le hěn jiǔ. Zuì ràng rén jīngyà de shì tā huì kàn hónglǜdēng. Měi cì zǒu dào lùkǒu, tā dōu huì tíng xiàlái děng. Lǜdēng liàng le, tā jiù hé xíngrén yìqǐ guò mǎlù, bùfá bù jǐn bú màn, shènzhì huì zǒu zài bānmǎ xiàn shàng. Hóngdēng de shíhou, jiùsuàn lù shàng méiyǒu chē, tā yě bú huì chuǎng guòqù, ānjìng de zuò zài lùbiān děng zhe. Fùjìn de shāngdiàn lǎobǎn gàosu wǒ, zhè zhī gǒu zài zhèlǐ zhìshǎo zhù le wǔ nián le. Zǎoshang tā zài xuéxiào ménkǒu děng xuéshēng gěi tā miànbāo chī, zhōngwǔ qù pángbiān de fàndiàn hòu mén zhǎo shèngcài, xiàwǔ zài gōngyuán lǐ shài tàiyáng. Tā méiyǒu zhǔrén, dàn zhěnggè jiēqū dōu zài zhàogù tā. Záhuò diàn de āyí gěi tā zhǔnbèi le yí gè zhǐxiāng dāng wō, xiū chē shīfu gěi tā qǔ le yí gè míngzi jiào「Lǎo Huáng」. Suīrán tā shì yì zhī liúlàng gǒu, dàn tā huó de yǒu zūnyán — tā zhīdào zhège shìjiè de guīzé, yě zhīdào zěnme zài guīzé lǐ zhǎo dào zìjǐ de wèizhi.
text_en: Near the school there's a stray dog, all brown, medium-sized, clearly having lived in this neighborhood a long time. Most surprisingly, it understands traffic lights. Every time it reaches an intersection it stops and waits. When the green light comes on, it crosses with the pedestrians at a steady pace, even walking on the crosswalk. During red lights, even if no cars are on the road, it won't cross — just sits quietly at the curb waiting. A nearby shop owner told me this dog has lived here at least five years. In the morning it waits at the school entrance for students to give it bread, at noon it goes to the back door of a nearby restaurant for leftovers, in the afternoon it sunbathes in the park. It has no owner, but the whole neighborhood looks after it. The grocery store auntie set up a cardboard box as its bed, the auto mechanic gave it a name — 'Old Huang.' Though it's a stray, it lives with dignity — it knows the rules of this world and knows how to find its place within them.

questions.q_zh: 这只狗最让人惊讶的地方是什么？
questions.q_en: What's most surprising about this dog?
questions.explanation: The stray dog waits at red lights and crosses at green — an unexpected learned behavior that surprised even regular observers.

questions.q_zh: 谁给这只狗取了名字？
questions.q_en: Who named this dog?
questions.explanation: The passage identifies: the grocery store auntie set up a cardboard.

questions.q_zh: 这段话如何处理'孤独'这个概念？
questions.q_en: How does this passage treat the concept of 'solitude'?

options.pinyin: bǎ tā dàng zuò yì zhǒng xiǎngshòu
options.text_en: treats it as something to enjoy

questions.explanation: Solitude here is framed as chosen and pleasurable, not as isolation or loneliness.

title: The Wisdom of Waiting in Line
text_zh: 我观察过很多排队的场景，发现了一些有趣的规律。在超市里，看起来最短的队往往不是最快的——因为前面可能有一个人买了很多东西，或者在用优惠券。真正快的队是结账员动作最熟练的那一条。在银行，排队的人会不自觉地跟着前面的人保持一个固定的距离，大约是一臂长。如果有人站得太近，前面的人会微微往前移一步，重新建立那个距离。在奶茶店，等待的时间和饮料的受欢迎程度成正比——如果排队的人很多，说明这家店不错，但也意味着你要等更久。最聪明的做法是在非高峰时间去。我还发现了一个有趣的心理现象：当我们知道前面还有多少人的时候，等待就没那么难受了。不确定才是最让人焦虑的。所以好的排队系统会告诉你「前面还有多少人，大约等多少分钟」——给等待一个终点，人就能耐心下来。
text_pinyin: Wǒ guānchá guò hěn duō páiduì de chǎngjǐng, fāxiàn le yìxiē yǒuqù de guīlǜ. Zài chāoshì lǐ, kàn qǐlái zuì duǎn de duì wǎngwǎng bú shì zuì kuài de — yīnwèi qiánmiàn kěnéng yǒu yí gè rén mǎi le hěn duō dōngxi, huòzhě zài yòng yōuhuì quàn. Zhēnzhèng kuài de duì shì jiézhàng yuán dòngzuò zuì shúliàn de nà yì tiáo. Zài yínháng, páiduì de rén huì bù zìjué de gēn zhe qiánmiàn de rén bǎochí yí gè gùdìng de jùlí, dàyuē shì yì bì cháng. Rúguǒ yǒu rén zhàn de tài jìn, qiánmiàn de rén huì wēiwēi wǎng qián yí yí bù, chóngxīn jiànlì nàge jùlí. Zài nǎichá diàn, děngdài de shíjiān hé yǐnliào de shòu huānyíng chéngdù chéng zhèngbǐ — rúguǒ páiduì de rén hěn duō, shuōmíng zhè jiā diàn búcuò, dàn yě yìwèi zhe nǐ yào děng gèng jiǔ. Zuì cōngmíng de zuòfǎ shì zài fēi gāofēng shíjiān qù. Wǒ hái fāxiàn le yí gè yǒuqù de xīnlǐ xiànxiàng: dāng wǒmen zhīdào qiánmiàn hái yǒu duōshao rén de shíhou, děngdài jiù méi nàme nánshòu le. Bú quèdìng cái shì zuì ràng rén jiāolǜ de. Suǒyǐ hǎo de páiduì xìtǒng huì gàosu nǐ「qiánmiàn hái yǒu duōshao rén, dàyuē děng duōshao fēnzhōng」— gěi děngdài yí gè zhōngdiǎn, rén jiù néng nàixīn xiàlái.
text_en: I've observed many queuing scenarios and found some interesting patterns. In supermarkets, the shortest-looking line often isn't the fastest — because the person in front might have bought a lot or is using coupons. The truly fast line is the one with the most skilled cashier. In banks, people unconsciously maintain a fixed distance from the person ahead — about an arm's length. If someone stands too close, the person ahead shifts forward slightly to re-establish that distance. At milk tea shops, wait time is proportional to the drink's popularity — a long line means the shop is good but you'll wait longer. The smartest approach is going during off-peak hours. I also discovered an interesting psychological phenomenon: when we know how many people are ahead, waiting becomes less unbearable. Uncertainty is what causes the most anxiety. So a good queuing system tells you 'how many people are ahead and approximately how many minutes to wait' — giving the wait an endpoint lets people stay patient.

questions.q_en: Why is this passage worth reading a second time?

options.text_en: you can discover more hidden meanings

options.text: 内容太复杂，一遍读不懂
options.pinyin: nèiróng tài fùzá, yí biàn dú bù dǒng
options.text_en: it's too complex to understand in one reading

questions.q_en: How far apart do people stand in a bank queue?
questions.explanation: Stated directly: about an arm's length.

questions.q_zh: 什么最让排队的人焦虑？
