questions.explanation: Rather than taking students at their word, she would assign a similar problem to verify they truly understood — testing comprehension through application, not just self-report.

title: The Recycling Routine
text_zh: 自从实行垃圾分类以后，扔垃圾变成了一件需要思考的事。以前是一个袋子装所有的垃圾，现在要分成四种：可回收、有害垃圾、湿垃圾和干垃圾。刚开始的时候大家都很不习惯，经常分错。骨头是湿垃圾还是干垃圾？用过的纸巾是可回收还是干垃圾？这些问题每天都有人在讨论。小区里有一个志愿者大妈，每天早上七点和晚上七点守在垃圾桶旁边，帮大家检查分类。她戴着红色的袖章，手里拿着一个夹子，看到分错的会用夹子帮你挑出来，然后微笑着告诉你应该放在哪个桶里。半年以后，大部分人已经养成了习惯。我现在做饭的时候就会自动把菜叶放一个袋子，包装纸放另一个袋子。这个习惯改变了我对垃圾的看法——它不是一堆无用的东西，而是可以被分类、被回收、被重新利用的资源。一个好的系统可以慢慢改变人的行为。
text_pinyin: Zìcóng shíxíng lājī fēnlèi yǐhòu, rēng lājī biàn chéng le yí jiàn xūyào sīkǎo de shì. Yǐqián shì yí gè dàizi zhuāng suǒyǒu de lājī, xiànzài yào fēn chéng sì zhǒng: kě huíshōu, yǒuhài lājī, shī lājī hé gān lājī. Gāng kāishǐ de shíhou dàjiā dōu hěn bù xíguàn, jīngcháng fēn cuò. Gǔtou shì shī lājī háishi gān lājī? Yòng guò de zhǐjīn shì kě huíshōu háishi gān lājī? Zhèxiē wèntí měi tiān dōu yǒu rén zài tǎolùn. Xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yí gè zhìyuànzhě dàmā, měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn hé wǎnshang qī diǎn shǒu zài lājī tǒng pángbiān, bāng dàjiā jiǎnchá fēnlèi. Tā dài zhe hóngsè de xiùzhāng, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí gè jiāzi, kàn dào fēn cuò de huì yòng jiāzi bāng nǐ tiāo chūlái, ránhòu wēixiào zhe gàosu nǐ yīnggāi fàng zài nǎge tǒng lǐ. Bàn nián yǐhòu, dà bùfen rén yǐjīng yǎng chéng le xíguàn. Wǒ xiànzài zuòfàn de shíhou jiù huì zìdòng bǎ cài yè fàng yí gè dàizi, bāozhuāng zhǐ fàng lìng yí gè dàizi. Zhège xíguàn gǎibiàn le wǒ duì lājī de kànfǎ — tā bú shì yì duī wúyòng de dōngxi, ér shì kěyǐ bèi fēnlèi, bèi huíshōu, bèi chóngxīn lìyòng de zīyuán. Yí gè hǎo de xìtǒng kěyǐ mànmàn gǎibiàn rén de xíngwéi.
text_en: Since waste sorting was implemented, throwing out trash became something requiring thought. Before, one bag held everything; now it's four categories: recyclable, hazardous, wet waste, and dry waste. At first nobody was used to it, constantly sorting wrong. Are bones wet waste or dry waste? Are used tissues recyclable or dry waste? These questions were discussed daily. A volunteer auntie in our compound stands by the bins at 7 AM and 7 PM every day, helping everyone check their sorting. She wears a red armband and holds tongs — when she spots something misclassified, she picks it out with the tongs and smilingly tells you which bin it belongs in. Six months later, most people had formed the habit. Now while cooking I automatically put vegetable leaves in one bag and packaging paper in another. This habit changed how I see trash — it's not a pile of useless stuff, but resources that can be sorted, recycled, and reused. A good system can gradually change people's behavior.

questions.q_zh: 场景的选择对读者有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the choice of setting affect the reader?

options.text_en: makes the reader want to travel

questions.explanation: The specific, recognizable setting grounds the passage and lets the reader feel present.

questions.q_zh: 根据这段话，人物接下来最可能做什么？
questions.q_en: Based on this passage, what would the character most likely do next?

options.pinyin: kāishǐ zuò wánquán bùtóng de shì

questions.explanation: The character's contentment and reflective stance suggest staying in this mode.

title: The Canteen Auntie
text_zh: 公司食堂有一位阿姨，大家都叫她张姐。她负责午餐的最后一个窗口——打汤的。每天中午她站在一大锅汤后面，手里拿着一个大勺子，面带微笑等着大家来盛汤。张姐有一个特别的习惯：她会记住每个人喜欢喝什么汤。看到不喜欢喝鸡蛋汤的人来了，她会主动说「今天有你喜欢的排骨汤，在旁边那个锅里」。有些新来的同事不知道这个，第一次来盛汤的时候张姐会问「你喜欢喝什么汤？辣的还是不辣的？」然后在脑子里记下来。有人问她怎么记得住那么多人的口味，她说「天天见，自然就记住了」。食堂的菜有时候好吃有时候一般，但张姐打的汤永远是热的，碗永远是满的。她不只是一个打汤的人，她是食堂这个系统里最温暖的部分。每个看起来冰冷的系统里，都需要一个像张姐这样的人。
text_pinyin: Gōngsī shítáng yǒu yí wèi āyí, dàjiā dōu jiào tā Zhāng jiě. Tā fùzé wǔcān de zuìhòu yí gè chuāngkǒu — dǎ tāng de. Měi tiān zhōngwǔ tā zhàn zài yí dà guō tāng hòumiàn, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí gè dà sháozi, miàn dài wēixiào děng zhe dàjiā lái chéng tāng. Zhāng jiě yǒu yí gè tèbié de xíguàn: tā huì jì zhù měi gè rén xǐhuan hē shénme tāng. Kàn dào bù xǐhuan hē jīdàn tāng de rén lái le, tā huì zhǔdòng shuō「jīntiān yǒu nǐ xǐhuan de páigǔ tāng, zài pángbiān nàge guō lǐ」. Yǒuxiē xīn lái de tóngshì bù zhīdào zhège, dì yī cì lái chéng tāng de shíhou Zhāng jiě huì wèn「nǐ xǐhuan hē shénme tāng? Là de háishi bú là de?」Ránhòu zài nǎozi lǐ jì xiàlái. Yǒu rén wèn tā zěnme jì de zhù nàme duō rén de kǒuwèi, tā shuō「tiāntiān jiàn, zìrán jiù jì zhù le」. Shítáng de cài yǒu shíhou hǎochī yǒu shíhou yìbān, dàn Zhāng jiě dǎ de tāng yǒngyuǎn shì rè de, wǎn yǒngyuǎn shì mǎn de. Tā bù zhǐ shì yí gè dǎ tāng de rén, tā shì shítáng zhège xìtǒng lǐ zuì wēnnuǎn de bùfen. Měi gè kàn qǐlái bīnglěng de xìtǒng lǐ, dōu xūyào yí gè xiàng Zhāng jiě zhèyàng de rén.
text_en: Our company cafeteria has an auntie everyone calls Sister Zhang. She handles the last counter at lunch — serving soup. Every noon she stands behind a big pot of soup, holding a large ladle, smiling as she waits for everyone. Sister Zhang has a special habit: she remembers what soup each person likes. When she sees someone who doesn't like egg soup, she'll say 'today there's your favorite pork rib soup, in the pot next to this one.' New colleagues don't know this — the first time they come for soup, Sister Zhang asks 'What soup do you like? Spicy or not?' then memorizes it. Someone asked how she remembers so many people's preferences. She said 'I see everyone every day, naturally I remember.' The cafeteria food is sometimes good, sometimes average, but Sister Zhang's soup is always hot and the bowls always full. She's not just someone who serves soup — she's the warmest part of the cafeteria system. Every seemingly cold system needs someone like Sister Zhang.

questions.q_zh: 张姐有什么特别的习惯？
questions.q_en: What special habit does Sister Zhang have?

options.text_en: Remembers what soup each person likes

zìjǐ xiān cháng tāng de wèidào

questions.explanation: Sister Zhang memorizes each regular customer's soup preference and serves it without being asked — personalized attention that makes diners feel known.

questions.explanation: The consistency of the habit signals deep personal importance, not mere routine.

title: The Shared Bicycle
text_zh: 共享单车改变了城市里「最后一公里」的问题。从地铁站出来以后，走路回家要二十分钟，但骑共享单车只要五分钟。用手机扫一下车上的二维码，听到「咔嗒」一声锁打开了，就可以骑了。到了目的地，把车停在指定的区域，关上锁，手机上自动扣费。整个过程不需要和任何人说话。但是这个系统也有不完美的地方。有时候车子坏了没有人修，刹车不好用或者座椅太高调不下来。有时候找不到可以停车的地方，只好把车停在很远的地方再走一段。还有些人把共享单车乱停在人行道上，影响了行人走路。我的邻居老赵每天早上都会把小区门口乱停的共享单车整理好。没有人要求他这样做，也没有人给他钱。他说「公共的东西就应该大家一起维护」。
text_pinyin: Gòngxiǎng dānchē gǎibiàn le chéngshì lǐ「zuìhòu yì gōnglǐ」de wèntí. Cóng dìtiě zhàn chūlái yǐhòu, zǒulù huí jiā yào èrshí fēnzhōng, dàn qí gòngxiǎng dānchē zhǐ yào wǔ fēnzhōng. Yòng shǒujī sǎo yíxià chē shàng de èrwéimǎ, tīng dào「kādā」yì shēng suǒ dǎkāi le, jiù kěyǐ qí le. Dào le mùdìdì, bǎ chē tíng zài zhǐdìng de qūyù, guān shàng suǒ, shǒujī shàng zìdòng kòu fèi. Zhěnggè guòchéng bù xūyào hé rènhé rén shuōhuà. Dànshì zhège xìtǒng yě yǒu bù wánměi de dìfāng. Yǒu shíhou chēzi huài le méiyǒu rén xiū, shāchē bù hǎo yòng huòzhě zuòyǐ tài gāo tiáo bù xiàlái. Yǒu shíhou zhǎo bú dào kěyǐ tíng chē de dìfāng, zhǐhǎo bǎ chē tíng zài hěn yuǎn de dìfāng zài zǒu yí duàn. Hái yǒu xiē rén bǎ gòngxiǎng dānchē luàn tíng zài rénxíngdào shàng, yǐngxiǎng le xíngrén zǒulù. Wǒ de línjū Lǎo Zhào měi tiān zǎoshang dōu huì bǎ xiǎoqū ménkǒu luàn tíng de gòngxiǎng dānchē zhěnglǐ hǎo. Méiyǒu rén yāoqiú tā zhèyàng zuò, yě méiyǒu rén gěi tā qián. Tā shuō「gōnggòng de dōngxi jiù yīnggāi dàjiā yìqǐ wéihù」. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì gòngxiǎng de zhēnzhèng yìsi — bù zhǐ shì gòngxiǎng shǐyòng quán, yě gòngxiǎng zérèn.
text_en: Shared bicycles solved the 'last mile' problem in cities. Walking home from the subway takes twenty minutes, but riding a shared bike takes only five. Scan the QR code on the bike with your phone, hear the lock 'click' open, and ride. At your destination, park in the designated area, close the lock, and fees are automatically deducted on your phone. The whole process requires no human interaction. But the system isn't perfect. Sometimes bikes are broken with nobody to fix them — bad brakes or seats stuck too high. Sometimes you can't find a parking spot and must park far away and walk the rest. Some people park shared bikes randomly on sidewalks, blocking pedestrians. My neighbor Old Zhao tidies up the messily parked shared bikes at our compound entrance every morning. Nobody asks him to, nobody pays him. He says 'public things should be maintained by everyone together.

questions.q_zh: 共享单车解决了什么问题？
questions.q_en: What problem do shared bikes solve?
questions.explanation: Shared bikes bridge the gap between the subway exit and one's final destination — the 'last mile' that's too far to walk comfortably but too short for another ride.

questions.q_zh: 这段话传达的情绪是克制的还是外露的？
questions.q_en: Is the emotion conveyed in this passage restrained or overt?
questions.explanation: The passage embeds feeling in concrete detail rather than stating emotions outright.

title: The Apartment Building Rules
text_zh: 住在一栋楼里的人，虽然大多互不相识，但慢慢地形成了一些没有人明确规定过的规则。比如：晚上十点以后不要大声说话或者放音乐；在电梯里遇到邻居要点个头或者微笑一下；不要在楼道里堆放东西；如果你家的狗在公共区域拉了屎，你要自己清理干净。这些规则没有写在任何地方，但大家都知道。偶尔有人不遵守——比如有一段时间六楼的一家人每天晚上十一点还在唱卡拉OK——其他邻居就会不高兴。一开始大家只是忍着，后来有人贴了一张条子在电梯里，写着「请注意休息时间」。条子贴了三天以后，唱卡拉OK的声音消失了。没有人直接去敲门说，但信息传达到了。我觉得这就是一种民间的自我管理系统。它不靠法律，不靠罚款，靠的是大家对共同生活空间的尊重。虽然有时候不完美，但大部分时候它是有效的。
text_pinyin: Zhù zài yí dòng lóu lǐ de rén, suīrán dàduō hù bù xiāngshí, dàn mànmàn de xíngchéng le yìxiē méiyǒu rén míngquè guīdìng guò de guīzé. Bǐrú: wǎnshang shí diǎn yǐhòu bú yào dà shēng shuōhuà huòzhě fàng yīnyuè; zài diàntī lǐ yù dào línjū yào diǎn gè tóu huòzhě wēixiào yíxià; bú yào zài lóudào lǐ duī fàng dōngxi; rúguǒ nǐ jiā de gǒu zài gōnggòng qūyù lā le shǐ, nǐ yào zìjǐ qīnglǐ gānjìng. Zhèxiē guīzé méiyǒu xiě zài rènhé dìfāng, dàn dàjiā dōu zhīdào. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén bù zūnshǒu — bǐrú yǒu yí duàn shíjiān liù lóu de yì jiā rén měi tiān wǎnshang shíyī diǎn hái zài chàng kǎlā OK — qítā línjū jiù huì bù gāoxìng. Yì kāishǐ dàjiā zhǐ shì rěn zhe, hòulái yǒu rén tiē le yì zhāng tiáozi zài diàntī lǐ, xiě zhe「qǐng zhùyì xiūxi shíjiān」. Tiáozi tiē le sān tiān yǐhòu, chàng kǎlā OK de shēngyīn xiāoshī le. Méiyǒu rén zhíjiē qù qiāo mén shuō, dàn xìnxī chuándá dào le. Wǒ juéde zhè jiù shì yì zhǒng mínjiān de zìwǒ guǎnlǐ xìtǒng. Tā bú kào fǎlǜ, bú kào fákuǎn, kào de shì dàjiā duì gòngtóng shēnghuó kōngjiān de zūnzhòng. Suīrán yǒu shíhou bù wánměi, dàn dà bùfen shíhou tā shì yǒuxiào de.
text_en: People living in the same building, though mostly strangers, gradually form rules nobody has explicitly set. For example: no loud talking or music after 10 PM; nod or smile when meeting neighbors in the elevator; don't pile things in the hallways; if your dog poops in common areas, clean it up yourself. These rules aren't written anywhere, but everyone knows them. Occasionally someone breaks them — for a while, a family on the sixth floor sang karaoke every night at 11. Other neighbors were unhappy. At first everyone just endured it, then someone posted a note in the elevator: 'Please be mindful of rest hours.' Three days after the note, the karaoke noise disappeared. Nobody went to knock on the door directly, but the message got through. I think this is a kind of grassroots self-management system. It doesn't rely on laws or fines, but on everyone's respect for shared living space. Though sometimes imperfect, most of the time it works.

questions.q_zh: 六楼唱卡拉OK的问题怎么解决的？
questions.q_en: How was the sixth-floor karaoke problem solved?

options.text: 有人在电梯里贴了一张条子
options.pinyin: yǒu rén zài diàntī lǐ tiē le yì zhāng tiáozi
options.text_en: Someone posted a note in the elevator

questions.explanation: Someone posted a polite note in the elevator, and after that the sixth-floor karaoke noise stopped.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'安静'不只是物理上的安静，还代表什么？
questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: The silence described extends inward -- it represents mental stillness, not just absence of sound.

title: The Company Meeting
text_zh: 我们公司每周一上午九点有一个例会。说是例会，其实大家都知道它通常比需要的时间长。会议室有一张长桌子，能坐十二个人。老板坐在最里面的位置，面对着一块白板。每个部门的负责人轮流汇报上周的工作和这周的计划。汇报完以后老板会提问，有时候一个问题能讨论二十分钟。我发现了一个有趣的现象：坐得离老板越近的人说得越多，坐得越远的人越安静。我通常坐在最远的位置，带着笔记本假装在记录，其实在想中午吃什么。但偶尔老板会突然点名问我的意见，这时候就需要马上把注意力拉回来。会议结束以后大家走出会议室，表情各不相同。有人觉得会开得太长了，有人觉得终于把问题说清楚了。我觉得会议最好的部分其实是散会以后——大家在走廊里三三两两地聊几句，那些在会上没说的话反而在这个时候说出来了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen gōngsī měi zhōu yī zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn yǒu yí gè lìhuì. Shuō shì lìhuì, qíshí dàjiā dōu zhīdào tā tōngcháng bǐ xūyào de shíjiān cháng. Huìyì shì yǒu yì zhāng cháng zhuōzi, néng zuò shí'èr gè rén. Lǎobǎn zuò zài zuì lǐmiàn de wèizhì, miàn duì zhe yí kuài báibǎn. Měi gè bùmén de fùzé rén lúnliú huìbào shàng zhōu de gōngzuò hé zhè zhōu de jìhuà. Huìbào wán yǐhòu lǎobǎn huì tíwèn, yǒu shíhou yí gè wèntí néng tǎolùn èrshí fēnzhōng. Wǒ fāxiàn le yí gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: zuò de lí lǎobǎn yuè jìn de rén shuō de yuè duō, zuò de yuè yuǎn de rén yuè ānjìng. Wǒ tōngcháng zuò zài zuì yuǎn de wèizhì, dài zhe bǐjìběn jiǎzhuāng zài jìlù, qíshí zài xiǎng zhōngwǔ chī shénme. Dàn ǒu'ěr lǎobǎn huì tūrán diǎnmíng wèn wǒ de yìjiàn, zhè shíhou jiù xūyào mǎshàng bǎ zhùyìlì lā huílái. Huìyì jiéshù yǐhòu dàjiā zǒu chū huìyì shì, biǎoqíng gè bù xiāngtóng. Yǒu rén juéde huì kāi de tài cháng le, yǒu rén juéde zhōngyú bǎ wèntí shuō qīngchu le. Wǒ juéde huìyì zuì hǎo de bùfen qíshí shì sàn huì yǐhòu — dàjiā zài zǒuláng lǐ sān sān liǎng liǎng de liáo jǐ jù, nàxiē zài huì shàng méi shuō de huà fǎn'ér zài zhège shíhou shuō chūlái le.
text_en: Our company has a regular Monday meeting at 9 AM. Though called a regular meeting, everyone knows it typically runs longer than necessary. The meeting room has a long table seating twelve. The boss sits at the far end facing a whiteboard. Each department head takes turns reporting last week's work and this week's plans. After reports the boss asks questions — sometimes one question sparks twenty minutes of discussion. I've noticed an interesting phenomenon: people sitting closer to the boss speak more; those farther away stay quieter. I usually sit in the farthest seat, notebook open pretending to take notes, actually thinking about what to eat for lunch. But occasionally the boss suddenly calls on me for my opinion, and I need to snap my attention back immediately. After the meeting everyone files out with varying expressions. Some feel the meeting ran too long; others feel issues were finally clarified. I think the best part of the meeting is actually after it ends — people cluster in twos and threes in the hallway chatting, and what wasn't said in the meeting gets said then.

questions.q_zh: 作者发现了什么有趣的现象？
questions.q_en: What interesting phenomenon did the author notice?

options.pinyin: lǎobǎn zǒng shì shuō de zuì duō
options.text_en: The boss always talks the most

options.text: 坐得离老板越近说得越多
options.pinyin: zuò de lí lǎobǎn yuè jìn shuō de yuè duō
options.text_en: Those sitting closer to the boss speak more

options.text: 每个人说的时间都一样
options.pinyin: měi gè rén shuō de shíjiān dōu yíyàng
options.text_en: Everyone speaks for the same amount of time

questions.explanation: Seating distance correlates with speaking frequency: those closer to the boss speak up more, while those farther away stay quiet — physical proximity shapes participation.

questions.q_zh: 作者想通过这个故事探讨什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author want to explore through this story?

options.text: 日常生活中的深层意义
options.pinyin: rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng de shēncéng yìyì

questions.explanation: The ordinary scenario serves as a vehicle for uncovering overlooked significance in routines.

title: The Parking Lot Attendant
text_zh: 我们小区的地下停车场有一位管理员，姓周，大家叫他周叔。他每天早上六点就到了，晚上十点才下班。他的工作看起来很简单：确保每辆车停在自己的车位上，处理停车纠纷，指挥进出的车辆。但其实他做的远不止这些。周叔记得几乎每辆车的车主是谁，什么时候通常出门，什么时候回来。他能通过发动机的声音判断是哪辆车来了。有一次我忘记关车窗就走了，是周叔打电话提醒我的。还有一次下大雨，地面上的水流进了停车场，周叔一个人用扫帚把水往外扫，衣服全湿了。他说「车泡了水可不行，那是人家的财产」。周叔的月工资不高，但他把这份工作做得非常认真。他说「做什么工作不重要，重要的是做得让自己满意」。每次开车进停车场，看到周叔站在那里朝我挥挥手，我就觉得今天回家了。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū de dìxià tíngchē chǎng yǒu yí wèi guǎnlǐyuán, xìng Zhōu, dàjiā jiào tā Zhōu shū. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn jiù dào le, wǎnshang shí diǎn cái xiàbān. Tā de gōngzuò kàn qǐlái hěn jiǎndān: quèbǎo měi liàng chē tíng zài zìjǐ de chēwèi shàng, chǔlǐ tíngchē jiūfēn, zhǐhuī jìn chū de chēliàng. Dàn qíshí tā zuò de yuǎn bù zhǐ zhèxiē. Zhōu shū jìde jīhū měi liàng chē de chēzhǔ shì shéi, shénme shíhou tōngcháng chūmén, shénme shíhou huílái. Tā néng tōngguò fādòngjī de shēngyīn pànduàn shì nǎ liàng chē lái le. Yǒu yí cì wǒ wàngjì guān chē chuāng jiù zǒu le, shì Zhōu shū dǎ diànhuà tíxǐng wǒ de. Hái yǒu yí cì xià dà yǔ, dìmiàn shàng de shuǐ liú jìn le tíngchē chǎng, Zhōu shū yí gè rén yòng sǎozhou bǎ shuǐ wǎng wài sǎo, yīfu quán shī le. Tā shuō「chē pào le shuǐ kě bù xíng, nà shì rénjiā de cáichǎn」. Zhōu shū de yuè gōngzī bù gāo, dàn tā bǎ zhè fèn gōngzuò zuò de fēicháng rènzhēn. Tā shuō「zuò shénme gōngzuò bú zhòngyào, zhòngyào de shì zuò de ràng zìjǐ mǎnyì」. Měi cì kāi chē jìn tíngchē chǎng, kàn dào Zhōu shū zhàn zài nàlǐ cháo wǒ huī huī shǒu, wǒ jiù juéde jīntiān huí jiā le.
text_en: Our compound's underground parking lot has an attendant surnamed Zhou — everyone calls him Uncle Zhou. He arrives at 6 AM and doesn't leave until 10 PM. His job looks simple: make sure every car is in its spot, handle parking disputes, direct vehicles in and out. But he actually does far more. Uncle Zhou remembers nearly every car's owner, when they usually leave, when they return. He can tell which car is coming by its engine sound. Once I forgot to close my car window and left — Uncle Zhou called to remind me. Another time during heavy rain, water flowed into the parking lot from the ground. Uncle Zhou swept it out alone with a broom, clothes completely soaked. He said 'can't let the cars get flooded — that's people's property.' Uncle Zhou's monthly salary isn't high, but he does the job extremely conscientiously. He says 'what job you do doesn't matter; what matters is doing it to your own satisfaction.' Every time I drive into the parking lot and see Uncle Zhou standing there waving at me, I feel like I'm home.

questions.q_zh: 周叔有什么特别的能力？
questions.q_en: What special ability does Uncle Zhou have?

options.text: 能通过发动机声音判断是哪辆车
options.pinyin: néng tōngguò fādòngjī shēngyīn pànduàn shì nǎ liàng chē
options.text_en: Can tell which car by engine sound

options.pinyin: néng jì zhù suǒyǒu chēpái hàomǎ
néng yùcè shénme shíhou xià yǔ
options.text_en: Can predict when it will rain

questions.explanation: Uncle Zhou has spent so many years in the parking lot that he can identify specific cars purely by their engine sound, without needing to see them.

questions.q_zh: 这段话暗示了什么情感？
questions.q_en: What emotion does this passage hint at?
questions.explanation: Beneath the surface details runs an undercurrent of something passing or already gone.

title: The Alley Behind the Building
text_zh: 我住的楼后面有一条很窄的小巷，只有两个人并排走那么宽。白天这条巷子几乎没有人走，因为它不通往任何主要的地方。但我喜欢偶尔从这里穿过去。巷子两边是老房子的墙，墙上长着一些青苔，有的地方还爬着绿色的藤蔓。地上铺着不太平整的石板，下过雨以后石板之间的缝隙里会积水，反射着天空的颜色。巷子中间有一扇小门，门是红色的，已经掉了很多漆。我从来没有看到这扇门打开过，也不知道里面住着谁。门前放着一个花盆，花盆里种着几棵辣椒，长得很好。每次走过的时候我都会看一眼那些辣椒，确认它们还在。巷子的尽头是一条大马路，一出去就是车水马龙的声音。从安静到嘈杂只需要几步路，就像是从一个世界走进了另一个世界。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de lóu hòumiàn yǒu yì tiáo hěn zhǎi de xiǎo xiàng, zhǐ yǒu liǎng gè rén bìng pái zǒu nàme kuān. Báitiān zhè tiáo xiàngzi jīhū méiyǒu rén zǒu, yīnwèi tā bù tōng wǎng rènhé zhǔyào de dìfāng. Dàn wǒ xǐhuan ǒu'ěr cóng zhèlǐ chuān guòqù. Xiàngzi liǎng biān shì lǎo fángzi de qiáng, qiáng shàng zhǎng zhe yìxiē qīngtái, yǒu de dìfāng hái pá zhe lǜsè de téngwàn. Dì shàng pū zhe bú tài píngzhěng de shíbǎn, xià guò yǔ yǐhòu shíbǎn zhījiān de fèngxì lǐ huì jī shuǐ, fǎnshè zhe tiānkōng de yánsè. Xiàngzi zhōngjiān yǒu yì shàn xiǎo mén, mén shì hóngsè de, yǐjīng diào le hěn duō qī. Wǒ cónglái méiyǒu kàn dào zhè shàn mén dǎkāi guò, yě bù zhīdào lǐmiàn zhù zhe shéi. Mén qián fàng zhe yí gè huāpén, huāpén lǐ zhòng zhe jǐ kē làjiāo, zhǎng de hěn hǎo. Měi cì zǒu guò de shíhou wǒ dōu huì kàn yì yǎn nàxiē làjiāo, quèrèn tāmen hái zài. Xiàngzi de jìntóu shì yì tiáo dà mǎlù, yí chūqù jiù shì chē shuǐ mǎ lóng de shēngyīn. Cóng ānjìng dào cáozá zhǐ xūyào jǐ bù lù, jiù xiàng shì cóng yí gè shìjiè zǒu jìn le lìng yí gè shìjiè.
text_en: Behind my building there's a narrow alley, only wide enough for two people side by side. During the day almost nobody walks through it, since it doesn't lead anywhere important. But I like to cut through occasionally. Both sides are walls of old houses, moss growing on them, green vines creeping in places. The ground is paved with uneven stone slabs; after rain, water collects in the gaps between them, reflecting the sky's colors. Midway through the alley there's a small door, painted red, much of the paint peeled away. I've never seen it open, don't know who lives inside. In front of the door sits a flowerpot with a few chili peppers growing well. Every time I pass I glance at those peppers to confirm they're still there. At the alley's end is a main road — step out and you're hit with the sound of heavy traffic. From quiet to noisy in just a few steps, like walking from one world into another.

questions.q_zh: 这段话会让不同的人有不同的理解吗？
questions.q_en: Would different people interpret this passage differently?

options.text: 是的，因为涉及个人经历
options.pinyin: shì de, yīnwèi shèjí gèrén jīnglì
options.text_en: yes, because it involves personal experience

options.pinyin: zhǐ yǒu zhuānjiā cái néng lǐjiě

questions.explanation: Individual background shapes interpretation -- the same details carry different weight for each reader.

questions.q_zh: 读完后，读者最可能有什么感受？
questions.q_en: How is the reader most likely to feel after reading this?
questions.explanation: The understated tone and lingering final image leave the reader in a contemplative state.

title: The Night Market
text_zh: 城市东边有一个夜市，每天傍晚六点开始，到深夜一两点才结束。入口处是卖水果的摊位，切好的西瓜和菠萝摆在透明的盒子里，五颜六色的。往里走是各种小吃：烤串、臭豆腐、炒面、糖葫芦、煎饼果子。空气里混合着烟火气和各种调料的味道，让人还没有决定吃什么肚子就已经饿了。夜市最热闹的时候是晚上八九点。摊位前面排着长队，老板们一边做一边大声招呼客人。有人站在路边吃着刚烤好的串，有人蹲在矮凳上喝啤酒。灯光是暖黄色的，照在每个人的脸上都特别好看。我最喜欢的是一个卖手抓饼的摊位，老板是一对夫妻，丈夫负责烤饼，妻子负责加料和收钱，配合得非常默契。每次去我都说「老样子」，他们就知道是加鸡蛋和生菜。夜市虽然嘈杂，但有一种让人放松的热闹，像是城市在白天结束以后深深地呼了一口气。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì dōngbiān yǒu yí gè yèshì, měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn kāishǐ, dào shēnyè yì liǎng diǎn cái jiéshù. Rùkǒu chù shì mài shuǐguǒ de tānwèi, qiē hǎo de xīguā hé bōluó bǎi zài tòumíng de hézi lǐ, wǔ yán liù sè de. Wǎng lǐ zǒu shì gè zhǒng xiǎochī: kǎo chuàn, chòu dòufu, chǎo miàn, tánghúlu, jiānbing guǒzi. Kōngqì lǐ hùnhé zhe yānhuǒ qì hé gè zhǒng tiáoliào de wèidào, ràng rén hái méiyǒu juédìng chī shénme dùzi jiù yǐjīng è le. Yèshì zuì rènao de shíhou shì wǎnshang bā jiǔ diǎn. Tānwèi qiánmiàn pái zhe cháng duì, lǎobǎn men yìbiān zuò yìbiān dà shēng zhāohu kèrén. Yǒu rén zhàn zài lù biān chī zhe gāng kǎo hǎo de chuàn, yǒu rén dūn zài ǎi dèng shàng hē píjiǔ. Dēngguāng shì nuǎn huángsè de, zhào zài měi gè rén de liǎn shàng dōu tèbié hǎokàn. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de shì yí gè mài shǒuzhuā bǐng de tānwèi, lǎobǎn shì yí duì fūqī, zhàngfu fùzé kǎo bǐng, qīzi fùzé jiā liào hé shōu qián, pèihé de fēicháng mòqì. Měi cì qù wǒ dōu shuō「lǎo yàngzi」, tāmen jiù zhīdào shì jiā jīdàn hé shēngcài. Yèshì suīrán cáozá, dàn yǒu yì zhǒng ràng rén fàngsōng de rènao, xiàng shì chéngshì zài báitiān jiéshù yǐhòu shēn shēn de hū le yì kǒu qì.
text_en: On the east side of the city there's a night market, starting at 6 PM every evening and running until 1 or 2 AM. At the entrance are fruit stalls — sliced watermelon and pineapple in clear containers, colorful. Deeper in are all kinds of street food: skewers, stinky tofu, fried noodles, candied hawthorn, jianbing. The air mixes smoky aromas with various spices, making you hungry before you've even decided what to eat. The busiest time is 8-9 PM. Long lines form at stalls, vendors cooking while loudly welcoming customers. Some stand by the road eating freshly grilled skewers; some squat on low stools drinking beer. The warm yellow lights look beautiful on everyone's faces. My favorite is a hand-grabbed pancake stall run by a married couple — the husband grills the pancake, the wife adds toppings and handles money, perfectly coordinated. Every time I go I say 'the usual,' and they know it's egg and lettuce. The night market is noisy, but it's a relaxing kind of liveliness — like the city taking a deep breath after the day ends.

questions.q_zh: 夜市入口处卖什么？
questions.q_en: What's sold at the night market entrance?
questions.explanation: Fruit vendors occupy the entrance stalls, greeting visitors first as they arrive at the night market.

questions.q_zh: 这段话如何处理'孤独'这个概念？
questions.q_en: How does this passage treat the concept of 'solitude'?

options.pinyin: bǎ tā dàng zuò yì zhǒng xiǎngshòu
options.text_en: treats it as something to enjoy

questions.explanation: Solitude here is framed as chosen and pleasurable, not as isolation or loneliness.

title: The Rooftop View
text_zh: 我住在七楼，楼顶的天台是对住户开放的。夏天的傍晚我喜欢上去坐一会儿，因为那个时候风最舒服。天台上没什么东西，只有几个水泥做的花坛，里面长着一些不知名的野草。站在天台边上往四周看，可以看到大半个城市。北边是商业区，高楼一栋接一栋，玻璃幕墙反射着夕阳的光。南边是老城区，房子矮矮的，密密麻麻地挤在一起，屋顶上能看到卫星锅和晾衣服的绳子。东边有一条河，从城市中间穿过去，河上有一座桥，桥上的车灯从远处看像是在流动的光带。天慢慢暗下来的时候，城市的灯一盏一盏地亮起来，先是路灯，然后是商铺的招牌灯，最后是千家万户的窗户里透出的灯光。从上面看，每一盏灯的后面都有一个家庭、一个故事。这座城市虽然大，但此刻它看起来很安静，也很温暖。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù zài qī lóu, lóudǐng de tiāntái shì duì zhùhù kāifàng de. Xiàtiān de bàngwǎn wǒ xǐhuan shàngqù zuò yíhuìr, yīnwèi nàge shíhou fēng zuì shūfu. Tiāntái shàng méi shénme dōngxi, zhǐ yǒu jǐ gè shuǐní zuò de huātán, lǐmiàn zhǎng zhe yìxiē bù zhī míng de yěcǎo. Zhàn zài tiāntái biān shàng wǎng sì zhōu kàn, kěyǐ kàn dào dà bàn gè chéngshì. Běibiān shì shāngyè qū, gāolóu yí dòng jiē yí dòng, bōli mùqiáng fǎnshè zhe xīyáng de guāng. Nánbiān shì lǎo chéngqū, fángzi ǎi ǎi de, mìmìmámá de jǐ zài yìqǐ, wūdǐng shàng néng kàn dào wèixīng guō hé liàng yīfu de shéngzi. Dōngbiān yǒu yì tiáo hé, cóng chéngshì zhōngjiān chuān guòqù, hé shàng yǒu yí zuò qiáo, qiáo shàng de chē dēng cóng yuǎn chù kàn xiàng shì zài liúdòng de guāng dài. Tiān mànmàn àn xiàlái de shíhou, chéngshì de dēng yì zhǎn yì zhǎn de liàng qǐlái, xiān shì lùdēng, ránhòu shì shāngpù de zhāopai dēng, zuìhòu shì qiān jiā wàn hù de chuānghu lǐ tòu chū de dēngguāng. Cóng shàngmiàn kàn, měi yì zhǎn dēng de hòumiàn dōu yǒu yí gè jiātíng, yí gè gùshi. Zhè zuò chéngshì suīrán dà, dàn cǐkè tā kàn qǐlái hěn ānjìng, yě hěn wēnnuǎn.
text_en: I live on the seventh floor, and the rooftop terrace is open to residents. On summer evenings I like to go up and sit a while, because the breeze is most comfortable then. Not much on the rooftop — just a few concrete planters with unknown wild grass growing in them. Standing at the edge and looking around, you can see most of the city. To the north is the commercial district — one skyscraper after another, glass curtain walls reflecting the sunset. To the south is the old district — low, densely packed houses, satellite dishes and clotheslines visible on rooftops. To the east a river cuts through the middle of the city, a bridge crossing it, car lights from afar looking like a flowing ribbon of light. As the sky slowly darkens, city lights come on one by one — first streetlights, then shop signs, finally the glow from the windows of countless homes. From above, behind every light is a family, a story. This city may be big, but at this moment it looks quiet, and warm.

questions.q_zh: 场景的选择对读者有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the choice of setting affect the reader?

options.text_en: makes the reader want to travel

questions.explanation: The specific, recognizable setting grounds the passage and lets the reader feel present.

questions.q_zh: 读完后，读者最可能有什么感受？
questions.q_en: How is the reader most likely to feel after reading this?
questions.explanation: The understated tone and lingering final image leave the reader in a contemplative state.

title: The Underground Passage
text_zh: 从地铁站出来有一条很长的地下通道，连接着商场和住宅区。这条通道大概有三百米长，两侧的墙上偶尔有一些广告。下班高峰期的时候，人群从两个方向涌来，在通道里形成两股人流。大家走得很快，目视前方，仿佛有一种看不见的力量在推着每个人往前走。但是通道的中段有一个弹吉他的年轻人。他坐在一个小板凳上，面前放着一个打开的琴盒，旁边有一个蓝牙音箱。他弹的都是很轻柔的曲子，声音被人群的脚步声淹没了一大半。但如果你放慢脚步，就能听到旋律。我注意到有些人经过的时候会稍微减速，往琴盒里扔几个硬币，然后继续赶路。也有人停下来听了十几秒，微微笑了一下再走。这个年轻人好像不太在意有多少人听，他只是专注地弹着自己的曲子。在一个匆忙的世界里，他创造了一个小小的安静角落。
text_pinyin: Cóng dìtiě zhàn chūlái yǒu yì tiáo hěn cháng de dìxià tōngdào, liánjiē zhe shāngchǎng hé zhùzhái qū. Zhè tiáo tōngdào dàgài yǒu sān bǎi mǐ cháng, liǎng cè de qiáng shàng ǒu'ěr yǒu yìxiē guǎnggào. Xiàbān gāofēng qī de shíhou, rénqún cóng liǎng gè fāngxiàng yǒng lái, zài tōngdào lǐ xíngchéng liǎng gǔ rénliú. Dàjiā zǒu de hěn kuài, mù shì qiánfāng, fǎngfú yǒu yì zhǒng kàn bú jiàn de lìliàng zài tuī zhe měi gè rén wǎng qián zǒu. Dànshì tōngdào de zhōng duàn yǒu yí gè tán jítā de niánqīng rén. Tā zuò zài yí gè xiǎo bǎndèng shàng, miànqián fàng zhe yí gè dǎkāi de qín hé, pángbiān yǒu yí gè lányá yīnxiāng. Tā tán de dōu shì hěn qīngróu de qǔzi, shēngyīn bèi rénqún de jiǎobù shēng yānmò le yí dà bàn. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ fàngmàn jiǎobù, jiù néng tīng dào xuánlǜ. Wǒ zhùyì dào yǒuxiē rén jīngguò de shíhou huì shāowēi jiǎnsù, wǎng qín hé lǐ rēng jǐ gè yìngbì, ránhòu jìxù gǎn lù. Yě yǒu rén tíng xiàlái tīng le shí jǐ miǎo, wēiwēi xiào le yíxià zài zǒu. Zhège niánqīng rén hǎoxiàng bú tài zàiyì yǒu duōshǎo rén tīng, tā zhǐ shì zhuānzhù de tán zhe zìjǐ de qǔzi. Zài yí gè cōngmáng de shìjiè lǐ, tā chuàngzào le yí gè xiǎo xiǎo de ānjìng jiǎoluò.
text_en: Exiting the subway station there's a long underground passage connecting a mall and a residential area. About three hundred meters long, with occasional ads on the walls. During rush hour, crowds surge from both directions, forming two streams of people. Everyone walks fast, eyes forward, as if an invisible force pushes everyone onward. But midway through the passage sits a young man playing guitar. He's on a small stool, an open guitar case before him, a Bluetooth speaker beside him. He plays gentle melodies, mostly drowned out by the crowd's footsteps. But if you slow down, you can hear the tune. I've noticed some people slightly decelerate as they pass, toss a few coins into the case, then hurry on. Others stop to listen for ten seconds or so, smile slightly, then continue. The young man doesn't seem to care how many people listen — he just focuses on playing his songs. In a hurried world, he's created a small, quiet corner.

questions.q_zh: 这段话让你想到了什么？
questions.q_en: What does this passage make you think of?
questions.explanation: The personal, sensory framing invites recall of the reader's own parallel moments.

questions.q_zh: 这段话会让不同的人有不同的理解吗？
questions.q_en: Would different people interpret this passage differently?

options.text: 是的，因为涉及个人经历
options.pinyin: shì de, yīnwèi shèjí gèrén jīnglì
options.text_en: yes, because it involves personal experience

options.pinyin: zhǐ yǒu zhuānjiā cái néng lǐjiě

questions.explanation: Individual background shapes interpretation -- the same details carry different weight for each reader.

title: The Corner Bookshop
text_zh: 在一条繁华的商业街的拐角处，有一家很小的独立书店。它夹在一家奶茶店和一家手机店中间，如果不注意很容易就走过去了。门面只有两三米宽，但里面比想象的深。推开玻璃门进去，右边是一面墙的书架，从地面一直到天花板。左边是一张旧木桌，上面摆着老板推荐的几本书，每本下面都有一张小卡片写着推荐理由。最里面有两张沙发和一个小桌子，可以坐着看书。这家书店不卖畅销书和教辅材料，只卖文学、哲学、艺术和一些独立出版物。老板是一个四十多岁的男人，很少说话，总是坐在收银台后面看书。他不会主动推荐，但如果你问他他会很认真地帮你选。有一次我问他这个位置的房租一定很贵，开书店能赚钱吗。他说「赚不了什么钱，但够活。一条街上如果连一家书店都没有，那这条街就没有灵魂了。」
text_pinyin: Zài yì tiáo fánhuá de shāngyè jiē de guǎijiǎo chù, yǒu yì jiā hěn xiǎo de dúlì shūdiàn. Tā jiā zài yì jiā nǎichá diàn hé yì jiā shǒujī diàn zhōngjiān, rúguǒ bú zhùyì hěn róngyì jiù zǒu guòqù le. Ménmiàn zhǐ yǒu liǎng sān mǐ kuān, dàn lǐmiàn bǐ xiǎngxiàng de shēn. Tuī kāi bōli mén jìnqù, yòubiān shì yí miàn qiáng de shūjià, cóng dìmiàn yìzhí dào tiānhuābǎn. Zuǒbiān shì yì zhāng jiù mù zhuō, shàngmiàn bǎi zhe lǎobǎn tuījiàn de jǐ běn shū, měi běn xiàmiàn dōu yǒu yì zhāng xiǎo kǎpiàn xiě zhe tuījiàn lǐyóu. Zuì lǐmiàn yǒu liǎng zhāng shāfā hé yí gè xiǎo zhuōzi, kěyǐ zuò zhe kàn shū. Zhè jiā shūdiàn bú mài chàngxiāo shū hé jiàofǔ cáiliào, zhǐ mài wénxué, zhéxué, yìshù hé yìxiē dúlì chūbǎnwù. Lǎobǎn shì yí gè sìshí duō suì de nánrén, hěn shǎo shuōhuà, zǒng shì zuò zài shōuyín tái hòumiàn kàn shū. Tā bú huì zhǔdòng tuījiàn, dàn rúguǒ nǐ wèn tā tā huì hěn rènzhēn de bāng nǐ xuǎn. Yǒu yí cì wǒ wèn tā zhège wèizhì de fángzū yídìng hěn guì, kāi shūdiàn néng zhuàn qián ma. Tā shuō「zhuàn bù liǎo shénme qián, dàn gòu huó. Yì tiáo jiē shàng rúguǒ lián yì jiā shūdiàn dōu méiyǒu, nà zhè tiáo jiē jiù méiyǒu línghún le.」
text_en: At the corner of a busy commercial street, there's a tiny independent bookshop. Squeezed between a milk tea shop and a phone store, easy to walk right past if you're not paying attention. The storefront is only two or three meters wide, but inside it's deeper than you'd expect. Push open the glass door and on the right is a wall of shelves from floor to ceiling. On the left is an old wooden table displaying the owner's recommended books, each with a small card explaining why. In the back are two sofas and a small table for sitting and reading. This shop doesn't sell bestsellers or textbooks — only literature, philosophy, art, and some independent publications. The owner is a man in his forties who rarely speaks, always sitting behind the register reading. He won't recommend unsolicited, but if you ask, he'll help you choose quite thoughtfully. Once I asked if the rent here must be expensive and whether a bookshop can make money. He said 'Can't really make money, but enough to live on. If a street doesn't even have one bookshop, then that street has no soul.'

questions.q_zh: 这个选择反映了什么？
questions.q_en: What does this choice reflect?
questions.explanation: The deliberate choice reveals underlying priorities and what the person finds worthwhile.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的节奏感对阅读体验有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the pacing of this passage affect the reading experience?

options.text: 让读者放慢速度去感受
options.pinyin: ràng dúzhě fàng màn sùdù qù gǎnshòu
options.text_en: slows the reader down to feel

options.pinyin: ràng dúzhě xiǎng kuài diǎn dú wán
options.text_en: makes the reader want to finish quickly

questions.explanation: The unhurried detail forces the reader to linger, mirroring the passage's contemplative mood.

title: The Construction Site Next Door
text_zh: 我家隔壁的空地上开始建一栋新的楼。工地从早上七点开始施工，下午六点停工。每天早上被机器的声音吵醒的时候，我都会有点生气。但是有一天中午休息的时候，我从窗户看了一下工地，发现了一些有意思的事。工人们坐在工地边上的阴凉处吃午饭，每个人面前有一个铝制饭盒。有人在打电话，声音很大地笑着说方言。有人躺在一块木板上午休，用安全帽遮住脸。旁边停着一辆小三轮车，车上装着工具和水壶。这些工人的脸被太阳晒得很黑，手上有很多老茧。他们大多是从外地来的，住在工地旁边的简易房里。这栋楼可能需要一两年才能建好，建好以后他们就会去另一个工地。城市里到处是他们建的房子，但他们自己大多买不起。我不再因为被吵醒而生气了，反而每天早上听到机器声的时候会想：今天他们又前进了一点。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā gébì de kòngdì shàng kāishǐ jiàn yí dòng xīn de lóu. Gōngdì cóng zǎoshang qī diǎn kāishǐ shīgōng, xiàwǔ liù diǎn tíng gōng. Měi tiān zǎoshang bèi jīqì de shēngyīn chǎo xǐng de shíhou, wǒ dōu huì yǒudiǎn shēngqì. Dànshì yǒu yì tiān zhōngwǔ xiūxi de shíhou, wǒ cóng chuānghu kàn le yíxià gōngdì, fāxiàn le yìxiē yǒu yìsi de shì. Gōngrén men zuò zài gōngdì biān shàng de yīnliáng chù chī wǔfàn, měi gè rén miànqián yǒu yí gè lǚzhì fànhé. Yǒu rén zài dǎ diànhuà, shēngyīn hěn dà de xiào zhe shuō fāngyán. Yǒu rén tǎng zài yí kuài mùbǎn shàng wǔ xiū, yòng ānquán mào zhē zhù liǎn. Pángbiān tíng zhe yí liàng xiǎo sānlún chē, chē shàng zhuāng zhe gōngjù hé shuǐhú. Zhèxiē gōngrén de liǎn bèi tàiyáng shài de hěn hēi, shǒu shàng yǒu hěn duō lǎojiǎn. Tāmen dàduō shì cóng wàidì lái de, zhù zài gōngdì pángbiān de jiǎnyì fáng lǐ. Zhè dòng lóu kěnéng xūyào yì liǎng nián cái néng jiàn hǎo, jiàn hǎo yǐhòu tāmen jiù huì qù lìng yí gè gōngdì. Chéngshì lǐ dàochù shì tāmen jiàn de fángzi, dàn tāmen zìjǐ dàduō mǎi bù qǐ. Wǒ bú zài yīnwèi bèi chǎo xǐng ér shēngqì le, fǎn'ér měi tiān zǎoshang tīng dào jīqì shēng de shíhou huì xiǎng: jīntiān tāmen yòu qiánjìn le yìdiǎn.
text_en: An empty lot next to my home started construction on a new building. The site runs from 7 AM to 6 PM. Every morning when the machinery wakes me, I feel a bit annoyed. But one day during the noon break, I looked at the site from my window and noticed some interesting things. Workers sat in the shade eating lunch, each with an aluminum lunch box. Some were on the phone, laughing loudly in dialect. Some napped on wooden boards, hard hats covering their faces. A small tricycle was parked nearby, loaded with tools and water bottles. These workers' faces were deeply tanned, hands full of calluses. Most came from other provinces, living in temporary shelters by the site. This building might take a year or two to finish — after that they'll move to another site. The city is full of buildings they've built, but most of them can't afford to buy one. I'm no longer angry about being woken up. Instead, each morning when I hear the machines, I think: today they've moved forward a little more.

questions.q_zh: 中午休息的时候工人用什么遮住脸午休？
questions.q_en: What do workers cover their faces with during the noon nap?
questions.explanation: During the noon nap, workers covered their faces with hard hats to block the sunlight.

questions.q_zh: 作者后来对工地的噪音态度有什么变化？
questions.q_en: How did the author's attitude toward the construction noise change?

options.text_en: Got used to it, no longer cares

options.text: 不再生气，反而想到他们又前进了一点
options.pinyin: bú zài shēngqì, fǎn'ér xiǎng dào tāmen yòu qiánjìn le yìdiǎn
options.text_en: No longer angry, instead thinks they've progressed a bit more

questions.explanation: The shift is described: I'm no longer angry about being woken up.

title: The City After Midnight
text_zh: 有一次加班到很晚，走出办公楼的时候已经凌晨一点了。平时热闹的街道安静了下来，大部分的店铺已经关门了，只有偶尔一辆出租车开过去。路灯照着空荡荡的人行道，地上有白天留下的落叶和几个被踩扁的易拉罐。一家二十四小时营业的面馆还亮着灯，里面坐着几个和我一样刚下班的人，低着头安静地吃面。我走进去点了一碗牛肉面。面端上来的时候热气扑在脸上，第一口汤的味道让我整个人都暖了起来。旁边桌的一个男人吃完面以后趴在桌上睡着了，老板没有叫醒他。凌晨的城市有一种白天没有的温柔。没有了人群的喧闹，每一个声音都变得清晰——远处的火车声，路边树叶被风吹动的声音，面馆里煮面的水咕嘟咕嘟的声音。这些声音组合在一起，就是午夜以后城市的心跳。
text_pinyin: Yǒu yí cì jiābān dào hěn wǎn, zǒu chū bàngōng lóu de shíhou yǐjīng língchén yì diǎn le. Píngshí rènao de jiēdào ānjìng le xiàlái, dà bùfen de diànpù yǐjīng guān mén le, zhǐ yǒu ǒu'ěr yí liàng chūzū chē kāi guòqù. Lùdēng zhào zhe kōngdàngdàng de rénxíngdào, dì shàng yǒu báitiān liú xià de luòyè hé jǐ gè bèi cǎi biǎn de yìlā guàn. Yì jiā èrshísì xiǎoshí yíngyè de miànguǎn hái liàng zhe dēng, lǐmiàn zuò zhe jǐ gè hé wǒ yíyàng gāng xiàbān de rén, dī zhe tóu ānjìng de chī miàn. Wǒ zǒu jìnqù diǎn le yì wǎn niúròu miàn. Miàn duān shàng lái de shíhou rè qì pū zài liǎn shàng, dì yì kǒu tāng de wèidào ràng wǒ zhěnggè rén dōu nuǎn le qǐlái. Pángbiān zhuō de yí gè nánrén chī wán miàn yǐhòu pā zài zhuō shàng shuì zháo le, lǎobǎn méiyǒu jiào xǐng tā. Língchén de chéngshì yǒu yì zhǒng báitiān méiyǒu de wēnróu. Méiyǒu le rénqún de xuānnào, měi yí gè shēngyīn dōu biàn de qīngxī — yuǎn chù de huǒchē shēng, lù biān shùyè bèi fēng chuī dòng de shēngyīn, miànguǎn lǐ zhǔ miàn de shuǐ gūdū gūdū de shēngyīn. Zhèxiē shēngyīn zǔhé zài yìqǐ, jiù shì wǔyè yǐhòu chéngshì de xīntiào.
text_en: Once I worked overtime until very late, stepping out of the office building at 1 AM. The normally bustling streets had quieted — most shops closed, only the occasional taxi passing by. Streetlights illuminated the empty sidewalks, fallen leaves and crushed cans from the day on the ground. A 24-hour noodle shop still had its lights on, a few people just off work like me sitting inside, heads down, quietly eating noodles. I walked in and ordered a bowl of beef noodles. When it arrived, the steam hit my face, and the first sip of broth warmed me through. At the next table, a man fell asleep slumped over the table after finishing his noodles — the owner didn't wake him. The pre-dawn city has a gentleness the daytime lacks. Without the crowd's noise, every sound becomes clear — distant train sounds, leaves rustling in the wind by the road, the bubbling of cooking water in the noodle shop. These sounds together are the heartbeat of the city after midnight.

questions.q_zh: 旁边桌的男人吃完面以后怎么了？
questions.q_en: What happened to the man at the next table after eating?

options.pinyin: pā zài zhuō shàng shuì zháo le
options.text_en: Fell asleep slumped over the table

questions.explanation: After eating, the man at the next table slumped over and fell asleep -- exhaustion visible in a public space.

questions.q_zh: 环境描写和人物心理之间有什么关系？
questions.q_en: What is the relationship between the setting and the character's psychology?

options.text_en: the environment mirrors the inner state

questions.explanation: The physical scene parallels the character's inner state -- outer landscape reflects inner landscape.

title: The Bridge in the Morning
text_zh: 我每天上班要经过一座桥。桥不大，就是一座普通的水泥桥，下面是一条不太宽的河。但我发现每个时间经过这座桥，感觉都不一样。早晨六七点的时候，桥上几乎没有人，只有几个跑步的人和一两个钓鱼的老人。河面上有薄薄的雾气，对岸的房子看起来模模糊糊的。上班高峰期的时候桥上全是人和车，大家都急着过桥去对面，没有人会停下来看风景。下午的时候桥上比较安静，偶尔有学生骑着自行车经过，书包在背后随着车子上下晃动。傍晚的时候是桥最好看的时候——夕阳把河水染成了金色，桥的影子长长地投在河面上。有人在桥上散步，有人靠着栏杆发呆。一座桥连接的不只是两岸，也连接着一天中不同的时刻。每次走到桥中间的时候我会停一两秒钟，低头看看河水，深吸一口气。这是我一天中最小但最确定的仪式。
text_pinyin: Wǒ měi tiān shàngbān yào jīngguò yí zuò qiáo. Qiáo bú dà, jiù shì yí zuò pǔtōng de shuǐní qiáo, xiàmiàn shì yì tiáo bú tài kuān de hé. Dàn wǒ fāxiàn měi gè shíjiān jīngguò zhè zuò qiáo, gǎnjué dōu bù yíyàng. Zǎochén liù qī diǎn de shíhou, qiáo shàng jīhū méiyǒu rén, zhǐ yǒu jǐ gè pǎobù de rén hé yì liǎng gè diàoyú de lǎorén. Hé miàn shàng yǒu báo báo de wùqì, duì àn de fángzi kàn qǐlái mómo hūhū de. Shàngbān gāofēng qī de shíhou qiáo shàng quán shì rén hé chē, dàjiā dōu jí zhe guò qiáo qù duìmiàn, méiyǒu rén huì tíng xiàlái kàn fēngjǐng. Xiàwǔ de shíhou qiáo shàng bǐjiào ānjìng, ǒu'ěr yǒu xuéshēng qí zhe zìxíngchē jīngguò, shūbāo zài bèi hòu suí zhe chēzi shàng xià huàngdòng. Bàngwǎn de shíhou shì qiáo zuì hǎokàn de shíhou — xīyáng bǎ hé shuǐ rǎn chéng le jīnsè, qiáo de yǐngzi cháng cháng de tóu zài hé miàn shàng. Yǒu rén zài qiáo shàng sànbù, yǒu rén kào zhe lángān fā dāi. Yí zuò qiáo liánjiē de bù zhǐ shì liǎng àn, yě liánjiē zhe yì tiān zhōng bùtóng de shíkè. Měi cì zǒu dào qiáo zhōngjiān de shíhou wǒ huì tíng yì liǎng miǎozhōng, dī tóu kàn kàn hé shuǐ, shēn xī yì kǒu qì. Zhè shì wǒ yì tiān zhōng zuì xiǎo dàn zuì quèdìng de yíshì.
text_en: I cross a bridge every day going to work. It's not big — just an ordinary concrete bridge over a not-too-wide river. But I've found that crossing at different times feels completely different. At 6-7 AM, the bridge is nearly empty — just a few joggers and an old fisherman or two. Thin mist hovers over the river, buildings on the opposite bank looking hazy. During rush hour the bridge is packed with people and cars, everyone hurrying across, nobody stopping to look at the scenery. Afternoons the bridge is quieter, occasional students cycling past, backpacks bobbing up and down behind them. Evening is when the bridge is most beautiful — sunset dyeing the river gold, the bridge's shadow stretching long across the water. Some people stroll on it, some lean on the railing and zone out. A bridge connects not just two banks, but different moments of the day. Every time I reach the middle of the bridge, I stop for a second or two, look down at the water, and take a deep breath. It's the smallest but most certain ritual of my day.

questions.q_zh: 这段话让你想到了什么？
questions.q_en: What does this passage make you think of?
questions.explanation: The personal, sensory framing invites recall of the reader's own parallel moments.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中的'重复'有什么深意？
questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the 'repetition' in this passage carry?
questions.explanation: The repeated routine signals comfort and constancy -- anchoring, not numbing.

title: The Coffee Shop Regulars
text_zh: 我家楼下有一家不大的咖啡店，我几乎每天早上都去买一杯美式咖啡。时间长了以后，我发现这家店有一群固定的常客，每个人都有自己的习惯。最早到的是一个穿西装的中年男人，他每天七点准时到，点一杯黑咖啡，坐在靠窗的位置看报纸。然后是一个背着电脑包的年轻女生，她会点一杯拿铁，然后打开电脑工作到中午。角落里有一对退休的老夫妻，他们每周来三次，总是点两杯卡布奇诺和一份蛋糕两个人分着吃。我和他们从来没有说过话，但每次看到他们在自己固定的位置上，就觉得今天是正常的一天。有一次连续好几天没看到那个穿西装的男人，我居然有一点担心。后来他又出现了，一切照旧。咖啡店老板跟我说「你们这些常客比我还准时」。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià yǒu yì jiā bú dà de kāfēi diàn, wǒ jīhū měi tiān zǎoshang dōu qù mǎi yì bēi měishì kāfēi. Shíjiān cháng le yǐhòu, wǒ fāxiàn zhè jiā diàn yǒu yì qún gùdìng de chángkè, měi gè rén dōu yǒu zìjǐ de xíguàn. Zuì zǎo dào de shì yí gè chuān xīzhuāng de zhōngnián nánrén, tā měi tiān qī diǎn zhǔnshí dào, diǎn yì bēi hēi kāfēi, zuò zài kào chuāng de wèizhì kàn bàozhǐ. Ránhòu shì yí gè bēi zhe diànnǎo bāo de niánqīng nǚshēng, tā huì diǎn yì bēi ná tiě, ránhòu dǎkāi diànnǎo gōngzuò dào zhōngwǔ. Jiǎoluò lǐ yǒu yí duì tuìxiū de lǎo fūqī, tāmen měi zhōu lái sān cì, zǒng shì diǎn liǎng bēi kǎbùqínuò hé yí fèn dàngāo liǎng gè rén fēn zhe chī. Wǒ hé tāmen cónglái méiyǒu shuō guò huà, dàn měi cì kàn dào tāmen zài zìjǐ gùdìng de wèizhì shàng, jiù juéde jīntiān shì zhèngcháng de yì tiān. Yǒu yí cì liánxù hǎo jǐ tiān méi kàn dào nàge chuān xīzhuāng de nánrén, wǒ jūrán yǒu yìdiǎn dānxīn. Hòulái tā yòu chūxiàn le, yíqiè zhào jiù. Kāfēi diàn lǎobǎn gēn wǒ shuō「nǐmen zhèxiē chángkè bǐ wǒ hái zhǔnshí」. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì chéngshì shēnghuó de yì zhǒng liánxì fāngshì — bù xūyào zhīdào duìfāng de míngzi, zhǐ xūyào zhīdào tā zài.
text_en: There's a small coffee shop downstairs from my apartment. I buy an Americano there almost every morning. Over time, I've noticed the shop has a group of regulars, each with their own habits. The earliest is a middle-aged man in a suit who arrives at 7 sharp every day, orders a black coffee, and sits by the window reading the newspaper. Then a young woman with a laptop bag orders a latte and works on her computer until noon. In the corner sits a retired elderly couple who come three times a week, always ordering two cappuccinos and one cake to share. I've never spoken to any of them, but every time I see them in their usual spots, I feel today is a normal day. Once the suited man was absent for several days in a row, and I was actually a little worried. Then he reappeared, everything back to normal.

questions.q_zh: 这段话想要读者从中获得什么？
questions.q_en: What does the passage want the reader to gain from it?

options.text: 一种新的看事物的方式
options.pinyin: yì zhǒng xīn de kàn shìwù de fāngshì
options.text_en: a new way of seeing things

questions.explanation: The passage reframes a familiar scene so the reader notices what they normally overlook.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为这种关系是什么？
questions.q_en: How does the author characterize this kind of relationship?

options.text: 不需要知道名字，只需要知道对方在
options.pinyin: bù xūyào zhīdào míngzi, zhǐ xūyào zhīdào duìfāng zài
options.text_en: No need for names, just knowing they're there

questions.explanation: The interactions reveal a no need for names, just knowing they're there relationship.

title: The Taxi Driver's Map
text_zh: 上周坐出租车的时候遇到了一个很健谈的司机。他开出租车已经十五年了，对这座城市的每一条路都非常熟悉。他说「以前没有导航的时候，全靠脑子记。这座城市的路我闭着眼睛都能开」。我问他这十五年城市变化大不大，他说「太大了。以前城东那一片全是农田，现在盖满了高楼。以前从南到北开车半个小时就够了，现在堵车的话要一两个小时」。他指着车窗外的一栋楼说「这里以前是一个很大的菜市场，我以前经常来这里买菜，老板娘做的豆腐特别好吃，后来拆了建了这个商场」。他说这些话的时候没有什么特别的感情，就像在说天气一样平淡。也许开了十五年的出租车，看多了变化，就学会了接受。他的脑子里有一张这座城市的地图，上面不只有路和建筑，还有那些已经消失的东西——一个菜市场、一条老街、一棵大树。地图上看不到的东西，他都记着。
text_pinyin: Shàng zhōu zuò chūzū chē de shíhou yù dào le yí gè hěn jiàntán de sījī. Tā kāi chūzū chē yǐjīng shíwǔ nián le, duì zhè zuò chéngshì de měi yì tiáo lù dōu fēicháng shúxī. Tā shuō「yǐqián méiyǒu dǎoháng de shíhou, quán kào nǎozi jì. Zhè zuò chéngshì de lù wǒ bì zhe yǎnjīng dōu néng kāi」. Wǒ wèn tā zhè shíwǔ nián chéngshì biànhuà dà bú dà, tā shuō「tài dà le. Yǐqián chéng dōng nà yí piàn quán shì nóngtián, xiànzài gài mǎn le gāolóu. Yǐqián cóng nán dào běi kāi chē bàn gè xiǎoshí jiù gòu le, xiànzài dǔ chē de huà yào yì liǎng gè xiǎoshí」. Tā zhǐ zhe chē chuāng wài de yí dòng lóu shuō「zhèlǐ yǐqián shì yí gè hěn dà de cài shìchǎng, wǒ yǐqián jīngcháng lái zhèlǐ mǎi cài, lǎobǎn niáng zuò de dòufu tèbié hǎochī, hòulái chāi le jiàn le zhège shāngchǎng」. Tā shuō zhèxiē huà de shíhou méiyǒu shénme tèbié de gǎnqíng, jiù xiàng zài shuō tiānqì yíyàng píngdàn. Yěxǔ kāi le shíwǔ nián de chūzū chē, kàn duō le biànhuà, jiù xuéhuì le jiēshòu. Tā de nǎozi lǐ yǒu yì zhāng zhè zuò chéngshì de dìtú, shàngmiàn bù zhǐ yǒu lù hé jiànzhù, hái yǒu nàxiē yǐjīng xiāoshī de dōngxi — yí gè cài shìchǎng, yì tiáo lǎo jiē, yì kē dà shù. Dìtú shàng kàn bú dào de dōngxi, tā dōu jì zhe.
text_en: Last week I met a very talkative taxi driver. He'd been driving a cab for fifteen years and knew every road in the city. He said 'Before navigation, it was all memory. I could drive this city's roads with my eyes closed.' I asked if the city had changed much in fifteen years. He said 'Tremendously. The east side was all farmland before, now covered in towers. Driving north to south used to take half an hour, now it takes an hour or two with traffic.' He pointed at a building outside the window: 'This used to be a big vegetable market. I used to buy groceries here, the owner's wife made amazing tofu. They demolished it and built this mall.' He said all this without particular emotion, as casually as discussing the weather. Maybe after fifteen years of driving a cab and seeing so much change, you learn to accept. In his mind is a map of this city — showing not just roads and buildings, but things that have disappeared: a vegetable market, an old street, a big tree. The things you can't see on a map, he remembers them all.

questions.q_zh: 以前城东那一片是什么？
questions.q_en: What used to be on the east side of the city?
questions.explanation: The driver remembers: the east side of the city used to be farmland, now covered in towers.

questions.q_zh: 司机脑子里的地图有什么特别的？
questions.q_en: What's special about the driver's mental map?

options.pinyin: jì zhe yǐjīng xiāoshī de dōngxi

questions.explanation: The driver's mental map includes landmarks that no longer exist -- a geography of memory, not just roads.

title: The Park Bench
text_zh: 公园里有一张绿色的长椅，就在湖边的一棵大柳树下面。我每个周末都会来这里坐一会儿。这张椅子不知道为什么特别舒服，靠背的角度刚刚好，坐上去以后整个人会自然地放松下来。柳树的枝条垂下来，风一吹就轻轻地摇晃，有时候会扫到你的肩膀。湖面上有几只鸭子游来游去，偶尔把头伸进水里找吃的。对面有几个老人在打太极拳，动作很慢很优美，像在水中游泳一样。坐久了以后你会注意到各种各样的人经过：牵着小狗散步的年轻人、推着婴儿车的妈妈、手拉着手的情侣、独自跑步的人。他们来了又走了，但椅子一直在这里。我想过很多人可能都在这张椅子上坐过，每个人来的时候带着不同的心情，走的时候也许会好一些。一张公园的椅子不会说话，但它默默地接纳了每一个需要坐下来休息一会儿的人。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu yì zhāng lǜsè de cháng yǐ, jiù zài hú biān de yì kē dà liǔ shù xiàmiàn. Wǒ měi gè zhōumò dōu huì lái zhèlǐ zuò yíhuìr. Zhè zhāng yǐzi bù zhīdào wèishénme tèbié shūfu, kào bèi de jiǎodù gānggāng hǎo, zuò shàngqù yǐhòu zhěnggè rén huì zìrán de fàngsōng xiàlái. Liǔ shù de zhītiáo chuí xiàlái, fēng yí chuī jiù qīngqīng de yáohuàng, yǒu shíhou huì sǎo dào nǐ de jiānbǎng. Hú miàn shàng yǒu jǐ zhī yāzi yóu lái yóu qù, ǒu'ěr bǎ tóu shēn jìn shuǐ lǐ zhǎo chī de. Duìmiàn yǒu jǐ gè lǎorén zài dǎ tàijí quán, dòngzuò hěn màn hěn yōuměi, xiàng zài shuǐ zhōng yóuyǒng yíyàng. Zuò jiǔ le yǐhòu nǐ huì zhùyì dào gè zhǒng gè yàng de rén jīngguò: qiān zhe xiǎo gǒu sànbù de niánqīng rén, tuī zhe yīng'ér chē de māma, shǒu lā zhe shǒu de qínglǚ, dúzì pǎobù de rén. Tāmen lái le yòu zǒu le, dàn yǐzi yìzhí zài zhèlǐ. Wǒ xiǎng guò hěn duō rén kěnéng dōu zài zhè zhāng yǐzi shàng zuò guò, měi gè rén lái de shíhou dài zhe bùtóng de xīnqíng, zǒu de shíhou yěxǔ huì hǎo yìxiē. Yì zhāng gōngyuán de yǐzi bú huì shuōhuà, dàn tā mòmò de jiēnà le měi yí gè xūyào zuò xiàlái xiūxi yíhuìr de rén.
text_en: In the park there's a green bench, right under a big willow tree by the lake. Every weekend I come sit here for a while. The bench is somehow especially comfortable — the backrest angle is just right, and sitting down you naturally relax. Willow branches hang down, swaying gently in the breeze, sometimes brushing your shoulders. A few ducks swim around on the lake, occasionally dipping heads underwater to find food. Across the way some elders practice tai chi, movements slow and graceful, like swimming through water. After sitting long enough you notice all sorts of people passing: young people walking small dogs, mothers pushing strollers, couples holding hands, solo joggers. They come and go, but the bench is always here. I've thought about how many people have probably sat on this bench, each arriving with a different mood, perhaps leaving feeling a little better. A park bench can't speak, but it silently accepts everyone who needs to sit down and rest a moment.

questions.q_zh: 重复的行为暗示了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the repeated behavior imply?

options.text_en: this matters a lot to them

options.pinyin: tā méiyǒu bié de shì zuò
options.text_en: they have nothing else to do
others require them to do it

questions.explanation: The consistency of the habit signals deep personal importance, not mere routine.

questions.q_zh: 如果打破了这个习惯，可能会怎样？
questions.q_en: What might happen if this routine were broken?

options.pinyin: huì zhǎodào gèng hǎo de xíguàn

questions.explanation: The routine provides emotional scaffolding; disrupting it would remove that stability.

title: The Vegetable Delivery
text_zh: 每天凌晨四点左右，一辆绿色的小货车会停在我们小区旁边的超市门口。司机是一个皮肤黝黑的中年男人，他从后面打开车门，车里装满了一筐一筐的蔬菜和水果。超市还没有开门，但已经有一个店员等在那里了。两个人配合着把菜一筐一筐地搬进去，动作很快，几乎不说话。整个过程大概二十分钟。有一次我因为失眠起得很早，看到了这个场景。那些摆在超市里整整齐齐的蔬菜，原来是在大部分人还在睡觉的时候就送到了。我想到了这些菜的旅程：前一天下午在某个农场被采摘，然后送到批发市场分装，深夜装上货车，凌晨到达超市。每一步都有人在工作。等到我上午九十点去买菜的时候，这些菜已经洗干净了，贴上了价格标签，安安静静地躺在货架上。它们看起来好像一直就在那里，但其实它们经历了很长的旅程才到这里。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān língchén sì diǎn zuǒyòu, yí liàng lǜsè de xiǎo huòchē huì tíng zài wǒmen xiǎoqū pángbiān de chāoshì ménkǒu. Sījī shì yí gè pífū yǒu hēi de zhōngnián nánrén, tā cóng hòumiàn dǎkāi chē mén, chē lǐ zhuāng mǎn le yì kuāng yì kuāng de shūcài hé shuǐguǒ. Chāoshì hái méiyǒu kāi mén, dàn yǐjīng yǒu yí gè diànyuán děng zài nàlǐ le. Liǎng gè rén pèihé zhe bǎ cài yì kuāng yì kuāng de bān jìnqù, dòngzuò hěn kuài, jīhū bù shuōhuà. Zhěnggè guòchéng dàgài èrshí fēnzhōng. Yǒu yí cì wǒ yīnwèi shīmián qǐ de hěn zǎo, kàn dào le zhège chǎngjǐng. Nàxiē bǎi zài chāoshì lǐ zhěngzhěng qíqí de shūcài, yuánlái shì zài dà bùfen rén hái zài shuìjiào de shíhou jiù sòng dào le. Wǒ xiǎng dào le zhèxiē cài de lǚchéng: qián yì tiān xiàwǔ zài mǒu gè nóngchǎng bèi cǎizhāi, ránhòu sòng dào pīfā shìchǎng fēnzhuāng, shēnyè zhuāng shàng huòchē, língchén dàodá chāoshì. Měi yí bù dōu yǒu rén zài gōngzuò. Děng dào wǒ shàngwǔ jiǔ shí diǎn qù mǎi cài de shíhou, zhèxiē cài yǐjīng xǐ gānjìng le, tiē shàng le jiàgé biāoqiān, ān ān jìng jìng de tǎng zài huòjià shàng. Tāmen kàn qǐlái hǎoxiàng yìzhí jiù zài nàlǐ, dàn qíshí tāmen jīnglì le hěn cháng de lǚchéng cái dào zhèlǐ.
text_en: Every day around 4 AM, a green delivery truck parks outside the supermarket next to our compound. The driver is a dark-skinned middle-aged man. He opens the rear doors, the truck packed with crates of vegetables and fruit. The supermarket hasn't opened yet, but an employee is already waiting. The two work together, carrying crates inside one by one, moving quickly, barely speaking. The whole process takes about twenty minutes. Once I woke early from insomnia and witnessed this scene. Those vegetables arranged neatly in the supermarket — they arrive while most people are still sleeping. I thought about these vegetables' journey: picked the previous afternoon at some farm, sent to a wholesale market for sorting, loaded onto a truck late at night, arriving at the supermarket at dawn. At every step, someone is working. By the time I go shopping at 9 or 10 AM, these vegetables have been washed, price-tagged, lying quietly on shelves. They look as if they've always been there, but they actually traveled a long journey to get here.

questions.q_zh: 送菜的车大概几点到？
questions.q_en: Around what time does the delivery truck arrive?
questions.explanation: Stated in the passage: day around 4 AM.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么能看到送菜的场景？
questions.q_en: Why was the author able to see the delivery scene?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi shīmián qǐ de hěn zǎo

questions.explanation: The passage explains: once I woke early from insomnia and witnessed.

title: The Laundromat
text_zh: 我家附近新开了一家自助洗衣店，二十四小时营业。里面有十台洗衣机和六台烘干机，全部是投币或者扫码使用。没有店员，也没有收银台，只有一台自助缴费的机器和一个留言板。留言板上贴着一些小纸条，有人写着「请不要占用烘干机太久」，有人写着「谁的蓝色袜子忘在三号机器里了」，还有人画了一个笑脸写着「祝大家衣服都洗干净」。我喜欢在等衣服洗好的那四十分钟里坐在里面的塑料椅子上看书。洗衣机运转的声音有一种催眠的效果，嗡嗡嗡的，很规律。偶尔有人进来放衣服或者取衣服，大家互相点个头就行了。有一次一个年轻人进来，发现自己没有零钱也没有手机信号，我帮他扫码付了钱。他说「谢谢」，我说「没事」。这就是自助洗衣店里人与人之间交流的全部了。简单，但够了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn xīn kāi le yì jiā zìzhù xǐyī diàn, èrshísì xiǎoshí yíngyè. Lǐmiàn yǒu shí tái xǐyījī hé liù tái hōnggānjī, quánbù shì tóu bì huòzhě sǎo mǎ shǐyòng. Méiyǒu diànyuán, yě méiyǒu shōuyín tái, zhǐ yǒu yì tái zìzhù jiǎofèi de jīqì hé yí gè liúyán bǎn. Liúyán bǎn shàng tiē zhe yìxiē xiǎo zhǐtiáo, yǒu rén xiě zhe「qǐng bú yào zhànyòng hōnggānjī tài jiǔ」, yǒu rén xiě zhe「shéi de lán sè wàzi wàng zài sān hào jīqì lǐ le」, hái yǒu rén huà le yí gè xiàoliǎn xiě zhe「zhù dàjiā yīfu dōu xǐ gānjìng」. Wǒ xǐhuan zài děng yīfu xǐ hǎo de nà sìshí fēnzhōng lǐ zuò zài lǐmiàn de sùliào yǐzi shàng kàn shū. Xǐyījī yùnzhuǎn de shēngyīn yǒu yì zhǒng cuīmián de xiàoguǒ, wēng wēng wēng de, hěn guīlǜ. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén jìnlái fàng yīfu huòzhě qǔ yīfu, dàjiā hùxiāng diǎn gè tóu jiù xíng le. Yǒu yí cì yí gè niánqīng rén jìnlái, fāxiàn zìjǐ méiyǒu língqián yě méiyǒu shǒujī xìnhào, wǒ bāng tā sǎo mǎ fù le qián. Tā shuō「xièxie」, wǒ shuō「méi shì」. Zhè jiù shì zìzhù xǐyī diàn lǐ rén yǔ rén zhījiān jiāoliú de quánbù le. Jiǎndān, dàn gòu le.
text_en: A new self-service laundromat opened near my home, operating 24 hours. Inside are ten washing machines and six dryers, all coin or QR code operated. No staff, no register — just a self-service payment machine and a bulletin board. The board has little notes: someone wrote 'please don't hog the dryer too long,' someone wrote 'whose blue socks were left in machine #3,' and someone drew a smiley face with 'wishing everyone clean clothes.' I like sitting on the plastic chairs inside reading during the forty minutes my clothes wash. The washing machine's hum has a hypnotic effect — steady and rhythmic. Occasionally someone comes in to load or retrieve clothes; everyone just nods to each other. Once a young man came in and found he had neither coins nor phone signal; I scanned the code and paid for him. He said 'thanks,' I said 'no problem.' That's the entirety of human interaction in a self-service laundromat. Simple, but enough.

questions.q_zh: 留言板上有什么内容？
questions.q_en: What's on the bulletin board?

options.pinyin: kèrén xiě de gè zhǒng xiǎo zhǐtiáo
options.text_en: Various little notes written by customers

questions.explanation: Customers have filled the board with handwritten notes — personal messages, jokes, and reflections — making it a living record of the community that passes through.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'安静'不只是物理上的安静，还代表什么？
questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: The silence described extends inward -- it represents mental stillness, not just absence of sound.

title: The Street After Rain
text_zh: 下了一整个下午的雨终于停了。我推开窗户，空气里有一股清新的味道，像是大地刚刚洗了一个澡。楼下的街道湿漉漉的，路面反射着路灯的光，像一面镜子。树叶上挂着水珠，风一吹就落下来几颗，掉在地上发出很轻很轻的声音。我决定出去走走。雨后的城市特别安静，行人比平时少很多。路边的积水映着对面商店招牌的灯光，红色和黄色的光在水面上晃动，好像城市在水里有了另一个自己。一辆车开过去，轮胎压过水面发出「刷」的一声，水花溅起来又落下去。我走到一个小广场，发现有几个小孩在积水里跳来跳去，鞋子已经全湿了，但他们笑得很开心。他们的妈妈站在旁边皱着眉头但也忍不住笑了。也许雨后的城市最好的礼物就是这些积水——给大人一面镜子照一照被洗干净的世界，给小孩一个跳来跳去的游乐场。
text_pinyin: Xià le yí zhěng gè xiàwǔ de yǔ zhōngyú tíng le. Wǒ tuī kāi chuānghu, kōngqì lǐ yǒu yì gǔ qīngxīn de wèidào, xiàng shì dàdì gānggāng xǐ le yí gè zǎo. Lóu xià de jiēdào shī lù lù de, lù miàn fǎnshè zhe lùdēng de guāng, xiàng yí miàn jìngzi. Shùyè shàng guà zhe shuǐzhū, fēng yí chuī jiù luò xiàlái jǐ kē, diào zài dì shàng fāchū hěn qīng hěn qīng de shēngyīn. Wǒ juédìng chūqù zǒu zǒu. Yǔ hòu de chéngshì tèbié ānjìng, xíngrén bǐ píngshí shǎo hěn duō. Lù biān de jī shuǐ yìng zhe duìmiàn shāngdiàn zhāopai de dēngguāng, hóngsè hé huángsè de guāng zài shuǐ miàn shàng huàngdòng, hǎoxiàng chéngshì zài shuǐ lǐ yǒu le lìng yí gè zìjǐ. Yí liàng chē kāi guòqù, lúntāi yā guò shuǐ miàn fāchū「shuā」de yì shēng, shuǐhuā jiàn qǐlái yòu luò xiàqù. Wǒ zǒu dào yí gè xiǎo guǎngchǎng, fāxiàn yǒu jǐ gè xiǎohái zài jī shuǐ lǐ tiào lái tiào qù, xiézi yǐjīng quán shī le, dàn tāmen xiào de hěn kāixīn. Tāmen de māma zhàn zài pángbiān zhòu zhe méitóu dàn yě rěn bú zhù xiào le. Yěxǔ yǔ hòu de chéngshì zuì hǎo de lǐwù jiù shì zhèxiē jī shuǐ — gěi dàrén yí miàn jìngzi zhào yí zhào bèi xǐ gānjìng de shìjiè, gěi xiǎohái yí gè tiào lái tiào qù de yóulèchǎng.
text_en: The rain that fell all afternoon finally stopped. I pushed open the window — the air had a fresh smell, as if the earth had just taken a bath. The street below was wet, the road surface reflecting streetlights like a mirror. Water droplets hung on leaves, a few falling when the wind blew, hitting the ground with the faintest sound. I decided to go for a walk. The post-rain city was especially quiet, far fewer pedestrians than usual. Puddles by the road reflected shop sign lights opposite — red and yellow light rippling on the water, as if the city had another self inside the puddles. A car drove past, tires swishing through water, splashing up and falling back down. I walked to a small plaza and found several children jumping in puddles, shoes completely soaked but laughing happily. Their mother stood nearby frowning but couldn't help laughing too. Maybe the post-rain city's best gift is these puddles — giving adults a mirror to see a freshly washed world, giving children a playground to jump in.

questions.q_zh: 环境描写和人物心理之间有什么关系？
questions.q_en: What is the relationship between the setting and the character's psychology?

options.text_en: the environment mirrors the inner state

questions.explanation: The physical scene parallels the character's inner state -- outer landscape reflects inner landscape.

questions.q_zh: 如果把这段话和新闻报道比较，最大的区别是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the biggest difference between this passage and a news report?
questions.explanation: A news report would give who/what/when; this passage prioritizes emotional texture instead.

title: The Old Neighborhood
text_zh: 我长大的那个老小区现在还在，但已经变了很多。上周我回去看了一下。小区门口的保安换了人，以前是一个很和蔼的老大爷，总是笑着和每个人打招呼，现在换成了一个戴墨镜的年轻人，不太爱说话。进门以后发现路面重新铺过了，以前坑坑洼洼的水泥路变成了平整的柏油路。小花园还在，但里面的大树被砍了几棵，说是根太大影响了地下管道。我家住过的那栋楼外墙重新刷了漆，原来的淡黄色变成了灰白色。窗户还是老式的铝合金窗户，但有些住户已经换成了新的。楼下那个我小时候经常玩的滑梯还在，但明显旧了很多，颜色也褪了。我站在那里看了一会儿，觉得这个地方既熟悉又陌生。那些记忆中的画面像是贴在玻璃上的旧照片，透过它们可以看到后面新的风景，但照片本身已经开始变黄了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhǎng dà de nàge lǎo xiǎoqū xiànzài hái zài, dàn yǐjīng biàn le hěn duō. Shàng zhōu wǒ huíqù kàn le yíxià. Xiǎoqū ménkǒu de bǎo'ān huàn le rén, yǐqián shì yí gè hěn hé'ǎi de lǎo dàyé, zǒng shì xiào zhe hé měi gè rén dǎ zhāohu, xiànzài huàn chéng le yí gè dài mòjìng de niánqīng rén, bú tài ài shuōhuà. Jìn mén yǐhòu fāxiàn lùmiàn chóngxīn pū guò le, yǐqián kēngkēngwāwā de shuǐní lù biàn chéng le píngzhěng de bǎiyóu lù. Xiǎo huāyuán hái zài, dàn lǐmiàn de dà shù bèi kǎn le jǐ kē, shuō shì gēn tài dà yǐngxiǎng le dìxià guǎndào. Wǒ jiā zhù guò de nà dòng lóu wàiqiáng chóngxīn shuā le qī, yuánlái de dàn huángsè biàn chéng le huī báisè. Chuānghu háishi lǎo shì de lǚ héjīn chuānghu, dàn yǒuxiē zhùhù yǐjīng huàn chéng le xīn de. Lóu xià nàge wǒ xiǎo shíhou jīngcháng wán de huátī hái zài, dàn míngxiǎn jiù le hěn duō, yánsè yě tuì le. Wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn le yíhuìr, juéde zhège dìfāng jì shúxī yòu mòshēng. Nàxiē jìyì zhōng de huàmiàn xiàng shì tiē zài bōli shàng de jiù zhàopiàn, tòuguò tāmen kěyǐ kàn dào hòumiàn xīn de fēngjǐng, dàn zhàopiàn běnshēn yǐjīng kāishǐ biàn huáng le.
text_en: The old compound where I grew up is still there, but has changed a lot. I went back last week to look. The gate security has been replaced — before it was a kindly old man who always greeted everyone with a smile, now it's a young man in sunglasses who doesn't talk much. Inside, the roads have been repaved — the bumpy concrete replaced with smooth asphalt. The small garden is still there, but some big trees were cut down, apparently their roots interfered with underground pipes. The building I lived in has been repainted — from pale yellow to gray-white. The windows are still old-style aluminum frames, though some residents have replaced theirs with new ones. The slide downstairs that I played on as a child is still there, but obviously much older, colors faded. I stood there for a while, feeling the place was both familiar and strange. Those images from memory are like old photos stuck on glass — you can see the new scenery behind them, but the photos themselves have started to yellow.

questions.q_zh: 小区的路变成了什么样？
questions.q_en: How have the compound's roads changed?

options.text: 从坑洼的水泥路变成了平整的柏油路
options.pinyin: cóng kēngwā de shuǐní lù biàn chéng le píngzhěng de bǎiyóu lù
options.text_en: From bumpy concrete to smooth asphalt

questions.explanation: The shift is described: inside, the roads have been repaved.

questions.q_zh: 作者回去以后的感觉是什么？
questions.q_en: How did the author feel upon returning?
questions.explanation: Returning to the old neighborhood, the author stood still -- everything both familiar and strangely altered.

title: The Bus Stop Shelter
text_zh: 有一天突然下起了大雨，我没有带伞，跑到最近的公交站的遮雨棚下面躲雨。棚子不大，已经挤了七八个人了。大家站得很近，但都很安静，只有雨打在棚顶的声音。一个穿校服的女孩把书包抱在怀里怕淋湿，一个穿工作服的男人在看手机上的天气预报，一个提着菜篮子的老太太嘀咕着「这雨什么时候停」。我们都是陌生人，因为同一场雨被困在了同一个小小的空间里。这种感觉很奇特——平时在城市里大家各走各的路，谁也不看谁。但是一场雨就把我们聚在了一起，共享着同一个屋檐。过了大概十分钟，雨小了一些。有人先走了，有人还在等。一辆公交车来了，上去了两三个人。又过了一会儿，雨停了，大家陆续离开，各自走向不同的方向。棚子又空了，等着下一场雨、下一群陌生人。
text_pinyin: Yǒu yì tiān tūrán xià qǐ le dà yǔ, wǒ méiyǒu dài sǎn, pǎo dào zuì jìn de gōngjiāo zhàn de zhē yǔ péng xiàmiàn duǒ yǔ. Péngzi bú dà, yǐjīng jǐ le qī bā gè rén le. Dàjiā zhàn de hěn jìn, dàn dōu hěn ānjìng, zhǐ yǒu yǔ dǎ zài péng dǐng de shēngyīn. Yí gè chuān xiàofú de nǚhái bǎ shūbāo bào zài huái lǐ pà lín shī, yí gè chuān gōngzuò fú de nánrén zài kàn shǒujī shàng de tiānqì yùbào, yí gè tí zhe cài lánzi de lǎo tàitai dīgu zhe「zhè yǔ shénme shíhou tíng」. Wǒmen dōu shì mòshēng rén, yīnwèi tóng yì chǎng yǔ bèi kùn zài le tóng yí gè xiǎo xiǎo de kōngjiān lǐ. Zhè zhǒng gǎnjué hěn qítè — píngshí zài chéngshì lǐ dàjiā gè zǒu gè de lù, shéi yě bú kàn shéi. Dànshì yì chǎng yǔ jiù bǎ wǒmen jù zài le yìqǐ, gòngxiǎng zhe tóng yí gè wūyán. Guò le dàgài shí fēnzhōng, yǔ xiǎo le yìxiē. Yǒu rén xiān zǒu le, yǒu rén hái zài děng. Yí liàng gōngjiāo chē lái le, shàngqù le liǎng sān gè rén. Yòu guò le yíhuìr, yǔ tíng le, dàjiā lùxù líkāi, gèzì zǒu xiàng bùtóng de fāngxiàng. Péngzi yòu kōng le, děng zhe xià yì chǎng yǔ, xià yì qún mòshēng rén.
text_en: One day a sudden downpour started and I had no umbrella. I ran to the nearest bus stop shelter to hide from the rain. The shelter was small, already packed with seven or eight people. Everyone stood close together but stayed quiet, only the sound of rain hitting the roof. A girl in school uniform clutched her backpack to her chest to keep it dry. A man in work clothes checked the weather forecast on his phone. An old lady with a grocery basket muttered 'when is this rain going to stop.' We were all strangers, trapped in the same small space by the same rain. The feeling was peculiar — normally in the city everyone walks their own path, nobody looking at each other. But one rainstorm gathered us together, sharing the same eave. After about ten minutes the rain eased. Some left first, others kept waiting. A bus came, two or three people got on. A while later the rain stopped, everyone gradually departed in different directions. The shelter was empty again, waiting for the next rain, the next group of strangers.

questions.q_zh: 如果把这段话和新闻报道比较，最大的区别是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the biggest difference between this passage and a news report?
questions.explanation: A news report would give who/what/when; this passage prioritizes emotional texture instead.

questions.q_zh: 这段话和说明文的写法有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does this passage differ from an expository text?

options.text_en: focuses more on emotion and experience

questions.explanation: Expository text explains; this passage immerses the reader in subjective, felt experience.

title: The Spice Drawer
text_zh: 我的厨房里有一个专门放调料的抽屉。每次打开它都能闻到各种味道混在一起的香气——八角的甜、花椒的麻、干辣椒的辣，还有桂皮那种温暖的木头味。这些调料大部分是妈妈从老家寄来的，她觉得超市卖的不如家乡的好。每隔几个月她就会寄一个包裹，里面装着用塑料袋分装好的各种调料，每个袋子上面用记号笔写着名字。有时候她还会放一张纸条，写着「这个花椒是今年新摘的，特别香」或者「干辣椒可以先用油炸一下再用」。我把这些纸条都留着，夹在一本书里。调料抽屉里的东西在慢慢变化——用完的会被新的替换，偶尔也会加入一些以前没用过的。但无论怎么变，打开这个抽屉的那一刻，那种混合的香气总是让我想起家。那不是某一种具体的菜的味道，而是整个家的味道。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de chúfáng lǐ yǒu yí gè zhuānmén fàng tiáoliào de chōuti. Měi cì dǎkāi tā dōu néng wén dào gè zhǒng wèidào hùn zài yìqǐ de xiāngqì — bājiǎo de tián, huājiāo de má, gān làjiāo de là, hái yǒu guìpí nà zhǒng wēnnuǎn de mùtou wèi. Zhèxiē tiáoliào dà bùfen shì māma cóng lǎojiā jì lái de, tā juéde chāoshì mài de bù rú jiāxiāng de hǎo. Měi gé jǐ gè yuè tā jiù huì jì yí gè bāoguǒ, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe yòng sùliào dài fēn zhuāng hǎo de gè zhǒng tiáoliào, měi gè dàizi shàngmiàn yòng jìhao bǐ xiě zhe míngzi. Yǒu shíhou tā hái huì fàng yì zhāng zhǐtiáo, xiě zhe「zhège huājiāo shì jīnnián xīn zhāi de, tèbié xiāng」huòzhě「gān làjiāo kěyǐ xiān yòng yóu zhá yíxià zài yòng」. Wǒ bǎ zhèxiē zhǐtiáo dōu liú zhe, jiā zài yì běn shū lǐ. Tiáoliào chōuti lǐ de dōngxi zài mànmàn biànhuà — yòng wán de huì bèi xīn de tìhuàn, ǒu'ěr yě huì jiā rù yìxiē yǐqián méi yòng guò de. Dàn wúlùn zěnme biàn, dǎkāi zhège chōuti de nà yí kè, nà zhǒng hùnhé de xiāngqì zǒngshì ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ jiā. Nà bú shì mǒu yì zhǒng jùtǐ de cài de wèidào, ér shì zhěng gè jiā de wèidào.
text_en: In my kitchen there's a drawer dedicated to spices. Every time I open it, a mixed fragrance hits me — the sweetness of star anise, the numbing of Sichuan peppercorn, the heat of dried chili, and the warm woody scent of cinnamon bark. Most of these spices were sent by Mom from our hometown — she thinks supermarket ones aren't as good. Every few months she sends a package, filled with various spices portioned into plastic bags, each labeled with a marker. Sometimes she tucks in a note: 'This Sichuan pepper was freshly picked this year, especially fragrant,' or 'Try frying the dried chilies in oil before using.' I keep all these notes, pressed between the pages of a book. The contents of the spice drawer slowly change — used-up ones get replaced, occasionally something new gets added. But no matter how it changes, the moment I open that drawer, that mixed fragrance always makes me think of home. It's not the smell of any particular dish — it's the smell of the entire home.

questions.q_zh: 这种坚持说明这个人怎么样？

options.text_en: has no ideas of their own

questions.explanation: The unwillingness to give up reveals deep commitment and stubbornness of purpose.
