questions.explanation: The unwillingness to give up reveals deep commitment and stubbornness of purpose.

title: Washing Rice
text_zh: 做饭的第一步是淘米。把米放进电饭锅的内胆里，加水，用手轻轻搅动。水马上变成白色的，那是米上的淀粉。把水倒掉，再加水，再搅，再倒。一般淘两三次，水就变清了。这个过程很简单，简单到不需要思考，但我特别喜欢。冷水从手指间流过，米粒在水里轻轻碰撞，发出很小很小的声音。冬天的时候水很冰，手会被冻得有点红，但我还是不想用温水——总觉得用冷水淘出来的米饭更好吃，虽然这可能只是我的心理作用。淘好米以后加上水，水量刚好到手指第一个关节的位置。这是奶奶教我的方法，她说不管煮多少米，水到这个位置就刚好。我试过很多次，确实每次都恰到好处。按下电饭锅的按钮，等待大约半个小时。等米饭的时间刚好可以炒两个菜。这种节奏感，是做饭最让人安心的地方。
text_pinyin: Zuòfàn de dì yī bù shì táo mǐ. Bǎ mǐ fàng jìn diànfànguō de nèidǎn lǐ, jiā shuǐ, yòng shǒu qīngqīng jiǎodòng. Shuǐ mǎshàng biàn chéng báisè de, nà shì mǐ shàng de diànfěn. Bǎ shuǐ dào diào, zài jiā shuǐ, zài jiǎo, zài dào. Yìbān táo liǎng sān cì, shuǐ jiù biàn qīng le. Zhège guòchéng hěn jiǎndān, jiǎndān dào bù xūyào sīkǎo, dàn wǒ tèbié xǐhuan. Lěng shuǐ cóng shǒuzhǐ jiān liú guò, mǐ lì zài shuǐ lǐ qīngqīng pèngzhuàng, fāchū hěn xiǎo hěn xiǎo de shēngyīn. Dōngtiān de shíhou shuǐ hěn bīng, shǒu huì bèi dòng de yǒudiǎn hóng, dàn wǒ háishi bù xiǎng yòng wēn shuǐ — zǒng juéde yòng lěng shuǐ táo chūlái de mǐfàn gèng hǎochī, suīrán zhè kěnéng zhǐ shì wǒ de xīnlǐ zuòyòng. Táo hǎo mǐ yǐhòu jiā shàng shuǐ, shuǐ liàng gānghǎo dào shǒuzhǐ dì yī gè guānjié de wèizhì. Zhè shì nǎinai jiāo wǒ de fāngfǎ, tā shuō bùguǎn zhǔ duōshǎo mǐ, shuǐ dào zhège wèizhì jiù gānghǎo. Wǒ shì guò hěn duō cì, quèshí měi cì dōu qià dào hǎo chù. Àn xià diànfànguō de ànniǔ, děngdài dàyuē bàn gè xiǎoshí. Děng mǐfàn de shíjiān gānghǎo kěyǐ chǎo liǎng gè cài. Zhè zhǒng jiézòu gǎn, shì zuòfàn zuì ràng rén ānxīn de dìfāng.
text_en: The first step of cooking is washing rice. Put the rice in the rice cooker's inner pot, add water, gently stir with your hand. The water immediately turns white — that's starch from the rice. Pour it out, add water again, stir again, pour again. Usually after two or three rinses, the water runs clear. The process is simple, so simple it requires no thought, but I especially love it. Cold water flowing between fingers, rice grains gently bumping in the water, making the tiniest sound. In winter the water is icy, hands turn a bit red from the cold, but I still don't want to use warm water — I always feel cold-water-washed rice tastes better, though this might just be psychological. After washing, add water to just the first knuckle of your finger. This is the method grandma taught me — she said no matter how much rice you cook, water to this level is just right. I've tried many times; it really is perfect every time. Press the rice cooker button, wait about half an hour. The waiting time is just enough to stir-fry two dishes. This sense of rhythm is what makes cooking most reassuring.

questions.q_zh: 淘米的时候水为什么变白了？
questions.q_en: Why does the water turn white when washing rice?

options.text_en: Because of starch on the rice

options.pinyin: shuǐ běnshēn jiù shì bái de

questions.explanation: The passage explains: that's starch from the rice.

questions.q_zh: 自然环境对故事有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the natural setting affect the story?
questions.explanation: The natural details set an emotional backdrop that colors the entire scene.

title: The Vendor Who Remembers
text_zh: 菜市场里有一个卖鱼的大叔，我每次去都会在他那里买鱼。去了几次以后他就记住了我，远远看到我就会招手说「来了啊，今天的鲈鱼特别新鲜」。有时候我没打算买鱼，只是路过他的摊位，他也会和我聊几句，问我最近忙不忙，有没有好好吃饭。他的摊位永远是市场里最干净的一个。他每天早上三点多就去进货，挑最好的鱼，回来以后把冰铺好，鱼一条一条整齐地摆在上面。他帮客人杀鱼的动作特别利落，三两下就弄好了，还会主动问你要不要帮你把鱼切成块或者片。有一次我告诉他我在学做糖醋鱼，他不但帮我选了一条最合适的鱼，还详细地告诉我他妻子做糖醋鱼的方法。那天的糖醋鱼做得特别成功，我专门去告诉他，他笑得很开心。在这样的菜市场里买菜，买的不只是食材，还有一种人与人之间简单的善意。
text_pinyin: Càishìchǎng lǐ yǒu yí gè mài yú de dàshū, wǒ měi cì qù dōu huì zài tā nàlǐ mǎi yú. Qù le jǐ cì yǐhòu tā jiù jì zhù le wǒ, yuǎn yuǎn kàn dào wǒ jiù huì zhāo shǒu shuō「lái le a, jīntiān de lúyú tèbié xīnxiān」. Yǒu shíhou wǒ méi dǎsuàn mǎi yú, zhǐ shì lù guò tā de tānwèi, tā yě huì hé wǒ liáo jǐ jù, wèn wǒ zuìjìn máng bù máng, yǒu méiyǒu hǎohǎo chīfàn. Tā de tānwèi yǒngyuǎn shì shìchǎng lǐ zuì gānjìng de yí gè. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang sān diǎn duō jiù qù jìn huò, tiāo zuì hǎo de yú, huílái yǐhòu bǎ bīng pū hǎo, yú yì tiáo yì tiáo zhěngqí de bǎi zài shàngmiàn. Tā bāng kèrén shā yú de dòngzuò tèbié lìluo, sān liǎng xià jiù nòng hǎo le, hái huì zhǔdòng wèn nǐ yào bú yào bāng nǐ bǎ yú qiē chéng kuài huòzhě piàn. Yǒu yí cì wǒ gàosu tā wǒ zài xué zuò tángcù yú, tā búdàn bāng wǒ xuǎn le yì tiáo zuì héshì de yú, hái xiángxì de gàosu wǒ tā qīzi zuò tángcù yú de fāngfǎ. Nà tiān de tángcù yú zuò de tèbié chénggōng, wǒ zhuānmén qù gàosu tā, tā xiào de hěn kāixīn. Zài zhèyàng de càishìchǎng lǐ mǎi cài, mǎi de bù zhǐ shì shícái, hái yǒu yì zhǒng rén yǔ rén zhījiān jiǎndān de shànyì.
text_en: There's a fish vendor at the market — every time I go, I buy fish from him. After a few visits he remembered me; seeing me from afar he'd wave and say, 'You're here! Today's sea bass is especially fresh.' Sometimes I wasn't planning to buy fish, passing his stall, and he'd still chat with me, asking if I've been busy, if I've been eating well. His stall is always the cleanest in the market. Every morning past 3 AM he goes to stock up, picks the best fish, comes back and lays out the ice, fish arranged neatly one by one. His fish-cleaning movements are swift — done in a few quick strokes — and he'll proactively ask if you want it cut into chunks or slices. Once I told him I was learning to make sweet and sour fish. He not only picked the most suitable fish for me, but also explained in detail his wife's method for making it. That day's sweet and sour fish turned out great; I went specifically to tell him, and he smiled happily. Shopping at a market like this, you're buying not ingredients, but a simple goodwill between people.

questions.q_zh: 卖鱼的大叔每天几点去进货？
questions.q_en: What time does the fish vendor go to stock up each day?
questions.explanation: The vendor goes to the wholesale market past 3 AM each day to select the freshest fish.

questions.q_zh: 作者做糖醋鱼的方法是谁教的？
questions.q_en: Who taught the author how to make sweet and sour fish?

options.text: 卖鱼大叔教了他妻子的做法
options.pinyin: mài yú dàshū jiāo le tā qīzi de zuòfǎ
options.text_en: The fish vendor shared his wife's method

questions.explanation: The passage identifies: not only picked the most suitable fish for me.

title: The Smell of Bread
text_zh: 我住的小区附近有一家面包店，每天下午三点多就开始飘出烤面包的香味。那种味道是温暖的、甜甜的，像一条看不见的绳子把你往店里拉。面包店不大，但每一样都是老板自己做的。他是一个四十岁左右的男人，以前在一家大公司工作，后来辞职去学了烘焙，回来开了这家店。他说他不想做太多种类，就做几种最基本的面包，但每一种都要做到最好。他的法式面包外面很脆，里面很软，有一种淡淡的麦香。吐司也好吃，切片以后烤一下，涂上黄油，就是最完美的早餐。我喜欢在下午三点多路过的时候进去坐一会儿。店里有一张小桌子和两把椅子，可以坐着吃一块刚出炉的面包，喝一杯咖啡。面包店里永远是温暖的，不管外面是什么天气。这种温暖不只是因为烤箱的热，还因为一个人在认真做一件事的那种专注和踏实。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de xiǎoqū fùjìn yǒu yì jiā miànbāo diàn, měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn duō jiù kāishǐ piāo chū kǎo miànbāo de xiāngwèi. Nà zhǒng wèidào shì wēnnuǎn de, tián tián de, xiàng yì tiáo kàn bú jiàn de shéngzi bǎ nǐ wǎng diàn lǐ lā. Miànbāo diàn bú dà, dàn měi yí yàng dōu shì lǎobǎn zìjǐ zuò de. Tā shì yí gè sìshí suì zuǒyòu de nánrén, yǐqián zài yì jiā dà gōngsī gōngzuò, hòulái cízhí qù xué le hōngbèi, huílái kāi le zhè jiā diàn. Tā shuō tā bù xiǎng zuò tài duō zhǒnglèi, jiù zuò jǐ zhǒng zuì jīběn de miànbāo, dàn měi yì zhǒng dōu yào zuò dào zuì hǎo. Tā de Fǎshì miànbāo wàimiàn hěn cuì, lǐmiàn hěn ruǎn, yǒu yì zhǒng dàn dàn de màixiāng. Tǔsī yě hǎochī, qiē piàn yǐhòu kǎo yíxià, tú shàng huángyóu, jiù shì zuì wánměi de zǎocān. Wǒ xǐhuan zài xiàwǔ sān diǎn duō lùguò de shíhou jìnqù zuò yíhuìr. Diàn lǐ yǒu yì zhāng xiǎo zhuōzi hé liǎng bǎ yǐzi, kěyǐ zuò zhe chī yí kuài gāng chū lú de miànbāo, hē yì bēi kāfēi. Miànbāo diàn lǐ yǒngyuǎn shì wēnnuǎn de, bùguǎn wàimiàn shì shénme tiānqì. Zhè zhǒng wēnnuǎn bù zhǐ shì yīnwèi kǎoxiāng de rè, hái yīnwèi yí gè rén zài rènzhēn zuò yí jiàn shì de nà zhǒng zhuānzhù hé tāshi.
text_en: Near my neighborhood there's a bakery that starts filling the air with the smell of baking bread past 3 PM every day. That scent is warm and sweet, like an invisible thread pulling you into the shop. The bakery isn't big, but everything is made by the owner himself. He's a man around forty who used to work at a large company, then quit to learn baking and came back to open this shop. He says he doesn't want to make too many kinds — a few basic breads, but each one has to be the best it can be. His French bread is crispy on the outside, soft inside, with a faint wheat fragrance. The toast is good too — slice and toast it, spread with butter, and it's the perfect breakfast. I like to stop in when passing by around 3 PM. There's a small table and two chairs where you can sit and eat a piece of freshly baked bread with a cup of coffee. The bakery is always warm, no matter the weather outside. That warmth isn't from the oven's heat — it comes from a person's focus and groundedness in doing one thing seriously.

questions.q_zh: 面包店老板以前做什么工作？
questions.q_en: What did the bakery owner do before?
questions.explanation: The bakery owner is around forty and previously worked at a large company before leaving to open his own shop — a career change from corporate life.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中什么是'看不见'但能感觉到的？
questions.q_en: What in this passage is 'invisible' but can be felt?

options.text: 人物的情感和内心世界
options.pinyin: rénwù de qínggǎn hé nèixīn shìjiè
options.text_en: the character's emotions and inner world

questions.explanation: No feelings are named outright, yet the actions and details radiate emotional undercurrent.

title: Cutting Vegetables Slowly
text_zh: 有些人觉得切菜是做饭里最无聊的部分，但我觉得这是最让人放松的一步。把所有食材洗干净，放在案板上，一样一样地切。切土豆的时候，先切成片，再切成丝，尽量让每一根丝都差不多粗细。切胡萝卜的时候，可以切成圆片也可以切成滚刀块，不同的切法适合不同的做法。切葱花的时候，葱的香味会慢慢散发出来，清清的、淡淡的。我喜欢用一把好刀，锋利的刀切起来很顺畅，每一刀下去都很干脆。切菜的过程让我的注意力完全集中在手上，脑子里不再想工作的事，不再想明天要做什么。只有刀和案板，只有食材在手里慢慢变成我想要的形状。这种专注本身就是一种休息。等所有的菜都切好了，整齐地摆在小盘子里，还没开始炒，心情就已经变好了。准备的过程和做出来的结果一样重要。
text_pinyin: Yǒuxiē rén juéde qiē cài shì zuòfàn lǐ zuì wúliáo de bùfen, dàn wǒ juéde zhè shì zuì ràng rén fàngsōng de yí bù. Bǎ suǒyǒu shícái xǐ gānjìng, fàng zài ànbǎn shàng, yí yàng yí yàng de qiē. Qiē tǔdòu de shíhou, xiān qiē chéng piàn, zài qiē chéng sī, jǐnliàng ràng měi yì gēn sī dōu chàbuduō cū xì. Qiē húluóbo de shíhou, kěyǐ qiē chéng yuán piàn yě kěyǐ qiē chéng gǔndāo kuài, bùtóng de qiēfǎ shìhé bùtóng de zuòfǎ. Qiē cōnghuā de shíhou, cōng de xiāngwèi huì mànmàn sànfā chūlái, qīng qīng de, dàn dàn de. Wǒ xǐhuan yòng yì bǎ hǎo dāo, fēnglì de dāo qiē qǐlái hěn shùnchàng, měi yí dāo xià qù dōu hěn gāncuì. Qiē cài de guòchéng ràng wǒ de zhùyìlì wánquán jízhōng zài shǒu shàng, nǎozi lǐ bú zài xiǎng gōngzuò de shì, bú zài xiǎng míngtiān yào zuò shénme. Zhǐ yǒu dāo hé ànbǎn, zhǐ yǒu shícái zài shǒu lǐ mànmàn biàn chéng wǒ xiǎng yào de xíngzhuàng. Zhè zhǒng zhuānzhù běnshēn jiù shì yì zhǒng xiūxi. Děng suǒyǒu de cài dōu qiē hǎo le, zhěngqí de bǎi zài xiǎo pánzi lǐ, hái méi kāishǐ chǎo, xīnqíng jiù yǐjīng biàn hǎo le. Zhǔnbèi de guòchéng hé zuò chūlái de jiéguǒ yíyàng zhòngyào.
text_en: Some people think chopping vegetables is the most boring part of cooking, but I think it's the most relaxing step. Wash all the ingredients, place them on the cutting board, and cut them one by one. When cutting potatoes, first slice, then julienne, trying to keep each strand roughly the same thickness. When cutting carrots, you can do round slices or rolling-knife chunks — different cuts suit different cooking methods. When chopping scallions, their fragrance slowly releases, light and delicate. I like using a good knife — a sharp knife cuts smoothly, each stroke clean and decisive. The process of cutting focuses my attention entirely on my hands. My mind stops thinking about work, stops thinking about tomorrow. Just the knife and the board, just ingredients slowly taking the shape I want. That focus itself is a form of rest. Once everything is cut, arranged neatly in small dishes, before I've even started cooking, my mood has already improved. The process of preparation is as important as the finished result.

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得切菜有什么好处？
questions.q_en: What benefit does the author find in chopping vegetables?

options.text: 注意力集中，不再想工作
options.pinyin: zhùyìlì jízhōng, bú zài xiǎng gōngzuò
cài qiē de hǎokàn gèng hǎochī

questions.explanation: The repetitive focus of chopping vegetables forces full attention on the knife and the cutting board, pushing out work-related thoughts and providing mental relief.

questions.q_zh: 这段话对工作态度的看法是什么？
questions.q_en: What view of work attitude does this passage present?

options.text_en: work is less important than life

questions.explanation: The careful, unhurried approach to each task signals that diligence carries its own dignity.

title: A Bowl of Noodles in the Rain
text_zh: 那天下了很大的雨，我忘了带伞，从地铁站跑到家的时候已经全身湿透了。换了干衣服以后，还是觉得很冷，身体里面好像有一种寒气赶不走。我决定给自己煮一碗热面条。锅里烧上水，找出家里的挂面。等水开了，把面条放进去，煮两三分钟。同时在另一个小锅里热了一些昨天的鸡汤，打了一个鸡蛋进去，等蛋白凝固了捞出来。面条煮好以后捞到碗里，浇上热鸡汤，放上荷包蛋，撒一点葱花和白芝麻。端着碗坐到沙发上，喝了第一口汤的时候，那种暖意从喉咙一直到了胃里，然后慢慢扩散到全身。窗外的雨还在下，但我已经不觉得冷了。面条很普通，但在那个时刻，它就是全世界最好吃的东西。有时候食物的意义不在于它本身有多特别，而在于你在什么时候遇到了它。
text_pinyin: Nà tiān xià le hěn dà de yǔ, wǒ wàng le dài sǎn, cóng dìtiě zhàn pǎo dào jiā de shíhou yǐjīng quánshēn shī tòu le. Huàn le gān yīfu yǐhòu, háishi juéde hěn lěng, shēntǐ lǐmiàn hǎoxiàng yǒu yì zhǒng hánqì gǎn bù zǒu. Wǒ juédìng gěi zìjǐ zhǔ yì wǎn rè miàntiáo. Guō lǐ shāo shàng shuǐ, zhǎo chū jiā lǐ de guàmiàn. Děng shuǐ kāi le, bǎ miàntiáo fàng jìnqù, zhǔ liǎng sān fēnzhōng. Tóngshí zài lìng yí gè xiǎo guō lǐ rè le yìxiē zuótiān de jītāng, dǎ le yí gè jīdàn jìnqù, děng dànbái nínggù le lāo chūlái. Miàntiáo zhǔ hǎo yǐhòu lāo dào wǎn lǐ, jiāo shàng rè jītāng, fàng shàng hébāo dàn, sā yìdiǎn cōnghuā hé bái zhīma. Duān zhe wǎn zuò dào shāfā shàng, hē le dì yī kǒu tāng de shíhou, nà zhǒng nuǎn yì cóng hóulóng yìzhí dào le wèi lǐ, ránhòu mànmàn kuòsàn dào quánshēn. Chuāng wài de yǔ hái zài xià, dàn wǒ yǐjīng bù juéde lěng le. Miàntiáo hěn pǔtōng, dàn zài nàge shíkè, tā jiù shì quán shìjiè zuì hǎochī de dōngxi. Yǒu shíhou shíwù de yìyì bú zài yú tā běnshēn yǒu duō tèbié, ér zài yú nǐ zài shénme shíhou yù dào le tā.
text_en: It rained heavily that day. I forgot my umbrella, and by the time I ran from the subway to home I was completely soaked. After changing into dry clothes, I still felt cold — as if a chill inside my body wouldn't leave. I decided to cook myself a bowl of hot noodles. Put water on to boil, found some dried noodles. When the water boiled, dropped them in, cooked two or three minutes. Meanwhile in another small pot I heated some of yesterday's chicken broth, cracked an egg in, waited for the white to set and scooped it out. Drained the noodles into a bowl, ladled on hot chicken broth, placed the poached egg on top, sprinkled some scallion and white sesame. Holding the bowl on the sofa, the first sip of broth — that warmth traveled from throat to stomach, then slowly spread through my whole body. Rain still falling outside, but I no longer felt cold. The noodles were ordinary, but in that moment they were the most delicious thing in the world. Sometimes food's meaning isn't in how special it is, but in when you encounter it.

questions.q_en: Why was the author soaked?

options.text_en: Forgot umbrella and ran home in rain

title: Fermenting Time
text_zh: 上个月我开始学做面包，才发现做面包最重要的一步不是揉面，而是等待。把面粉、水、酵母和盐混合在一起，揉成一个光滑的面团，然后盖上湿布，放在温暖的地方，等它慢慢发酵。这个过程需要一到两个小时，有时候更长。你什么都不需要做，只是等。等待的时候我会看看书或者做做家务，偶尔去看一眼面团有没有变大。每次揭开布看到面团涨到原来两倍大的时候，都会有一种小小的惊喜。酵母是一种活的生物，它在面团里慢慢地工作，产生气泡，让面团变得蓬松。这个过程不能着急，温度太高面团会发酸，温度太低又发不起来。你只能给它合适的条件，然后相信时间会做好它的工作。做面包教会了我一件事：有些东西是催不出来的，你只能耐心等待，让它按照自己的节奏完成。
text_pinyin: Shàng gè yuè wǒ kāishǐ xué zuò miànbāo, cái fāxiàn zuò miànbāo zuì zhòngyào de yí bù bú shì róu miàn, ér shì děngdài. Bǎ miànfěn, shuǐ, jiàomǔ hé yán hùnhé zài yìqǐ, róu chéng yí gè guānghuá de miàntuán, ránhòu gài shàng shī bù, fàng zài wēnnuǎn de dìfāng, děng tā mànmàn fājiào. Zhège guòchéng xūyào yí dào liǎng gè xiǎoshí, yǒu shíhou gèng cháng. Nǐ shénme dōu bù xūyào zuò, zhǐ shì děng. Děngdài de shíhou wǒ huì kànkan shū huòzhě zuòzuo jiāwù, ǒu'ěr qù kàn yì yǎn miàntuán yǒu méiyǒu biàn dà. Měi cì jiē kāi bù kàn dào miàntuán zhǎng dào yuánlái liǎng bèi dà de shíhou, dōu huì yǒu yì zhǒng xiǎo xiǎo de jīngxǐ. Jiàomǔ shì yì zhǒng huó de shēngwù, tā zài miàntuán lǐ mànmàn de gōngzuò, chǎnshēng qìpào, ràng miàntuán biàn de péng sōng. Zhège guòchéng bù néng zhāojí, wēndù tài gāo miàntuán huì fā suān, wēndù tài dī yòu fā bù qǐlái. Nǐ zhǐ néng gěi tā héshì de tiáojiàn, ránhòu xiāngxìn shíjiān huì zuò hǎo tā de gōngzuò. Zuò miànbāo jiāo huì le wǒ yí jiàn shì: yǒuxiē dōngxi shì cuī bù chūlái de, nǐ zhǐ néng nàixīn děngdài, ràng tā ànzhào zìjǐ de jiézòu wánchéng.
text_en: Last month I started learning to bake bread and discovered the most important step isn't kneading — it's waiting. Mix flour, water, yeast, and salt together, knead into a smooth dough, then cover with a damp cloth, place somewhere warm, and let it slowly ferment. This takes one to two hours, sometimes longer. You don't need to do anything — just wait. While waiting I'd read or do housework, occasionally checking if the dough has grown. Every time I lift the cloth and see it's doubled in size, there's a small thrill. Yeast is a living organism, working slowly inside the dough, producing bubbles, making it fluffy. The process can't be rushed — too warm and the dough turns sour, too cool and it won't rise. You can only provide the right conditions, then trust time to do its work. Bread-making taught me something: some things can't be hurried. You can only wait patiently, letting them complete at their own pace.

questions.explanation: Too-high temperature makes the dough ferment too fast and turn sour -- precision matters.

title: Lunch with an Old Colleague
text_zh: 昨天和一个三年没见的老同事约了午饭。她现在换了一份新工作，比以前轻松一些，人看起来也比以前开朗了。我们去了一家安静的日式餐厅，点了两份定食。等菜的时候聊了聊各自的近况，说着说着就说到了以前一起加班的日子。那时候我们经常一起加班到晚上十点，然后去公司楼下的便利店买关东煮。两个人站在便利店门口吃着热乎乎的萝卜和豆腐，说着明天还要干什么。那些日子虽然累，但回忆起来竟然有一种温暖的感觉。菜上来了，我们边吃边聊。她说她最近开始学烹饪了，周末会花半天的时间做一顿饭，慢慢吃。「以前觉得做饭浪费时间，」她说，「现在觉得那是给自己最好的时间。」我们吃了两个小时才离开。分别的时候她说下次她做饭请我吃。走在回去的路上，我觉得很幸运，有一些朋友即使很久不见，一坐下来就能接上以前的话题。
text_pinyin: Zuótiān hé yí gè sān nián méi jiàn de lǎo tóngshì yuē le wǔfàn. Tā xiànzài huàn le yí fèn xīn gōngzuò, bǐ yǐqián qīngsōng yìxiē, rén kàn qǐlái yě bǐ yǐqián kāilǎng le. Wǒmen qù le yì jiā ānjìng de Rìshì cāntīng, diǎn le liǎng fèn dìngshí. Děng cài de shíhou liáo le liáo gèzì de jìnkuàng, shuō zhe shuō zhe jiù shuō dào le yǐqián yìqǐ jiābān de rìzi. Nà shíhou wǒmen jīngcháng yìqǐ jiābān dào wǎnshang shí diǎn, ránhòu qù gōngsī lóu xià de biànlì diàn mǎi guāndōng zhǔ. Liǎng gè rén zhàn zài biànlì diàn ménkǒu chī zhe rè hūhū de luóbo hé dòufu, shuō zhe míngtiān hái yào gàn shénme. Nàxiē rìzi suīrán lèi, dàn huíyì qǐlái jìngrán yǒu yì zhǒng wēnnuǎn de gǎnjué. Cài shàng lái le, wǒmen biān chī biān liáo. Tā shuō tā zuìjìn kāishǐ xué pēngrèn le, zhōumò huì huā bàn tiān de shíjiān zuò yí dùn fàn, mànmàn chī.「Yǐqián juéde zuòfàn làngfèi shíjiān,」tā shuō,「xiànzài juéde nà shì gěi zìjǐ zuì hǎo de shíjiān.」Wǒmen chī le liǎng gè xiǎoshí cái líkāi. Fēnbié de shíhou tā shuō xià cì tā zuòfàn qǐng wǒ chī. Zǒu zài huíqù de lù shàng, wǒ juéde hěn xìngyùn, yǒu yìxiē péngyou jíshǐ hěn jiǔ bú jiàn, yí zuò xiàlái jiù néng jiē shàng yǐqián de huàtí.
text_en: Yesterday I had lunch with a former colleague I hadn't seen in three years. She has a new job now, more relaxed than before, and she looks brighter. We went to a quiet Japanese restaurant and ordered two set meals. While waiting we caught up, and the conversation drifted to our old days of working overtime together. Back then we'd often work until 10 PM, then go to the convenience store downstairs for oden. Standing outside the store eating hot radish and tofu, talking about what we had to do tomorrow. Those days were tiring, but looking back, there's a surprising warmth to them. The food arrived and we ate and talked. She said she's recently started learning to cook — spending half a day on weekends making a meal, eating slowly. 'I used to think cooking was a waste of time,' she said. 'Now I think it's the best time I give myself.' We didn't leave for two hours. When we parted she said next time she'd cook for me. Walking back, I felt lucky — some friends, even after years apart, can pick right up where the conversation left off the moment you sit down.
