title: The Egg That Took Practice
text_zh: 煎一个完美的荷包蛋比想象的要难。我刚开始学做饭的时候，以为煎蛋是最简单的事情——锅里放油，打个蛋，等一等就好了。结果不是焦了就是散了，要么蛋黄已经全熟了但蛋白下面还是生的。后来我上网查了一下，才知道煎蛋有很多讲究。首先油的温度很重要，太热蛋一下去就会起泡，边缘变焦；太冷又粘锅。最好的方法是中小火热锅，加一点油，等油微微冒烟的时候把蛋打进去。然后不要急着翻面，让蛋白慢慢凝固。如果想要蛋黄还是流动的，大概煎两分钟就够了；如果想要全熟，可以盖上锅盖焖一分钟。我练了大概一个星期，每天早上煎一个蛋，终于煎出了让自己满意的荷包蛋——蛋白底部微微焦黄，蛋黄还是流动的，撒一点盐和黑胡椒就很好吃。一个小小的荷包蛋，让我明白了一件事：即使是最简单的东西，要做好也需要反复练习。
text_pinyin: Jiān yí gè wánměi de hébāo dàn bǐ xiǎngxiàng de yào nán. Wǒ gāng kāishǐ xué zuòfàn de shíhou, yǐwéi jiān dàn shì zuì jiǎndān de shìqing — guō lǐ fàng yóu, dǎ gè dàn, děng yì děng jiù hǎo le. Jiéguǒ bú shì jiāo le jiù shì sàn le, yàome dànhuáng yǐjīng quán shú le dàn dànbái xiàmiàn háishi shēng de. Hòulái wǒ shàng wǎng chá le yíxià, cái zhīdào jiān dàn yǒu hěn duō jiǎngjiū. Shǒuxiān yóu de wēndù hěn zhòngyào, tài rè dàn yí xià qù jiù huì qǐ pào, biānyuán biàn jiāo; tài lěng yòu zhān guō. Zuì hǎo de fāngfǎ shì zhōng xiǎo huǒ rè guō, jiā yìdiǎn yóu, děng yóu wēiwēi mào yān de shíhou bǎ dàn dǎ jìnqù. Ránhòu bú yào jí zhe fān miàn, ràng dànbái mànmàn nínggù. Rúguǒ xiǎng yào dànhuáng háishi liúdòng de, dàgài jiān liǎng fēnzhōng jiù gòu le; rúguǒ xiǎng yào quán shú, kěyǐ gài shàng guō gài mèn yì fēnzhōng. Wǒ liàn le dàgài yí gè xīngqī, měi tiān zǎoshang jiān yí gè dàn, zhōngyú jiān chū le ràng zìjǐ mǎnyì de hébāo dàn — dànbái dǐbù wēiwēi jiāo huáng, dànhuáng háishi liúdòng de, sā yìdiǎn yán hé hēi hújiāo jiù hěn hǎochī. Yí gè xiǎo xiǎo de hébāo dàn, ràng wǒ míngbai le yí jiàn shì: jíshǐ shì zuì jiǎndān de dōngxi, yào zuò hǎo yě xūyào fǎnfù liànxí.
text_en: Frying a perfect sunny-side-up egg is harder than you'd think. When I first started cooking, I thought frying an egg was the simplest thing — oil in the pan, crack an egg, wait and done. But it either burned or fell apart, or the yolk was fully cooked while the white underneath was still raw. Later I looked it up online and discovered there's a lot to it. First, oil temperature matters — too hot and the egg bubbles immediately, edges burn; too cold and it sticks. The best method is medium-low heat, a bit of oil, and when the oil just barely smokes, crack the egg in. Then don't rush to flip — let the white slowly set. For a runny yolk, about two minutes is enough; for fully cooked, cover with a lid for one minute. I practiced about a week, frying one egg every morning, and finally made an egg I was satisfied with — white slightly golden on the bottom, yolk still runny, sprinkle of salt and black pepper and it's delicious. One small fried egg taught me something: even the simplest things require repeated practice to do well.

questions.q_zh: 刚开始煎蛋的时候出了什么问题？
questions.q_en: What went wrong when first frying eggs?

options.pinyin: bú shì jiāo le jiù shì sàn le

questions.explanation: Early attempts at frying eggs failed in two predictable ways — either the bottom burned from too much heat, or the egg broke apart from clumsy flipping.

questions.q_zh: 作者练了多长时间才学会煎蛋？
questions.q_en: How long did the author practice before learning to fry eggs?
questions.explanation: The author practiced for about a week before the egg came out right -- mastery through repetition.

title: Tea Eggs on the Train
text_zh: 小时候坐火车回老家，车程大概八个小时。那时候还没有高铁，绿皮火车慢慢地穿过一个又一个小站。车厢里总是有一种特别的味道——方便面、橘子皮，还有茶叶蛋。卖茶叶蛋的阿姨推着一个小车，从车厢的一头走到另一头，一边走一边喊「茶叶蛋、茶叶蛋，热乎的茶叶蛋」。那声音在嘈杂的车厢里特别好听。茶叶蛋是用茶叶、八角、桂皮和酱油一起煮的，蛋壳上有一道一道的裂纹，像是一幅画。剥开以后蛋白是深棕色的，有一种特别的咸香味。咬一口，蛋黄粉粉的，配上那种茶叶和香料的味道，比家里煮的白水蛋好吃太多了。我每次坐火车都会买两个。到了深夜，大部分人都睡了，车厢的灯暗了下来，只有车轮碰到铁轨的声音。偶尔醒来，看看窗外黑黑的夜，再闻闻手上还残留的茶叶蛋的味道，就觉得很安心。那种安心和现在坐高铁的感觉完全不一样。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo shíhou zuò huǒchē huí lǎojiā, chēchéng dàgài bā gè xiǎoshí. Nà shíhou hái méiyǒu gāotiě, lǜpí huǒchē mànmàn de chuān guò yí gè yòu yí gè xiǎo zhàn. Chēxiāng lǐ zǒngshì yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de wèidào — fāngbiàn miàn, júzi pí, hái yǒu cháyè dàn. Mài cháyè dàn de āyí tuī zhe yí gè xiǎo chē, cóng chēxiāng de yì tóu zǒu dào lìng yì tóu, yìbiān zǒu yìbiān hǎn「cháyè dàn, cháyè dàn, rè hū de cháyè dàn」. Nà shēngyīn zài cáozá de chēxiāng lǐ tèbié hǎotīng. Cháyè dàn shì yòng cháyè, bājiǎo, guìpí hé jiàngyóu yìqǐ zhǔ de, dànké shàng yǒu yí dào yí dào de lièwén, xiàng shì yì fú huà. Bāo kāi yǐhòu dànbái shì shēn zōngsè de, yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de xián xiāng wèi. Yǎo yì kǒu, dànhuáng fěn fěn de, pèi shàng nà zhǒng cháyè hé xiāngliào de wèidào, bǐ jiā lǐ zhǔ de bái shuǐ dàn hǎochī tài duō le. Wǒ měi cì zuò huǒchē dōu huì mǎi liǎng gè. Dào le shēnyè, dà bùfen rén dōu shuì le, chēxiāng de dēng àn le xiàlái, zhǐ yǒu chēlún pèng dào tiěguǐ de shēngyīn. Ǒu'ěr xǐng lái, kànkan chuāng wài hēi hēi de yè, zài wén wen shǒu shàng hái cánliú de cháyè dàn de wèidào, jiù juéde hěn ānxīn. Nà zhǒng ānxīn hé xiànzài zuò gāotiě de gǎnjué wánquán bù yíyàng.
text_en: As a child, taking the train back to my hometown was about eight hours. High-speed rail didn't exist yet; the old green trains crawled through station after station. The carriages always had a distinctive smell — instant noodles, orange peels, and tea eggs. The auntie selling tea eggs pushed a small cart from one end of the car to the other, calling out, 'Tea eggs, tea eggs, hot tea eggs.' Her voice sounded wonderful amid the carriage noise. Tea eggs are simmered with tea leaves, star anise, cinnamon bark, and soy sauce. The shells have a web of cracks, like a painting. Peeled, the white is deep brown with a special savory fragrance. One bite — the yolk is powdery, paired with the tea and spice flavors, so much better than plain boiled eggs at home. I'd buy two every train ride. Late at night, most people asleep, carriage lights dimmed, only the sound of wheels on tracks. Waking occasionally, looking at the dark night outside, smelling the lingering tea egg scent on my hands, I felt at peace. That kind of peace is nothing like riding high-speed rail today.

questions.q_zh: 回老家坐火车要多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long was the train ride home?
questions.explanation: Stated directly: as a child, taking the train back to my.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'安静'不只是物理上的安静，还代表什么？
questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: The silence described extends inward -- it represents mental stillness, not just absence of sound.

title: The Tofu Seller's Philosophy
text_zh: 早市里卖豆腐的老张每天凌晨三点就开始做豆腐了。他做了三十多年，从他父亲那里学来的手艺。我有一次很早去市场，看到他的摊位还在准备。他正把刚做好的豆腐从模具里小心地拿出来，一块一块地摆好。我问他做了这么多年不觉得无聊吗。他想了一下说，「每天做的豆腐都不完全一样。水的温度、黄豆的品质、天气的变化，都会影响最后的结果。做了三十年我还在学习怎么做得更好。」他又说，「有些人觉得豆腐就是豆腐，没什么特别的。但如果你仔细尝，好的豆腐有一种淡淡的甜味，口感滑嫩，入口即化。做到这个程度不容易。」我买了一块他的豆腐回家，没有做任何复杂的菜，就是凉拌豆腐——切成块，放上葱花、香菜、酱油和香油。一口吃下去，确实有一种淡淡的甜。我想起老张的话，觉得他说的不只是豆腐，也是一种对生活的态度。
text_pinyin: Zǎoshì lǐ mài dòufu de Lǎo Zhāng měi tiān língchén sān diǎn jiù kāishǐ zuò dòufu le. Tā zuò le sānshí duō nián, cóng tā fùqīn nàlǐ xué lái de shǒuyì. Wǒ yǒu yí cì hěn zǎo qù shìchǎng, kàn dào tā de tānwèi hái zài zhǔnbèi. Tā zhèng bǎ gāng zuò hǎo de dòufu cóng mújù lǐ xiǎoxīn de ná chūlái, yí kuài yí kuài de bǎi hǎo. Wǒ wèn tā zuò le zhème duō nián bù juéde wúliáo ma. Tā xiǎng le yíxià shuō,「měi tiān zuò de dòufu dōu bù wánquán yíyàng. Shuǐ de wēndù, huángdòu de pǐnzhì, tiānqì de biànhuà, dōu huì yǐngxiǎng zuìhòu de jiéguǒ. Zuò le sānshí nián wǒ hái zài xuéxí zěnme zuò de gèng hǎo.」Tā yòu shuō,「yǒuxiē rén juéde dòufu jiù shì dòufu, méi shénme tèbié de. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ zǐxì cháng, hǎo de dòufu yǒu yì zhǒng dàn dàn de tián wèi, kǒugǎn huá nèn, rù kǒu jí huà. Zuò dào zhège chéngdù bù róngyì.」Wǒ mǎi le yí kuài tā de dòufu huí jiā, méiyǒu zuò rènhé fùzá de cài, jiù shì liángbàn dòufu — qiē chéng kuài, fàng shàng cōnghuā, xiāngcài, jiàngyóu hé xiāngyóu. Yì kǒu chī xiàqù, quèshí yǒu yì zhǒng dàn dàn de tián. Wǒ xiǎng qǐ Lǎo Zhāng de huà, juéde tā shuō de bù zhǐ shì dòufu, yě shì yì zhǒng duì shēnghuó de tàidù.
text_en: Old Zhang, the tofu seller at the morning market, starts making tofu at 3 AM every day. He's been at it over thirty years, a craft learned from his father. Once I went to the market very early and saw his stall still being set up. He was carefully removing freshly made tofu from the mold, arranging blocks one by one. I asked if thirty years of this wasn't boring. He thought for a moment: 'The tofu I make every day isn't exactly the same. Water temperature, soybean quality, weather changes — they all affect the final result. Thirty years and I'm still learning to do it better.' He added, 'Some people think tofu is just tofu, nothing special. But if you taste carefully, good tofu has a faint sweetness, a silky texture, melts in your mouth. Reaching that level isn't easy.' I bought a block of his tofu and went home. No complicated dish — just cold-dressed tofu, cut into pieces with scallion, cilantro, soy sauce, and sesame oil. One bite, and indeed there was a faint sweetness. I thought of Old Zhang's words and felt he wasn't just talking about tofu — it was an attitude toward life.

questions.q_zh: 老张的手艺是从谁那里学来的？
questions.q_en: Who did Old Zhang learn his craft from?
questions.explanation: The passage identifies: a craft learned from his father.

questions.q_zh: 从这段话可以推断人物是什么样的人？
questions.q_en: What kind of person can we infer the character is?
questions.explanation: The slow, attentive approach to each task reveals patience and meticulousness.

title: The Smell of Home
text_zh: 过年回家的第一件事不是放下行李，不是和爸妈打招呼，而是深深地吸一口气。家的味道是一种混合的气息——木地板的味道、阳台上晒过的被子的味道、厨房里飘出来的饭菜香。这种味道在外面是闻不到的，只有推开家门的那一刻才能完整地感受到。每次闻到的时候我都会愣一下，然后心里有一种说不出的放松。妈妈总是在我到家之前就开始做饭了。她会做我最爱吃的几道菜：红烧鱼、炒腊肉、凉拌豆皮，还有一个蛋花汤。这些菜在外面的餐厅都能吃到，但味道就是不一样。也许差别不在于食材或者做法，而在于做菜的那个人。知道你要回来，提前想好你爱吃什么，一大早去市场买最新鲜的食材，花一整个下午慢慢准备。这些事情不会写在菜谱里，但它们才是「家的味道」真正的配方。坐在桌前吃第一口饭的时候，一切旅途的疲惫都消失了。
text_pinyin: Guònián huí jiā de dì yī jiàn shì bú shì fàng xià xíngli, bú shì hé bà mā dǎ zhāohu, ér shì shēn shēn de xī yì kǒu qì. Jiā de wèidào shì yì zhǒng hùnhé de qìxī — mù dìbǎn de wèidào, yángtái shàng shài guò de bèizi de wèidào, chúfáng lǐ piāo chūlái de fàncài xiāng. Zhè zhǒng wèidào zài wàimiàn shì wén bú dào de, zhǐ yǒu tuī kāi jiā mén de nà yí kè cái néng wánzhěng de gǎnshòu dào. Měi cì wén dào de shíhou wǒ dōu huì lèng yíxià, ránhòu xīnlǐ yǒu yì zhǒng shuō bu chū de fàngsōng. Māma zǒngshì zài wǒ dào jiā zhīqián jiù kāishǐ zuòfàn le. Tā huì zuò wǒ zuì ài chī de jǐ dào cài: hóngshāo yú, chǎo làròu, liángbàn dòupí, hái yǒu yí gè dànhuā tāng. Zhèxiē cài zài wàimiàn de cāntīng dōu néng chī dào, dàn wèidào jiù shì bù yíyàng. Yěxǔ chābié bú zài yú shícái huòzhě zuòfǎ, ér zài yú zuò cài de nàge rén. Zhīdào nǐ yào huílái, tíqián xiǎng hǎo nǐ ài chī shénme, yí dà zǎo qù shìchǎng mǎi zuì xīnxiān de shícái, huā yí zhěng gè xiàwǔ mànmàn zhǔnbèi. Zhèxiē shìqing bú huì xiě zài càipǔ lǐ, dàn tāmen cái shì「jiā de wèidào」zhēnzhèng de pèifāng. Zuò zài zhuō qián chī dì yī kǒu fàn de shíhou, yíqiè lǚtú de píbèi dōu xiāoshī le.
text_en: The first thing about coming home for New Year isn't putting down luggage or greeting your parents — it's taking a deep breath. The smell of home is a blended scent — wood floors, sun-dried quilts from the balcony, cooking fragrance drifting from the kitchen. You can't smell this anywhere else; only the moment you push open the front door can you fully feel it. Every time I catch it I pause, then feel an indescribable relaxation. Mom always starts cooking before I arrive. She makes my favorite dishes: braised fish, stir-fried cured meat, cold-dressed tofu skin, and egg drop soup. You can eat all these at restaurants outside, but the taste is just different. Perhaps the difference isn't in the ingredients or the method, but in the person cooking. Knowing you're coming home, thinking ahead about what you like, going to the market early for the freshest ingredients, spending an entire afternoon preparing. These things won't be written in any recipe, but they are the true formula for 'the taste of home.' Sitting at the table, eating that first bite, all the fatigue of the journey vanishes.

questions.q_zh: 这段话和说明文的写法有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does this passage differ from an expository text?

options.text_en: focuses more on emotion and experience

questions.explanation: Expository text explains; this passage immerses the reader in subjective, felt experience.

questions.q_zh: 这段话用什么来代替直接的情感表达？
questions.q_en: What does this passage use instead of direct emotional expression?
questions.explanation: Emotion surfaces through described actions and settings rather than explicit statements of feeling.

title: Leftovers for Breakfast
text_zh: 我有一个可能不太健康但让我很开心的习惯：用昨晚的剩菜做早餐。把冰箱里剩下的菜拿出来，不管是什么——可能是半盘回锅肉、一小碗番茄炒蛋、几根炒青菜——全都倒进锅里和米饭一起炒。大火翻炒几分钟，让所有的味道混在一起，米饭被菜汁染成了各种颜色。盛到碗里，冒着热气，是这个世界上最随意但也最让人满足的早餐。我知道有些人觉得吃剩菜不好，但我觉得剩菜炒饭有一种特别的味道。那些菜在冰箱里待了一晚上，味道反而变得更浓了。不同的菜混在一起，有时候会产生意想不到的搭配。而且剩菜炒饭有一种节约的美感——什么都不浪费，所有的东西都被好好地利用了。吃着剩菜炒饭的时候，我常常想起小时候妈妈也是这样做的。她每天早上都会把昨晚的剩菜变成一锅好吃的炒饭。也许这不是什么高级的烹饪，但它是真实的、日常的、温暖的。这就够了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yí gè kěnéng bú tài jiànkāng dàn ràng wǒ hěn kāixīn de xíguàn: yòng zuó wǎn de shèng cài zuò zǎocān. Bǎ bīngxiāng lǐ shèng xià de cài ná chūlái, bùguǎn shì shénme — kěnéng shì bàn pán huíguō ròu, yì xiǎo wǎn fānqié chǎo dàn, jǐ gēn chǎo qīngcài — quán dōu dào jìn guō lǐ hé mǐfàn yìqǐ chǎo. Dà huǒ fānchǎo jǐ fēnzhōng, ràng suǒyǒu de wèidào hùn zài yìqǐ, mǐfàn bèi cài zhī rǎn chéng le gè zhǒng yánsè. Chéng dào wǎn lǐ, mào zhe rèqì, shì zhège shìjiè shàng zuì suíyì dàn yě zuì ràng rén mǎnzú de zǎocān. Wǒ zhīdào yǒuxiē rén juéde chī shèng cài bù hǎo, dàn wǒ juéde shèng cài chǎofàn yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de wèidào. Nàxiē cài zài bīngxiāng lǐ dāi le yì wǎnshang, wèidào fǎn'ér biàn de gèng nóng le. Bùtóng de cài hùn zài yìqǐ, yǒu shíhou huì chǎnshēng yì xiǎng bú dào de dāpèi. Érqiě shèng cài chǎofàn yǒu yì zhǒng jiéyuē de měigǎn — shénme dōu bù làngfèi, suǒyǒu de dōngxi dōu bèi hǎohǎo de lìyòng le. Chī zhe shèng cài chǎofàn de shíhou, wǒ chángcháng xiǎng qǐ xiǎo shíhou māma yě shì zhèyàng zuò de. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang dōu huì bǎ zuó wǎn de shèng cài biàn chéng yì guō hǎochī de chǎofàn. Yěxǔ zhè bú shì shénme gāojí de pēngrèn, dàn tā shì zhēnshí de, rìcháng de, wēnnuǎn de. Zhè jiù gòu le.
text_en: I have a habit that might not be the healthiest but makes me happy: using last night's leftovers for breakfast. Take whatever's left in the fridge — maybe half a plate of twice-cooked pork, a small bowl of scrambled eggs with tomato, a few stalks of stir-fried greens — dump it all in the wok with rice and stir-fry. High heat for a few minutes, letting all the flavors meld, the rice stained various colors by the sauce. Scooped into a bowl, steaming, it's the most casual but most satisfying breakfast in the world. I know some people think eating leftovers isn't great, but I think leftover fried rice has a special taste. Those dishes sitting overnight in the fridge actually develop deeper flavor. Different dishes mixed together sometimes create unexpected combinations. And there's an aesthetic of thrift to leftover fried rice — nothing wasted, everything put to good use. Eating it, I often think of how my mom did exactly this. Every morning she'd turn last night's leftovers into a delicious wok of fried rice. Maybe it's not haute cuisine, but it's real, it's everyday, it's warm. That's enough.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中什么是'看不见'但能感觉到的？
questions.q_en: What in this passage is 'invisible' but can be felt?

options.text: 人物的情感和内心世界
options.pinyin: rénwù de qínggǎn hé nèixīn shìjiè
options.text_en: the character's emotions and inner world

questions.explanation: No feelings are named outright, yet the actions and details radiate emotional undercurrent.

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得剩菜炒饭有什么美感？
questions.q_en: What aesthetic does the author find in leftover fried rice?
questions.explanation: Using every leftover ingredient represents an aesthetic of thrift -- nothing wasted, everything repurposed.

title: Grandmother's Soup
text_zh: 奶奶做的汤，我到现在还记得那个味道。她总是很早起来，先去菜市场买最新鲜的排骨和萝卜。回到家以后，她把排骨洗干净，放进一个很大的砂锅里，加满水，用小火慢慢煮。整个上午厨房里都是咕嘟咕嘟的声音，那个声音让人觉得很安心。中午的时候汤已经变成了白色，浓浓的，香气飘到了每一个房间。奶奶会用一个大勺子轻轻地搅一搅，然后尝一口，再决定要不要加盐。她说「好的汤不需要太多调料，食材本身的味道就是最好的味道」。那时候我不太理解这句话，觉得汤就是汤。可是后来离开家以后，在外面喝过很多汤，才明白奶奶说的是对的。那种简单的、慢慢熬出来的味道，是任何餐厅都做不出来的。
text_pinyin: Nǎinai zuò de tāng, wǒ dào xiànzài hái jìde nàge wèidào. Tā zǒngshì hěn zǎo qǐlái, xiān qù càishìchǎng mǎi zuì xīnxiān de páigǔ hé luóbo. Huí dào jiā yǐhòu, tā bǎ páigǔ xǐ gānjìng, fàng jìn yí gè hěn dà de shāguō lǐ, jiā mǎn shuǐ, yòng xiǎo huǒ mànmàn zhǔ. Zhěng gè shàngwǔ chúfáng lǐ dōu shì gūdū gūdū de shēngyīn, nàge shēngyīn ràng rén juéde hěn ānxīn. Zhōngwǔ de shíhou tāng yǐjīng biàn chéng le báisè, nóng nóng de, xiāngqì piāo dào le měi yí gè fángjiān. Nǎinai huì yòng yí gè dà sháozi qīngqīng de jiǎo yì jiǎo, ránhòu cháng yì kǒu, zài juédìng yào bú yào jiā yán. Tā shuō「hǎo de tāng bù xūyào tài duō tiáoliào, shícái běnshēn de wèidào jiù shì zuì hǎo de wèidào」. Nà shíhou wǒ bú tài lǐjiě zhè jù huà, juéde tāng jiù shì tāng. Kěshì hòulái líkāi jiā yǐhòu, zài wàimiàn hē guò hěn duō tāng, cái míngbai nǎinai shuō de shì duì de. Nà zhǒng jiǎndān de, mànmàn áo chūlái de wèidào, shì rènhé cāntīng dōu zuò bù chūlái de.
text_en: Grandmother's soup — I can still remember that taste today. She always got up very early, first going to the market to buy the freshest spare ribs and radish. Back home, she washed the ribs clean, put them in a large clay pot, filled it with water, and simmered slowly over low heat. All morning the kitchen was filled with a gentle bubbling sound, a sound that made you feel at ease. By noon the soup had turned white and thick, its fragrance drifting into every room. Grandmother would gently stir with a big ladle, take a sip, then decide whether to add salt. She said, 'Good soup doesn't need much seasoning — the natural flavor of the ingredients is the best flavor.' At the time I didn't understand. Soup was just soup. But after leaving home and tasting many soups elsewhere, I finally understood she was right. That simple taste, slowly simmered out, is something no restaurant can replicate.

questions.q_zh: 奶奶做汤的时候用什么火？
questions.q_en: What heat did grandmother use to make the soup?
questions.explanation: Grandmother always used low heat, letting the soup simmer slowly to draw out natural flavor.

questions.q_zh: 奶奶觉得好的汤需要什么？
questions.q_en: What did grandmother think good soup needs?
questions.explanation: Grandmother believed good soup relies on the natural flavor of its ingredients rather than heavy seasoning — a philosophy of simplicity over addition.

title: The Morning Market
text_zh: 每个周末我都会去家附近的早市买菜。早市六点就开了，但最好的时间是七点左右，那时候摊位都摆好了，人也不算太多。卖菜的大多是附近农村来的老人，他们的菜是自己种的，品种不多，但特别新鲜。有一个卖豆腐的阿姨，每次看到我都会笑着说「小伙子，今天的豆腐特别嫩」。她的豆腐确实好吃，白白的，滑滑的，拿回家放点葱花和酱油就很好了。旁边有一个卖鸡蛋的大叔，鸡蛋都是散养的鸡下的，蛋黄的颜色比超市的深很多。我喜欢在早市慢慢走，不着急，看看今天有什么好的食材，再决定晚饭做什么。这个过程本身就让人开心。买完菜回去的路上，袋子里装着新鲜的蔬菜和豆腐，心里已经开始想象它们变成一道道菜的样子了。
text_pinyin: Měi gè zhōumò wǒ dōu huì qù jiā fùjìn de zǎoshì mǎi cài. Zǎoshì liù diǎn jiù kāi le, dàn zuì hǎo de shíjiān shì qī diǎn zuǒyòu, nà shíhou tānwèi dōu bǎi hǎo le, rén yě bú suàn tài duō. Mài cài de dàduō shì fùjìn nóngcūn lái de lǎorén, tāmen de cài shì zìjǐ zhòng de, pǐnzhǒng bù duō, dàn tèbié xīnxiān. Yǒu yí gè mài dòufu de āyí, měi cì kàn dào wǒ dōu huì xiào zhe shuō「xiǎohuǒzi, jīntiān de dòufu tèbié nèn」. Tā de dòufu quèshí hǎochī, bái bái de, huá huá de, ná huí jiā fàng diǎn cōnghuā hé jiàngyóu jiù hěn hǎo le. Pángbiān yǒu yí gè mài jīdàn de dàshū, jīdàn dōu shì sǎn yǎng de jī xià de, dànhuáng de yánsè bǐ chāoshì de shēn hěn duō. Wǒ xǐhuan zài zǎoshì mànmàn zǒu, bù zhāojí, kànkan jīntiān yǒu shénme hǎo de shícái, zài juédìng wǎnfàn zuò shénme. Zhège guòchéng běnshēn jiù ràng rén kāixīn. Mǎi wán cài huíqù de lù shàng, dàizi lǐ zhuāng zhe xīnxiān de shūcài hé dòufu, xīnlǐ yǐjīng kāishǐ xiǎngxiàng tāmen biàn chéng yí dào dào cài de yàngzi le.
text_en: Every weekend I go to the morning market near my home to buy groceries. It opens at six, but the best time is around seven — stalls are all set up and it's not too crowded yet. Most vendors are elderly people from nearby villages; their vegetables are home-grown, not many varieties, but exceptionally fresh. There's a tofu-selling auntie who smiles every time she sees me and says, 'Young man, today's tofu is especially tender.' Her tofu really is delicious — white and silky, just add some chopped scallions and soy sauce at home and it's perfect. Next to her is an uncle selling eggs, all from free-range chickens, the yolks much deeper in color than supermarket ones. I like to walk through the market slowly, unhurried, seeing what good ingredients are available today, then deciding what to cook for dinner. The process itself makes me happy. On the walk home, bag full of fresh vegetables and tofu, my mind already starts imagining them transformed into dish after dish.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的整体情感是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the overall emotional tone of this passage?
questions.explanation: Fond details and appreciative language create a warm register throughout.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的整体情感是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the overall emotional tone of this passage?
questions.explanation: Fond details and appreciative language create a warm register throughout.

title: Cooking for One
text_zh: 一个人住以后，我开始学做饭。一开始觉得给自己一个人做饭不值得花太多时间，所以经常随便煮碗面条就算了。后来有一天下班回来特别累，不想出门也不想叫外卖，冰箱里只剩下几个西红柿和两个鸡蛋。我就把西红柿切了，鸡蛋打散，先炒鸡蛋再炒西红柿，加了一点糖和盐。做好以后端到桌子上，配一碗白米饭，慢慢吃。那一刻我突然觉得，一个人做饭也可以是一件很温暖的事。从那以后我开始认真对待每一顿饭。买一棵好看的西兰花，把它切成小朵，焯水以后加点蒜泥。或者做一碗蛋炒饭，把米饭炒到粒粒分明。厨房里的声音、食物的香气、盘子里好看的颜色，这些都是给自己的小小礼物。
text_pinyin: Yí gè rén zhù yǐhòu, wǒ kāishǐ xué zuòfàn. Yì kāishǐ juéde gěi zìjǐ yí gè rén zuòfàn bù zhídé huā tài duō shíjiān, suǒyǐ jīngcháng suíbiàn zhǔ wǎn miàntiáo jiù suàn le. Hòulái yǒu yì tiān xiàbān huílái tèbié lèi, bù xiǎng chūmén yě bù xiǎng jiào wàimài, bīngxiāng lǐ zhǐ shèng xià jǐ gè xīhóngshì hé liǎng gè jīdàn. Wǒ jiù bǎ xīhóngshì qiē le, jīdàn dǎ sàn, xiān chǎo jīdàn zài chǎo xīhóngshì, jiā le yìdiǎn táng hé yán. Zuò hǎo yǐhòu duān dào zhuōzi shàng, pèi yì wǎn bái mǐfàn, mànmàn chī. Nà yí kè wǒ tūrán juéde, yí gè rén zuòfàn yě kěyǐ shì yí jiàn hěn wēnnuǎn de shì. Cóng nà yǐhòu wǒ kāishǐ rènzhēn duìdài měi yí dùn fàn. Mǎi yì kē hǎokàn de xīlánhuā, bǎ tā qiē chéng xiǎo duǒ, chāo shuǐ yǐhòu jiā diǎn suànní. Huòzhě zuò yì wǎn dàn chǎo fàn, bǎ mǐfàn chǎo dào lì lì fēnmíng. Chúfáng lǐ de shēngyīn, shíwù de xiāngqì, pánzi lǐ hǎokàn de yánsè, zhèxiē dōu shì gěi zìjǐ de xiǎo xiǎo lǐwù.
text_en: After living alone, I started learning to cook. At first I thought cooking just for myself wasn't worth much time, so I'd often just boil a bowl of noodles and call it done. Then one day I came home from work exhausted, didn't want to go out or order delivery, and only had a few tomatoes and two eggs left in the fridge. So I sliced the tomatoes, scrambled the eggs, fried the eggs first then the tomatoes, added a little sugar and salt. Brought it to the table with a bowl of white rice, ate slowly. Then I suddenly felt that cooking for one can also be something warm. From then on I started taking every meal seriously. Buying a nice-looking broccoli, cutting it into small florets, blanching it and adding garlic paste. Or making a bowl of egg fried rice, frying until every grain is separate. The sounds in the kitchen, the fragrance of food, the beautiful colors on the plate — these are all small gifts to yourself.

questions.q_zh: 作者以前一个人吃饭的时候经常吃什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author often eat when eating alone before?
questions.explanation: Before learning to cook properly, the author defaulted to noodles as the simplest solo meal — quick, easy, and requiring no real cooking skill.

questions.q_zh: 那天冰箱里剩下了什么？
questions.q_en: What was left in the fridge that day?
questions.explanation: Coming home exhausted, the author found only tomatoes and eggs left in the fridge — the raw ingredients that would become a turning-point meal.

title: The Late-Night Noodle Shop
text_zh: 公司附近有一家面馆，开到凌晨两点。加班晚了的时候，我常常去那里吃一碗面。面馆很小，只有六张桌子，灯光是暖黄色的，墙上挂着一张旧旧的菜单。老板是个五十多岁的男人，话不多，但做面很认真。他的招牌是牛肉面，汤是每天早上熬的，用牛骨和好几种香料，要煮四五个小时。面条是手工做的，有嚼劲。碗端上来的时候，热气腾腾的，上面飘着几片牛肉和切碎的香菜。深夜的面馆里很安静，客人大多是加完班的人，大家都低着头默默地吃面，偶尔能听到吸面条的声音。没有人说话，但那种安静不让人觉得孤单。也许是因为大家都累了一天，在这碗面里找到了一点安慰。吃完以后暖暖的，走在回家的路上，夜风吹过来，觉得世界还是不错的。
text_pinyin: Gōngsī fùjìn yǒu yì jiā miànguǎn, kāi dào língchén liǎng diǎn. Jiābān wǎn le de shíhou, wǒ chángcháng qù nàlǐ chī yì wǎn miàn. Miànguǎn hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu liù zhāng zhuōzi, dēngguāng shì nuǎn huángsè de, qiáng shàng guà zhe yì zhāng jiù jiù de càidān. Lǎobǎn shì ge wǔshí duō suì de nánrén, huà bù duō, dàn zuò miàn hěn rènzhēn. Tā de zhāopái shì niúròu miàn, tāng shì měi tiān zǎoshang áo de, yòng niúgǔ hé hǎo jǐ zhǒng xiāngliào, yào zhǔ sì wǔ gè xiǎoshí. Miàntiáo shì shǒugōng zuò de, yǒu jiáojìn. Wǎn duān shànglái de shíhou, rèqì téngténg de, shàngmiàn piāo zhe jǐ piàn niúròu hé qiē suì de xiāngcài. Shēnyè de miànguǎn lǐ hěn ānjìng, kèrén dàduō shì jiā wán bān de rén, dàjiā dōu dī zhe tóu mòmò de chī miàn, ǒu'ěr néng tīng dào xī miàntiáo de shēngyīn. Méiyǒu rén shuōhuà, dàn nà zhǒng ānjìng bú ràng rén juéde gūdān. Yěxǔ shì yīnwèi dàjiā dōu lèi le yì tiān, zài zhè wǎn miàn lǐ zhǎo dào le yìdiǎn ānwèi. Chī wán yǐhòu nuǎn nuǎn de, zǒu zài huí jiā de lù shàng, yè fēng chuī guòlái, juéde shìjiè hái shì búcuò de.
text_en: Near my office there's a noodle shop that stays open until 2 AM. When I work late, I often go there for a bowl of noodles. The shop is small — only six tables, warm yellow lighting, an old menu hanging on the wall. The owner is a man in his fifties, doesn't talk much, but takes his noodles seriously. His specialty is beef noodle soup, broth simmered every morning with beef bones and several spices for four or five hours. The noodles are handmade, with a nice chew. When the bowl arrives, steaming hot, a few slices of beef and chopped cilantro floating on top. The late-night noodle shop is quiet, most customers are people who just finished overtime, everyone heads down eating silently, occasionally you hear the sound of slurping noodles. Nobody talks, but that kind of quiet doesn't feel lonely. Maybe it's because everyone's been tired all day, finding a bit of comfort in this bowl of noodles. After eating, warm inside, walking home, the night breeze blowing past, you feel the world is still alright.

questions.q_zh: 牛肉面的汤需要煮多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long does the beef noodle broth need to cook?
questions.explanation: The beef noodle broth needs four or five hours of slow cooking -- time is the key ingredient.

questions.q_zh: 这个选择反映了什么？
questions.q_en: What does this choice reflect?
questions.explanation: The deliberate choice reveals underlying priorities and what the person finds worthwhile.

title: Tea on a Rainy Afternoon
text_zh: 下雨的下午，最适合泡一壶茶。我有一套很简单的茶具，是搬家的时候在旧货市场买的——一个小茶壶，两个小茶杯，放在一个竹子做的茶盘上。泡茶的步骤不复杂，但每一步都让人慢下来。先烧水，等水开了以后稍微放凉一点。把茶叶放进茶壶里，倒入热水，第一泡是洗茶，水倒掉。第二泡才是真正开始喝。看着茶叶在热水里慢慢展开，水的颜色渐渐变深，有一种时间被拉长的感觉。窗外的雨声和茶的香气混在一起，让整个房间都变得很柔软。我不是什么懂茶的人，不会像茶艺师那样讲究，但我喜欢泡茶这个过程。它让我的手有事情做，让我的心安静下来。一杯茶，一本书，一个下雨的下午，这已经足够了。
text_pinyin: Xià yǔ de xiàwǔ, zuì shìhé pào yì hú chá. Wǒ yǒu yí tào hěn jiǎndān de chájù, shì bānjiā de shíhou zài jiùhuò shìchǎng mǎi de — yí gè xiǎo cháhú, liǎng gè xiǎo chábēi, fàng zài yí gè zhúzi zuò de chápán shàng. Pào chá de bùzhòu bù fùzá, dàn měi yí bù dōu ràng rén màn xiàlái. Xiān shāo shuǐ, děng shuǐ kāi le yǐhòu shāowēi fàng liáng yìdiǎn. Bǎ cháyè fàng jìn cháhú lǐ, dào rù rè shuǐ, dì yī pào shì xǐ chá, shuǐ dào diào. Dì èr pào cái shì zhēnzhèng kāishǐ hē. Kàn zhe cháyè zài rè shuǐ lǐ mànmàn zhǎnkāi, shuǐ de yánsè jiànjiàn biàn shēn, yǒu yì zhǒng shíjiān bèi lā cháng de gǎnjué. Chuāng wài de yǔ shēng hé chá de xiāngqì hùn zài yìqǐ, ràng zhěng gè fángjiān dōu biàn de hěn róuruǎn. Wǒ bú shì shénme dǒng chá de rén, bú huì xiàng cháyì shī nàyàng jiǎngjiū, dàn wǒ xǐhuan pào chá zhège guòchéng. Tā ràng wǒ de shǒu yǒu shìqing zuò, ràng wǒ de xīn ānjìng xiàlái. Yì bēi chá, yì běn shū, yí gè xià yǔ de xiàwǔ, zhè yǐjīng zúgòu le.
text_en: A rainy afternoon is perfect for brewing a pot of tea. I have a simple tea set, bought at a flea market when I was moving — a small teapot, two small cups, placed on a bamboo tea tray. The steps for brewing tea aren't complicated, but each one makes you slow down. First boil the water, let it cool slightly after boiling. Put tea leaves in the teapot, pour in hot water — the first steep is for rinsing the leaves, pour that water out. The second steep is when you actually start drinking. Watching the tea leaves slowly unfurl in hot water, the color gradually deepening, there's a feeling of time being stretched. The rain outside and the tea's fragrance blend together, making the whole room feel soft. I'm not someone who really understands tea — I can't be as particular as a tea ceremony master, but I love the process of brewing tea. It gives my hands something to do, and lets my heart quiet down. A cup of tea, a book, a rainy afternoon — that's enough.

questions.q_zh: 天气在文中起什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the weather play in this passage?
questions.explanation: The weather operates as emotional mirror -- outer conditions track the character's inner state.

questions.q_zh: 自然环境对故事有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the natural setting affect the story?
questions.explanation: The natural details set an emotional backdrop that colors the entire scene.

title: Sharing Dumplings
text_zh: 冬至那天，同事们决定下班以后一起包饺子。办公室的会议室被收拾出来，桌子上铺了干净的塑料布。有人从家里带来了和好的面，有人负责准备馅儿——猪肉白菜馅和韭菜鸡蛋馅两种。大家围着桌子坐下来，开始包饺子。有些人包得很好看，皮薄馅大，形状整齐；有些人包得奇奇怪怪的，但大家都笑着说「没关系，煮出来都一样好吃」。我的同事小王是东北人，她包饺子的速度最快，一分钟能包好几个。她一边包一边教我们怎么捏褶子，还说小时候每到过年全家人都会坐在一起包饺子，一包就是一整天。饺子煮好以后，每个人端着一碗热腾腾的饺子，蘸着醋和酱油吃。大家一边吃一边聊天，平时工作中的压力好像都消失了。那天晚上回家的路上很冷，但心里是暖的。
text_pinyin: Dōngzhì nà tiān, tóngshì men juédìng xiàbān yǐhòu yìqǐ bāo jiǎozi. Bàngōngshì de huìyìshì bèi shōushi chūlái, zhuōzi shàng pū le gānjìng de sùliào bù. Yǒu rén cóng jiā lǐ dài lái le huó hǎo de miàn, yǒu rén fùzé zhǔnbèi xiànr — zhūròu báicài xiànr hé jiǔcài jīdàn xiànr liǎng zhǒng. Dàjiā wéi zhe zhuōzi zuò xiàlái, kāishǐ bāo jiǎozi. Yǒuxiē rén bāo de hěn hǎokàn, pí báo xiàn dà, xíngzhuàng zhěngqí; yǒuxiē rén bāo de qí qí guài guài de, dàn dàjiā dōu xiào zhe shuō「méi guānxi, zhǔ chūlái dōu yíyàng hǎochī」. Wǒ de tóngshì Xiǎo Wáng shì dōngběi rén, tā bāo jiǎozi de sùdù zuì kuài, yì fēnzhōng néng bāo hǎo jǐ gè. Tā yìbiān bāo yìbiān jiāo wǒmen zěnme niē zhězi, hái shuō xiǎo shíhou měi dào guònián quán jiā rén dōu huì zuò zài yìqǐ bāo jiǎozi, yí bāo jiù shì yì zhěng tiān. Jiǎozi zhǔ hǎo yǐhòu, měi gè rén duān zhe yì wǎn rè téngténg de jiǎozi, zhàn zhe cù hé jiàngyóu chī. Dàjiā yìbiān chī yìbiān liáotiān, píngshí gōngzuò zhōng de yālì hǎoxiàng dōu xiāoshī le. Nà tiān wǎnshang huí jiā de lù shàng hěn lěng, dàn xīnlǐ shì nuǎn de.
text_en: On the winter solstice, my colleagues decided to make dumplings together after work. The office meeting room was cleared out, the table covered with clean plastic sheeting. Someone brought pre-kneaded dough from home; others prepared the fillings — pork and cabbage, and chive and egg, two kinds. Everyone sat around the table and started wrapping dumplings. Some people's looked beautiful — thin skin, generous filling, neat shapes; some looked oddly shaped, but and said, 'It's fine, they'll taste the same once boiled.' My colleague Xiao Wang is from the northeast; she wraps the fastest, several per minute. While wrapping she taught us how to pinch the pleats, and said that when she was little, the whole family would sit together making dumplings every New Year, spending the entire day. After the dumplings were cooked, everyone held a bowl of steaming dumplings, dipping in vinegar and soy sauce. Eating and chatting, the pressures of daily work seemed to vanish. The walk home that night was cold, but my heart was warm.

questions.q_zh: 作者想让读者感受到什么？
questions.q_en: What feeling does the author want the reader to experience?
questions.explanation: The warm sensory detail and gentle pacing are calibrated to produce a sense of simple satisfaction.

questions.q_zh: 小王为什么包饺子包得特别快？
questions.q_en: Why is Xiao Wang especially fast at wrapping dumplings?

options.pinyin: tā shì dōngběi rén, cóng xiǎo jiù bāo
options.text_en: She's from the northeast, she's wrapped since childhood

questions.explanation: Xiao Wang is from the northeast where dumpling-wrapping starts in childhood, so speed comes from deep habit.

title: The Taste of Homesickness
text_zh: 在国外留学的第一个冬天，我特别想家。不是因为天气冷，也不是因为功课多，而是因为吃不到妈妈做的红烧肉。超市里的猪肉和国内的不太一样，酱油的牌子也不同，但我还是决定试一试。我按照记忆中妈妈的做法，先把肉切成方块，用热水焯一下。然后在锅里放糖，等糖融化变成棕色，再把肉放进去翻炒。加入酱油、料酒、姜片和八角，最后加水，盖上锅盖，小火慢慢炖。一个多小时以后，厨房里终于飘出了那个熟悉的香味。我迫不及待地尝了一口，味道和妈妈做的不完全一样，但那个方向是对的。我把做好的红烧肉拍了照片发给妈妈，她回了一条语音消息，说「看起来不错啊，下次少放一点酱油会更好」。听到妈妈的声音，我的眼睛突然有点湿了。
text_pinyin: Zài guówài liúxué de dì yí gè dōngtiān, wǒ tèbié xiǎng jiā. Bú shì yīnwèi tiānqì lěng, yě bú shì yīnwèi gōngkè duō, ér shì yīnwèi chī bú dào māma zuò de hóngshāo ròu. Chāoshì lǐ de zhūròu hé guónèi de bú tài yíyàng, jiàngyóu de páizi yě bù tóng, dàn wǒ háishi juédìng shì yí shì. Wǒ ànzhào jìyì zhōng māma de zuòfǎ, xiān bǎ ròu qiē chéng fāng kuài, yòng rè shuǐ chāo yíxià. Ránhòu zài guō lǐ fàng táng, děng táng rónghuà biàn chéng zōngsè, zài bǎ ròu fàng jìnqù fānchǎo. Jiā rù jiàngyóu, liàojiǔ, jiāng piàn hé bājiǎo, zuìhòu jiā shuǐ, gài shàng guō gài, xiǎo huǒ mànmàn dùn. Yí gè duō xiǎoshí yǐhòu, chúfáng lǐ zhōngyú piāo chū le nàge shúxī de xiāngwèi. Wǒ pòbùjídài de cháng le yì kǒu, wèidào hé māma zuò de bù wánquán yíyàng, dàn nàge fāngxiàng shì duì de. Wǒ bǎ zuò hǎo de hóngshāo ròu pāi le zhàopiàn fā gěi māma, tā huí le yì tiáo yǔyīn xiāoxi, shuō「kàn qǐlái búcuò a, xià cì shǎo fàng yìdiǎn jiàngyóu huì gèng hǎo」. Tīng dào māma de shēngyīn, wǒ de yǎnjing tūrán yǒudiǎn shī le.
text_en: The first winter studying abroad, I missed home terribly. Not because of the cold, not because of heavy coursework, but because I couldn't eat my mom's braised pork. The pork at the supermarket was different from back home, the soy sauce brand was different too, but I decided to try anyway. Following my memory of mom's method, I first cut the meat into cubes, blanched in hot water. Then put sugar in the pan, waited until it melted to brown, then added the meat and stir-fried. Added soy sauce, cooking wine, ginger slices, and star anise, finally added water, covered the lid, simmered on low heat. After about an hour, the kitchen finally filled with that familiar aroma. I couldn't wait to take a bite — it wasn't exactly the same as mom's, but the direction was right. I took a photo of the finished braised pork and sent it to mom. She replied with a voice message: 'Looks pretty good — next time use a little less soy sauce and it'll be even better.' Hearing mom's voice, my eyes suddenly felt a bit wet.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么想家？
questions.q_en: Why did the author feel homesick?

options.text: 吃不到妈妈做的红烧肉
options.pinyin: chī bú dào māma zuò de hóngshāo ròu

questions.explanation: Homesickness struck not from cold or loneliness but from missing mom's braised pork specifically.

questions.q_zh: 妈妈给了什么建议？
questions.q_en: What advice did mom give?

options.text_en: Use a little less soy sauce

questions.explanation: Mom's practical advice cut through the emotion: just use a little less soy sauce next time.

title: Kitchen Sounds
text_zh: 我觉得厨房是家里最有生命力的地方，因为它总是充满各种各样的声音。早上烧水壶发出的咕噜声，是一天开始的信号。油锅里放入食材时「滋啦」一声，是最让人兴奋的声音。切菜的时候刀碰到案板发出的「哒哒哒」声，有一种稳定的节奏。炖汤的时候锅盖被蒸汽顶起来又落下去，发出轻轻的碰撞声。甚至连洗碗的水声，也有一种让人放松的效果。小时候放学回家，还没进门就能听到厨房里的声音，那些声音告诉我妈妈在家，晚饭快好了，一切都是安全的。现在我自己做饭的时候，也慢慢学会了听这些声音。油的温度够不够，可以听它响不响。菜炒得差不多了，声音会变小。米饭快好的时候，电饭锅会发出特别的声音。厨房的声音就像一种语言，只要你愿意听，它会告诉你很多事情。
text_pinyin: Wǒ juéde chúfáng shì jiā lǐ zuì yǒu shēngmìnglì de dìfāng, yīnwèi tā zǒngshì chōngmǎn gè zhǒng gè yàng de shēngyīn. Zǎoshang shāo shuǐ hú fāchū de gūlū shēng, shì yì tiān kāishǐ de xìnhào. Yóu guō lǐ fàng rù shícái shí「zī lā」yì shēng, shì zuì ràng rén xīngfèn de shēngyīn. Qiē cài de shíhou dāo pèng dào ànbǎn fāchū de「dā dā dā」shēng, yǒu yì zhǒng wěndìng de jiézòu. Dùn tāng de shíhou guō gài bèi zhēngqì dǐng qǐlái yòu luò xiàqù, fāchū qīngqīng de pèngzhuàng shēng. Shènzhì lián xǐ wǎn de shuǐ shēng, yě yǒu yì zhǒng ràng rén fàngsōng de xiàoguǒ. Xiǎo shíhou fàngxué huí jiā, hái méi jìn mén jiù néng tīng dào chúfáng lǐ de shēngyīn, nàxiē shēngyīn gàosu wǒ māma zài jiā, wǎnfàn kuài hǎo le, yíqiè dōu shì ānquán de. Xiànzài wǒ zìjǐ zuòfàn de shíhou, yě mànmàn xuéhuì le tīng zhèxiē shēngyīn. Yóu de wēndù gòu bú gòu, kěyǐ tīng tā xiǎng bù xiǎng. Cài chǎo de chàbuduō le, shēngyīn huì biàn xiǎo. Mǐfàn kuài hǎo de shíhou, diànfànguō huì fāchū tèbié de shēngyīn. Chúfáng de shēngyīn jiù xiàng yì zhǒng yǔyán, zhǐyào nǐ yuànyì tīng, tā huì gàosu nǐ hěn duō shìqing.
text_en: I think the kitchen is the most alive place in a home, because it's always full of all kinds of sounds. The gurgling of the kettle in the morning signals the start of a new day. The sizzle when ingredients hit the oil pan — that's the most exciting sound. The tapping of a knife on the cutting board while chopping vegetables has a steady rhythm. When simmering soup, the lid gets pushed up by steam then falls back down with a soft clink. Even the sound of dishwater has a relaxing effect. When I was little, coming home from school, I could hear the kitchen sounds before even opening the door — those sounds told me mom was home, dinner was almost ready, everything was safe. Now when I cook myself, I've gradually learned to listen to these sounds. Whether the oil is hot enough — you can tell by how it sounds. When the stir-fry is nearly done, the sound gets softer. When the rice is almost ready, the rice cooker makes a special sound. Kitchen sounds are like a language — as long as you're willing to listen, they'll tell you many things.

questions.q_zh: 作者想让读者感受到什么？
questions.q_en: What feeling does the author want the reader to experience?
questions.explanation: The warm sensory detail and gentle pacing are calibrated to produce a sense of simple satisfaction.

questions.q_zh: 小时候听到厨房的声音，作者有什么感觉？
questions.q_en: How did the author feel hearing kitchen sounds as a child?
questions.explanation: As a child, hearing kitchen sounds meant someone was home cooking -- the feeling was safety.

title: The Recipe Notebook
text_zh: 搬家的时候，我在一个旧箱子里找到了妈妈的菜谱本。那是一个普通的笔记本，封面已经发黄了，角上有油渍。翻开来看，里面是妈妈的字迹，一道一道菜写得很仔细。每道菜都写了需要什么材料、多少分量、怎么做，有些地方还画了简单的图。有的页面旁边写着小小的注释，比如「这个菜你爸爸特别喜欢吃」「小宝第一次吃就吃了两碗饭」。看到「小宝」两个字我笑了，那是我小时候的小名。菜谱里有些菜我吃过很多次，比如糖醋排骨和蒸鸡蛋羹；有些菜我从来没有印象，也许是妈妈学了但后来没有做过的。我拍了每一页的照片存在手机里，然后小心地把菜谱本放回箱子。这个本子不只是菜谱，它记录的是一个家庭的日常，是妈妈用心生活的证据。
text_pinyin: Bānjiā de shíhou, wǒ zài yí gè jiù xiāngzi lǐ zhǎo dào le māma de càipǔ běn. Nà shì yí gè pǔtōng de bǐjìběn, fēngmiàn yǐjīng fā huáng le, jiǎo shàng yǒu yóuzì. Fānkāi lái kàn, lǐmiàn shì māma de zìjì, yí dào yí dào cài xiě de hěn zǐxì. Měi dào cài dōu xiě le xūyào shénme cáiliào, duōshǎo fènliàng, zěnme zuò, yǒuxiē dìfāng hái huà le jiǎndān de tú. Yǒu de yèmiàn pángbiān xiě zhe xiǎo xiǎo de zhùshì, bǐrú「zhège cài nǐ bàba tèbié xǐhuan chī」「Xiǎo Bǎo dì yī cì chī jiù chī le liǎng wǎn fàn」. Kàn dào「Xiǎo Bǎo」liǎng gè zì wǒ xiào le, nà shì wǒ xiǎo shíhou de xiǎomíng. Càipǔ lǐ yǒuxiē cài wǒ chī guò hěn duō cì, bǐrú tángcù páigǔ hé zhēng jīdàn gēng; yǒuxiē cài wǒ cónglái méiyǒu yìnxiàng, yěxǔ shì māma xué le dàn hòulái méiyǒu zuò guò de. Wǒ pāi le měi yí yè de zhàopiàn cún zài shǒujī lǐ, ránhòu xiǎoxīn de bǎ càipǔ běn fàng huí xiāngzi. Zhège běnzi bù zhǐ shì càipǔ, tā jìlù de shì yí gè jiātíng de rìcháng, shì māma yòngxīn shēnghuó de zhèngjù.
text_en: While moving, I found my mom's recipe notebook in an old box. It was an ordinary notebook, its cover yellowed, oil stains on the corners. Opening it, inside was mom's handwriting, each dish recorded carefully. Every recipe listed the ingredients, amounts, and steps — some even had simple sketches. Some pages had little notes in the margins, like 'Dad especially loves this dish' and 'Little Bao ate two bowls of rice the first time he tried this.' Seeing 'Little Bao' made me smile — that was my childhood nickname. Some recipes I'd eaten many times, like sweet and sour ribs and steamed egg custard; some I had no memory of, maybe dishes mom learned but never ended up making. I photographed every page and saved them on my phone, then carefully put the notebook back in the box. This notebook isn't just recipes — it records a family's daily life, evidence of a mother living with heart.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的整体情感是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the overall emotional tone of this passage?
questions.explanation: Fond details and appreciative language create a warm register throughout.

questions.q_zh: 作者找到菜谱本以后做了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author do after finding the recipe notebook?

options.text_en: Took it to the new home

options.pinyin: pāi le zhàopiàn cún zài shǒujī lǐ
options.text_en: Photographed each page and saved on phone

questions.explanation: The author photographed every page of the notebook and saved them on the phone -- preserving the handwriting digitally.

title: Seasonal Strawberries
text_zh: 每年春天，菜市场里最让我期待的就是草莓。第一批草莓上市的时候，个头不大，颜色也不是特别红，但咬一口，那种酸甜的味道会让你马上知道：春天来了。我喜欢买回来以后不洗，先闻一闻。新鲜的草莓有一种淡淡的花香，要凑近才闻得到。洗干净以后放在白色的盘子里，红红的，很好看。我不喜欢在草莓上加糖或者做成其他东西，觉得那样会盖住它本来的味道。最好的吃法就是洗干净直接吃，一颗一颗，慢慢品尝。草莓的季节很短，大概只有一个多月。过了这段时间，虽然超市里一年四季都能买到草莓，但那些大棚种出来的，味道总是差一些。
text_pinyin: Měi nián chūntiān, càishìchǎng lǐ zuì ràng wǒ qīdài de jiù shì cǎoméi. Dì yī pī cǎoméi shàng shì de shíhou, gètóu bú dà, yánsè yě bú shì tèbié hóng, dàn yǎo yì kǒu, nà zhǒng suān tián de wèidào huì ràng nǐ mǎshàng zhīdào: chūntiān lái le. Wǒ xǐhuan mǎi huílái yǐhòu bù xǐ, xiān wén yì wén. Xīnxiān de cǎoméi yǒu yì zhǒng dàn dàn de huāxiāng, yào còu jìn cái wén de dào. Xǐ gānjìng yǐhòu fàng zài báisè de pánzi lǐ, hóng hóng de, hěn hǎokàn. Wǒ bù xǐhuan zài cǎoméi shàng jiā táng huòzhě zuò chéng qítā dōngxi, juéde nàyàng huì gài zhù tā běnlái de wèidào. Zuì hǎo de chīfǎ jiù shì xǐ gānjìng zhíjiē chī, yì kē yì kē, mànmàn pǐncháng. Cǎoméi de jìjié hěn duǎn, dàgài zhǐ yǒu yí gè duō yuè. Guò le zhè duàn shíjiān, suīrán chāoshì lǐ yì nián sì jì dōu néng mǎi dào cǎoméi, dàn nàxiē dàpéng zhòng chūlái de, wèidào zǒngshì chà yìxiē. Yěxǔ zhèng shì yīnwèi duǎnzàn, suǒyǐ měi nián chūntiān chī dào dì yī kē cǎoméi de shíhou, nà zhǒng kāixīn cái tèbié zhēnguì.
text_en: Every spring, what I look forward to most at the market is strawberries. When the first batch arrives, they're not big and not particularly red, but one bite and that sweet-tart flavor tells you immediately: spring has come. I like to buy them and not wash them right away — first, I smell them. Fresh strawberries have a faint floral scent you can only catch up close. After washing, placed on a white plate, bright red, beautiful. I don't like adding sugar or turning them into something else — I think that covers their natural flavor. The best way is to wash them and eat them as they are, one by one, savoring slowly. Strawberry season is short, only about a month. After that, supermarkets carry strawberries year-round, but the greenhouse-grown ones always taste a bit off.

questions.q_zh: 作者想让读者感受到什么？
questions.q_en: What feeling does the author want the reader to experience?
questions.explanation: The warm sensory detail and gentle pacing are calibrated to produce a sense of simple satisfaction.

questions.q_zh: 草莓的季节大概有多长？
questions.q_en: How long is strawberry season approximately?
questions.explanation: Stated directly: strawberry season is short, only about a month.

title: The Neighborhood Restaurant
text_zh: 我家楼下的街角有一家小饭馆，开了快二十年了。店面不大，装修也很普通，但每到饭点都坐满了人。老板娘是一个很爱笑的中年女人，见到每个客人都像见到老朋友一样热情。她能记住很多常客的口味，有的人一进门她就会说「今天有你爱吃的干煸四季豆」或者「给你留了一碗酸辣汤」。饭馆的菜单很简单，都是家常菜，但味道特别好。尤其是他们家的麻婆豆腐，豆腐嫩得像果冻，麻辣的味道恰到好处。我搬来这个小区以后，几乎每个星期都去吃一两次。有时候一个人去，有时候和朋友一起。一个人去的时候，老板娘会特意多给我夹一块肉，说「一个人也要吃好」。这种小饭馆在大城市越来越少了，但只要它还在，这条街就有一种家的感觉。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià de jiējiǎo yǒu yì jiā xiǎo fànguǎn, kāi le kuài èrshí nián le. Diànmiàn bú dà, zhuāngxiū yě hěn pǔtōng, dàn měi dào fàndiǎn dōu zuò mǎn le rén. Lǎobǎnniáng shì yí gè hěn ài xiào de zhōngnián nǚrén, jiàn dào měi gè kèrén dōu xiàng jiàn dào lǎo péngyou yíyàng rèqíng. Tā néng jì zhù hěn duō chángkè de kǒuwèi, yǒu de rén yí jìn mén tā jiù huì shuō「jīntiān yǒu nǐ ài chī de gānbiān sìjìdòu」huòzhě「gěi nǐ liú le yì wǎn suānlà tāng」. Fànguǎn de càidān hěn jiǎndān, dōu shì jiāchángcài, dàn wèidào tèbié hǎo. Yóuqí shì tāmen jiā de mápó dòufu, dòufu nèn de xiàng guǒdòng, málà de wèidào qià dào hǎo chù. Wǒ bān lái zhège xiǎoqū yǐhòu, jīhū měi gè xīngqī dōu qù chī yí liǎng cì. Yǒu shíhou yí gè rén qù, yǒu shíhou hé péngyou yìqǐ. Yí gè rén qù de shíhou, lǎobǎnniáng huì tèyì duō gěi wǒ jiā yí kuài ròu, shuō「yí gè rén yě yào chī hǎo」. Zhè zhǒng xiǎo fànguǎn zài dà chéngshì yuè lái yuè shǎo le, dàn zhǐyào tā hái zài, zhè tiáo jiē jiù yǒu yì zhǒng jiā de gǎnjué.
text_en: At the corner below my building there's a small restaurant that's been open for nearly twenty years. The space is small, the decor ordinary, but at mealtimes every seat is taken. The owner's wife is a cheerful middle-aged woman who greets every customer like an old friend. She remembers many regulars' preferences — some people walk in and she'll say, 'Today we have your favorite dry-fried green beans,' or 'I saved you a bowl of hot and sour soup.' The menu is simple, all home-style dishes, but the flavors are exceptional. Especially their mapo tofu — the tofu as tender as jelly, the numbing-spicy flavor just right. Since moving to this neighborhood, I go almost once or twice a week. Sometimes alone, sometimes with friends. When I go alone, the owner's wife will deliberately put an extra piece of meat in my dish, saying, 'Even eating alone, you should eat well.' These kinds of small restaurants are becoming rarer in big cities, but as long as it's still here, this street has a feeling of home.

questions.q_zh: 这家饭馆开了多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long has this restaurant been open?
questions.explanation: Stated directly: at the corner below my building there's a.

questions.q_zh: 这段话暗示了什么情感？
questions.q_en: What emotion does this passage hint at?
questions.explanation: Beneath the surface details runs an undercurrent of something passing or already gone.

title: The Last Dumpling
text_zh: 我们家吃饭有一个习惯：盘子里最后一个饺子，谁都不好意思吃。每次过年包了很多饺子，一家人围着桌子吃得热热闹闹的，一盘接一盘地上，但到了最后总会剩下那么一两个。爸爸会说「你吃吧」，妈妈会说「给孩子吃」，我会说「我已经吃饱了」。那个饺子就在盘子里安安静静地待着，没有人动它。后来妈妈想了一个办法，她说最后一个饺子里包着好运气，谁吃了谁新的一年就会特别顺利。这样一来，大家反而开始抢了。有时候爸爸会故意让给我，说「年轻人更需要好运气」。我知道他是让着我，但还是会假装相信地开开心心吃掉。现在想起来，那个饺子里包的不是好运气，是一家人互相让来让去的温暖。那种温暖比任何馅料都珍贵。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen jiā chīfàn yǒu yí gè xíguàn: pánzi lǐ zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi, shéi dōu bù hǎoyìsi chī. Měi cì guònián bāo le hěn duō jiǎozi, yì jiā rén wéi zhe zhuōzi chī de rè rè nào nào de, yì pán jiē yì pán de shàng, dàn dào le zuìhòu zǒng huì shèng xià nàme yí liǎng gè. Bàba huì shuō「nǐ chī ba」, māma huì shuō「gěi háizi chī」, wǒ huì shuō「wǒ yǐjīng chī bǎo le」. Nàge jiǎozi jiù zài pánzi lǐ ān ān jìng jìng de dāi zhe, méiyǒu rén dòng tā. Hòulái māma xiǎng le yí gè bànfǎ, tā shuō zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi lǐ bāo zhe hǎo yùnqi, shéi chī le shéi xīn de yì nián jiù huì tèbié shùnlì. Zhèyàng yì lái, dàjiā fǎn'ér kāishǐ qiǎng le. Yǒu shíhou bàba huì gùyì ràng gěi wǒ, shuō「niánqīng rén gèng xūyào hǎo yùnqi」. Wǒ zhīdào tā shì ràng zhe wǒ, dàn háishi huì jiǎzhuāng xiāngxìn de kāi kāi xīn xīn chī diào. Xiànzài xiǎng qǐlái, nàge jiǎozi lǐ bāo de bú shì hǎo yùnqi, shì yì jiā rén hùxiāng ràng lái ràng qù de wēnnuǎn. Nà zhǒng wēnnuǎn bǐ rènhé xiànliào dōu zhēnguì.
text_en: Our family has a habit when eating: nobody feels comfortable taking the last dumpling on the plate. Every New Year we'd make lots of dumplings, the whole family eating lively around the table, plate after plate served, but in the end there'd always be one or two left. Dad would say 'You eat it,' Mom would say 'Give it to the child,' I'd say 'I'm already full.' That dumpling would just sit quietly on the plate, untouched. Later Mom came up with an idea — she said the last dumpling contains good luck, and whoever eats it will have a smooth new year. After that, everyone actually started competing for it. Sometimes Dad would deliberately let me have it, saying 'Young people need good luck more.' I knew he was being generous, but I'd pretend to believe it and eat it happily. Thinking about it now, what was wrapped inside that dumpling wasn't good luck — it was the warmth of a family yielding to each other back and forth. That kind of warmth is more precious than any filling.

questions.q_zh: 妈妈想的办法是什么？
questions.q_en: What was mom's idea?

options.text: 把最后的饺子分成几份
options.pinyin: bǎ zuìhòu de jiǎozi fēn chéng jǐ fèn
options.text_en: Split the last dumpling into pieces

options.text: 说最后一个饺子里有好运气
options.pinyin: shuō zuìhòu yí gè jiǎozi lǐ yǒu hǎo yùnqi
options.text_en: Said the last dumpling contains good luck

options.pinyin: měi cì shǎo bāo jǐ gè

questions.explanation: Mom declared the last dumpling carried good luck for the new year, turning a potential squabble over leftovers into a game everyone wanted to win.

questions.q_zh: 这种坚持说明这个人怎么样？
questions.q_en: What does this persistence tell us about the person?

options.text_en: has no ideas of their own

questions.explanation: The unwillingness to give up reveals deep commitment and stubbornness of purpose.

title: Harvest Moon Dinner
text_zh: 秋天是我最喜欢做饭的季节，因为这个季节的食材最丰富。市场上到处是金黄色的南瓜、深紫色的茄子、新鲜的板栗和各种各样的蘑菇。我喜欢买一个小南瓜回来，切开以后挖掉籽，切成小块蒸熟。蒸好的南瓜又甜又软，可以直接吃，也可以做成南瓜粥。板栗是秋天特有的味道，剥起来虽然有点麻烦，但那种甜甜的、粉粉的口感值得花时间。有时候我会做板栗烧鸡，板栗和鸡肉一起炖，味道特别好。秋天的晚上天黑得早，六点多天就暗了。在灯下做一顿暖和的晚饭，窗外的风有点凉，厨房里却是热乎乎的。把做好的菜摆在桌上，开一瓶啤酒，慢慢吃。秋天的晚餐不需要很丰盛，但一定要有季节的味道。这种跟着季节吃东西的习惯，让我觉得自己和自然还保持着一种联系。
text_pinyin: Qiūtiān shì wǒ zuì xǐhuan zuòfàn de jìjié, yīnwèi zhège jìjié de shícái zuì fēngfù. Shìchǎng shàng dàochù shì jīnhuángsè de nánguā, shēn zǐsè de qiézi, xīnxiān de bǎnlì hé gè zhǒng gè yàng de mógū. Wǒ xǐhuan mǎi yí gè xiǎo nánguā huílái, qiē kāi yǐhòu wā diào zǐ, qiē chéng xiǎo kuài zhēng shú. Zhēng hǎo de nánguā yòu tián yòu ruǎn, kěyǐ zhíjiē chī, yě kěyǐ zuò chéng nánguā zhōu. Bǎnlì shì qiūtiān tèyǒu de wèidào, bāo qǐlái suīrán yǒudiǎn máfan, dàn nà zhǒng tián tián de, fěn fěn de kǒugǎn zhídé huā shíjiān. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì zuò bǎnlì shāo jī, bǎnlì hé jīròu yìqǐ dùn, wèidào tèbié hǎo. Qiūtiān de wǎnshang tiān hēi de zǎo, liù diǎn duō tiān jiù àn le. Zài dēng xià zuò yí dùn nuǎnhuo de wǎnfàn, chuāng wài de fēng yǒudiǎn liáng, chúfáng lǐ què shì rè hūhū de. Bǎ zuò hǎo de cài bǎi zài zhuō shàng, kāi yì píng píjiǔ, mànmàn chī. Qiūtiān de wǎncān bù xūyào hěn fēngshèng, dàn yídìng yào yǒu jìjié de wèidào. Zhè zhǒng gēn zhe jìjié chī dōngxi de xíguàn, ràng wǒ juéde zìjǐ hé zìrán hái bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng liánxì.
text_en: Autumn is my favorite season to cook, because the ingredients are richest then. The market is full of golden pumpkins, deep purple eggplants, fresh chestnuts, and all kinds of mushrooms. I like to buy a small pumpkin, cut it open, scoop out the seeds, cut into chunks and steam. Steamed pumpkin is sweet and soft — eat it directly or make pumpkin porridge. Chestnuts have a flavor unique to autumn; peeling them is a bit tedious, but that sweet, starchy taste is worth the time. Sometimes I make braised chicken with chestnuts — stewed together, the flavor is wonderful. Autumn evenings get dark early, past six and it's already dim. Under the light, making a warm dinner, the wind outside a bit cool, the kitchen warm and cozy. Arrange the finished dishes on the table, open a bottle of beer, eat slowly. An autumn dinner doesn't need to be elaborate, but it must have the taste of the season. This habit of eating with the seasons makes me feel I still maintain a connection with nature.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么最喜欢秋天做饭？
questions.q_en: Why does the author most enjoy cooking in autumn?
questions.explanation: The passage explains: autumn is my favorite season to cook.

questions.q_zh: 天气在文中起什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the weather play in this passage?
questions.explanation: The weather operates as emotional mirror -- outer conditions track the character's inner state.

title: Sharing Food Across Cultures
text_zh: 我的邻居是一对日本夫妻，他们搬来的时候送了我一盒日式点心。我尝了以后觉得很好吃，就做了一盘红烧茄子送过去作为回礼。没想到他们特别喜欢，第二天就来敲门问我怎么做的。从那以后我们经常交换食物。他们教我做日式咖喱饭和味噌汤，我教他们做番茄炒蛋和凉拌黄瓜。有意思的是，同样一个食材，不同文化的做法完全不同。比如豆腐，中国人喜欢炒着吃或者炖着吃，日本人喜欢凉着吃，放上葱花和酱油。两种吃法都好吃，但是感觉完全不一样。有一次我们决定一起做一顿中日混合的晚餐。我做了几道中国菜，他们做了几道日本菜，摆满了整张桌子。大家一边吃一边用简单的英语和手势交流。语言不通也没关系，因为好吃的表情是全世界通用的。那顿饭让我明白，食物是最好的沟通方式之一。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de línjū shì yí duì Rìběn fūqī, tāmen bān lái de shíhou sòng le wǒ yì hé Rìshì diǎnxīn. Wǒ cháng le yǐhòu juéde hěn hǎochī, jiù zuò le yì pán hóngshāo qiézi sòng guòqù zuòwéi huílǐ. Méi xiǎng dào tāmen tèbié xǐhuan, dì èr tiān jiù lái qiāo mén wèn wǒ zěnme zuò de. Cóng nà yǐhòu wǒmen jīngcháng jiāohuàn shíwù. Tāmen jiāo wǒ zuò Rìshì gālí fàn hé wèizēng tāng, wǒ jiāo tāmen zuò fānqié chǎo dàn hé liángbàn huángguā. Yǒu yìsi de shì, tóngyàng yí gè shícái, bùtóng wénhuà de zuòfǎ wánquán bùtóng. Bǐrú dòufu, Zhōngguó rén xǐhuan chǎo zhe chī huòzhě dùn zhe chī, Rìběn rén xǐhuan liáng zhe chī, fàng shàng cōnghuā hé jiàngyóu. Liǎng zhǒng chīfǎ dōu hǎochī, dànshì gǎnjué wánquán bù yíyàng. Yǒu yí cì wǒmen juédìng yìqǐ zuò yí dùn Zhōng Rì hùnhé de wǎncān. Wǒ zuò le jǐ dào Zhōngguó cài, tāmen zuò le jǐ dào Rìběn cài, bǎi mǎn le zhěng zhāng zhuōzi. Dàjiā yìbiān chī yìbiān yòng jiǎndān de Yīngyǔ hé shǒushì jiāoliú. Yǔyán bù tōng yě méi guānxi, yīnwèi hǎochī de biǎoqíng shì quán shìjiè tōngyòng de. Nà dùn fàn ràng wǒ míngbai, shíwù shì zuì hǎo de gōutōng fāngshì zhī yī.
text_en: My neighbors are a Japanese couple. When they moved in, they gave me a box of Japanese sweets. I thought they were delicious and sent over a plate of braised eggplant in return. To my surprise they loved it, and the next day knocked on my door asking how to make it. Since then we often exchange food. They taught me Japanese curry rice and miso soup; I taught them scrambled eggs with tomato and cold cucumber salad. The interesting thing is, the same ingredient is prepared completely differently across cultures. Take tofu — Chinese people like to stir-fry or braise it, Japanese people like to eat it cold with scallions and soy sauce. Both ways are delicious, but the feeling is entirely different. Once we decided to make a Chinese-Japanese fusion dinner together. I made several Chinese dishes, they made several Japanese ones, filling the entire table. Everyone ate while communicating in simple English and gestures. Not speaking the same language didn't matter, because the expression for 'delicious' is universal. That meal taught me that food is one of the best forms of communication.

questions.q_zh: 邻居搬来的时候送了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the neighbors bring when they moved in?
questions.explanation: The Japanese neighbors brought a box of traditional sweets when they moved in, starting a food exchange.

questions.q_zh: 如果打破了这个习惯，可能会怎样？
questions.q_en: What might happen if this routine were broken?

options.pinyin: huì zhǎodào gèng hǎo de xíguàn

questions.explanation: The routine provides emotional scaffolding; disrupting it would remove that stability.

title: The Comfort of Congee
text_zh: 生病的时候最想吃的就是一碗白粥。不是那种加了很多东西的粥，就是最普通的白米粥，熬得稠稠的，米粒已经几乎化开了。小时候每次发烧，妈妈都会熬一锅白粥，盛在一个小碗里，旁边放一碟子咸菜。她会坐在床边，用勺子一口一口地喂我。那时候觉得白粥没什么味道，不如肉和菜好吃。但生病的时候胃口不好，别的东西都吃不下，只有白粥可以一口一口慢慢咽下去。温温的，软软的，到了肚子里很舒服。现在我自己生病了也会熬粥。把米洗好放进锅里，加很多水，开大火煮开以后转小火，偶尔搅一搅，防止粘锅。大概四十分钟以后，粥就熬好了。一个人坐在桌前喝着粥，虽然没有妈妈坐在旁边，但那种被温暖包围的感觉，还是一样的。
text_pinyin: Shēngbìng de shíhou zuì xiǎng chī de jiù shì yì wǎn bái zhōu. Bú shì nà zhǒng jiā le hěn duō dōngxi de zhōu, jiù shì zuì pǔtōng de bái mǐ zhōu, áo de chóu chóu de, mǐ lì yǐjīng jīhū huà kāi le. Xiǎo shíhou měi cì fāshāo, māma dōu huì áo yì guō bái zhōu, chéng zài yí gè xiǎo wǎn lǐ, pángbiān fàng yí diézi xiáncài. Tā huì zuò zài chuáng biān, yòng sháozi yì kǒu yì kǒu de wèi wǒ. Nà shíhou juéde bái zhōu méi shénme wèidào, bù rú ròu hé cài hǎochī. Dàn shēngbìng de shíhou wèikǒu bù hǎo, bié de dōngxi dōu chī bú xià, zhǐ yǒu bái zhōu kěyǐ yì kǒu yì kǒu mànmàn yàn xiàqù. Wēn wēn de, ruǎn ruǎn de, dào le dùzi lǐ hěn shūfu. Xiànzài wǒ zìjǐ shēngbìng le yě huì áo zhōu. Bǎ mǐ xǐ hǎo fàng jìn guō lǐ, jiā hěn duō shuǐ, kāi dà huǒ zhǔ kāi yǐhòu zhuǎn xiǎo huǒ, ǒu'ěr jiǎo yì jiǎo, fángzhǐ zhān guō. Dàgài sìshí fēnzhōng yǐhòu, zhōu jiù áo hǎo le. Yí gè rén zuò zài zhuō qián hē zhe zhōu, suīrán méiyǒu māma zuò zài pángbiān, dàn nà zhǒng bèi wēnnuǎn bāowéi de gǎnjué, háishi yíyàng de. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì shíwù de lìliàng — zài nǐ zuì cuìruò de shíhou, yì wǎn jiǎndān de zhōu jiù néng gěi nǐ lìliàng.
text_en: When sick, what I most want to eat is a bowl of plain congee. Not the kind loaded with things, just the most ordinary white rice porridge, simmered thick until the grains have nearly dissolved. As a child, every time I had a fever, Mom would make a pot of plain congee, served in a small bowl with a dish of pickled vegetables on the side. She'd sit at the bedside, feeding me spoonful by spoonful. Back then I thought congee had no flavor, not as good as meat and vegetables. But when sick, with no appetite, nothing else goes down — only congee can be swallowed slowly, mouthful by mouthful. Warm, soft, comfortable in the stomach. Now when I'm sick, I also make congee. Wash the rice, put it in the pot, add lots of water, bring to a boil on high then turn to low, stir occasionally to prevent sticking. About forty minutes later, the congee is done. Sitting alone at the table drinking congee, even without Mom beside me, that feeling of being wrapped in warmth is still the same.

questions.q_zh: 小时候妈妈熬粥的时候旁边放什么？
questions.q_en: What did mom put beside the congee when making it in childhood?
questions.explanation: Whenever the author had a fever as a child, mom would place pickled vegetables beside the congee.

questions.q_zh: 熬粥大概需要多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long does it take to make congee?
questions.explanation: Stated directly: about forty minutes later, the congee is done.

title: Morning Buns at the Corner Shop
text_zh: 每天早上六点半，街口的包子铺就开始冒蒸汽了。还没走近就能闻到面粉和肉的香味，那种味道在清晨的冷空气里特别好闻。包子铺很小，只有一个窗口，里面是两个忙碌的人——老板和他的妻子。老板负责包，他的手特别快，一个包子只需要几秒钟就包好了。妻子负责蒸和卖，她会把蒸好的包子一笼一笼地摆在窗口，热气从竹蒸笼里往上飘。他们家的包子有三种馅：猪肉大葱、韭菜鸡蛋和豆沙的。我最喜欢猪肉大葱的，皮薄馅多，咬一口会流出一点汤汁。每天早上买两个包子加一杯豆浆，站在路边吃完，然后去上班。这个小小的仪式感让每一天的开始都有了温度。有时候排队的人多，大家在寒风里站着等，但没有人抱怨。因为大家都知道，等一等是值得的。好的早餐，值得你早起五分钟。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn bàn, jiēkǒu de bāozi pù jiù kāishǐ mào zhēngqì le. Hái méi zǒu jìn jiù néng wén dào miànfěn hé ròu de xiāngwèi, nà zhǒng wèidào zài qīngchén de lěng kōngqì lǐ tèbié hǎo wén. Bāozi pù hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu yí gè chuāngkǒu, lǐmiàn shì liǎng gè mánglù de rén — lǎobǎn hé tā de qīzi. Lǎobǎn fùzé bāo, tā de shǒu tèbié kuài, yí gè bāozi zhǐ xūyào jǐ miǎozhōng jiù bāo hǎo le. Qīzi fùzé zhēng hé mài, tā huì bǎ zhēng hǎo de bāozi yì lóng yì lóng de bǎi zài chuāngkǒu, rèqì cóng zhú zhēnglóng lǐ wǎng shàng piāo. Tāmen jiā de bāozi yǒu sān zhǒng xiàn: zhūròu dàcōng, jiǔcài jīdàn hé dòushā de. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan zhūròu dàcōng de, pí báo xiàn duō, yǎo yì kǒu huì liú chū yìdiǎn tāngzhī. Měi tiān zǎoshang mǎi liǎng gè bāozi jiā yì bēi dòujiāng, zhàn zài lù biān chī wán, ránhòu qù shàngbān. Zhège xiǎo xiǎo de yíshì gǎn ràng měi yì tiān de kāishǐ dōu yǒu le wēndù. Yǒu shíhou páiduì de rén duō, dàjiā zài hánfēng lǐ zhàn zhe děng, dàn méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn. Yīnwèi dàjiā dōu zhīdào, děng yì děng shì zhídé de. Hǎo de zǎocān, zhídé nǐ zǎo qǐ wǔ fēnzhōng.
text_en: Every morning at 6:30, the bun shop on the corner starts steaming. Before you even get close you can smell the flour and meat — that scent is especially wonderful in the cold morning air. The shop is tiny, just one window, behind it two busy people — the owner and his wife. He's in charge of wrapping, his hands incredibly fast, each bun done in just seconds. His wife handles steaming and selling, placing finished buns basket by basket at the window, steam rising from the bamboo steamers. They have three fillings: pork and scallion, chive and egg, and red bean paste. My favorite is pork and scallion — thin skin, generous filling, a bit of broth flows out when you bite. Every morning I buy two buns and a cup of soy milk, eat standing by the road, then head to work. This small ritual gives every day's beginning a warmth. Sometimes the line is long, everyone standing in the cold wind waiting, but nobody complains. Because everyone knows the wait is worth it. A good breakfast is worth getting up five minutes early.

questions.q_zh: 天气在文中起什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the weather play in this passage?
questions.explanation: The weather operates as emotional mirror -- outer conditions track the character's inner state.

questions.q_zh: 自然环境对故事有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the natural setting affect the story?
questions.explanation: The natural details set an emotional backdrop that colors the entire scene.
